# Terrestrial Coordinatesand the Rotation of the Earth

BRENTARCHINAL,BETHSTETZLER, JAMESSLATER,&NIKOLAOSPAVLIS

# 5.1 Introduction

The task of establishing ordefining the terrestrial coordinates of apoint is inextricably linked to establishing the rotationof the Earth over time.The definition ofany terrestrial reference system(TRS) is given by establishing acelestial reference coordinate system(CRS) and a suitable transformation between them.This transformationis whatis generallycalled Earth orientation (or Earth rotation),including the various changes inthe orientationof the Earth's axis in space and internally,and the rotation (and rotational variations) about that axis.(This latter rotationisalso oftendefined as Earth rotation—both uses of this termwill bemade here with the meaning being given from context.) One could connect the celestial and terrestrial coordinate systemat any giveninstant using threeEulerianangles,and then forma time seriesof such angles to define Earth orientation.However,for historical reasons (mostly in order to associate the motions of the Earth with physical processes and to allow for successive approximations of the motion), in practice these angles are expressed in terms of precession, nutation,polar motion,and rotation about the Earth's axis;see Chapter6fordetails on these topics.

Before we proceed into any explanation of terrestrial coordinates,it is also necessary todistinguish between a(celestial or terrestrial) referencesystemandareference frame. Areference system is the conceptual definitionofan “ideal”coordinate system,based on some abstract principle(s).For example,an ideal terrestrial system would be one in which the Earth's crust shows only deformation with no rotations or translations.A conventional reference systemisone where the model used to define coordinates is given in detail;thatis, specific model choices have been made.The realization of a reference system,using actual observations,adopted station coordinates,etc.,providesareference frame (Kovalevskyand Mueller1981;Mueller1988;Kovalevsky,Mueller,andKalaczek 1989).

This chapter will begin with a discussion on the concept of terrestrial reference systems andframes and give some detail on the current standard system(ITRS)and some widely used frames (ITRF,WGS 84).Various coordinate systems for measuring longitude and latitude areintroduced.Alsoincluded is information on the actual transformationof coordinates from one frame to another.This will be followed by information,mostly extracted from the IERS Conventions (20lO) (IERS201O),regardingthe Earth's gravity field (toa limited extent), tides,and plate tectonics.Methods for monitoringtheEarth'srotation (past,present,and future)are described and information on the IERSand thedata it produces is provided.

An overview of Earth rotation and its causes in general has not been given because space is lacking for such a complete treatment,and the ample coverage from this viewpoint by


<!-- chunk 0005: pages 161-200 -->
Teisseyre(1989),MoritzandMueller(1987),Lambeck(1980),andMunk andMacDonald (1960).The motion of the Earth's celestial pole due to precesson and nutation is covered in Chapter 6.[Readers seeking more detailed information on the subjects of gravity,geodesy, geodetic astronomy,and terrestrial reference systems should consult standard texts such as Mueller and Rockie (1966),Heiskanen and Moritz (1967),Mueller(1969),Bomford(1971), Moritz(1980),MoritzandMueller(1987),Kovalevsky,Mueller,andKolaczek(1989),rge (2001),and McCarthy and Seidelmann (2009).]

# 5.2 Terrestrial Reference Systems

# 5.2.1 Reference Systems and Reference Frame Concepts

Locating a point on or near the Earth's surface requires the definition of areference system that enables aset of coordinates to be unambiguously associated with or assigned to the point. It also provides a consistent means for identifying the location of one point with respect to another.Todothis,the conceptofaterrestrialreference systemis introduced.Suchasystem is generally defined by three orthogonal Cartesian coordinate axes $( x , y , z )$ fixed to the Earth's crust,thelocationof the origin,a specified orientation in space,anda setofmathematical and physical models,constants,conventions,and parameters.A geocentric terrestrial reference system is one in which the origin coincides with the geocenter-the center of mass of the solid Earth,oceans and atmosphere-and in which the coordinate axes rotate with the Earth. Thishas been adopted by the international scientific communityas the standard conceptual definition of a global geodetic reference system.The $z$ -axis is oriented along the rotationaxis of the Earth,the $x$ -and $y$ -axesare in the equatorial plane,the $x$ -axis intersects the equator at the point where areference(or zero)meridian crosses it,and the $y$ -axisis perpendicular to the $x$ -and $z$ -axestocompletearight-handed system.Astandard set of proceduresand models (conventions),prescribedby the International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS),formally relates this Earth-centered Earth-fixed reference system to the Celestial Reference System (see Chapter 4).The geocentric terrestrial reference system with these adopted conventionsis designatedas the International Terrestrial ReferenceSystem (ITRS) (IERS 2004).

The practical realization of a terrestrial reference system is accomplished by establishing a set of reference points on the topographic surface of the Earth and determining their 3- dimensional Cartesian coordinates and velocities from actual observations,currently done byusing space geodetic techniques and applying the reference system conventions.These physical points and theunderlying conventions then define the geocentric terrestrialreference frame.The more accurately the reference point positions can be determined,the better the reference framecan be realized.

Two of the most widely used geocentric terrestrial reference frames,the International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF) and theWorld Geodetic System (WGS),are described in more detail below.Inaddition,South Americaand Europe have implemented regional realizations of the ITRS—Sistemade Referencia Geocentrico para Las Américas (SIRGAS) (Hoyeret al.1998;URL[1]) and the European TerrestrialReferenceFrame(ETRF),(URL[2]) respectively.By adopting the IERS Conventions and incorporating ITRF reference points (stations) into the individual reference frame realizations,WGS,SIRGAS and ETRF(and others)attempt to constrain theirframes to have the same origin,orientation and scale as the ITRF.

# 5.2.1.1The International Terrestrial Reference System

The International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS) specifiesaset of physical constants, conventions and models to be used for estimating the coordinates of points with respect to the Earth.The ITRS is defined to be geocentric with its orientation traceable to that of the Bureau International deI'Heure (BIH) Terrestrial Systemat 1984.O; its standard unit of length is the meter(SI),and its time evolution is constrained toa conditionof no-net-rotation with regard to horizontal plate tectonic motions over the Earth asawhole (IERS2OO4,p.25).

Thephysical realization (or materialization) of the ITRS is the International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF),definedby the 3-dimensional coordinatesand linearvelocities ofa global network of ground stations(on the Earth's crust) at a specific epoch in time. Thesecoordinates and velocities are derived from observations collected at the stations using four space-based techniques:Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI),satellite laser ranging (SLR),the Global Positioning System (GPS),and Doppler Orbitography and Radiopositioning Integratedby Satellite (DORIS). The origin,coordinate system orientation andthe scaleareimplicitlydefined by theCartesiancoordinatesand velocities ofthe stations, and the solutions conform to the ITRS and IERS conventions and standards.This represents the fundamental scientific standard reference frame (Boucher and Altamimi1996).

The ITRS isa direct continuation of the BIHTerrestrial System (BTS),first established in 1985and continued through 1987.The BTS had in turn evolved fromthe BIH1968 System, which was established in 1969 by the BIHon the basis of a comparison of data collected by the BIHand the International Polar Motion Service (IPMS) during the mid-196Os.However, it should be emphasized that this was not a complete terrestrial reference system; it was a referencesystemfor orientation only,being based on the observed astronomical coordinates of the involved observing stations.The comparison of the BIHcollected data with the IPMS data allowed the BIH1968 System to be referenced to the IPMS reference pole.This pole is the Conventional International Originl which was the approximate average position of the true celestial pole ofthe Earth from19o0.O to 1905.O,but wasactually definedby the adopted latitudes of five International Latitude Service (ILS)stations.(For further information on the history of the terrestrial reference frame prior to 1988,see IERS (1989),or the BIH Annual Reports for the appropriate year(s),or see Mueller(1969) for further information on these systems for the pre-1969 period.)

The IERSReference Pole (IRP)and Reference Meridianare consistentwith the corresponding directions in the BIH Terrestrial System(BTS) within $\pm 0 . 0 0 5$ The BIH reference pole was adjusted to the Conventional International Origin in 1967.It was then kept stable independentlyuntil1987.Re-reductionof the available data (BIH1989,p.A-3)tied the BTS (and therefore the IRP) with the Conventional International Originto $\pm 0 \prime \prime 0 3$ .

Asnoted in the2O10 update to the IERSconventions (IERS 2O1O,chapter4),there have been 12 versions of the ITRF released since 1988.ITRF2o05 used,for the first time as input, time seriesof station positions and Earth orientation parametervalues from each of the space geodesy techniques.The most recent realization of the ITRF was computed by the IERS in 2008 with an epoch of 2Oo5.0 (Altamimi,Collilieux and Métivier 2O11).This solution used the same procedure as ITRF2oo5,incorporating observations from VLBI (29 years), SLR(26 years),DORIS (16 years) and GPS (12.5 years).ITRF2008 is constituted by 934 stations at 580 sites, with 105 of those sites supporting two or more of the four space geodesy techniques.Transformation parameters computed from ITRF20o8 to ITRF2005 show no rotational differences,about a $5 \mathrm { m m }$ difference in the location of the origins,and O.94 parts per billion scale difference (IERS 2010,Table 4.1).

# 5.2.1.2 The World Geodetic System1984

The terrestrial reference system used by the U.S.Department of Defense is the World GeodeticSystem 1984(WGS84) (NIMA1997).It isalso the standard reference system for the Global Positioning System(GPS).WGS 84 actually constitutes acoherent set of global modelsand definitions that include the WGS 84 terrestrial reference frame,aprescribed set of constants,models and conventions that are largely adopted from the IERS Standards (IERS 2004;IERS1996),an Earth Gravitational Model (currently EGM2008),and a World MagneticModel (currentlyWMM201O).For more detailsonEGM2008,seePavlis etal.(2012) and URL[4],and formore detailsonWMM2010,seeURL[5],URL[6],andMaus (2010). The Defense Mapping Agency was responsible for the initial development and maintenance oftheWorld Geodetic System,and this responsibility passed to its successor agencies,the National ImageryandMappingAgency (NIMA) and theNational Geospatial-Intelligence Agency (NGA).The World Geodetic System geocentric terrestrial reference frame Was first developed in 1960 with subsequent updates in 1966 (WGS 66),1972 (WGS 72),and 1984 (WGS 84).The original WGS 84 reference frame was realized from aset of Transit satellite (Doppler) stationcoordinates and aligned ascloselyas possible with the origin,scale and orientation of the BIH Terrestrial System.Transit-realized WGS84 coordinates were derived for the Department of Defense GPS monitor stations and were used until1994.Refinements were made to theWGS84 reference frame in1994,1996,and 2002and the reference frames are respectively designated asWGS 84(G730),WGS84 (G873) and WGS 84(G1150).The G'indicates that the reference frame is derived from GPS observations and the number is the GPS Week Number that the reference frame became operationally effective for GPS orbit production by NGA and the Air Force's GPS Operations.

TheWGS 84 reference frame is defined bya global network of GPS stationswhose coordinatesare closely alignedto the ITRF.This has beendonebyconstrainingthe coordinate solutions to the ITRF through the incorporation of up to several dozen independent GPS reference stations with well-defined ITRF positions and velocities in these solutions.Each realizationof the WGS 84 reference frame has improved the reference frame accuracy relative totheITRF.WGS84(G1150) wasaligned to ITRF200O to approximately $1 \ \mathrm { c m }$ ineach coordinate (Malysand Slater 1994;SlaterandMalys1998;Merriganetal.20O2).Another realignment of the WGS 84 referenceframe to ITRF2008 is expected inthe 2011-2012 time frame,as wellas adoption of relevant parts of the IERS Conventions (2OlO)(IERS 2010) into theupdatedWGS 84 reference system.

Since the GPS reference orbits computed by the Air Force are in the WGS 84reference frame,the broadcast satelite navigation message orbits generated from these are also referenced to WGS 84.Therefore,any position coordinates derived directly from the GPS broadcast orbits willalso bein the WGS 84 reference frame.Due to the few centimeter alignment of the WGS 84 reference frame with the ITRF,for most practical applications,WGS 84 and ITRFare considered equivalent,and no transformation is required between the reference frames.

# 5.2.2The Figure of the Earth and Geodetic Reference Surfaces

Notethat,up to this point,there has been noexplicit need to introduce aconceptual model for the Earth's surface;however,most positioning and navigation applications need to convert Cartesian coordinates to more meaningful coordinates that refer to some representation of the surface fromalocal,regional or global perspective.Froma purely geometric perspective, thebest fiting figureof theEarth isarotational elipsoid since its shapecanconform to theflatteningof theEarthatthepolesand itisrelatively simplemathematically.(Amore complicated surface,such asa triaxial ellipsoid,will not greatly improve the representation, but will greatly complicate the mathematics involved in using it.) Latitude and longitude can be defined as horizontal coordinateswith respect to the elipsoid surface,and heights can bereferenced to the ellipsoid aswell. Conventionally,the longitude line passing through Greenwich,England was chosen as the zero longitude and the equator defined zero latitude.

Prior to the era of satellite geodesy,individual countries or geographic regions would adoptreference ellipsoids that seemed to best fit the country or region,but asa result, these ellipsoids were frequently not geocentric and not necessarily aligned with the Earth's rotationaxis and equator. For global applications and especially satellite operations,reference ellipsoids are defined to be geocentric and their semimajor and semiminor axes aligned with the axes of a geocentric terrestrial reference frame.Coordinates such as those derived from GlobalPositioningSystemobservationscanbereadilyconverted fromCartesian $x$ , $y$ ,zto latitude,longitudeand height above the ellipsoid or the reverse (see $\ S 5 . 2 . 4 )$ .Thechoiceofa reference ellipsoid is inherent in the definitions of geodetic datums (see $\ S 5 . 2 . 5 . 3 \}$ and map projections (see $\ S 5 . 2 . 5 . 2 \}$ which will be discussed laterin thischapter.

Although mathematically pleasing,the abstract nature of the ellipsoid makes it less than optimal for visualizing terrain or the sea surface,and it cannot be used to determine the direction that water willflow.A reference surface that is better suited for these purposes is onedefinedbyalevel surface of equal gravity potential,also calleda geopotential surface or geop.Note that gravity potential here includes the centrifugal potential and the gravitational potential.There are aninfinite number of geopotential surfaces that theoreticallycan be defined;however,the one that“best fits”the mean sea level ischosenasthe standard geopotential reference surface.This particular level surface is called the geoid.Conceptually, thegeoid can be thought of as an imaginary“surface coincidingwith the mean sea-level in the oceans,and lying under the land at the level to which thesea would reach if admited by smallfrictionless channels"(Bomford1971).Various otherdefinitions are givenin Heiskanen and Moritz(1967,pp.48-50);Moritz(1980,p.6);and Torge(1980,pp.2-3).Elevation can then be expressed as height above mean sea level or height with respect to the geoid, where mean sea level and the geoid define zero elevation.Atany point on the Earth,the gravity vector will be perpendicular to the level surface (geop) that passes through that point. Frequently,instruments or sensors operating on the topographic surface of the Earth need to bealigned with the local vertical,in effect the local gravity vector,and accomplish this through the use of plumb bobs,bubble levelsand poolsof mercury.The geopotential surface isalso a critical construct in hydrology,since water bodies conform to a level surface and water flows“downhill’from higher potential to lower potential.Itis important to realize thatthe geoid surface is irregular and determined by the distribution of the local underlying mass.In practice,gravity potential must be derived fromactual aerial and terrestrial gravity measurements,and satellite orbital data.On the other hand,the mean sea surface is derived from global satellite altimetry measurements of the sea surface and local coastal tide gauge observations.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/75f7322c77ea6c35091f5364c71aecafa134700f39aee264bc50f8a44b7f0d7b.jpg)  
Figure5.1 The Earth'ssurface and reference surfaces

Therelationships among the three reference surfaces—the topography,ellpsoid and geopotential—are illustrated in Fig.5.1.The observerisat point $p$ on the surface of the Earth (topography).Areference ellipsoid lies below the topography.A geop passes through $\boldsymbol { p }$ ,but the geoid itself lies close to the reference ellipsoid.Note that although the geop and geoid surfacesare not the sameas that of the topography,there are some similarities since the mass of the topography influences the local gravity.Also shown in the figure are the geoid undulation $( N )$ ,whichistheheight separationbetween the geoid and the ellipsoid, the ellipsoidal height $( h )$ ，which isthe heightof $p$ above the ellipsoid,and the mean sea level height or orthometric height ( $\boldsymbol { H }$ ),whichisthe heightof $\boldsymbol { p }$ above the geoid. These are related as:

$$
N \approx h - H .
$$

Thisrelationship isapproximate since $H$ isactually measured along the curved vertical or plumb line through $p$ ，whereas $h$ and $N$ are measured along a straight line through $P$ perpendicular to the ellipsoid.For the best current reference ellipsoid and global geoid models,the separation between them does not exceed $1 0 0 \mathrm { m }$ .

# 5.2.3 Geodetic Datums

The set of parameters that defines the relationship between a specific reference ellipsoid and aterrestrial referencesystem iscalledageodeticdatum.A global geodeticdatumisone in which the reference ellipsoid is the“best fit”for the Earth as whole,its origin is geocentric, andits major and minor axes are coincident with the coordinate axes of the adopted terrestrial reference system.A local geodetic datum,on the other hand,is one in which the reference ellpsoid is frequently not geocentricand itssurfaceisabest fitonlytothelocal orregional areafor which it is intended to be used.The axes of the local reference ellipsoid also may not be aligned with the coordinate axes ofa geocentric terrestrialreference system.For mapping andsurveying applications,especiallypriorto satellite technology,datums were traditionally defined aseither horizontal datums or vertical datums.The horizontaldatumprovidesameans of identifying latitude and longitude of apoint on the surface of the reference ellipsoid,while the height of the point is separately determined with respectto local meansea level orageoid model (the vertical datum).

Alocal horizontal datum is defined by the size and shape of the reference ellipsoid and by the positioning of the ellipsoid relative to the figure of the Earth (represented by the geoid). The ellipsoid's size and shape are specified by two parameters,generally its semimajor axis $( a )$ and flattening $( f )$ (therelative difference in length of the semimajor and semiminor axes).An initial point (origin) is chosen near the surface of the ellipsoid (specified by its astronomical latitude and longitude, $( \Phi , \Lambda ) )$ ,andthedeflectionoftheverticalandgeoid undulation $( N )$ determined at the chosen origin along with an azimuth $( \alpha )$ from the origin to another point. See $\ S 5 . 2 . 5 . 3$ andFigs.5.2and5.3.The ellipsoid ischosen so as to represent the geoid aswellaspossible over the area of interest (a region or the entire Earth).The position of the ellipsoid with respect to the geoid is given by the deflection of the vertical (theangle between the local vertical gravity direction and the elipsoidal normal) and the geoid undulation.Historically,local datums were usually established in support of large triangulation networks.The origin was chosen to be at the center of the network in order to minimize errors in measurements relative to the origin as one moved outward in the network.In order to simplify the datum definition,the deflectionof the vertical and geoid undulation were frequently chosen to be zero at the origin,in effect forcing the ellipsoid and geoid to be coincident at that point.By imposing the condition that the vertical axis of the reference ellipsoid be parallel with the Earth's rotationaxis,the deflection of the vertical and geoid undulation can then be related to the offset between the center of the ellipsoid and the geocenter. Thisis equivalentto a translational shift in $x$ , $y$ and $z$ between a coordinate system whose origin isat the geocenter andacoordinate system whose origin isat the center of the local datum's ellipsoid. Specific datums and datum conversions are discussed in $\ S 5 . 2 . 5 . 3$ and $\ S 5 . 2 . 5 . 4$ .

Many maps are still based on local datums.In these cases the reference ellipsoid is used onlyas a horizontal (2-dimensional) datum,whose origin and orientation are defined by six topocentric parameters.North American Datum 1927 (NAD 27) is one example.Its origin isasurvey point inKansas.North American Datum 1983(NAD 83) replacedNAD 27 asthe offcial horizontal datum for the U.S.in 1989 (NOAA 1989).It was derived from Doppler satellite observationsasageocentric global datum.NAD 83 removed significant local distortions inNAD 27and replaced the Clarke 1866 reference elipsoid with the Geodetic Reference System 198O (GRS 80) elipsoid.However,it still retained some biases in the origin and orientation and wasused primarily as a horizontal datum.Beginning in 1994,to meet the growing demands and capabilities of geodetic surveying with GPS,the National Geodetic Survey organized a network of continuously operating reference stations that were positioned using GPS and tied to the ITRF.Additional stations have been added to the network over time and new 3-dimensional coordinates have been computed,tying these stations to the latest realizations of the ITRF(Snay and Soler 2000).

Avertical datum is the reference surface for orthometric heights.Vertical datums are traditionally definedasMean SeaLevel (MSL) oraveraged tidal observations based on low or high water (for example,Mean LowerLow Water).Many national and regional vertical datums are tied to a local mean sea level,which may differ significantly (decimeters) from global meansea level due to local effects such as river run off and extremes in coastal tidal effects.Thus,such nationaland regional vertical datumsaround the world willdifferfrom one another significantly when considered onaglobal basis.North American Vertical Datum1988 (NAVD 88)applied this concept by adopting the single tide gauge elevationatPoint Rimouski, Quebec,Canada as the continental elevation reference point and essentially references all other elevationsin the U.S.to this.NAVD88 was designated the official vertical datum of theU.S.in1993.Bycontrast,theU.S.National VerticalDatum1929(NGVD29)was fixed to a set of reference tide gauges,without correction forlocal variations in the sea state,as a method of defining the vertical reference.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/ae53ced20853be6b69aa66f666abf9147f9c386987b595cf0ae95906d5a7fb60.jpg)  
Figure 5.2 Geocentric $( \phi ^ { \prime } )$ and geodetic $( \phi )$ latitude

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/3d9f8dfc22c2d911876c0d0a5b69987051383874481b5aba33cc94e79e5728fc.jpg)  
Figure5.3 Astronomical latitude and longitude

Fromaglobal perspective,the best fiting approximation of the mean sea surface is the geopotential surface represented by the global geoid.Due to effects such as atmospheric pressure,temperature,prevailing windsand currents,and salinity variations,the mean sea level will depart from the geoid bya meter or more.Thus,the geoid can be considered as a surrogateformeansealevel,butnotequal tomeansealevel,whichisanimportantdistinction. Once defined,the geoid becomes the zero-elevation surface to which orthometric heights can be referenced worldwide.The best model currently is the WGS84 EGM2oO8 geoid,which is accurate to better than $1 5 \mathrm { { c m } }$ (RMS error) over areaswhere high accuracy gravity data were available for inclusion in the model (Pavlis etal.2O12;URL[4]).

The pervasiveness of global navigation satellite systems has shifted the traditional concept ofseparate horizontal and vertical datums to one of 3-dimensional global datums.Satellite positioning enables one to obtain a 3-dimensional position in Cartesian $( x , y , z )$ coordinates, orlatitude,longitudeand height,with respect to aglobal geocentric datumand itsreference ellipsoidanywherein theworld.Ageoid modelcan thenbeused to convert ellipsoidal heights to orthometric heights if needed.For such a global geodetic datum,the reference ellipsoid is defined to beanequipotential surface,with itsangular velocity equal to that of the Earth,and an associated gravitational constant $_ { G M }$ .Fourfundamental parameters must be chosen as defining constants for an equipotential ellipsoid.The semimajor axis (a), inverseflattening $( 1 / f$ ）， $G M$ ,and the angular velocity are one possible set,but other related groups of independent parameters also may be used.Inthe case of the International Terrestrial Reference System,the GRs 8O ellipsoid is specified as the reference ellipsoid (Moritz 1992). Instead of specifying $f$ ,itusesasa fundamentalconstant $J _ { 2 }$ ,the coefficient in the spherical harmonic representation of the gravitational potential thatis related to the dynamical form of the Earth (Stacey and Davis 2008,pp.81-89).The WGS 84 reference ellipsoid is essentially the same as GRS80,with the same values for $a$ , $_ { G M }$ and theangular velocity,but it specifies a value for $f$ ,not $J _ { 2 }$ .Theresultisadifference of $0 . 1 \mathrm { m m }$ inthe derivedvalue of the semiminor axis $^ b$ in the two ellipsoids,so forall practical purposes the ellipsoidsare identical (NIMA 1997,p.3-2)).For more information on datums and map projections see,for example,Iliffe and Lott (2008).

# 5.2.4Geocentric,Geodetic,and Astronomical Coordinates

Variouscoordinate systems exist for measuring longitude and latitude.Geocentric coordinatesaredeterminedfromthe centerofthe Earth; geodeticcoordinatesare determinedrelative toareferenceellipsoid;and astronomical coordinatesare determinedrelative to theobserver's local vertical.See,for example,Misra and Enge(2Oo1,chapter3),Seeber (2Oo3,chapter2) and Torge(2Oo1).Figure 5.2illustrates the difference between geocentric and geodetic latitude,once again for a point $p$ on the Earth'ssurface.The geocentric latitude $\phi ^ { \prime }$ ismeasured in the meridian plane,and is the angle between the ellipsoid’s equatorial plane and the line from $p$ to the center of the ellipsoid.The geodetic latitude $\phi$ is the angle between the elipsoid's equatorial plane and the normal from $P$ to the ellipsoid (at point $p$ ）； $^ a$ and $^ b$ are the semimajor and semiminor axes of the ellipsoid, respectively; $f$ ,the flattening,or $1 / f$ ,the inverse flattening,is often used,with

$$
f = { \frac { a - b } { a } } .
$$

The geocentric longitude $( \lambda )$ is measured in the equatorial plane,and is defined by the angle between the reference meridian and the meridian of point $p$ (and $p ^ { \prime }$ ),measuredeastward around the Earth from $0 ^ { \circ }$ to $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ (IAU1983,p.47).The geodetic longitudewillbe the same as the geocentric longitude if the reference ellipsoid has been chosen with the same axes, reference meridianandcenteras the Earth-this isassumedtobe the case in furtherdiscussion here.The astronomical coordinates of point $p$ aredetermined relative to the local vertical (plumb line) direction (or zenith) at point $p$ (seeFig.5.3,where $z$ indicates the direction of the zenith and $z ^ { \prime }$ the direction of the ellipsoidal normal).A vertical (or plumb)line passing through $p$ will intersect the Earth's equatorial plane with theangle $\Phi$ ,theastronomical latitude of $P$ .Astronomical longitude $\Lambda$ istheangle between the reference meridian plane and the plane including the local vertical through $\boldsymbol { p }$ ,measured along the equator. Note that the local vertical doesnot necessarily pass through the Earth's axis.

Theangle between the local vertical directionand ellpsoidal normal through $P$ is known asthedeflectionofthevertical $\theta$ .Thedeflection of the verticalisoftenbroken intotwo orthogonal components, $\xi$ and $\eta$ ,(seeFig.5.4,where $z$ indicates the direction of the zenith, and $z ^ { \prime }$ the direction of the ellipsoidal normal),with $\xi$ in the meridional direction,and $\eta$ in the orthogonal prime vertical direction.Relations between the geodetic and astronomical longitude and latitude and these components of the deflection of the vertical are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \sin \phi = \cos \eta \sin ( \Phi - \xi ) } \\ { \sin \eta = \cos \phi \sin ( \Lambda - \lambda ) . } \end{array}
$$

Using small-angle formulae (sin $\alpha \simeq \alpha$ ,cos $\alpha \simeq 1$ )these can alsobe given as

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \xi = \Phi - \phi } } \\ { { \eta = ( \Lambda - \lambda ) \cos \phi . } } \end{array}
$$

The deflection of the vertical at a point on the Earth can be determined with high accuracy from stellar observations using a specially designed vertical telescope to obtain the astronomical position $( \Phi , \Lambda )$ and GPS satellite observations to obtain the geodetic position $( \phi , \lambda )$ at the same point,and then combining them in the equations above $( \mathrm { H i r t } 2 0 0 1$ ).

The height or radial component of point $p$ is expressed in different ways depending upon the coordinate system being used.As discussed in $\ S 5 . 2 . 2$ ,for geodetic coordinates, the ellipsoidal height $( h )$ is the height component,while in other cases,height above mean sea level or the geoid ( $H$ )is appropriate,especially when the coordinates arerelated to the leveling of an instrument with respect to local gravity.Ina geocentric Cartesian coordinate system,the geocentric radius $r$ is used to define the radial position of a point using the algebraicrelationship $r = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } }$ . $r$ is also the radial component for a geocentric geographical coordinate system $( \phi ^ { \prime } , \lambda , r )$ ,where $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is the angle between the $x$ -yplane and the radial vector to point $p$ ,and $\lambda$ is the angle in the $x$ -yplanemeasured from the $x$ -axisto the projection of $p$ on the $x$ $y$ plane;seeFig.5.5.Conversion from(geocentric) geographical coordinates to (geocentric) Cartesian coordinates is given by

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/74c803d4a9cf5fe02f1f9cf79915c0e8b15fb30d46a8d20ef91c3050733caec8.jpg)  
Figure5.4 Deflectionof the verticalonaunit sphere

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/b257e1242f748e107a20feffaa33c438bad3e3f5063f663bbf6313a220583baa.jpg)  
Figure 5.5 Geocentric Cartesian coordinates and geographical coordinates

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { X } \\ { Y } \\ { Z } \end{array} \right] } _ { p } = r \left[ { \begin{array} { c } { \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos \lambda } \\ { \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin \lambda } \\ { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \end{array} } \right] .
$$

Conversion from (geocentric)geodetic to (geocentric) Cartesian coordinates is given by

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { X } \\ { Y } \\ { Z } \end{array} \right] } _ { p } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { ( N _ { \phi } + h ) \cos \phi \cos \lambda } \\ { ( N _ { \phi } + h ) \cos \phi \sin \lambda } \\ { ( ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) N _ { \phi } + h ) \sin \phi } \end{array} \right] }
$$

where $e$ is the eccentricity of the ellipsoid

$$
e = { \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } } } { a } } = { \sqrt { 2 f - f ^ { 2 } } }
$$

and $N _ { \phi }$ is theellipsoidal radius ofcurvature in the prime vertical,and is given by

$$
N _ { \phi } = \frac { a } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } .
$$

Conversion from (geocentric) Cartesian to (geocentric) geographical coordinates is given by

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { c } { { \phi ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \lambda } } \\ { { } } \\ { { r } } \end{array} \right] _ { p } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { { \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { Z } { \sqrt { X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { { \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { Y } { X } } } \\ { { \sqrt { X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + Z ^ { 2 } } } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Note that $\phi ^ { \prime } = \sin ^ { - 1 } Z / \sqrt { X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + Z ^ { 2 } }$ may also be used to determine $\phi ^ { \prime }$ ,and that these functions are evaluated such that $- 9 0 ^ { \circ } \leq \phi$ (or $\phi ^ { \prime } ) \leq + 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,and $0 ^ { \circ } \leq \lambda < 3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ .

Conversion from geocentric Cartesian to geodetic coordinates is not as simple a matter,however.The geodetic longitude $\lambda$ can be determined by inverting the relationships in Eq.5.8: $Y / X = \tan \lambda$ or $\lambda = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( Y / X )$ .Determining $\phi$ and $h$ are a much more difficult problem.Two general approaches have been used—(a) iterative and non-iterative approximation methods,and (b) closed-form solutions.Most ofthe proposed methods are of the first type and include Bowring (1976),Linand Wang (1995),Fukishima (1999,2006),Pollard (2002),Turner(2009)and Feltens (2008,2009).Borkowski (1989) derived an exact solution, but it has some singularities and special conditions.Much of the work on this problem has been devoted to finding faster algorithms and better approximation techniques to solve the problem.

# 5.2.5 Local Coordinate Systems

Many possibilities exist for establishing local coordinate systems,either systems designed fora specific observer or systems designed to cover a given local area.Most systems specific to an observer are horizon (or altitude and azimuth) coordinate systemsand a“standard” oneis described below.Coordinate systems designed to cover a localized area are usually based ona map projection coordinate system,such that the coordinates ofa point relative to the reference elipsoid are projected onto a plane upon which computations can be done with relative simplicity (as opposed to working on the ellipsoid itself).A few of the most commonlyused systems—Universal Transverse Mercator,Lambert conformal conic and (U.S.) state plane coordinates-are described below.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/14c7b6f3128959796112020dbdd880d85eba250b2402522dcf7df16364b0b8a8.jpg)  
Figure 5.6 Horizon system altitude and azimuth

# 5.2.5.1 Horizon System

Depending on the author,slight variations exist in the definition of any local horizon coordinatesystem.However,a more or less“standard”system is described as follows.

First,any such system must have at least two reference planes.The primary reference planehereisthe local horizon,and the secondary is the localmeridian (seeFig.5.6).The horizonis theplaneperpendicular to the observer’slocal vertical,while the meridianis the plane perpendicular to the horizon that passes through the north and south celestial poles,the observer and the zenith.The direction of any point $p _ { 2 }$ (from the observer at $p _ { 1 } )$ in thissystem is then given by its altitude and azimuth.Ifwe imagine a plane passing through $p _ { 2 }$ and the zenith $z$ ,theanglebetween the horizonand thepoint $p _ { 2 }$ inthisplaneis thealtitude $a$ (also sometimes knownas the elevation angle).The angle between the meridianand thisplane measuredclockwisefromnorth,asviewed fromabove,istheazimuth $A$ .(Some users in the past,particularly for geodetic purposes,have defined azimuth as being measured clockwise from south.This practice isno longer much in use but users of old observations should be aware of it.) Thealtitudeisdefined from $- 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ to $+ 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ (negative if the object is below the horizon and positive if above),and the azimuth ismeasured from $0 ^ { \circ }$ to $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Instead of this spherical coordinate system,a left-handed three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system is also used,with the $z$ -axispositive up,the $x$ -axispositive north,and the $y$ -axispositive east (through $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ azimuth).

Afurther variation of this system is to use the zenith distance $z$ instead of the altitude. The zenith distanceis simply the complement of the altitude,i.e.,the angular distance of $p _ { 2 }$ from the zenith z.It has the advantage of always being positive,with values from $0 ^ { \circ }$ to $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ It is computed from altitude as

$$
z = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - a
$$

where the $z$ and $a$ are in degrees.

# 5.2.5.2 Grid Coordinate Systems

For computational convenience and for ease of use inlocal and regional areas,itis common to represent sections of the curved surface of the Earth with a plane.Various systems have been devised to do this,all based on given transformation equations from the sphere or ellipsoid to the plane.These transformations are,in fact,map projections,in many cases the same as those used for mapping large regional areas or the entire Earth.Conformal projections arecommonly used because they preserve the true shapes of small areas;relative angles (directions) about any point are approximately correct;and the local scale inall directions about any given point is constant.In these projections,the meridians and parallels of latitude intersect at right angles.

The coordinate systems based on such map projections are usually termed grid coordinate systems.Themostwidelyusedare theUniversalTransverseMercator(UTM),UniversalPolar Stereographic (UPS),and Lambert conformal conic (LCC) projections.In the United States, the state plane coordinate systems are based on UTMorLCC projections (Burkholder 2008, chapter1O) while the U.S.National Grid (USNG) System(FGDC 2001) is based on UTM and theMilitary Grid Reference System (seeUniversal TransverseMercator grid section below).Obviously many other systems exist for various countriesand areas,but the basic principles (and many times the projection formulae,with the exception of constants) are the same.

Forany grid coordinate system,several fundamental transformations and quantities are of importance.The fundamental transformations are the direct conversion from geodetic coordinates $( \phi , \lambda )$ to grid coordinates $( X , Y )$ ，and the inverse transformation from grid coordinates to geodetic coordinates.Other quantities of interest include:(a) the convergence of the meridians,i.e.,the angle between grid north and true north on the grid(y);(b) the angle on the grid between the geodesic (the shortest path connecting twopoints on the surface of the reference ellipsoid) and the straight line between them (known in some of the references as‘ $\cdot t { - } T ^ { \ast } )$ ;(c) the scale ata given point $( k )$ ；and (d) thescale factor alonga line.Equations for the transformations and scale are given below (based on Snyder(1983)); equations for the otherquantities are available fromthe other references,particularly Thomas (1952).The specific coordinates of a point on the grid are determined by the choice of the reference ellipsoid (in effect,the geodetic datum)to which latitudeand longitude are referred and whose parameters are used in the map projection equations,as shown below.Software forcoordinate conversions of UTM and USNG grid coordinates is available from the U.S. National Geodetic Survey (URL[7],URL[8]).UTM,UPSandLCCarealso discussed in Parker and Bartholomew (1989) and ISO (2009).

Fundamental quantities and expressions used in the transformation equations are summarized as follows:

$\phi =$ geodetic latitude of point; $\lambda =$ geodetic longitude of point;   
$\phi _ { 0 } =$ latitudeof the origin;   
$\lambda _ { 0 } =$ longitude of the origin (and the central meridian for the UTM grid);   
$\Delta \lambda =$ difference of longitude from the central meridian( $\Delta \lambda = \lambda - \lambda _ { 0 } )$ (in radians);

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a = \mathrm { s e m i m a j o r ~ a x i s ~ o f ~ t h e ~ e l l i p s o i d } ; } } \\ { { b = \mathrm { s e m i m i n o r ~ a x i s ~ o f ~ t h e ~ e l l i p s o i d } ; } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { e = \mathrm { e c c e n t r i c i t y ~ o f ~ t h e ~ e l l i p s o i d ~ } ( e ^ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } ) ; } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { e ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { s e c o n d ~ e c c e n t r i c i t y ~ o f ~ t h e ~ e l l i p s o i d ~ } ( e ^ { \prime ^ { 2 } } = \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { 2 } - b ^ { 2 } } { b ^ { 2 } } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations that are given below for the Transverse Mercator,Polar Stereographic,and Lambert Conformal Conic projections can be used with many other grid systems with just a change of the appropriate constants.

Universal TransverseMercator grid.The Universal Transverse Mercator(UTM） grid coordinate system divides the world into sixty ${ { 6 } ^ { \circ } }$ -wide zones of longitude,with zone1 going from $- 1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ to $- 1 7 4 ^ { \circ }$ longitude,zone2 from $- 1 7 4 ^ { \circ }$ to $- 1 6 8 ^ { \circ }$ longitude,etc.Latitude bands are designated with letters at $8 ^ { \circ }$ intervals from ${ } ^ { 8 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { S } }$ to $7 2 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { N }$ and one $1 2 ^ { \circ }$ interval from 72- $8 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { N }$ (DMA199o). The grid zone designations are shown in Fig.5.7 and were devised by theU.S.Armyin1947asaway to systematicallyrepresent smallergeographicareason largescale maps.ThisMilitary Grid ReferenceSystemorMGRS(DMA199O),originally designed for artillery operations,has become a standard for commercial applications and was adopted in the U.S.as the U.S.National Grid (FGDC 2Oo1).It essentially divides each $_ { 6 \mathrm { x } 8 }$ degree zone into 1Oo,0oO-meter squares,which are each given a two-letter designation. Within each of these squares the coordinates of a point can be specified in terms of Easting $( X )$ ）andNorthing(Y） grid coordinates,inunitsof meters,with precisions of10,ooo meters to 1 meter depending on the application.

IntheUTM grid system(DMA1989),thecentral meridian is the central meridian of each zone,with a scale factor of $k _ { 0 } = 0 . 9 9 9 6$ .Thisresultsinamaximum scale error of1/1000 anywhere ina zone,or $1 / 2 5 0 0$ forpoints on the central meridian or above $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ orbelow $- 4 5 ^ { \circ }$ latitude.(Of course,as with all grid systems,there is no scale error if the appropriate corrections for scale are made.)In each zone,the central meridian is defined as the Easting ${ \mathrm { ( X ) } }$ coordinate originwithavalue of $\mathrm { X } = 5 0 0 0 0 0$ meters (called the falseEasting). The Northing(Y) origin forall zonesis the equatoratwhich falseNorthingsare defined as $\mathbf { Y } =$ O meters for northern hemisphere zones and $\mathrm { Y = } 1 0 0 0 0 0 0 0$ meters for southern hemisphere zones.This scheme avoids the need for negative coordinate values.UTM grid coordinates always increase from south to north and west to east withina zone.

For UTM,starting with our previously given expressions,we continue by computing $S _ { \phi }$ , thelength ofthe meridional arc,the true meridional distance on the ellipsoid from the equator to latitude $\phi$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { S _ { \phi } = a [ ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } / 4 - 3 e ^ { 4 } / 6 4 - 5 e ^ { 6 } / 2 5 6 - \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \phi } } \\ { { \qquad - ( 3 e ^ { 2 } / 8 + 3 e ^ { 4 } / 3 2 + 4 5 e ^ { 6 } / 1 0 2 4 + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 2 \phi } } \\ { { \qquad + ( 1 5 e ^ { 4 } / 2 5 6 + 4 5 e ^ { 6 } / 1 0 2 4 + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 4 \phi } } \\ { { \qquad - ( 3 5 e ^ { 6 } / 3 0 7 2 + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 6 \phi + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] } } \end{array}
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0005_pages_0161-0200/ocr/images/08ba2a839a0cd97f1ac612fe61a4e20148f348f4362021c9967092ca7f566435.jpg)  
Figure5.7UTMand UPS grid zone designations

where $\phi$ is in radians.Also needed are other auxiliary quantities:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { T = \tan ^ { 2 } \phi } \\ { C = e ^ { \prime 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } \\ { A = \Delta \lambda \cos \phi . } \end{array}
$$

We also compute $S _ { \phi _ { 0 } }$ as given by $s$ computed at the latitude $\phi _ { 0 }$ at the origin of the $X$ and $Y$ coordinates.Then the direct transformation from $( \phi , \lambda )$ to $( X , Y )$ is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { X = k _ { 0 } N _ { \phi } [ A + ( 1 - T + C ) A ^ { 3 } / 6 } \\ & { \qquad + ( 5 - 1 8 T + T ^ { 2 } + 7 2 C - 5 8 e ^ { \prime 2 } ) A ^ { 5 } / 1 2 0 ] } \\ & { Y = k _ { 0 } \{ S _ { \phi } - S _ { \phi _ { 0 } } + N _ { \phi } \tan \phi [ A ^ { 2 } / 2 + ( 5 - T + 9 C + 4 C ^ { 2 } ) A ^ { 4 } / 2 4 } \\ & { \qquad + ( 6 1 - 5 8 T + T ^ { 2 } + 6 0 0 C - 3 3 0 e ^ { \prime 2 } ) A ^ { 6 } / 7 2 0 ] \} . } \end{array}
$$

The actual UTMcoordinates are obtained by adding the appropriate false Easting and false Northing values to these $\mathrm { _ X }$ and $\mathrm { Y }$ coordinates.The scale factor at any given point is given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { k = k _ { 0 } \bigg [ 1 + ( 1 + C ) \frac { A ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + ( 5 - 4 T + 4 2 C + 1 3 C ^ { 2 } - 2 8 e ^ { \prime 2 } ) \frac { A ^ { 4 } } { 2 4 } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } + ( 6 1 - 1 4 8 T + 1 6 T ^ { 2 } ) \frac { A ^ { 6 } } { 7 2 0 } \bigg ] . } } \end{array}
$$

The inverse formulae,for converting $( X , Y )$ to $( \phi , \lambda )$ are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi = \phi _ { 1 } - ( N _ { \phi _ { 1 } } \tan \phi _ { 1 } / R _ { 1 } ) [ D ^ { 2 } / 2 - ( 5 + 3 T _ { 1 } + 1 0 C _ { 1 } - 4 C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 9 e ^ { \prime 2 } ) D ^ { 4 } / 2 4 } \\ & { \qquad + ( 6 1 + 9 0 T _ { 1 } + 2 9 8 C _ { 1 } + 4 5 T _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 5 2 e ^ { \prime 2 } - 3 C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) D ^ { 6 } / 7 2 0 ] } \\ & { \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 } + [ D - ( 1 + 2 T _ { 1 } + C _ { 1 } ) D ^ { 3 } / 6 } \\ & { \qquad + ( 5 - 2 C _ { 1 } + 2 8 T _ { 1 } - 3 C _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + 8 e ^ { \prime 2 } + 2 4 T _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) D ^ { 5 } / 1 2 0 ] / \cos \phi _ { 1 } } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { 1 }$ isthe footpoint latitude or the latitudeat the central meridianwhich has the same $Y$ coordinate as that of the point $( \phi , \lambda )$ .It is given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi _ { 1 } = \mu + ( 3 e _ { 1 } / 2 - 2 7 e _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } / 3 2 + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 2 \mu + ( 2 1 e _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } / 1 6 - 5 5 e _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } / 3 2 - \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 4 \mu } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { + ( 1 5 1 e _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } / 9 6 + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \sin 6 \mu + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { e _ { 1 } = { \frac { 1 - { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } { 1 + { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } } } \\ & { \mu = S / [ a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } / 4 - 3 e ^ { 4 } / 6 4 - 5 e ^ { 6 } / 2 5 6 - \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ] } \\ & { S = S _ { \phi _ { 0 } } + Y / k _ { 0 } . } \end{array} }
$$

For the other terms,inaddition to needing $C , T$ ,and $N _ { \phi }$ for the footpoint latitude $\phi _ { 1 }$ we also have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { R _ { 1 } = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) / ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 1 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } } \\ { { D = X / ( N _ { \phi _ { 1 } } k _ { 0 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

$R _ { 1 }$ is the ellipsoidal radius of curvature in the meridian.

Universal Polar Stereographic grid.The Universal Polar Stereographic Grid System (UPS)consists of two polar stereographic projections,one for the north polar region and one for the south polar region (DMA 1989).The north region covers all areas north of $8 4 ^ { \circ }$ latitude,and the south region all areas south of $- 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ latitude.Thegrid schemeisdefined bythe MGRS.A single letter is used to designate the eastern or western half of each region in thegrid,instead of the zone numbersused for theUTM grid.Two-letter identifiers are used again to represent 100,oo0-meter squares in each region (DMA 1990).The scale factor at the origin (the poles) is $k _ { 0 } = 0 . 9 9 4$ .Inboth regions,a2,000,000-meter $X$ -coordinateline coincideswith the $0 ^ { \circ }$ and $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ meridians.The polewill have coordinates of $X = 2$ ，000,000 meters,and $Y = 2$ ,000,000 meters.

For UPS coordinates,the direct equations are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { X = 2 0 0 0 0 0 0 + \rho \sin \Delta \lambda } \\ { Y _ { \mathrm { n o r t h } } = 2 0 0 0 0 0 0 - \rho \cos \Delta \lambda } \\ { Y _ { \mathrm { s o u t h } } = 2 0 0 0 0 0 0 + \rho \cos \Delta \lambda . } \end{array}
$$

The first $Y$ is for the north zone,the second for the south zone.The auxiliary quantities are obtained from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \rho = \frac { 2 a k _ { 0 } t } { \sqrt { ( 1 + e ) ^ { 1 + \epsilon } ( 1 - e ) ^ { 1 - e } } } } \\ & { t = \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { | \phi | } { 2 } \right) \left[ \frac { 1 + e \sin | \phi | } { 1 - e \sin | \phi | } \right] ^ { e / 2 } } \\ & { \mathrm { f o r ~ } 0 \le \phi < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \quad \mathrm { n o r t h ~ z o n e } } \\ & { \mathrm { a n d ~ } - \frac { \pi } { 2 } < \phi \le 0 \quad \mathrm { s o u t h ~ z o n e } . } \end{array}
$$

Wealso have for the scale

$$
k = \frac { \rho } { a m }
$$

where

$$
m = { \frac { \cos \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } } .
$$

The inverse solution for $\phi$ and $\lambda$ for the north zone is given by

$$
\left. \begin{array} { l } { { \phi = \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \arctan \left\{ t \left[ \frac { 1 - e \sin \phi } { 1 + e \sin \phi } \right] ^ { e / 2 } \right\} } } \\ { { \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 } + \arctan \displaystyle \frac { X } { - Y } } } \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\phi$ is solved iteratively using as the starting value

$$
\phi = { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } - 2 \arctan t .
$$

The inverse solutions for the south zone are the same as the north zone,but the signs of $\phi$ , $\lambda$ , $\lambda _ { 0 }$ ， $X$ ,and $Y$ arereversed.For additional details,see Snyder(1983,pp.16O-164),ISO (2009,pp.77-80),and DMA (1989).

Lambert conformal conic grid. The equations for the Lambert conformal conic (LCC) projection are taken from Snyder(1983,pp.107-109);see also ISO(2009,pp.74-76).Define

Φ=latitude of the origin;

1， $\phi _ { 2 } =$ latitude of the standard parallels for the Lambert conformal conic grid.

The direct equations are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X = \rho \sin \theta } } \\ { { Y = \rho _ { 0 } - \rho \cos \theta } } \\ { { { k = } { \frac { \rho _ { 0 } n } { a m } } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \rho = a F _ { i } ^ { n } } \\ & { \theta = n ( \Delta _ { \mathrm { - } } \lambda _ { 0 } ) } \\ & { \rho _ { 0 } = a F _ { i } ^ { n } } \\ & { n = \frac { \mathrm { l n m } _ { 1 } - \mathrm { l n } m _ { 2 } } { \mathrm { l n } t _ { 1 } - \mathrm { l n } t _ { 2 } } } \\ & { m = \frac { \cos \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } } \\ & { t = \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \frac { \phi } { 2 } \right) \left[ \frac { ( 1 + e \sin \phi ) } { ( 1 - e \sin \phi ) } \right] ^ { e / 2 } } \\ & { F = \frac { m _ { 1 } } { n t _ { 1 } ^ { n } } } \end{array}
$$

with the same subscripts1,2,or none applied to $m$ and $\phi$ in the equation for $m$ ，andthe subscripts O,1,2,or none applied to $t$ and $\phi$ in the equation for $t , n$ ， $F$ ,and $\rho _ { 0 }$ need be computed only once per zone.Note that published (in some cases oficially adopted) tables maygive (negligible)differences,asthe tables were computed with simpler,less-precise formulae (Snyder 1983,p.108).

The inverse formulae are

where and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi = \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \arctan \left\{ t \left[ \frac { 1 - e \sin \phi } { 1 + e \sin \phi } \right] ^ { e / 2 } \right\} } } \\ { { t = \displaystyle ( \frac { \rho } { a F } ) ^ { 1 / n } } } \\ { { \rho = \displaystyle \pm \sqrt { X ^ { 2 } + ( \rho _ { 0 } - Y ) ^ { 2 } } } } \\ { { \lambda = \displaystyle \frac { \theta } { n } + \lambda _ { 0 } } } \\ { { \theta = \arctan \frac { X } { \rho _ { 0 } - Y } . } } \end{array}
$$

where

The sign of $\rho$ is taken from $n$ .If $n$ is negative,the signs of $X$ , $Y$ ,and $\rho _ { 0 }$ in the equation for $\theta$ must be reversed,and the quadrant of $\theta$ determined accordingly.Also note that the equation for $\phi$ requires iteration on $\phi$ ,asin the UPS case,and will converge rapidly with the same initial assumption

$$
\phi = { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } - 2 \arctan t .
$$

State plane coordinates.In the United States,a state plane coordinate system has been established so that each state has its own grid coordinate system.These systems were originally designed by theU.S.Geological Surveyin the193Osand194Os,and have since been legallyadopted by the statelegislatures of most states.States orareas of states with amostly north-south orientation are represented in a transverse Mercator map projection, whereas states or areas with a predominantly east-west orientation use a Lambert conformal conic projection.(An exception is the oblique transverse Mercator projection used for part ofAlaska.) There are 136 separate zones covering all of the 5O states.(The U.S.Virgin Islands,American Samoa,and other territories all also have their own projections,some of which are not conformal).Snyder(1983,pp.58-62,Table8) lists allthe zones,by projection and state,with thecentral meridian,its scale error for theLambert conformal conic zones, and the standard parallels and origin for the transverse Mercator zones.The coordinates of the originsare also given ina footnote to that table.Unlike UTM grid zones,state plane coordinate grid zonesare very irregular and are defined by county,state and international boundaries.Software to convert state plane coordinates to or from geographic coordinates (latitude,longitude) isavailable from theNational Geodetic Survey(URL[8]).Note that U.S.survey feet(1U.S.survey foot $= 1 2 0 0 / 3 9 3 7 \mathrm { ~ m } )$ areused rather than international feet (1 International foot $= 0 . 3 0 4 8 ~ \mathrm { m } = 0 . 9 9 9 9 9 3$ U.S.survey feet exactly).Inaddition,state plane coordinates derived from NAD 83 are given in meters,while state plane coordinates derivedfromNAD27are givenin U.S.survey fet.These systems were originally established under the assumption that any calculations done assuming plane coordinates would not have errors Worse than1in10,Ooo.Their definition in many states isalso legally tied to NAD27 orNAD83,and land parcelsmay be defined or even must be defined in termsof state planecoordinates.Many governmental and commercial organizations currentlyuse state plane coordinates heavily.[For more information on state plane coordinates and projections, see Stem (1989),Mitchell and Simmons (1945),and Snyder (1983,pp.56-63).General surveying texts,such as Mofit and Bouchard (1975)and Burkholder (2Oo8),also contain information on state plane coordinates.]

The equations for the transverse Mercator and Lambert conformal conic projections have already been given above.The only changes for their application with state plane coordinates will be for constants such as the central meridian $( \lambda _ { 0 } )$ ,the scale factor on the central meridian $( k _ { 0 } )$ ,and the grid coordinates of the origin.

# 5.2.5.3Specific Ellipsoids, Datums,and Reference Systems

Table 5.1 lists various ellipsoids that have been used as the basis of geodetic datums and reference systems.Note that as previously mentioned,only $a$ and $1 / f$ are necessary for the definition of anellipsoid.Table 5.2 lists geodetic datums (also giving the origin station if any and its latitude and longitude)and notes that are associated with specific terrestrial reference systems.Similar,but abbreviated versionsof these tablesare givenin Nautical Almanac Offices of UK and USA,(1989,p.K13).
