# 5.2.5.4 Datum Conversions

The subject of converting coordinates from one datum to another is a complex one,with a very extensive literature.Indeed,the problem almost always arises and isusually discussed whenever a new datum or reference system is formed,since there will usually be a need to transform the coordinates of points in other systems to coordinates in the new system.

Two general methods have been used in the past for datum transformations.The first isa differential method,where differential (in the partial-derivative sense) equations are formed that give the change in station coordinates $( \Delta \phi , \Delta \lambda )$ as a function of changes in datum/ellipsoid parameters (such as for changes in $^ a$ ， $f$ ,scale,etc.).Thistype of method has been most useful in the past where small differences exist between the two datums,or a minor change inan existing datum was being made.However,the derivation ofthe differential equations for a specific case and their application and accuracy is a complicated subject and will not be discussed further here.[Information on this type of method is available in Rapp (1980,pp.42-74),Bomford (1971,pp.199-207),Heiskanen and Moritz (1967,pp.204- 210),Molodenskii,Eremeev,and Yurkina (1962,pp.13-17),Torge(1980,pp.150-152), and DMA (1987,part 1,pp.7-1 to 8-8).]

A second method is to do a rigorous transformation between the systems of each datum. Thisisoftenrequired if highaccuracy isdesired,or thereare several or large differences between the datums.For any pair of datums,transformation parameters may have already been determined,at least for offsets and sometimes for rotation and scale.A three-step procedure is required to convert $( \phi , \lambda , h )$ from system1 to system 2

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( \phi , \lambda , h ) _ { 1 } \Rightarrow ( X , Y , Z ) _ { 1 } } \\ { ( X , Y , Z ) _ { 1 } \Rightarrow ( X , Y , Z ) _ { 2 } } \\ { ( X , Y , Z ) _ { 2 } \Rightarrow ( \phi , \lambda , h ) _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

The geodetic to Cartesian and Cartesian to geodetic transformations have already been described in $\ S \ 5 . 2 . 4$ .Ingeneral,the transformation of one Cartesian system into another Cartesian system requiresa translation of the origin and arotation of the coordinate axes of the first system toalign itwith the second system.This takes the form ofa7-parameter similarity transformation:

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { X } \\ { Y } \\ { Z } \end{array} \right] } _ { 2 } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \Delta X } \\ { \Delta Y } \\ { \Delta Z } \end{array} \right] } + { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { ~ D } & { - R _ { 3 } } & { R _ { 2 } } \\ { ~ R _ { 3 } } & { ~ D } & { - R _ { 1 } } \\ { - R _ { 2 } } & { ~ R _ { 1 } } & { ~ D } \end{array} \right] } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { X } \\ { Y } \\ { Z } \end{array} \right] } _ { 1 }
$$

where $\Delta X , \Delta Y$ and $\Delta Z$ are the components of the origin translation; $R _ { 1 } , R _ { 2 }$ ,and $R _ { 3 }$ are the rotation angles (using small-angle formulae here); and $D$ is the scale factor (ISO 2009, p.343).Various forms of this 7-parameter transformation exist under various names (some notasrigorousasothers).(SeeKumar1972,Rapp1980,p.48,NIMA1997,Seeber2003, pp.27-28,and Hofmann-Wellenhof,Lichtenegger,and Collins2oo1.)It isalso possible that some of the parameters may not be known or can be assumed to be zero.

Insome cases,the transformationparametersarenotwel-determined or not knownat all. The user must then determine the parameters directly from observations that tie stations together between the two systems.If enough points have their coordinates determinedin both systems,Eq.5.57or similar equations can be solved(usuallyinaleast-squares solution) for thetransformation parameters.Of course,this may not takeinto account any deformations of either of the datums from their idealized reference systems (due to surveying errors).Any deformations willbe absorbed into the transformation parameters when they are solved for. Software for converting geographic coordinates among a wide variety of coordinate systems, map projections,and datums can be found at URL[9].

In comparing or transforming modern geocentric terrestrial reference frames,in contrast to classical datums,all the transformation parameters tend to be very small or close to zero. Thisis due to the adoption of common standards and conventions (e.g.,IERS conventions), use of common equipment,and similar data processng techniques.The realizations of the reference frames willdifferdue todifferences in the observing stations andthe software used, among other things.There are essentially two options in satellite geodesy forcomputing transformations between reference frames.One can compute the orbits of the same satelites (for example,GPS) in twodifferent reference framesand then determine7-parameter transformationsbetween the orbits.Alternatively,ifanetwork of stations isavailable with coordinates independently determined from both reference frames,then one can computea7-parameter transformation between the two sets of station coordinates.As the accuracy and precision of measurements and modeling have improved,it is now common to take into account for scientific purposes the time-dependency of the7 parameters,resultingin computationof 14-parameter transformations (see URL[3]).

To avoid transformations completely,one could measure the coordinates of all the desired points with a GPS satellite receiver—thus determining the coordinates of the points in some current reference system,bypassnganytransformationsentirely.Thiseliminates theneed to use the old datums or old observations.The coordinates can be measured in an absolute sense,relative to the center of the Earth at decimeter accuracy or betterif sufcient data arecollected (Leandro,Santos,andLangley 2Oo7;Bisnath and Gao 2Oo9;Zumberge et al. 1997).Coordinates can also be determined relative to known reference points that are close by( $1 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ orless) orlonger distances away(1Os to 1oOs of kilometers) to accuracies of a few centimeters or even millimeters (URL[1O];Dixon 2Oo6) by using the appropriate receiver equipment,data processing techniques and sufficient data,depending upon the application and theaccuracy required.The resultingcoordinates will be consistent with the geodetic reference frame and datum used for the data processing.

# 5.3 Gravity,Tides,and Motions of the Crust

In thissection,variousphenomenawill be covered that affect theEarth'sgravity field and coordinates of a given station.A very short summary of one method of modeling the Earth's gravity field (as a spherical harmonic series)is given,along with the IERS recommendations fora“standard”gravity field.Thisis followed by,almost verbatim,theIERSConventions (2010)recommendations on Earth tides,ocean tides,ocean and atmospheric loading,and plate motions (IERS2010,2O04).Finally,the effect of tides on the Earth's rotation itself is described,as given by the IERSConventions(2010).

# 5.3.1Modeling the Earth's Gravity Field

TheEarth'sgravitycan be considered as a force that is the sum of the Earth's gravitational force and the centrifugal force resulting from the Earth's rotation.

The Earth's external gravitational field can be considered a potential field whose strength $V$ fulfills theLaplaceequation

$$
\Delta V = 0
$$

where $\Delta$ is the Laplacian operator

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial X ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial Y ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial Z ^ { 2 } }
$$

in a cartesian $X$ , $Y$ ,and $Z$ coordinate system.

It is possible to develop a solution to Eq.5.58 (HeiskanenandMoritz 1967,pp.15-35; Torge1980,pp.26-29) in terms of a series of Legendrepolynomials:

$$
P _ { n } ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { n } \times n ! } \times \frac { d ^ { n } } { d t ^ { n } } ( t ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { n }
$$

with

$$
t = \cos \psi = \cos \theta \cos \theta ^ { \prime } + \sin \theta \sin \theta ^ { \prime } \cos ( \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda ) .
$$

The variable $\psi$ is the spherical distance on a unit sphere between an atracted point (at spherical coordinates $r , \theta , \lambda )$ andanattractingpoint (at $r ^ { \prime } , \theta ^ { \prime } , \lambda ^ { \prime } )$

In order to express $P _ { n } ( t )$ as a function of the spherical coordinates $\theta$ and $\lambda$ ,itmaybe decomposed using associated Legendre Functions of thefirstkind of degree $n$ and order $m$ obtainedbydifferentiating $P _ { n } ( t ) m$ times

$$
P _ { n m } ( t ) = ( 1 - t ^ { 2 } ) ^ { m / 2 } \frac { d ^ { m } } { d t ^ { m } } P _ { n } ( t ) .
$$

It can then be expressed by the functions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) \cos m \lambda } \\ { P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) \sin m \lambda } \end{array}
$$

known as surface spherical harmonic functions.

A general equation for the gravitational potential then becomes

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle V = \frac { G } { r } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n } { k \frac { ( n - m ) ! } { ( n + m ) ! } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \times ~ \frac { 1 } { r ^ { n } } \bigg ( P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) \cos m \lambda \int \int \int _ { \mathrm { E a r t h } } r ^ { m } P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ^ { \prime } ) \cos m \lambda d m } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) \sin m \lambda \int \int \int _ { \mathrm { E a r t h } } r ^ { m } P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ^ { \prime } ) \sin m \lambda d m \bigg ) } ; }  \\ { { \displaystyle k = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle 1 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r ~ } m = 0 } } \\ { { \displaystyle 2 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r ~ } m \neq 0 } } \end{array} \right. } . }  \end{array}
$$

If the spherical component of the potential of the Earth's mass $M$ is separated out (with $n = 0$ ),theellipsoidal semimajoraxis $^ a$ is used to providea scale for the unit sphere,and the integralsare replaced by harmonic coefficients, $C _ { n m }$ and $S _ { n m }$ ,we then have

$$
V = { \frac { G M } { r } } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n } \left( { \frac { a } { r } } \right) ^ { n } ( C _ { n m } \cos m \lambda + S _ { n m } \sin m \lambda ) P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) \right] .
$$

In many cases,an alternative notation is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { J _ { n } = - C _ { n } } } \\ { { J _ { n m } = - C _ { n m } } } \\ { { K _ { n m } = - S _ { n m } . } } \end{array}
$$

It may be convenientto use fully normalized surface spherical harmonics:

$$
\overline { { { P } } } _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) = \sqrt { k ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { ( n - m ) ! } { ( n + m ) ! } } P _ { n m } ( \cos \theta ) ; \quad k = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { { 1 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r } m = 0 } } \\ { { 2 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r } m \ne 0 } } \end{array} \right. .
$$

Thisresultsin fully normalizedcoefficients

$$
\left\{ \begin{array} { c } { { \overline { { { C } } } _ { n m } } } \\ { { \overline { { { S } } } _ { n m } } } \end{array} \right\} = \sqrt { \frac { ( n + m ) ! } { k ( 2 n + 1 ) ( n - m ) ! } } \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { { C _ { n m } } } \\ { { S _ { n m } } } \end{array} \right\} ; k = \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { { 1 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r } m = 0 } } \\ { { 2 } } & { { \mathrm { f o r } m \ne 0 } } \end{array} \right. .
$$

It should be noted that the expansions for $V$ converge rigorously only outside a sphere of radius $a$ that just encloses the Earth,so thattheLaplace equationmaybe satisfied.In practice, however,since the coefficients are normally determined empirically from measurement (e.g., from satellite observations),the seriescan always be considered to converge.Oncea set of coefficients are determined (see $\ S _ { \textrm { \scriptsize 8 5 . 3 . 2 } }$ the expression for $V$ becomes valid and useful for anywhere in space.

The same expression may be used for determining the gravitationalpotential onthe Earth's surface.However,snotedpreviously,itisthegravitypotential, heredenoted $W$ (including centrifugal force) that is of primary interest on or near (i.e.,moving with) the Earth's surface. We have

$$
W = V + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \omega _ { \mathrm { e } } ^ { 2 } ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } )
$$

where $\omega _ { \mathrm { e } }$ is theangular velocity of the Earth,and $\boldsymbol { \cal X }$ and $Y$ are the coordinates of the point in question $( P ( X , Y , Z ) )$

It should be noted that many other representations of the Earth's gravity field exist and are widely used,depending on the application.Most commonly, $W$ may be determined over a wide area or the Earth itself,using measured gravity-related data.Any of the standard references previously mentioned on geodesy and geodeticastronomy contain detailed information on these types of computations.

# 5.3.2Representation of the Earth's Gravity Field

Gravitational models commonly used in current (2O11) precision orbital analysis include, among others,the GGM series(URL[11]) of the Center for Space Research,Austin and the EIGEN series (URL[12]) of the collaboration of GeoForschungsZentrum,Potsdam and Groupe de Recherche en Geodésie Spatiale,Toulouse.They all represent a significant improvement with respect to EGM96,the past conventional model of the IERS Conventions (2003)(IERS 2004),thanks to the availability ofCHAMP(URL[13]) andGRACE(URL[14]) data in the 2000s.

Table5.3 Suggested truncation levels for use of EGM2008 at different orbits   

<table><tr><td>Orbit Radius /km</td><td>Example</td><td>Truncation Level</td></tr><tr><td>7331</td><td>Starlette</td><td>90</td></tr><tr><td>12270</td><td>Lageos</td><td>20</td></tr><tr><td>26600</td><td>GPS</td><td>12</td></tr></table>

The IERS,recognizing the recent development of new gravitational models derived from theoptimal combination of GRACE datawith high resolution gravitational information obtained from surface gravimetry and satelite altimetry data,recommends at this time the EGM2oo8 model as the conventional model.

The EGM2008 model (Pavlis et al.2012) is complete to degree and order 2159,and contains additional spherical harmonic coefficients extending to degree 219O and order 2159.The geocentric gravitational constant, $G M _ { \oplus }$ ,andradius, ${ \bf a } _ { \mathrm { e } }$ ，valuesreported with EGM2008(398600.4415km³/s² and $6 3 7 8 1 3 6 . 3 \mathrm { m }$ should be used as scaling parameters with itsgravitational potential coefficients.They are to be considered as TT-compatible values. Therecommended TCG-compatible value, $G M _ { \oplus } = 3 9 8 6 0 0 . 4 4 1 8$ ,should be used with the two-body term when working with Geocentric Coordinate Time (TCG) (398600.4415 or 398600.4356 should be used by those working with Terrestrial Time(TT) orBarycentric Dynamical Time (TDB),respectively).The EGM2008 model complete with error estimates isavailableatURL[15].

Although EGM2008 is complete to degree and order 2159,most users in spacegeodesy will find their needs covered by a truncated version of the model. Suggested truncation levels as afunction of the orbit of interest are listed in Table 5.3.It is expected that these truncation levelsprovide an accuracy of $1 ~ \mathrm { m m }$ on the orbits of the indicated satellites.

The EGM2008 model was based on the ITG-GRACE03S GRACE-only gravitational model(URL[16];see also Mayer-Gurr 2Oo7)which is available alongwith itscomplete error covariance matrix to degree and order18O.Therefore the static gravitational field was developed assuming models complyingwith the IERS Conventions (2003) (IERS 2004)and complemented by the following:

·ocean tides:FES2oo4(Lyard,Lefevre,and Letellier 2006);see $\ S 5 . 3 . 4$ ;   
·ocean pole tide:Desai (2002); see $\ S 5 . 3 . 5 . 2$ ;   
·atmosphere and ocean de-aliasing:AOD1B RLO4(Flechtner 2007).

Forsome of the low-degree coefficients,the conventional geopotential model uses values which are different from the original EGM2oo8 values.The static field also assumes values for thesecular rates of low-degree coefficients.In order to use the static field properly and project it in time,the secular rates should be accounted for.The instantaneous values of coefficients $\bar { C } _ { n 0 }$ to be used when computing orbits are given by:

Table 5.4 Low-degree coeficients of the conventional geopotential model.   

<table><tr><td>Coefficient</td><td>Value at to</td><td>Reference</td><td>Rate/yr-1</td><td>Reference</td></tr><tr><td>C20 (zero-tide)</td><td>-0.48416948×10-3</td><td>Cheng et al. 2011</td><td>1.16×10-11</td><td>Nerem et al.1993</td></tr><tr><td>C30</td><td>0.9571612×10-6</td><td>EGM2008</td><td>4.9×10-12</td><td>Cheng et al. 1997</td></tr><tr><td>C40</td><td>0.5399659×10-6</td><td>EGM2008</td><td>4.7×10-12</td><td>Cheng et al. 1997</td></tr></table>

$$
\bar { C } _ { n 0 } ( t ) = \bar { C } _ { n 0 } ( t _ { 0 } ) + d \bar { C } _ { n 0 } / d t \times ( t - t _ { 0 } )
$$

where $t _ { 0 }$ is the epoch of J20o0.O and the values of $\bar { C } _ { n 0 } ( t _ { 0 } )$ and $d \bar { C } _ { n 0 } / d t$ are given in Table5.4.

Note that the zero-tide $C _ { 2 0 }$ coefficient in the conventional geopotential model is obtained from the analysis of 17 years of SLR data approximately centered on year 2Ooo and has an uncertainty of $2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 }$ (Cheng,Riesand Tapley 2O11).Itdiffers significantly from the EGM20o8 value obtained from4 years of GRACE data,as it isexpected that tide-likealiases will affect GRACE-based $C _ { 2 0 }$ values,depending on theaveraging interval used.The tide-free value of $C _ { 2 0 }$ canbe obtained asdescribed in $\ S 5 . 3 . 3 . 2$ .

Values for the $C _ { 2 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 1 }$ coefficients are included in the conventional geopotential model. The $C _ { 2 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 1 }$ coefficients describe the position of the Earth's figure axis.When averaged over many years,the figure axis should closely coincide with the observed position of the rotation pole averaged over the same time period.Any differences between the averaged positions of the mean figure and the mean rotation pole would be due to long-period fluid motions in theatmosphere,oceans,orEarth'sfluid core (Wahr1987,199O).Atpresent,there isno independent evidence that such motions are important. The conventional values for $C _ { 2 1 } ( t )$ and $S _ { 2 1 } ( t )$ areintended to give a mean figure axis that corresponds to the mean pole position consistent with the terrestrial reference frame defined in IERS Conventions (2010) chapter4(IERS2010).

This choice for $C _ { 2 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 1 }$ is realized as follows.First,to use the gravitational potential coefficients tosolve fora satellite orbit,itisnecessary to rotate fromthe Earth-fixed frame, where the coefficients are pertinent,to an inertial frame,where the satelite motion is computed.This transformation between framesshould include polar motion.We assume the polar motion parameters used are relative to the IERSReference Pole.Then the values

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \bar { C } _ { 2 1 } ( t ) = \sqrt { 3 } \bar { x } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { C } _ { 2 0 } - \bar { x } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { C } _ { 2 2 } + \bar { y } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { S } _ { 2 2 } } \\ { \bar { S } _ { 2 1 } ( t ) = - \sqrt { 3 } \bar { y } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { C } _ { 2 0 } - \bar { y } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { C } _ { 2 2 } - \bar { x } _ { p } ( t ) \bar { S } _ { 2 2 } } \end{array}
$$

where $\bar { x } _ { p } ( t )$ and $\bar { y } _ { p } ( t )$ (in radians) represent the IERS conventional mean pole (see section 7.1.4of IERS Conventions (201O) (IERS201O)),provide a mean figure axis which coincides with the mean pole consistent with the TRF defined in IERS Conventions (2010) chapter 4(Ibid.).Any recent value of $C _ { 2 0 } , C _ { 2 2 }$ ,and $S _ { 2 2 }$ is adequate for $1 0 ^ { - 1 4 }$ accuracy in the aboveequations,e.g.,the values of the present conventional model $( - 0 . 4 8 4 1 6 9 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 }$ , $2 . 4 3 9 3 8 3 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 }$ and $- 1 . 4 0 0 2 7 3 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 }$ respectively) can be used.

The models for the low-degree terms are generally consistent with the past long-term trends,but these are not strictly linearin reality.There may be decadal variations that are notcapturedby the models.Inaddition,they may notbe consistent with more recent surface mass trends due to increased ice sheet melting and other results of global climate change.

# 5.3.3 Solid Earth Tides

The solid Earth tide modelis based onanabbreviated form of the Wahr model(Wahr1981) using the preliminary reference Earth model (PREM) of Dziewonskiand Anderson (1981) corresponding to a 1-second reference period.The Love numbers for the induced free space potential, $k$ ,and for the vertical and horizontal displacements, $h$ and $l$ ,have been taken $C _ { 2 m }$ ad $S _ { 2 m }$ $k _ { 2 m } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ $1 0 ^ { - 8 }$ $3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 }$ $C _ { 4 m }$ and $S _ { 4 m }$ through $k _ { 2 m } ^ { ( + ) }$ i coefficients are negligible.) The only other changes exceeding this cutoff amplitude are in $C _ { 3 m }$ and $S _ { 3 m }$ ,producedby the degree 3 part of the tide generatingpotential.

# 5.3.3.1 Calculations of the Tide Generating Potential Coefficients

The solid-tide-induced free-space potential is most conveniently modeled as variations in the standard geopotential coefficients $C _ { n m }$ and $S _ { n m }$ (Eanes,Schutz,and Tapley1983).TheWahr model (or any other model having frequency-dependent Love numbers) is most eficiently computed in three steps.The first step uses a frequency-independent Love number $k _ { 2 }$ and an evaluation of the tidal potential in the time domain usinga lunar and solar ephemeris.The second step corrects those arguments of a harmonic expansion of the tide-generating potential for which the error from using the $k _ { 2 }$ of step1 is above some cutoff amplitude (see Table 5.5).

Steps1 and 2 can be used to compute the total tidal contribution,including the time independent (permanent) contribution to the geopotential coeficient $\bar { C } _ { 2 0 }$ ,whichisadequate foraconventional tide free model such as EGM2o08 (see $\ S \ 1 . 1$ of the IERSConventions (2010)(IERS 201o)).When using azero tide model,this permanent part should notbe counted twice;this is the goalof Step3of the computation.See $\ S 5 . 3 . 3 . 2$ which discusses the treatment of the permanent tide.

The changes in normalized second-degree geopotential coeficients for Step l are

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 5 } } k _ { 2 } \frac { R _ { \mathrm { e } } ^ { 3 } } { G M _ { \oplus } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } P _ { 2 0 } ( \sin \phi _ { j } )
$$

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 1 } - i \Delta \overline { { S } } _ { 2 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \sqrt { \frac { 3 } { 5 } } k _ { 2 } \frac { R _ { \mathrm { e } } ^ { 3 } } { G M _ { \oplus } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } P _ { 2 1 } ( \sin \phi _ { j } ) e ^ { - i \lambda _ { j } }
$$

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 2 } - i \Delta \overline { { S } } _ { 2 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } \sqrt { \frac { 1 2 } { 5 } } k _ { 2 } \frac { R _ { \mathrm { e } } ^ { 3 } } { G M _ { \oplus } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } P _ { 2 2 } ( \sin \phi _ { j } ) e ^ { - i 2 \lambda _ { j } }
$$

Table 5.5 Step 2 solid tide corrections when $k _ { 2 } = 0 . 3 0$ in step 1 (using a cutoff amplitude of $9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 }$ for $A _ { \mathrm { m } } \delta k _ { s } H _ { s } )$   

<table><tr><td colspan="8">Long-Period Tides (n=2,m=0) None except zero-frequency tide.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="8">Diurnal Tides (n=2,m=1)</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2">Doodson Number</td><td>T</td><td>S</td><td>h</td><td>n,argument multipliers P</td><td>N&#x27;</td><td>P1</td><td>Am8kH×1012</td></tr><tr><td>145.555</td><td>(01)</td><td>1</td><td>-1</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>-16.4</td></tr><tr><td>163.555</td><td>(P1)</td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>-2</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>-49.6</td></tr><tr><td>165.545</td><td></td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>-1</td><td>0</td><td>-9.4</td></tr><tr><td>165.555</td><td>(K1)</td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>507.4</td></tr><tr><td>165.565</td><td></td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>1</td><td>0</td><td>73.5</td></tr><tr><td>166.554</td><td>(1）</td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>-1</td><td>-15.2</td></tr></table>

Semi-Diurnal Tides $( n = 2$ , $m = 2$

n,argument multipliers   

<table><tr><td colspan="2">Doodson Number</td><td>T</td><td>s</td><td>h</td><td>P</td><td>N&#x27;</td><td>P1</td><td>Am8kH×1012</td></tr><tr><td>255.555</td><td>(M2)</td><td>2</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>39.5</td></tr><tr><td>273.555</td><td>(S2)</td><td>2</td><td>2</td><td>-2</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>18.4</td></tr></table>

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { k _ { 2 } = \mathrm { n o m i n a l ~ s e c o n d } \deg \mathrm { r e e ~ L o v e ~ n u m b e r } ; } \\ & { ~ R _ { e } = \mathrm { e q u a t o r i a l ~ r a d i u s ~ o f ~ t h e ~ E a r t h } ; } \\ & { G M _ { \oplus } = \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ p a r a m e t e r ~ f o r ~ t h e ~ E a r t h } ; } \\ & { G M _ { j } = \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ p a r a m e t e r ~ f o r ~ t h e ~ M o o n ~ } ( j = 2 ) \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } \mathrm { S u n ~ } ( j = 3 ) ; } \\ & { ~ r _ { j } = \mathrm { d i s t a n c e ~ f r o m ~ g e o c e n t e r ~ t o ~ M o o n ~ o r ~ S u n } ; } \\ & { ~ \phi _ { j } = \mathrm { b o o d y ~ f i x e d ~ g e o c e n t r i c ~ l a t i u d e ~ o f ~ M o o n ~ o r ~ S u n } ; } \\ & { \lambda _ { j } = \mathrm { b o d y ~ f i x e d ~ e a s t ~ l o n g i t u d e ~ ( f r o m ~ G r e n w i c h ) ~ o f ~ M o o n ~ o r ~ S u n } . } \end{array}
$$

One further computation to be donein Step 1is that of the changes in the degree 4 coeficients produced by the degree2tides.They are given by

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 4 m } - i \Delta \overline { { S } } _ { 4 m } = \frac { k _ { 2 m } ^ { ( + ) } } { 5 } \frac { R _ { \mathrm { e } } ^ { 3 } } { G M _ { \oplus } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } } { r _ { j } ^ { 3 } } P _ { 2 m } ( \sin \phi _ { j } ) e ^ { - i m \lambda _ { j } } .
$$

The frequency dependence corrections to the $\Delta \bar { C } _ { n m }$ and $\Delta \bar { S } _ { n m }$ values obtained from Step 1are computed in Step 2 as the sum ofcontributions from a number of tidal constituents belonging to the respective bands.The changes in normalized coefficients from Step 2 are:

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { n m } - i \Delta \overline { { S } } _ { n m } = A _ { \mathrm { m } } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } \delta k _ { s } H _ { s } \left( \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } \\ { - i } \end{array} \right) \begin{array} { l l } { n + m } & { \mathrm { e v e n } } \\ { n + m } & { \mathrm { o d d } } \end{array} e ^ { i \Theta _ { s } }
$$

where

$$
A _ { 0 } = { \frac { 1 } { R _ { e } { \sqrt { 4 \pi } } } } = 4 . 4 2 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } { \mathrm { m } } ^ { - 1 }
$$

$$
A _ { \mathrm { m } } = { \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { m } } { R _ { \mathrm { e } } { \sqrt { 4 \pi ( 2 - \delta _ { 0 m } ) } } } } , \quad \delta _ { 0 m } = { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { { \mathrm { i f } } m = 0 } \\ { 0 } & { { \mathrm { i f } } m \neq 0 } \end{array} \right. }
$$

$\delta k _ { s } =$ difference between $k$ at frequency $s$ and the nominal value $k _ { 2 }$ in the sense $k _ { \mathrm { s } } - k _ { 2 }$

plus a contribution from ocean loading;

$H _ { s } =$ amplitude $( m )$ of term at frequency s from theCartwright and Tayler(1971) and

Cartwright and Edden (1973) harmonic expansion of the tide-generating potential;

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Theta _ { s } = \overline { { n } } \cdot \overline { { \beta } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 6 } n _ { i } \beta _ { i } ; } \end{array}
$$

$\bar { n } = \mathrm { s i x }$ vector of multipliers of the Doodson (1921) variables;

$\bar { \beta } =$ the Doodson variables;

$$
\delta S _ { 2 0 } = 0 .
$$

The Doodson variables are related to the fundamental arguments (the Delaunay variables $l , l ^ { \prime } , F , D , \Omega )$ ofthe nutation series (see chapter 5 of IERSConventions(2OlO) (IERS 2010)) by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \begin{array} { r l } & { s = F + \Omega = \beta _ { 2 } \left( \mathrm { M o o n } ; \mathrm { s ~ m e a n ~ l o n g i m u d e } \right) ; } \\ & { h = s - D = \beta _ { 3 } \left( \mathrm { S u n } ; \mathrm { s ~ m e a n ~ l o n g i t u d e } \right) ; } \\ & { p = s - l = \beta _ { 4 } \left( \mathrm { l o n g i t u d e o f ~ M o o n } ; \mathrm { s ~ m e a n ~ p e r i g e e } \right) ; } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { r l } & { N ^ { \prime } = - \Omega = \beta _ { 5 } \left( \mathrm { n e g a t i v e ~ l o n g i t u d e ~ o f ~ M o o n } ; \mathrm { s ~ m e a n ~ n o d e } \right) ; } \\ & { p _ { 1 } = s - D - l ^ { \prime } = \beta _ { 6 } \left( \mathrm { l o n g i t u d e ~ o f ~ S u n } ; \mathrm { s ~ m e a n ~ p e r i g e e } \right) ; } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { r l } & { \tau = \theta _ { g } + \pi - s = \beta _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { t i m e ~ a n g l e i n ~ l u n a r ~ d a y ~ r e c k o n ~ l o w e r ~ t r o n ~ l o w e r ~ t r a n s i t } \right) ; } \\ & { \theta _ { g } = \mathrm { G r e e n v i c h ~ M e a n ~ S i d e r e a l ~ T i m e ~ e x p r e s s e d ~ i n ~ a n g l e ~ u n i t s ~ ( i . e . , ~ 2 4 ^ { h } = 3 6 0 ^ { o } ) ; } } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { r l } & { \pi \mathrm { i n ~ } ( \theta _ { g } + \pi ) \mathrm { ~ i s ~ n o w ~ t o ~ b e ~ r e p l a c e d ~ b y ~ 1 8 0 ^ o r . } } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

The normalized geopotential coeficients $( \overline { { C } } _ { n m } , \overline { { S } } _ { n m } )$ are related to the unnormalized coefficients $( C _ { n m } , S _ { n m } )$ by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { C _ { n m } = N _ { n m } \overline { { C } } _ { n m } } \\ & { S _ { n m } = N _ { n m } \overline { { S } } _ { n m } } \\ & { N _ { n m } = \left[ \frac { ( n + m ) ! ( 2 n + 1 ) ( 2 - \delta _ { m 0 } ) } { ( n + m ) ! } \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Using a nominal $k _ { 2 }$ of 0.3 and an amplitude cutoff of $9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 }$ change in normalized geopotential coefficients,the summation $S ( n , m )$ requires six diurnal terms（ $n = 2$ , $m = 1$ ） modifying $\overline { { C } } _ { 2 1 }$ and $\overline { { S } } _ { 2 1 }$ and two semi-diurnal terms $( n = 2 , m = 2 )$ modifying $\overline { { C } } _ { 2 2 }$ and $\overline { { S } } _ { 2 2 }$ . With the exception of the zero-frequency tide,no long-period terms are necessary.Table 5.5 gives the required quantities for correcting the (2,1) and (2,2) coefficients.The correction to $\overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 }$ is discussed in more detail in the next section ( $\ S \ 5 . 3 . 3 . 2 \ O$

Calculation of thecorrection due to any tidal constituent is illustrated by the following example for $K _ { 1 }$ Giventhat $A _ { m } = A _ { 1 } = - 3 . 1 2 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 }$ ,and that $H _ { s } = 0 . 3 6 8 7 0$ ， $\Theta _ { s } =$ $( \theta _ { g } + \pi )$ ,and $k _ { 2 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = ( 0 . 2 5 7 4 6 + 0 . 0 0 1 1 8 i )$ forthistideesbracl value $( 0 . 2 9 8 3 0 - 0 . 0 0 1 4 4 i )$ that $\delta k _ { s } = ( - 0 . 0 4 0 8 4 + 0 . 0 0 2 6 2 i )$ .Equation5.78 thenyields:

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { ( \Delta \bar { C } _ { 2 1 } ) _ { K _ { 1 } } } } & { { = } } & { { 4 7 0 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 } \sin ( \theta _ { g } + \pi ) - 3 0 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 } \cos ( \theta _ { g } + \pi ) } } \\ { { ( \Delta \bar { S } _ { 2 1 } ) _ { K _ { 1 } } } } & { { = } } & { { 4 7 0 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 } \cos ( \theta _ { g } + \pi ) + 3 0 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 2 } \sin ( \theta _ { g } + \pi ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The total variation in geopotential coefficients due to the solid tide is obtained by adding theresults of Step2(Eq.5.78)to those of Step1(Eqs.5.74,5.75,and5.76).The sum of these steps constitutes a model of the observed instantaneous geopotential.

# 5.3.3.2 Treatment of the Permanent Tide

The mean value of $\Delta C _ { 2 0 }$ from Eq.5.74 is not zero,and this permanent tide deserves special attention.Ideally,the mean value of the correction should be included in the adopted value of $\overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 }$ and,hence,not included in the $\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 }$ .The practical situation isnot soclear,because satellite-derived values of $C _ { 2 0 }$ ,asin the GEM geopotentials,have been obtained usinga mixture of methods,some applying the corrections and others not applying it.There is no way to ensure consistency in this regard short of re-estimating $C _ { 2 0 }$ with a consistent technique.If thisis done,the inclusion of the zero-frequency terminEq.5.74 should beavoided,because $k _ { 2 }$ is not the appropriateLove number to use for such aterm.

The zero-frequency change in $\overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 }$ can be removed by computing $\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 }$ as

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } ^ { * } = \Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } \left( \mathrm { f r o m ~ E q . ~ } 5 . 7 4 \right) - \left. \Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } \right.
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left. \Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } \right. = A _ { 0 } H _ { 0 } k _ { 2 } } \\ & { ~ = ( 4 . 4 2 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } ) ( - 0 . 3 1 4 6 0 ) k _ { 2 } } \\ & { ~ = - 1 . 3 9 1 4 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } k _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Using $k _ { 2 } = 0 . 3 0$ then

$$
\left. \Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } \right. = - 4 . 1 7 4 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 }
$$

or

$$
\left. \Delta J _ { 2 } \right. = - \left. \Delta \overline { { C } } _ { 2 0 } \right. \sqrt { 5 } = 9 . 3 3 3 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 } .
$$

The decision to remove or not to remove the mean from the corrections depends on whether the adopted $C _ { 2 0 }$ does or does not already contain it and on whether $k _ { 2 }$ is a potential “solve for”parameter.If $k _ { 2 }$ is to be estimated,then it must not multiply the zero frequency term in the correction.

In thecase of EGM2O08,the diference between the zero-tide and tide-free value of $C _ { 2 0 }$ is $- 4 . 1 7 3 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 }$ Assuming the same values from Eq.5.86 for $A _ { 0 }$ , $H _ { 0 }$ and $k _ { 2 }$ ,the tide-free value of $C _ { 2 0 }$ corresponding to Table5.4would be $- 0 . 4 8 4 1 6 5 3 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 }$

The use of zero-tide values and the subsequent removal of the effect of the permanent tide from the tide model is presented for consistency with the 18th IAG General Assembly Resolution 16.

# 5.3.3.3 Solid Tide Effect on Station Coordinates

The variations of station coordinates caused by solid Earth tides predicted using Wahr's theory are also most effciently implemented using a two-step procedure.Only the second degree tides are necessary to retain $0 . 0 1 \mathrm { m }$ precision. Third degree tidesare needed for high accuracy( $\mathrm { 1 m m }$ or better) applications.The first step uses frequency-independent Love and Shida numbers and a computation of the tidal potential in the time domain.A convenient formulationof the displacement is given inthe documentation for theDEHANTTIDEINEL program (URL[17];Mathews,Dehant,and Gipson 1997).The vector displacement of the station due to tidal deformation for step 1 can be computed from

$$
\Delta \vec { r } = \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } R _ { e } ^ { 4 } } { G M _ { \oplus } R _ { j } ^ { 3 } } \left\{ h _ { 2 } \hat { r } \left( \frac { 3 ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) ^ { 2 } - 1 } { 2 } \right) + 3 l _ { 2 } ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) \left[ \hat { R } _ { j } - ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) \hat { r } \right] \right\}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { G M _ { j } = \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ p a r a m e t e r ~ f o r ~ t h e ~ M o o n ~ } ( j = 2 ) ~ } { \mathrm { o r ~ t h e ~ S u n ~ } } ( j = 3 ) ;  \\ & { G M _ { \oplus } = \mathrm { g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ p a r a m e t e r ~ f o r ~ t h e ~ E a r t h } ; } \\ & { \hat { R } _ { j } , R _ { j } = \mathrm { u n i t ~ v e c t o r ~ f r o m ~ t h e ~ g e o c e n t e r ~ t o ~ M o o n ~ o r ~ S u n ~ a n d ~ t h e ~ m a g n i t u d e ~ o f ~ t h a t ~ v e c t o r } } \\ & { \quad R _ { e } = \mathrm { E a r t h } ^ { \ast } \mathrm { ~ s ~ e q u a t o r i a l ~ r a d i u s } ; } \\ & { \quad \hat { r } , r = \mathrm { u n i t ~ v e c t o r ~ f r o m ~ t h e ~ g e o c e n t e r ~ t o ~ t h e ~ s t a t i o n ~ a n d ~ t h e ~ m a g n i t u d e ~ o f ~ t h a t ~ v e c t o r } ; } \\ & { \quad h _ { 2 } = \mathrm { n o m i n a l ~ s e c o n d - d e g r e e ~ L o v e ~ n u m b e r } ; } \\ & { \quad l _ { 2 } = \mathrm { n o m i n a l ~ S h i d a ~ n u m b e r } . } \end{array}
$$

The largest radial and transverse displacements due to the degree 2 tide are found to be $3 1 4 \mathrm { m m }$ and $6 5 \mathrm { m m }$ ,respectively.

The Love numbers of the degree 3 tides may be taken as real and constant incomputations to the degree of accuracy aimed at here.The displacement vector due to these tides is then givenby

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \Delta \vec { r } = \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 } \frac { G M _ { j } R _ { e } ^ { 5 } } { G M _ { \oplus } R _ { j } ^ { 4 } } \{ h _ { 3 } \hat { r } ( \frac { 5 } { 2 } ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) )  } } \\ & { } & {  + l _ { 3 } ( \frac { 1 5 } { 2 } ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } ) [ \hat { R } _ { j } - ( \hat { R } _ { j } \cdot \hat { r } ) \hat { r } ] \} . } \end{array}
$$

OnlytheMoon's contribution $( j = 2$ )needs to be computed because the Sun's contribution $( j = 3 )$ is negligible.The transverse part ofthe displacement (from Eq.5.9o) does not exceed $0 . 2 \mathrm { m m }$ ,butthe radial displacement can reach $1 . 7 \mathrm { m m }$ .

If nominal values for $h _ { 2 }$ and $l _ { 2 }$ of 0.6078and O.o847,respectively,are used with a cutoff of $0 . 0 0 0 4 \mathrm { m }$ of radial displacement,only one term needs to be corrected in Step 2. This is the $K _ { 1 }$ frequency where $h$ from Wahr's theory is O.52O3.Only theradial displacement needs

to be corrected,and to sufficient accuracy,this can be implemented as a periodic change in station height given by

$$
\delta h _ { S T A } = \delta h _ { K _ { 1 } } H _ { K _ { 1 } } \left( - \sqrt { \frac { 5 } { 2 4 \pi } } \right) 3 \sin \phi \cos \phi \sin ( \Theta _ { K _ { 1 } } + \lambda )
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \delta h _ { K _ { 1 } } = h _ { K _ { 1 } } ( \mathrm { W a h r } ) - h _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { N o m i n a l } ) = - 0 . 0 8 7 5 ; } \\ & { H _ { K _ { 1 } } = \mathrm { a m p l i t u d e ~ o f } \ K _ { 1 } \mathrm { ~ t e r m } \left( 1 6 5 . 5 5 5 \right) \mathrm { i n ~ t h e ~ h a r m o n i c ~ e x p a n s i o n ~ o f ~ t h e ~ t i d e ~ g e n e r a t } } \\ & { \qquad \mathrm { p o t e n t i a l } = 0 . 3 6 8 7 8 \mathrm { ~ m } ; } \\ & { \phi = \mathrm { g e o c e n t r i c ~ l a t i t u d e ~ o f ~ s t a t i o n } ; } \\ & { \lambda = \mathrm { e a s t ~ l o n g i t u d e ~ o f ~ s t a t i o n } ; } \\ & { \Theta _ { K _ { 1 } } = K _ { 1 } \mathrm { t i d e ~ a r g u m e n t } = \tau \ + s = \Theta _ { g } + \pi ; } \end{array}
$$

or,simplifying,

$$
\delta h _ { S T A } = - 0 . 0 2 5 3 \sin \phi \cos \phi \sin ( \Theta _ { g } + \lambda ) .
$$

The effect is maximum at $\phi = 4 5 ^ { \circ }$ where the amplitude is $0 . 0 1 3 \mathrm { m }$ .

There is also a zero-frequency station displacement that may or may not be included in thenominal station coordinates.The mean correction could be removed as in the discussion above.When baselines or coordinates are compared at the few-cm level,care must be taken that the correction was handled consistently.It is essential that published station coordinates identify how the zero-frequency contribution was included.

If nominalLove and Shida numbers of O.6O78 and0.O847,respectively,along with $h ^ { ( 2 ) } =$ $- 0 . 0 0 0 6$ and $l ^ { ( 2 ) } = 0 . 0 0 0 2$ ,are used with Eq.5.89,the permanentdeformation introduced is

$$
[ - 0 . 1 2 0 6 + 0 . 0 0 0 1 P _ { 2 } ( \sin \phi ) ] P _ { 2 } ( \sin \phi ) \mathrm { ~ m ~ }
$$

in the radial direction, and

$$
[ - 0 . 0 2 5 2 - 0 . 0 0 0 1 P _ { 2 } ( \sin \phi ) ] \sin 2 \phi \mathrm { m }
$$

in the north direction, where $P _ { 2 } ( \sin \phi ) = ( 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \phi - 1 ) / 2$ .These are the components of the vector to be added to the conventional tide free computed tide-corrected position to obtain themean tide position.The radial component of this restitution to obtain the mean tide value amounts to about $- 1 2 \mathrm { c m }$ at the poles and about $+ 6 \mathrm { c m }$ at the equator.

# 5.3.4 Ocean Tide Model

The dynamical effect of ocean tides is most easily implemented as periodic variations in the normalized geopotential coefficients.The variations can be written as follows (Eanes,Schutz, and Tapley 1983):

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { n m } - i \Delta \overline { { S } } _ { n m } = F _ { n m } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } \sum _ { + } ^ { - } ( C _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } \mp i S _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } ) e ^ { \pm i \Theta _ { s } }
$$

where

$$
F _ { n m } = \frac { 4 \pi G \rho _ { w } } { g _ { e } } \left[ \frac { ( n + m ) ! } { ( n - m ) ! ( 2 n + 1 ) ( 2 - \delta _ { m 0 } ) } \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { 1 + k _ { n } ^ { \prime } } { 2 n + 1 }
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { g _ { e } = G M _ { \oplus } / a _ { e } ^ { 2 } = 9 . 7 8 0 3 2 7 8 \mathrm { m s } ^ { - 2 } ; } \\ & { G = \mathrm { t h e ~ u n i v e r s a l ~ g r a v i t a t i o n a l ~ c o n s t a n t } = 6 . 6 7 4 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { k g } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { s } ^ { - 2 } ; } \\ & { \rho _ { w } = \mathrm { d e n s i t y ~ o f ~ s e a w a t e r } = 1 0 2 5 \mathrm { k g } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 } ; } \\ & { k _ { n } ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { l o a d d e f o r m a t i o n ~ c o e f f i c i e n t s } ( k _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 3 0 7 5 , ~ k _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 1 9 5 4 , ~ k _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 1 3 2 , } \\ & { k _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 1 0 3 2 , ~ k _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 0 8 9 2 ) ; } \end{array}
$$

$C _ { s n m } ^ { \pm }$ snm $S _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } =$ oceaide $s$ (see Table 5.6);

$\Theta _ { s } =$ argument of the tide constituent $s$ as defined in the solid tide model

In thecurrent (2O10) recommended ocean tidemodel,FES2OO4(Lyard,Lefevre,and Letellier 2Oo6),the global ocean tides were solved from basin-scale solutions with elevation boundary conditions extracted from the Naval Surface Weapon Center (NSWC) model and fromthe geodetic satellite (GEOSAT) empirical model (Cartwright and Ray 1991),as well astidegauge data in dynamicallycomplexareas.Foreach constituent $s$ in the diurnal and semi-diurnal tidal bands,both hydrodynamical modeling and data assimilation from TOPEX/Poseidon satellite altimetry data and European remote-sensing satellite (ERS) data were performed.In the long period tidal band case,no data assimilation was performed; therefore,for each constituent $\mathbf { \Delta } _ { \mathbf { { \cal { S } } } }$ ,the solutions from FES204 are purely theoretical. The digitalFES2oo4 atlas (tidal elevationandloading/self-attraction) isavailableat URL[18] in the“Sea level and tides”submenu.

The summation, $\Sigma _ { + } ^ { - }$ ,implies addition of the expression using the top signs (the prograde wavesC+. and $S _ { s n m } ^ { + }$ )tothatusingthebosigns(terogdeaves $C _ { s n m } ^ { - }$ and $S _ { s n m } ^ { - }$ ） The ocean-tide coefficients $C _ { s n m } ^ { \pm }$ and $S _ { s n m } ^ { \pm }$ as used here are related to the Schwiderski(1983) ocean-tide amplitude and phase by

$$
C _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } - i S _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } = - i \hat { C } _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } e ^ { i ( \epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } + \chi _ { s } ) }
$$

where

$\hat { C } _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } =$ ocean-tide amplitude for constituent s using the Schwiderski(Schwiderski 1983) notation;   
$\epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { \pm } = $ ocean-tide phase for constituents;

0 Semi-diurnal,and long period; Xs=π/2 K1;(or in general for constituents with $H _ { s } > 0$ ）； -π/2O,Pl,Q;(or in general for constituents with $H _ { s } < 0$ ).

d><td>.1029</td><td>-.0247</td></tr><tr><td>275.555</td><td>K</td><td>5</td><td>2</td><td>.0382</td><td>.411</td><td>.0003</td><td>.0382</td></tr><tr><td>275.555</td><td>K</td><td>6</td><td>2</td><td>.0467</td><td>281.357</td><td>-.0458</td><td>.0092</td></tr></table>

ä The spherical harmonic decomposition of Schwiderski's models was computed by Duncan Agnew(Agnew 1996) of UniversityofCalifornia,SanDiego. b The Doodson variable multipliers $( \overline { { n } } )$ are coded into the argument number $( A )$ after Doodson (1921)as $A = { n _ { 1 } } ( n _ { 2 } + 5 ) ( { n _ { 3 } } + 5 ) . ( { n _ { 4 } } + 5 ) ( { n _ { 5 } } + 5 ) ( { n _ { 6 } } + 5 )$ . cFor the long-period tides $( m = 0$ ),the valueof $\hat { C } _ { s n m } ^ { + }$ used to compute $C _ { s n m } ^ { + }$ and $S _ { s n m } ^ { + }$ was twice that shown to account for the combined effect of the retrograde and prograde waves.

For clarity,Eq.5.95 is rewritten in two forms:

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { n m } = F _ { n m } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } [ ( C _ { s n m } ^ { + } + C _ { s n m } ^ { - } ) \cos \Theta _ { s } + ( S _ { s n m } ^ { + } + S _ { s n m } ^ { - } ) \sin \Theta _ { s } ]
$$

or

$$
\Delta \overline { { C } } _ { n m } = F _ { n m } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } [ \hat { C } _ { s n m } ^ { + } \sin ( \Theta _ { s } + \epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { + } + \chi _ { s } ) + C _ { s n m } ^ { - } \sin ( \Theta _ { s } + \epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { - } + \chi _ { s } ) ]
$$

$$
\Delta \overline { { S } } _ { n m } = F _ { n m } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } [ ( S _ { s n m } ^ { + } + S _ { s n m } ^ { - } ) \cos \Theta _ { s } - ( C _ { s n m } ^ { + } + C _ { s n m } ^ { - } ) \sin \Theta _ { s } ]
$$

or

$$
\Delta \overline { { S } } _ { n m } = F _ { n m } \sum _ { s ( n , m ) } [ \hat { C } _ { s n m } ^ { + } \cos ( \Theta _ { s } + \epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { + } + \chi _ { s } ) + C _ { s n m } ^ { - } \cos ( \Theta _ { s } + \epsilon _ { s n m } ^ { - } + \chi _ { s } ) ] .
$$

The summation over $s ( n , m )$ should include all constituents for which Schwiderski has computed a model. Except for cases of near resonance,the retrograde terms do not produce long-period( $> 1$ day)orbit perturbations for the diurnal and semi-diurnal tides.For high altitude geodetic satellites likeLageos,in order to reduce the required computing time,it is recommended that out of the complete selection,only the constituents whose Cartwright and Tayler amplitudes $H _ { s }$ are greater than $0 . 5 ~ \mathrm { m m }$ be used,with their spherical harmonic expansion terminated at maximum degree and order 8.

Table 5.6 gives the values required for each of the constituents for which Schwiderski has computed a model.Note that the units in Table5.6are centimeters and hence must be scaled to meters for use with the constants given with Eq.5.95.

The( $n = 2$ ， $m = 2$ ）termfor the $S _ { 2 }$ argument can be modified to account for the atmospheric tide using the results of Chapman and Lindzen (197O).The modified values to be used instead of those in Table 5.6 are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { 2 2 } ^ { + } = - 0 . 5 3 7 \mathrm { c m } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { S _ { 2 2 } ^ { + } = 0 . 3 2 1 \mathrm { c m } . } } \end{array}
$$

For themost precise applications,more than the eleven terms listed in Table 5.6 need to be modeled.This can be accomplished by assuming that the ocean tide admittance varies smoothly with frequency andby using the Schwiderski values asaguide to the interpolation to other frequencies.

# 5.3.5Site Displacement Due to Ocean and Atmospheric Loading

# 5.3.5.1 Ocean Loading

In the past few decades,displacements due to oceanloading have been calculated by several authors [e.g.,Schwiderski (198O),Eanes (1994),Egbert,Bennett,andForeman (1994),Le Provost et al. (1994),Eanes and Bettadpur (1995),Le Provost et al. (1998),Ray (1999), Lefevre,Lyard,andLeProvost (2ooo),Matsumoto,Takanezawa,and Ooe (2Ooo),Egbert andErofeeva (2002),Lefevre etal.(2002),Letellier (2004),Andersen (2006),and Savcenko and Bosch(2Oo8)].Most of these modern models assimilate sea surface height measurements madebyaltimetry satelitesand integrate the tide height overall oceanmasseswitha weighting function [Green’s function,see Farrell(1972),Zschau (1983),and Scherneck (1990)].

Inall these models,the locally referenced displacement vector $\mathbf { E }$ in the vertical (radial), N-S and E-Wlocal coordinate systemat time $t$ can be computed as a sum of the contributions of $n$ individual ocean tides

$$
\mathbf { E } ( t ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { A _ { i } ^ { r } \cos ( \omega _ { i } t + \phi _ { i } - \delta _ { i } ^ { r } ) } \\ { A _ { i } ^ { N S } \cos ( \omega _ { i } t + \phi _ { i } - \delta _ { i } ^ { N S } ) } \\ { A _ { i } ^ { E W } \cos ( \omega _ { i } t + \phi _ { i } - \delta _ { i } ^ { E W } ) } \end{array} \right.
$$

where $\omega _ { i }$ is the angular frequency of the tidalconstituents and $\phi _ { i }$ the corresponding astronomical argument.The astronomical argument can be computed from the subroutine ARG2.F availableat URL[19].The amplitudes $A _ { i } ^ { r }$ ， $A _ { i } ^ { N S }$ ， $A _ { i } ^ { E W }$ ,and the Greenwich phase lags $\delta _ { i } ^ { r }$ ， SNS , $\delta _ { i } ^ { E W }$ of each tidal component are determined bythe particular model assumed for the deformation of the Earth.

The site displacements resulting from the ocean tides have been compiled by D.Agnew (Agnew 1997).Tidal loading radial (height) displacement amplitude and phase values for selected laser and VLBI station locations have been computed for the semi-diurnal $M _ { 2 }$ , $S _ { 2 }$ , $K _ { 2 }$ , $N _ { 2 }$ ,diurmal $O _ { 1 }$ , $K _ { 1 }$ ， $P _ { 1 }$ ， $Q _ { 1 } ,$ ,and long-period $M _ { f }$ ， $M _ { m }$ ,and $S _ { s a }$ ocean tides using any of theleading global ocean models available from the ocean loading service website (URL[20]; Scherneck 1991) providedbyM.S.Bos andH.-G.Scherneck.The implementationconsiders atotal of 342 constituent tides whose amplitudes and phases are foundby spline interpolation ofthe tidal admittancesbased on the11main tides.

Table 5.7 gives the amplitude and phase values for the eleven main tides at the selected SLR and VLBI stations (using ITRF2O08 and ocean tide model TPXO.7.2).The phase convention of Schwiderski has been preserved.His phases for the diurnal tides differby $\pm 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ from the standard Doodson definition.The height displacement,in meters,can be found for a given constituent $i$ as follows:

$$
h ( i ) = \mathrm { a m p } ( i ) \times \mathrm { c o s } ( \mathrm { a r g } ( i , t ) - \mathrm { p h a s e } ( i ) )
$$

where $\mathrm { a r g } ( i , t )$ is the proper angular argument to be used with the Schwiderski phases (a Fortran subroutine,HARDISP.F,for this purpose was introduced in an update to the IERSConventions (2003) on November 25,2006 provided at URL[19]).The total tidal displacement is found by summing over all 342 constituents.A negative $h ( i )$ means that the surface at the predicted point has been lowered.

# 5.3.5.2Ocean Pole Tide Loading

Theocean pole tide is generated by the centrifugal effect of polar motion on the oceans, characterizedby the potential defined in $\ S \ 6 . 9 . 2 . 1$ of this book.Polar motion isdominated by the 14-month Chandler wobble and annual variations.At these long periods,the ocean pole tide is expected to have an equilibrium response,where the displaced ocean surface is in equilibrium with the forcing equipotential surface.

Desai (2Oo2) provides an equilibrium model of the ocean pole tide.This model accounts for continental boundaries,mass conservation over the oceans,self-gravitation,and loading of the ocean floor. Using this model,the load of the ocean pole tide produces the following deformation vector at any point on the surface of the Earth with latitude $\phi$ and longitude $\lambda$ . The load deformation vector is expressed here in terms of radial, north and east components, $u _ { r } , u _ { n }$ ,and $u _ { e }$ ,respectively,and isa function of the wobble parameters $( m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } )$

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { u _ { r } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { n } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { e } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \end{array} \right] = K \left\{ ( m _ { 1 } \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { R } + m _ { 2 } \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { I } ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { u _ { r } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { n } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { e } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \end{array} \right] + ( m _ { 2 } \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { R } - m _ { 1 } \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { I } ) \left[ \begin{array} { l } { u _ { r } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { n } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \\ { u _ { e } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda ) } \end{array} \right] \right\}
$$

where

$$
K = \frac { 4 \pi G a _ { e } \rho _ { w } H _ { p } } { 3 g _ { e } }
$$

$$
H _ { p } = \left( \frac { 8 \pi } { 1 5 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { \omega ^ { 2 } a _ { e } ^ { 4 } } { G M _ { \oplus } }
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \omega = \mathrm { n o m i n a l ~ m e a n ~ a n g u l a r ~ v e l o c i t y ~ o f ~ t h e ~ E a r t h } = 7 . 2 9 2 1 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { r a d ~ s } ^ { - 1 } } \\ & { a _ { e } = \mathrm { e q u a t o r i a l ~ r a d i u s ~ o f ~ t h e ~ E a r t h } = 6 3 7 8 1 3 6 . 6 ~ \mathrm { m } ; } \end{array}
$$

$G M _ { \oplus }$ is defined in $\ S 5 . 3 . 3 . 3$ ; $g _ { e }$ , $G$ ,and $\rho _ { w }$ are defined in $\ S 5 . 3 . 4$ ;

$$
\gamma = ( 1 + k _ { 2 } - h _ { 2 } ) = \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { R } + i \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { I } = 0 . 6 8 7 0 + 0 . 0 0 3 6 i ;
$$

$( m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } )$ are the wobble parameters.Refer to $\ S \ 6 . 9 . 2 . 1$ of this book for the relationship between the wobble variables $( m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } )$ and the polar motion variables $( x _ { p } , y _ { p } )$ .Functions $u _ { r } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda )$ ， $u _ { n } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda )$ ， $u _ { e } ^ { R } ( \phi , \lambda )$ are the real part of the ocean pole load tide coefficients. Functions $u _ { r } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda )$ , $u _ { n } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda )$ , $u _ { e } ^ { I } ( \phi , \lambda )$ are the imaginary part of the ocean pole load tide coefficients.

Maps of the required ocean pole load tide coeffcients on an equally spaced 0.5 by 0.5degree global grid are available at URL[21].These coefficientsprovide the surface deformations with respect to the instantaneous center of mass of the deformed Earth including the mass of the loading ocean pole tide.

# 5.3.5.3Atmospheric Loading

The procedure described below is taken from the IERS Conventions (2O03) (IERS 2004).A time-varying atmospheric pressure distribution can induce crustal deformation.For example, pressure variations on the orderof2O hectoPascal (hPa)—and even larger—at mid-latitudes are observed in synoptic pressure systems with length scales for $1 0 0 0 { - } 2 0 0 0 \ \mathrm { k m }$ and periods of approximately two weeks.Seasonal pressure changes due to air mass movements between the continents and oceans can have amplitudes of up to $1 0 \ \mathrm { h P a }$ in particular over the large land masses of the northern hemisphere.At the interannual periods,basin-wide air pressure signals with amplitudes of several hPa also contribute to the spectrum of the loading signal.

Other surface loads due to changes in snow and ice cover,soil moisture and groundwater, as well as ocean-bottom pressure also contribute to surface displacements.For example,at seasonal time scales,itisexpected that the contributionof hydrological loads to surface displacements exceeds the one from air pressure (Blewitt et al.2Oo1).However,while the atmospheric load is fairly wellknown from global air pressure data sets,no suficient models for ocean bottom pressure,snow and soil moisture exists at this time.Therefore,in the following,focusison atmospheric loading although the discussion appliesto any other surface loading.

Theoretical studies by Rabbel and Zschau (1985),Rabbel and Schuh (1986),vanDam and Wahr (1987),and Manabe et al.(1991) demonstrate that vertical crustal displacements of up to $2 5 \mathrm { m m }$ are possible at mid-latitude stations due to synoptic pressure systems.Annual signals in the vertical are on the order of $1 { - } 2 \mathrm { m m }$ ,butmaximum signals of more than $3 \mathrm { m m }$ arepossible over largepartsofAsia,Antarctica,Australia and Greenland (Mangaroti et al.2Oo1；Dong et al.2Oo2).Pressure loading effects are larger at higherlatitude sites due to the more intensive weather systems (larger in amplitude and more spatially coherent).Effects are smaller at mid-latitude sites and at locations within $5 0 0 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ of the sea or ocean due to the inverted barometer response of the ocean.In all cases,horizontal crustal deformationsare about one-third the amplitude of the vertical effects.

Two basic methods for computing atmospheric loading corrections to geodetic data have beenapplied so far:1） using geophysical models or simple approximations derived from thesemodels;and2)using empirical models based on site-dependent data.The geophysical model approach is analogous to the methods described to compute ocean tidal loading effects. Both methods suffer from a number of disadvantages (see IERS 2004, $\ S \ 7 . 1 . 6 )$ .In2002 the IERS recommended thatcorrections for,among other surface loadings,atmospheric loading should be determined using the geophysical model approach and obtained from the Special Bureau on Loading (SBL) at URL[22].

Regression coefficients based ona geophysical model are already available for anumber ofVLBI sites through the SBL web page and the IERS Conventions’web page,URL[23]. The regresson coefficients were computed using 18 years of the National Centers for EnvironmentalPrediction(NCEP)ReanalysisData(1Jan.1980 to 31Dec.1997).The data are 6-hour values of surface pressure given on a $2 . 5 ^ { \circ } \times 2 . 5 ^ { \circ }$ global grid.Vertical crustal motions at a particular site are modeled by convolving Farrell's (1972) Green's functions for aGutenberg-BullenA Earth model. The regression results $\left( { \mathrm { m m } } / { \mathrm { m b a r } } \right)$ are determined via a linear regresson between the modeled crustal displacements and the local surface pressure determined from the NCEP data set.An inverted barometer model was used in determining the ocean's response to pressure.

# 5.3.6 Plate Motions

One of the factors that affect the definition of a terrestrial reference frame is the motion of the tectonic plates that make up the Earth's surface.As the plates move,fixed coordinates for the observing stationswillbecome inconsistent witheach other.The rates of relative motions forsome regular observing sites are believed to be approximately 5centimeters per year or larger(derived from the dating of rocksand other geophysical information).Plate motions can be measured to a precision of a fewmillimeters per year or better using space geodetic techniques suchasVLBI, SLR,and GPS.There isconsiderableagreement between motions measured overa few years by space geodesy andthe predictions of global plate motion models that average over the pastfew million years.

Work by Argus and Gordon (1991),which incorporated the plate motion model NUVEL-1 ofDeMets etal.(1990),showed differencesas large as $1 5 \mathrm { m m }$ per year between their model NNR-NUVEL-1and AM0-2(Minster and Jordan1978).All plates in thismodel move in aright-handed sense relative to the no net rotation reference frame.This no net rotation reference frame differs substantially from the hotspot reference frame (Norton 2Ooo)where volcanic“hotspots”are tied to the interior of theEarth.

Table5.8 Cartesian rotation vector for each plate using absolute plate model NNR-NUVEL-1A (no net rotation)

Estimates from space geodetic measurements prompted DeMets et al. (1994) to modifyNUVEL-1 angular velocitieswhich were found to be systematically too fast.This recalibratedplatemotion model isreferred toasNNR-NUVEL-1A.Inaddition to the plate motions determined by DeMets et al.(199O),recalibrated angular velocities for the Juan de Fuca,Philippine,Rivera,and Scotia plateswere provided.Although individual plate discrepancies between angular velocity measurements by space geodetic techniquesand model predictions may exceed $3 \mathrm { m m }$ per year (corresponding to ITRF2000 orientation rate alignment differences;see IERS (2O1O),chapter 4)，globally averaged plate motions are very steady,within $2 \%$ to $6 \%$ ，overa time scale from several years toseveral Myr.The cartesian rotation vector for each of the major plates isgiven in Table5.8.

# 5.3.7Tidal Effects in UT1

To properly compare UT1results obtainedatdifferent epochs,the high-frequency tidaleffects mustbeaccounted for. Thus,a standard expression for the tidal variations in UT1is required. The periodic variations in UT1 due to tidal deformation of the polar moment of inertia have beenrederived(Yoder,Williams,andParke1981)including the tidal deformationofthe Earth with a decoupled core.

This model leads to effective Love numbers that differ from the bulk value of 0.301 because of the oceansand the fluid core,which give rise to different theoretical values of the ratio $k / C$ forthe fortnightly and monthly terms.However, Yoder etal.recommend the value of 0.94 for $k / C$ forboth cases.Periodic terms in UT1are given in Table5.9and Table5.10 andare discussed in IERS Explanatory Supplement (IERS 2OO9)and Gross (20O7).Table 5.9 includes terms due to zonal tides,with periodsup to 35 days,with $k / C = 0 . 9 4$ Table5.10 lists the remainder of the tidal terms (with periods greater than 35 days).

UT1R, $\Delta R$ ,and $\omega R$ represent the regularized forms of UT1(including periods up to 35 days),of the duration of the day, $\Delta$ ,andof theangularvelocityof the Earth, $\omega$ Theunits are $1 0 ^ { - 4 }$ s for UT, $1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ s/day for $\Delta$ ,and $1 0 ^ { - 1 4 }$ rad/s for $\omega$ .To avoid confusion among possible tidal models,itis recommended thatthe termsδUT1, $\delta \Delta , \delta \omega$ be followed by the model name in parenthesis,e.g.,δUT1(Yoder et al.1981).

Oceanic tides with short periods (diurnal and sub-diurnal) cause variations in UTl and polar motion that are only partially representedby the model given below.According to Brosche etal.(1989) and Seiler and Wuinsch (1995),the contribution of the oceanic tides should be split into a part that is in phase with the solid Earth tidesand an out-of-phase part.The discrepancy between themodel of Yoder etal.(which alreadycontains the general influence of the oceans)and the model of Brosche et al.,amounts to a maximum of $\pm 0 . 0 8$ msec for thefortnightly and monthly terms.The influence of the ocean tides with periods less than or equal to $2 4 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ is at the level of $\pm 0 . 1$ msec.Kantha,Stewart,and Desai (1998) found that both $M _ { f }$ and $M _ { m }$ tides have amplitudes of $\sim 0 . 1 1 5$ msec.Thus,the impact of these tides on length of day changes is significant.

Following the IERSConventions(20l0) (IERS201O),it is recommended thata combination of the Yoder et al.(1981) elastic body tide,Wahr and Bergen (1986)anelastic body tide,and Kantha,Stewart,and Desai (1998)ocean tide models are to be used to model longperiod ocean tide effects on the rotation of the Earth.The Kantha,Stewart,andDesai (1998) ocean tide model replaces the Defraigne and Smits (1999) model recommended in the IERS Conventions (2Oo3) (IERS 2O04).That model leavesa large signal at the fortnightly tidal period when removed from length of day observations (Gross 2009).

# 5.3.8Tidal Effects inPolar Motion

As mentioned in Gross (2Oo8),several authors have produced both dynamical and empirical models for determining the effect of ocean tides on polar motion.The IERS Conventions (2010)recommended model for long-period ocean tide effects on polar motion (IERS 2010, chapter 8)is the dynamical model of Dickman and Nam (1995)as reported in Dickman and Gross (2O10).Dickman and Nam (1995) estimated polar motion by Fourier spectral analysis of the Space92 polar motion time series (Gross 1993).The effect on polar motion is greatest forthe fortnightly $M _ { f }$ tide.Empirical fitstopolar motion observational measurements give (prograde,retrograde） amplitudes of (69,89) $\mu$ asat $M _ { f }$ .Themodeledeffectyields (prograde,retrograde) polar motion amplitudes of (66,74) $\mu$ asat $M _ { f }$ .Table5.11provides corrections for the tidal variations in polar motion and polar motion excitation with periods fromnine days to 18.6 years.The sum of these corrections should be subtracted from the observed polar motion and polar motion excitation values so that variations are“tidefree.”

# 5.4Monitoring the Orientation of the Earth

Continuous monitoring of the Earth'sorientation began after Seth CarloChandlerJr.'sdiscovery of the variation of latitude in 1884(Carter and Carter 2OoO)and Chandler’s explanation of it as polar motion (Chandler wobble). Optical astrometric methods then in use continued to be the only source of Earth orientation information up until the mid-196Os.More modern techniques then gradually took over the bulk of the activity of monitoring the orientation of the Earth,with Doppler tracking of satellites,Connected-Element Radio Interferometry (CERI),lunarlaserranging (LLR),satellitelaserranging (SLR),VeryLongBaseline Interferometry (VLBI),Doppler orbitography and radiopositioning integrated by satelite(DORIS), and Global Navigation Satellite System(GNSS),such as Global Positioning System(GPS), assisting in such monitoring.Currently,the techniques of SLR,VLBI,and GPS provide most of the current high-accuracy observations for this purpose.Table 5.12 provides a listing of EOP techniques and estimated accuracies for one year.

Table 5.12 Estimated accuracies of EOP techniques in 20O8 provided by IERS Rapid Service/Prediction Center (unless indicated otherwise).
