# Precession,Nutation,Polar Motion, and Earth Rotation

JAMESL.HILTON&DENNISD.McCARTHY

# 6.1 Introduction

Tospecify positions of celestial objects it is necessary to have a reference system.An ideal reference system would provide an inertial reference frame that could be used at all frequencies of the electromagnetic spectrum,and forall magnitudes or intensities.Anumber of problems,unfortunately,interfere with realizing such a reference system.Among thoseis thefact that most observers are situated on the Earth,an observing platform that is constantly changing in orientation with respect to inertial space.

This chapterdeals with those transformations necessary to determine the orientation of the Earth,the terrestrial reference system (TRS),with respect to the sky,the celestial reference system (CRS).It also describes the transformation from a reference system attached to a rotating Earth to that of a non-rotating object in the Earth's orbit about the solar system barycenter.This chapter does not include:those changes inthe reference systemarising from the Earth's nonlinear motion about the solar system barycenter,such as the annual aberration oflight;relativistic effects,such as the deflection of light by massive objects;or changes in coordinates arising from an observer being situated on the Earth's surface rather than at the geocenter,such as horizontal parallax or diurnal aberration of light.These topicsare discussed in Chapter7.Nor doesthis chapter deal with those changes in position caused bythe flexure of a non-rigid Earth,discussed in Chapter5.This chapter describes how The Astronomical Almanac determines the orientation of the TRS with respect to the CRS.The method of transforming from the CRS to the TRS underwent major changesin 2O03 and 2006.Both the old and new paradigmsare discussed.The procedures for these reductions arefound mainly in SectionBof The Astronomical Almanac and Chapter7of this book.

At the most basic level,the transformation for a vector, $\mathbf { X }$ ,from the TRS, ${ \bf x } _ { T R S }$ ,tothe CRS, $\mathbf { x } _ { C R S }$ ,iscarried outby the transformation:

$$
\mathbf { x } _ { C R S } = \mathbf { P } \mathbf { N } \mathbf { T } \mathbf { W } \mathbf { x } _ { T R S }
$$

where P, N, $\mathbf { T }$ and $\mathbf { W }$ are a set of rotation matrices that account for precession, nutation, Earth rotation,and polar motion,respectively.Each of these rotation matrices is the product of the three basic rotation matrices, ${ \bf R } _ { 1 } ( \theta ) , { \bf R } _ { 2 } ( \theta )$ ,and ${ \bf R } _ { 3 } ( \theta )$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] \qquad \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { 0 } & { \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { 0 } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] \quad \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } } \\ & { \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \theta ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] } \end{array}
$$

where $\theta$ is the rotation angle,and the subscripts refer to rotations about the $x , y ,$ and $z$ axes, respectively,of a right-handed Cartesian reference system.Any rigid coordinate system can be mapped into any other rigid coordinate system with the same geometric origin bya series of three,non-unique,rotations (see Chapter14).

The first two matrices on the right hand side of Eq. 6.1, $\mathbf { P }$ andN,represent the dynamical motionof the Celestial Intermediate Pole (CIP; $\ S \ 6 . 2 . 3$ in response to the torques arising fromthe gravitational attraction of the Sun,Moon,and planets of the solar system.This motion has traditionally been separated into a secular part,precession,and a periodic part, nutation.Precession, $\mathbf { P }$ ,and nutation, $\mathbf { N }$ ,are treated as a separate transformations.The details of these transformations are covered in $\ S 6 . 6$ .

The third matrix on the right hand side of Eq.6.1, $\mathbf { T }$ ,is the rotation of the terrestrial reference systemwith respect to the celestial reference system.Thus,thismatrix is

$$
{ \bf T } = { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( T )
$$

where $T$ istheangle between the Terrestrial Intermediate Origin,TIO $( \ S 6 . 9 )$ ,and the origin of right ascension.In the past,the origin of right ascension was either the mean equinox of date,or the true equinox of date.Beginning in 20O3,the IAU (20old) recommends replacing the equinox and equator of datewith the Celestial Intermediate Origin,CIO $( \ S \ 6 . 7 )$ .The name for the angle $T$ depends on the origin of right ascension used.When the origin of rightascension is the mean equinox of date it iscalled theGreenwichMean Sidereal Time, GMST(g 6.8.5.1).When the origin of right ascension is the true equinox of date it is called the Greenwich Apparent Sidereal Time,GAST( $\textrm { \AA } 6 . 8 . 5 . 2 )$ .And when the origin of right ascension is the CIO it iscalled the Earth Rotation Angle,ERA( $\textrm { \AA } 6 . 8 . 4 )$ .The relationships between theERA,GMST,andGASTare discussed in $\ S 6 . 3$ and $\ S \ : 6 . 4$ .

Finally,the fourth matrix on the right hand sideofEq.6.1, $\mathbf { W }$ ,is the polar motion,and gives the position of the terrestrial reference frame with respect to the TIO ( $\lbrace 6 . 9 . 2 )$ .This matrix is

$$
{ \bf W } = { \bf R } _ { 1 } ( s ^ { \prime } ) { \bf R } _ { 2 } ( x ) { \bf R } _ { 1 } ( y )
$$

where $s ^ { \prime }$ is the TIO locator,and the angles $x$ and $y$ are the angular coordinates of the CIP $( \ S \ 6 . 2 . 3 )$ with respect to the terrestrial pole measured along the meridians at $0 ^ { \circ }$ and $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ $( 9 0 ^ { \circ } W )$ ,respectively.

# 6.2 The Celestial Reference System (CRS) and the Terrestrial Reference System (TRS)

Detailed information on the celestial reference system(CRS) itselfcan be found in Chapter4, and details of the terrestrial reference system(TRS) are in Chapter5.Both the CRS and the

TRS are rigid coordinate systems.Any such coordinate systemcan be described by a set of three mutually perpendicular coordinate axes.Alternatively,a direction vector, the pole;a plane perpendicular to that vector,the equator;anda fiducial point on the equator,the origin ofright ascension1,may describe the coordinate system.

Resolution A4 VII of theXXIst General Assembly of the International Astronomical Union (IAU 1992;URL[4]) defines the CRS.This resolution recommends the adoption of a celestial reference system based on VeryLong Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations of extragalactic radio sources aligned as near as possible to the mean equator of J2ooo.O with the origin of right ascension as near as possible to the dynamical equinox of J2ooo.0.This reference systemistobe,“...computed initially for the equator and equinox J2ooo.O(Julian date2451545.O TT)using the best available values of thecelestial pole offset with respect to the IAU expressions for precession and nutation...”A reference system based on this recommendation,the International Celestial Reference System (ICRS),was adopted in1997 inresolutionB2of theXXIIIrd General Assemblyof theIAU(IAU1997;URL[4]).The positive $z .$ -axis of the complimentary Cartesian coordinate is the north direction of the pole. The positive $x$ -axisis the direction towards the origin of right ascension.And thepositive $y$ -axis is the direction of $z \times x$ to give a right-handed coordinate system.

The ICRS is epochless,that is,unlike prior celestial reference systems it is not tied to the orientation of the Earth at some fiducial epoch.Instead,the only way in which the ICRS will change is if the positions of the defining sources on the sky are determined more accurately. Thus,although its initial construction aligned it with the best determination of the mean equatorand equinox of J2Ooo.0,subsequent improvement of the knowledge of that equator and equinox do not alter the orientation of the ICRS.Rather,the position of the mean equator and equinox in the ICRS isaltered.

It is necessary to distinguish whether the geometric origin is the solar system barycenter or theEarth's center of mass,the geocenter.If the origin is the solar system barycenter, itisreferred toas the BarycentricCelestial Reference System (BCRS);if the origin is the geocenter,it is referred toas the GeocentricCelestial ReferenceSystem(GCRS).TheGCRS and the BCRS are connected by a relativistic coordinate transformation (IAU 2Oo1a).In general,theGCRS shouldbe used when the objectofinterest is within severalEarth diameters of the geocenter.Beyond this distance,the BCRS should be used.It is common for users to refer to the ICRS when they specifically mean the BCRS;however,the term BCRS ought to be used in these cases to prevent confusion with the more general term.

Thegeometric origin of the Terrestrial Reference System(TRS) isthe geocenter.It is described by the three orthogonal reference directionswith respect to the body of the Earth. The Earth rotates.Thus,the TRS is a non-inertial reference system and its orientationin space is constantly changing.

Formany applications itis desirableto useaCelestial Intermediate Reference Systemthat is related to the TRSataparticular epoch.In the past the direction of the celestial ephemeris pole defined the equator of this system.The CIP replaced the celestial ephemeris pole after 1Jan.2003.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0006_pages_0201-0240/ocr/images/bc4c7f082b0318174f73d422e78c4b5c5a83fe02f168e42f43e8d0cfe8f73c35.jpg)  
Figure6.1 Therelationship between the Celestial Intermediate Origin (CIO), $\sigma$ ,the origin of right ascension of the Geocentric Celestial Reference System (GCRS), $\Sigma$ ,the true equinox of J2000.0, $\Upsilon _ { 0 }$ ,the mean equinox of date, $\Upsilon _ { M }$ ,and the true equinox of date, $\mathrm { ~ \textit ~ { ~ T ~ } ~ } _ { T }$ .The true equator of date is identical withtheequatorof theCelestial IntermediatePole(CIP).The intersectionof theequatorof the GCRS and the true equator of date occurs at $N ^ { \prime }$ .The,GCRSisnearlyalignedwith the Earth's mean equator of J2ooo.O.Thus,there isa significant separation between $\mathrm { ~ \mathcal ~ { ~ T ~ } ~ } _ { 0 }$ and $\Sigma$

# 6.2.1Reference Systems in TheAstronomical Almanac

Itis useful todistinguish between areference systemand areference frame.Inthe terminology that is now commonly used,a reference system is the complete specification of how a coordinate systemis to beformed.It defines theoriginand axesofthe coordinate system. Italso specifies all of the constants,models,and algorithms used to transform between observable quantities and reference data that conform to the system.A reference frame consists of a set of identifiable fiducial points alongwith their coordinates,which serves asthe practical realization of a reference system.

In practice,thereare eight reference systems thatareused in The Astronomical Almanac. These are:

1.TheBarycentricCelestialReferenceSystem(BCRS):TheBCRSisdescribed by the equator,pole,and origin of right ascension $( \ S \ : 6 . 2 )$ as defined in IAU (1997).   
2.The Geocentric Celestial Reference System (GCRS): The equator,pole and origin of right ascension (S 6.2), $\Sigma$ inFig.6.1,of the GCRSare the BCRS equator,pole,and origin of right ascension transformed relativistically from the solarsystem barycenter to the geocenter.   
3.The mean equator and equinox of J2ooo.O:The equator and pole $( \ S \ : 6 . 2 )$ of J2000.0 arethe CIP $\lbrace \lbrace 6 . 2 . 3 )$ at the particular epoch J2Ooo.0,Julian date $2 4 5 1 5 4 5 . 0 \ \mathrm { T T }$ , and the plane perpendicular to it.The origin of right ascension is theposition of the mean equinox of J2ooo.0.Except fora constant offset of about 23 milliarcseconds represented by the frame bias matrix (§ 6.5),this reference system is the same as the GCRS.

4.The mean equator and equinox of date: The origin of right ascension, $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon } _ { M }$ in Fig. 6.1, is the position of the mean equinox(§ 6.2.4)of date.The difference between the mean equator of date and the mean equator of J2ooo.O is due to precession (§ 6.6.2).

5.The true equator and equinox of date:The equator $( \ S \ 6 . 2 )$ is the equator of the CIP(8 6.2.3). The origin of right ascension is the position of the true equinox of date ( 6.2.4), $\mathrm { ~ \textit ~ { ~ T ~ } ~ } _ { T }$ inFig.6.1,.The right ascension of this reference system may be called apparent right ascension to distinguish it from the right ascension used by the Intermediate Reference System below.Apparent right ascension is subtracted from Greenwich Apparent Solar Time(GAST), $\left( \ S \ 6 . 8 . 5 . 2 \right)$ ,to give the Greenwich Hour Angle,GHA,( $\ S$ 6.8.8).The difference between the equator of the CIP and the mean equator of date is the motion arising from nutation $( \ S \ : 6 . 6 . 3 )$

6.The Intermediate Reference System:Like the true equator and equinox of date,the equator(S 6.2)of the Intermediate Reference System is the equator ofthe CIP( $\ S 6 . 2 . 3 )$ However,the origin of right ascension $( \ S \ : 6 . 2 )$ is the CIO, $( \ S 6 . 7 )$ , $\sigma$ in Fig. 6.1,.The rightascension for thisreference systemmay becalled intermediate right ascension to distinguish it from the right ascension used by the true equator and equinox of date above.The choice of the CIO as the origin of right ascension makes the relation betweenUT1( $\ S 6 . 8 . 3 )$ and Earth rotation a linear function.Intermediate right ascension issubtracted fromthe EarthRotationAngle,ERAor $\theta$ ,(8 6.8.4),to give GHA.

7.The International Terrestrial ReferenceSystem(ITRS):The International Terrestrial Reference System was definedby the International Union ofGeodesy andGeophysics in1991.In practice,the ITRS is realized by the adopted coordinatesand velocities, arising fromplate tectonics and post-glacial rebound,ofalarge group of observing stations.These coordinates are not dependent on a reference ellipsoid,but are expressed asgeocentric rectangular 3-vectors.

8.The Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System (TIRS):The equator and pole are the CIP equator and poleof date,and theoriginof longitudeisthe Terrestrial Intermediate Origin(TIO), $( \ S 6 . 9 )$ .The difference betweentheITRSandtheTIRSis thattheTIRSis corrected for polar motion. The $z$ -axis oftheITRS is realized by the adopted positions of sites on the geodetic surface of the Earth while the $z$ -axisof theTIRSisin the direction of the CIP.

# 6.2.2The Conventional International Origin and the International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF)

The principal axes of abody are the orthogonal directions that define a Cartesian coordinate systemin which the momentof inertia tensorisa diagonal matrix,i.e.,the products ofinertia are zero.For an oblate rigid elipsoid of revolution,two of the principal moments of inertia areequal,and the third principal moment is larger than the other two.In this case,the axis of thethird principal momentof inertiaisalso theaxisoffigure.TheEarth,however,is elastic and is not a perfect ellipsoid of revolution.Thus,its inertia tensor and the directions of the principal axesof inertiaare functions of time.Nevertheless,an instantaneous axis offigure, $F$ ,may be definedasthe line passing through the center of massof the Earth,parallel to the eigenvector of the instantaneous inertia tensor of the Earth.The intersection of $F$ with the surface of the Earth isthe instantaneouspoleoffigure.


<!-- chunk 0007: pages 241-280 -->
The position of the instantaneousaxisof figure of adeformableEarthissubjectto motions from distortions of the Earth,suchas the body tides.The motion ofthis point makes it difficult to interpret the motions of $F$ in terms of rigid-body concepts.It thus becomes useful to define themean surface geographic axis, $B$ ,asanaxisattached inaleast-squares sense to the Earth's outersurface.Consideranetwork of observatoriesonarigid Earthand anaxis fixedwith respect to the positions of the observatories.The only possible motion of that network is a time-dependent,rigid rotation;the portion of the rigid rotation thatis not parallel to the axis will cause motion of the axis as the latter follows motions of the observatories defining it. On adeformable planet,other motions of the observatories may bepossible,and the definition of theaxis is generalized by the adopted positions of the observatories. $B$ is this axis in the limit of an infinite numberof uniformly distributed observatories.Thus,if we decompose the motion of the Earth'ssurface into a rigid rotation plus a residual deformation, $B$ moveswith thesense prescribed bytherigid rotation.The axis $B$ doesnot respond to body tides.For a rigidEarth $B$ and $F$ are coincident. Crustal motions and tidal forces cause motions of those observatories with respect to $B$ .

The conventional international origin ² was defined originally by the adopted astronomical latitudes of the five observatories contributing to the International Latitude Service (ILS) (Markowitz and Guinot1968).Itroughly coincides with the mean pole $F$ over the period from 1900 to 19O5.Later,the terrestrial pole was defined by alarger number of observatories operating optical equipment (e.g.,photographic zenith tubes)that reported to the International PolarMotion Service (IPMS) and the Bureau International de I'Heure (BIH).Both BIH and IPMS systems were compatible with the CIO.Satelite laser ranging(SLR) and VLBI(§ 6.2) became more accurate techniques for determining the position of the pole,and the BIH pole became strongly dependent on these data.In January 1988 the maintenance of the terrestrial reference frame was taken over by the International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS).The IERS maintains apole consistent within the observational errors with those of the conventional international origin and BIH.Current higher accuracy observations require a model of plate motions in the analysis of observations.This new reference frame is calledthe International TerrestrialReferenceFrame(ITRF).

# 6.2.3The Celestial Intermediate Reference Systemand theCelestial Intermediate Pole (CIP)

Prior to the 2Oo6 edition of The Astronomical Almanac the algorithms used to determine the precession and nutation of the Earth were based on the use of the Earth'srotation axis or Celestial Ephemeris Pole (CEP)rather than the CIP.The IAU(198O) defined the Celestial EphemerisPoleas having,“no nearly-diurnal nutation with respect toa space-fixed or Earthfixed coordinate system."Moritz and Mueller(1988) point out that this was often mistakenly assumed to be the axis offigure.However,the axis offigure does not conform to the definition of the CEP.Instead,the CEP is dynamically the same as the Tisserand mean polar axis.The Tisserand meanaxesare defined,as that set of axeswhere thedeformation fieldof the Earth integrated over the volume of the Earth contains no rotation (Moritz and Mueller 1988).

Beginningwith the 2oo6editionof The Astronomical Almanac thecelestial reference systemaligned with theCelestial IntermediatePole (CIP)iscalledtheCelestial Intermediate Reference System.Ideally,the CIP would be the projection of the line passing through the Earth's center of mass and the ITRF pole onto the sky.However,the free motion of the pole ofrotation within the body of the Earth is partially stochasticand,hence,cannot be predicted with certainty.

Beginningwith the 2Oo6 edition of The Astronomical Almanac the algorithmsused to determine the precession and nutation of the Earth use the CIP.The CIP is defined as the Tisserand mean axis of the Earth whose motion is comprised of those periodic components, observed inacelestial reference,that have periods greater than two days (IAU 2OOlc).For the CIP nutation is defined as the forced motions with periods greater than two days with respect to the CIP,and polar motion is defined as the free motion and the forced motions with periods lessthan or equal to two days.The forced motion is the solution of the Earth'sequations of rotational motion accounting for all external torques (specifically the gravitational forces due to the Sun,Moon,and planets).The free motionis that motion which would exist if there were no external torques on the rotating Earth (the particular solution of the differential equations).If the Earth were rigid,the free motion wobble would have a period of about 304 days.However,forthephysical, non-rigidEarth,thismotion,called theChandler wobble, has a period of about 14 months.Polar motion also includes the motions arising from changes in the distribution of the Earth's mass from the climate along with motions resulting from geophysical strains and meteorological effects.

If the two principal axes of inertia that lie in the Earth's equatorial plane were identical, then the shortest periodic motion arising from the torques of the Moon,Sun,and planets would have a period of about a month.And,the CIP would be identical to the CEP.However, these two principal axes of inertia differby about $0 . 0 0 2 \%$ (Moritz and Mueller1988).The difference in the torques about these two principal axes of inertia gives rise to small motions of the Tisserand mean axes with periods less than or approximately equal to a day.These motions have periods that are exactly equal to those of many of the tidal deformations in the Earth,and cannot be separated by observation.Thus,the requirement of dropping those terms with periods of less than two dayswas imposed on the CIP.One result of this definition is that the TRS is no longer fixed by the loosely defined Greenwich meridian but is moving eastward at about $0 . 0 1 6 \mathrm { m m } \mathrm { y r } ^ { - 1 }$ ,on average (Lambert and Bizouard 2002).

Inaddition to the free nutation observed in all spinning bodies,there is a second free motion for the Earth.This second free motion arises from the difference in the alignment of the Earth's core and mantle,and iscalled the free core nutation.It is also known as the diurnal free polar motion or nearly diurnal free wobble.It produces a periodic motion of approximately 432daysof the CIP in theGCRS.

# 6.2.3.1 The Ecliptic

The ecliptic is generally defined as the mean plane of the Earth'sorbit around the Sun. The actual plane of the Earth's orbit is constantly changing in response to the perturbations from the otherplanets in the solar system,making theactual determination of the eclipticfar from easy. One problem in realizing the ecliptic is a practical definition ofa mean plane.To reduce the uncertainties in the definition of the ecliptic the IAU Working Group on Precession and the Ecliptic (Hilton et al.2OO6) have recommended that the ecliptic be more precisely defined as the plane perpendicular to the mean orbital angular momentum vector of the Earth-Moon barycenter passing through the Sun in the BCRS.

The“inertial”and the“rotating”ecliptics.Prior to the advent of Lunar Laser Ranging (LLR)observations,it was impossible to determine whether or not the space motions of solarsystem objects contained a residual global rate of rotation.Newcomb (1898) defined the“rotating”ecliptic as the plane perpendicular to the mean motion of the Earth-Moon barycenter from the point of view of an observer co-moving with the ecliptic.For such a comoving observer,there isacomponent to the orientation of the ecliptic that arises from the motion of the ecliptic itself.Onthe other hand,Le Verrier(1855) defined the ecliptic from the point of view of an observer in inertial space.Thus,older publications used two different definitions of the ecliptic.Standish (1981) showed that the equinox based on this “rotating” definition of the mean ecliptic is 93.66 milliarcseconds (mas)to the east of the equinox based onan inertially defined ecliptic,and theobliquityof theEarth is 3.34mas greaterusing the rotating definition ofthe ecliptic than ifaninertial eclipticisused.Thus,the ecliptic definition used can cause significant errors for applications that require accuracy better than about $0 ^ { \prime \prime } 1$ The BCRS isoriented with respect to the inertial ecliptic while many,but not all,older reference systems,such as theFK5and the reference systemusedbyDE2OO (Folkner et al. 1994),are oriented with respect to the rotating ecliptic.Thus,care must be used when using older data to determine if they are referring to the inertial or rotating definition of the ecliptic.

# 6.2.4 The Equinoxes

The classic concept ofthe originof right ascension,the longitudinal coordinate of the celestial reference system,is the dynamical vernal equinox:The point onthecelestial sphere where the celestial equator intersects the ecliptic and through which the center of the mean Sun passes asitcrosses the equator going north.More specifically,it is the directionof the intersectionof the projection of the Earth's equator onto the sky with the mean orbit plane of the Earth-Moon barycenter near which the Sun passes on its way north of the Earth's equator. Inaddition to the vernal equinox,there isalso the autumnal equinox,which is the direction of the intersection between the Earth's equator and the ecliptic near where the center of the mean Sun passes going south. Generally,the use of the word “equinox”implies the vernal equinox

Likethe ecliptic,the Earth's equatoris a dynamicallydefined plane.Thus,neither plane is fixed in space,and the equinox is constantly changing.The change of the equinox with time requires thata date beaffixed to itinorder to specifya fixed direction.The reference epoch for the equinox in common use curently is J2ooo.O (Julian date $2 4 5 1 5 4 5 . 0 \mathrm { T T }$ ),see $\ S 6 . 7$

It is possible to refer to either the mean equator or the true equator.The mean equator is amoving equator prescribed only by secular (non-periodic)motion;that is,motion due to precession.The true equator,on the other hand,includes both secular and periodic motions; that is,motions due to both precession and nutation.Beginning with the 2Oo6 edition of The AstronomicalAlmanac,the equator was defined as the plane perpendicular to the CIP(§ 6.2.3) that passes through the Earth's center of mass.Prior to the 2OO6 edition the equator was defined as the plane perpendicular to the CEP( $\ S 6 . 2 . 3 )$ that passes through the Earth'scenter of mass.The existence of aseparate mean equator and true equator implies the existence of a mean equinox and a true equinox corresponding to the directions where the mean equator intersects the ecliptic or where the true equator intersects the ecliptic.The CIO( $\ S 6 . 2 . 2 )$ used inthe Celestial Intermediate Reference System is independent of the dynamical equinox.

# 6.3 The Equation ofthe Equinoxes, $E _ { e } ( T )$

The equation of the equinoxes

$$
E _ { e } ( T ) =  { \mathcal { T } } _ { T } -  { \mathcal { T } } _ { M } = \mathrm { G A S T } - \mathrm { G M S T }
$$

where ${ } ^ { \prime } \Gamma _ { T }$ is the true equinox of date and $\Upsilon _ { M }$ is the mean equinox of date,is thediference between the positions of the true and mean equinoxes of date.It is also equivalent to the difference between apparent and mean sidereal time,

$$
\alpha _ { e T } = \alpha _ { e M } - E _ { e } ( T ) .
$$

The equation of the equinoxes may be expressed in series form as

$$
E _ { e } ( T ) = \Delta \psi \cos { \varepsilon _ { A } } + \sum _ { k } ( C _ { k } ^ { \prime } \sin { A _ { k } } + S _ { k } ^ { \prime } \cos { A _ { k } } ) + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 8 7 T \sin { \Omega }
$$

where $\Delta \psi$ is the total nutation in longitude, $\varepsilon _ { A }$ is the mean obliquity of the ecliptic, $\Omega$ is the mean longitude of the ascending node ofthe Moon,and the coeficients $C _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ and $S _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ and the angular arguments $A _ { k }$ are used to describe the complementary terms arising from coupling between precession and nutation (Capitaine and Wallace 2Oo6).Table 6.1 contains the values of the coefficientsand angular arguments for all the complementary terms exceeding $0 . 5 \mu$ as (microarcseconds)between 1975and 2025.These values are also given in Section B of The Astronomical Almanac.The resulting value for $E _ { e } ( T )$ is accurate to $\pm 3 0 \mu \mathrm { a s }$ .

Table 6.1 Coeficients of the series for the equation of the equinoxes, $E _ { e } ,$ largerthan $0 . 5 ~ \mu$ asbetween 1975 and2025   

<table><tr><td>Aa</td><td>Ck (arcsec.)</td><td>sb</td></tr><tr><td>Ω</td><td>-0.002 640 96</td><td>一</td></tr><tr><td>22</td><td>-0.00006352</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2F-2D+3Ω</td><td>-0.000 01175</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2F-2D+Ω</td><td>-0.000 01121</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2F-2D+2S2</td><td>+0.000 00455</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2F+3</td><td>-0.00000202</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2F+Ω</td><td>-0.00000198</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>32</td><td>+0.000 00172</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>1&#x27;+Ω</td><td>+0.000 00141</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>1&#x27;-Ω</td><td>+0.000 00126</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>1+</td><td>+0.000000 63</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>1-Ω</td><td>+0.000 000 63</td><td></td></tr></table>

“ Thedefinitions of the critical arguments and the formulae to compute their values are given in $\ S 6 . 7$ . bAll of the amplitudes for $S _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ are less than $0 . 5 \ \mu \mathrm { a } ;$ s.Thus, there are no entries for this column.

# 6.4 The Equation of the Origins, $E _ { o } ( T )$

Beginning on1 January 2Oo3,the IAU recommends that the azimuthal coordinate of the celestial reference system be measured from the CIO $( \ S ~ 6 . 7 )$ rather than the equinox. One consequence of using the CIO in place of the equinox is that there is no need to compute the equation of the equinoxes.Instead,realizing the position of the true equinox requires theequation of the origins, $E _ { o } ( T )$ .The equation of the origins is the difference between the position of the CIO and true equinox of date.This difference is identical to the difference between the ERA( $\ S$ 6.8.4) and GAST( $\ S$ 6.8.5.2). Thus,

$$
E _ { o } ( T ) = \mathbf { C } \mathbf { I  O } - \boldsymbol { \mathcal { T } } _ { T } = \theta - \mathbf { G } \mathbf { A } \mathbf { S } \mathbf { T } .
$$

Figure 6.1 illustrates the relation between the TIO( $\textrm { \AA } 6 . 9 )$ ,the CIO,and the true equinox of date.

Any celestial object can be assigned an equinox right ascension, $\alpha _ { e }$ ,with respect to the equinox.Italso can be assigned an intermediate right ascension, $\alpha _ { i }$ ,with respect to the CIO (6.7).The equinox right ascension is related to the intermediate right ascension by

$$
\alpha _ { e } = \alpha _ { i } - E _ { o } ( T ) .
$$

Since $E _ { o }$ is the difference between the position of the CIO and the true equinox of date, its value is a function of the position of date of both of these points.Thus,

$$
E _ { o } ( T ) = s - \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \mathbf { N P B } _ { 1 } ^ { \mathrm { T } } \cdot \mathcal { R } _ { 1 } } { \mathbf { N P B } _ { 2 } ^ { \mathrm { T } } \cdot \mathcal { R } _ { 1 } }
$$

where $s$ is the CIO locator (§ 6.7), $\mathbf { N P B } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathbf { N P B } _ { 2 }$ are row matrices consisting of the first and second rows,respectively,of the matrix product of the frame bias, $\mathbf { B }$ ,(6.5);precession, $\mathbf { P }$ ,(6.6.2)；and nutation,N,(86.6.3)matriceswith respect to the equinox,and $\mathcal { R } _ { 1 }$ isa row matrix consisting of the first row of Eq. 6.51,the $\mathcal { X }$ $_ { \mathcal { V } }$ matrix for the rotation from the intermediate reference system to the GCRS.

Alternatively,the equation of the equinoxes may be given to an accuracy of $2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 }$ s of time by

$$
E _ { e } ( T ) = \frac { 1 } { 1 5 } ( \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon _ { A } + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 2 6 4 \sin \Omega + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 0 6 \sin 2 \Omega )
$$

provided $\Delta \psi$ and $\varepsilon _ { A }$ are given with sufficient accuracy.

# 6.5 The J2000.0 to ICRS Frame Bias Matrix

TheBCRS(§ 6.2) was designed to be aligned asclosely as possible to that of the mean equator and equinox of J2ooo.0 (IAU 1992).However,since the time of its adoption,a small, but significant ofset between the mean coordinate system of J2Ooo.O and the BCRS has been determined to exist.Thus,when the BCRS is used as the celestial reference system,Eq. 6.1 becomes:

$$
\mathbf { x } _ { C R S } = \mathbf { B P N T W } \mathbf { x } _ { T R S }
$$

where $\mathbf { B }$ is the frame bias matrix from the mean equator and equinox of J2ooo.O to the BCRS. The International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (McCarthy and Petit 2004) conventions have adopted a frame bias matrix in which the position of the pole of the BCRS with respect to the mean CIP of J2ooo.0 was determined from VLBI(§ 6.2) observations and the position of the origin of right ascension in the BCRS was determined from Lunar Laser Ranging (LLR)observations with respect to the inertial definition of the ecliptic.The value of $\mathbf { B }$ is:

$$
{ \bf B } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 . 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 4 2 5 } & { - 7 . 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } & { 8 . 0 5 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } \\ { 7 . 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } & { 0 . 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 5 } & { 3 . 3 0 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } \\ { - 8 . 0 5 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } & { 3 . 3 0 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } & { 0 . 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 9 6 2 0 8 } \end{array} \right] .
$$

This is therotation from the BCRS to the mean CIPof J20o0.O used in The Astronomical Almanac.However,if thecelestial pole offset positions were determined fromLLR(Chapront, Chapront-Touzé,and Francou 2Oo2)or the rotating definition of the ecliptic were used to determine the equinox,asignificantly different rotation matrix would be obtained.Fordetails see Hilton and Hohenkerk (2003).

# 6.6 Precession and Nutation

The first two matrices on the right hand side of Eq. 6.1, $\mathbf { P }$ and $\mathbf { N }$ ，are the precession and nutation matrices.Acoordinate system in which precession,but not nutation,has been applied to the CIP(§ 6.2.3) isdescribed as being the mean coordinate system ofdate.Ifboth precession andnutation havebeenincluded,thecoordinatesystemisknownas the true coordinatesystem of date.The difference between the mean and true coordinate systems depends on both the position of the equinox and the obliquity of the ecliptic.Thus,the mean coordinate system is oftenreferred to as the mean equinox and ecliptic ofdate,while the true coordinate system isreferredtoasthetrueequinoxandeclipticofdate.

Classically,the origin of right ascension has been the intersection of the ascending node of the ecliptic on the Earth's equator.The orientations of both of these planes are functions of time.Thus,general precession includes the motion of the ecliptic as well as the mean motion of Earth’s equator.The motion of the poles of the ecliptic,the mean equator of date and the CIP are shown in Fig. 6.2.

The distinction between the mean system of date and the true system of date only applies to those coordinate systems where the origin of right ascension is the equinox.When the CIO $( \ S 6 . 7 )$ isused as the origin of right ascension,the coordinate system of date always includes both precession and nutation.

# 6.6.1The IAU 2000 Precession-Nutation Theory and IAU 2006/2000A

When it was adopted (IAU 2Oo1b) theIAU 20oO precession-nutation theory was intended to replace both the previous precession (Lieske et al.1977) and nutation (Seidelmann 1982). In addition to thefullhigh-accuracy precession-nutationtheory,IAU 2oooA (Mathews,Herring, and Buffett 20o2),a second lower accuracy theory,IAU 2OooB (McCarthy andLuzum 2003) was formulated for lower accuracy, $\geq 1$ mas,applications. Software implementing both of thesevariants of the original IAU 2OoO precession-nutationtheory inFortranare available from the International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS) at URL[1].

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/bc46b179b522f54edd5bb73163ee92d922fae7c72e8b832ae1b08224d216d25a.jpg)  
Figure 6.2 The motions of the ecliptic pole,the Earth's mean pole and the CIPon the plane of the sky.(a)Themotion of the ecliptic over6,OOO years.The dot denotes the positionof the ecliptic at J2000.0.(b)The motions of the ecliptic and the Earth's mean pole over 6,OoO years.The dot denotes the positionof the mean pole at J2Ooo.O.(c) The motions if the mean pole and the CIP over 20 years.The dots denote the positions of the two polesat J2ooo.O.The scale in the $x$ -directionhasbeen stretchedbya factorof ten.(d) Themotion of theCIP with respect to the mean pole of date over 20 years.In(a-c),the geometric origin is the position of the ecliptic ofJ2ooo.O and the $x$ -axisisthe arc connecting the ecliptic of J2Oo0.O to the mean pole of J20oo.0.In(d),the geometric origin is the mean pole of date and the $x$ -axisis the arc connecting the ecliptic of date to the mean pole of date.

However,the change in precession portion of the IAU 2OoO precession-nutation theory in Mathews,Herring,and Buffett (20O2)only modified theLieske et al.(1977) precession bytheadditionofa linear changein the rateand ignored thehigher-order terms that,at that time could not be observed, but only computed from theory.Thus,the IAU Working Group onPrecession and the Ecliptic (Hilton et al.2Oo6) recommended the use of the Capitaine, Wallace,and Chapront (2Oo3) precession theory to modify the precession portion of the IAU precession-nutation theory.This was officially adopted by the IAU in 2O06 for use beginning on1January 2009 (IAU 20o8,Res.B1).In this book,the combined models are referred to as IAU 2006/2000A precession-nutation.

To be strictly correct, the adoption of the $P 0 3$ (Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront 2003) precession theory required small changes in the amplitudes of the nutation series arising from the difference in the position of the CIP( $\ S \left. 6 . 2 . 3 \right)$ .The difference isonly of significance for the most precise calculations. The differences between the nutation angles, $( \Delta \psi , \Delta \varepsilon )$ ,using the $P 0 3$ precession and the angles, $\Delta \psi _ { 2 0 0 0 A }$ ， $\Delta \varepsilon _ { 2 0 0 0 A }$ ,in the IAU 20o0A precession theory are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta \psi - \Delta \psi _ { 2 0 0 0 A } = ( 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 4 6 9 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } - 2 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 7 7 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ) \Delta \psi _ { 2 0 0 0 A } } \\ & { \quad \Delta \varepsilon - \Delta \varepsilon _ { 2 0 0 0 A } = - 2 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 7 7 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T \Delta \varepsilon _ { 2 0 0 0 A } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus,the maximum adjustment in the amplitudes of the nutation after a century is only $<$ $5 0 \mu$ as (microarcseconds)in longitude and $< 3 0 \mu$ as in obliquity.Whena distinctionbetween the adjusted and non-adjusted models is important,this book uses the term $I A U 2 0 0 6 / 2 0 0 0 A _ { R }$ for the former and IAU 20o6/2Oo0A for the latter;when unimportant,the term IAU 2006/ 2000A is used.

Software implementing IAU 2006/20o0A precession-nutation theory in Fortran and C may be found in the Naval Observatory Vector Astrometry Subroutines (NOVAS)at URL[2] and in the Standards of Fundamental Astronomy (SOFA) software URL[3].

The NOVAS software is used by the U.S.Nautical Almanac Ofice in the preparation of its sections of The Astronomical Almanac.NOVAS does not implement the correction to the nutation in Eqs.6.14.In other words,itimplements IAU 2006/20o0A.The decision was made not to implement the correction because it was not mentioned in Hilton et al.(2006), and thus judged notto bepart of theIAU standard.

Meanwhile,the SOFAsoftware is used byHerMajesty's Nautical Almanac Office in the preparation of its sections of The Astronomical Almanac.SOFA,however,does implement the correction to the nutation in Eqs.6.14.In other words,it implements IAU $2 0 0 6 / 2 0 0 0 \mathrm { A _ { R } }$ The reason for implementing the correction is that it is technically more correct.

Ineither case,the difference in the output arising fromthe difference between the NOVAS and the SOFA implementations are too small to be significant in the values tabulated in The Astronomical Almanac.The difference does not become significant unless the required accuracyis $\sim 6 0$ μas or better.

# 6.6.2 Precession

Both the ecliptic and the plane of the equator and their intersection,the equinox,are in motion. The component of the precession due to the motion of the ecliptic (§ 6.2.3.1) is known as precession ofthe ecliptic’.If the equator were fixed,the motion of the ecliptic alone would produce a precession of the equinox of about $1 2 ^ { \prime \prime }$ per century anda decrease in the obliquity of the ecliptic of about $4 7 ^ { \prime \prime }$ per century.

The motion of the equator arises from the torque of the Sun,Moon,and planets on the dynamical figureof the Earth.Itisseparated into twoparts,theprecessionoftheequator,the smooth, secular motion of the mean CIP ( $\left( \ S 6 . 2 \right)$ of the equator about the pole of the ecliptic, with a period of about 26,ooo years,and nutation,the sum of the periodic motions,observed ina celestial reference frame,with periods greater than two daysof the true CIP around the mean CIP with anamplitude of about $9 ^ { \prime \prime }$ .The combination of precession of the equator and precession of the ecliptic is referred to as general precession.

The notation used here follows Lieske et al.(1977).Lieske etal.also give a useful table containing cross-references to the notation used by previous authors.The motion of the eclipticdescribedby theangles $\pi _ { A }$ and $\Pi _ { A }$ ,where $\pi _ { A }$ is theangle between the ecliptic at a fixed epoch and the ecliptic of date,and $\Pi _ { A }$ is the angular distance from the mean equinox, $\mathrm { ~  ~ \omega ~ } ^ { \mathrm { ~ T ~ } } =$ ,thefixedepochalongthefixedelipticofepochtotheascendingnodeoftheliptic of date (Fig.6.3).The expressions for $\pi _ { A }$ and $\Pi _ { A }$ are given as a pair of polynomials

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P _ { A } = \sin \pi _ { A } \sin \Pi _ { A } = s _ { 1 } t + s _ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } + s _ { 3 } t ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { Q _ { A } = \sin \pi _ { A } \cos \Pi _ { A } = c _ { 1 } t + c _ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } t ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

Beginningwith the 2Oo9 edition of TheAstronomical Almanac,theCapitaine,Wallace, and Chapront (2oo3) expressions for $\pi _ { A }$ and $\Pi _ { A }$ derived from DE406 (Standish 1998) are used.Prior to this edition,they were obtained from Newcomb's theory of the Earth's orbital motion about the Sun with modifications to allow for improved determinations of the planetary masses.For continuity, $\pi _ { A } > 0$ for $t > 5 2 0 0 0 . 0$ and $\pi _ { A } < 0$ for $t < \mathrm { J } 2 0 0 0 . 0$ Numerical values for the current coeffcients $s _ { n }$ and $c _ { n }$ are given in Table 6.2.The precessional effect due to the motion of the ecliptic is described by the angle $c \mathcal { P } \mathcal { C } _ { 0 }$ inFig. 6.4,referred to as the accumulated precession of the ecliptic, $\chi _ { A }$ ·

Thebasic parameter for describing precession of the equator is $\psi _ { A }$ ,measured along the ecliptic due to the motion of the equator at epoch $t _ { 0 }$ .This is related to Newcomb's precessional constant,which has the value $P _ { 0 }$ at epoch $t _ { 0 }$

$$
P _ { 0 } = \left( \frac { d \psi _ { A } } { d t } - P _ { g } \right) \cos ^ { - 1 } \varepsilon _ { 0 }
$$

where $P _ { g }$ is called the geodesic precession,which is a relativistic non-periodic Coriolis effect amounting to $1 / 9 1 9 6$ per Julian century (Fukushima 2Oo3).The value of $\psi _ { A }$ and the geophysical parameters it depends on must be derived from observations.

The combined effect of precession of the ecliptic and precession of the equator is called the general precession.Lieske et al.(1977)adopted the definition of the accumulated general precession $p _ { A }$ ,shown inFig.6.5,definedbyNewcomb as

$$
p _ { A } = \Upsilon { \bf R } - \Upsilon _ { 0 } { \bf R } .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/fbf1f0f2c843c2fe5b87afaf1dc58f469467c35b1973f8535a62339635355b5b.jpg)  
Figure 6.3 The general precession connects the mean equinox of epoch, $\Upsilon _ { 0 }$ to the mean equinox of date, $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon } _ { M }$

Table 6.2 Coefficients describing the position of the ecliptic of date with respect to the ecliptic of J2000.0   

<table><tr><td>Quantity</td><td>(&quot;cy-1)</td><td>(&quot;cy-2)</td><td>(&quot;cy-3)</td><td>(&quot;cy-4）</td><td>(&quot;cy-5)</td></tr><tr><td>sinπA sin IIA</td><td>4199 094</td><td>01939873</td><td>-0.000 224 66</td><td>-9&quot;12×10-7</td><td>1&quot;20×10-8</td></tr><tr><td>sinπA cos IA</td><td>-46!811015</td><td>0&#x27;0510283</td><td>0&#x27;000 52413</td><td>-6.46×10-7</td><td>-172 ×10-8</td></tr><tr><td>(Prior to 2006)</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>sin πA sin IA</td><td>4&quot;1976</td><td>0.194 47</td><td>-0.000 179</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>sinπA Cos IIA</td><td>-46&quot;8150</td><td>0.05059</td><td>0.000 344</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

Newcomb (1895),however,adopted an expression for $p _ { A }$ that computed the angle $\Upsilon _ { 0 } Q$ in Fig.6.5,where Qis the projection of the equinox of date on the ecliptic at the fixed epoch $t _ { 0 }$ .Therates diverge by about $0 . 5 \mathrm { \ m a s \ c y } ^ { - 2 }$ .At epoch $t _ { 0 }$ ,however,both procedures reduce to the same rate, $p$ ,

$$
p ( t _ { 0 } ) = p _ { A } ( t _ { 0 } ) = \frac { d \psi _ { A } } { d t } - \frac { d \chi _ { A } } { d t } \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } = P _ { 0 } \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } - P _ { g } - \frac { s _ { 1 } } { \tan \varepsilon _ { 0 } } .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/9ea1d678170b31ffa2d9da60700c1d4a590d24311ffb0222054d48bcf483af60.jpg)  
Figure 6.4 The precession angles connect the mean pole of epoch, $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ ,to the mean pole of date, $\mathcal { P }$ ,ecliptic pole of epoch, $\mathcal { C } _ { 0 } ^ { } ,$ ,and the ecliptic pole of date, $\mathcal { C }$

The general precession, $p _ { A }$ ,may beresolved into general precession inright ascension, $m$ ， and general precession in declination, $n$ ,where

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { p _ { A } = m \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } + n \sin \varepsilon _ { 0 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { m = \displaystyle \frac { d \psi _ { A } } { d t } \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } - \frac { d \chi _ { A } } { d t } = \left( P _ { 0 } \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } - P _ { g } \right) \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } - \frac { s _ { 1 } } { \sin \varepsilon _ { 0 } } , \quad \mathrm { a n d } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { n = \displaystyle \frac { d \psi _ { A } } { d t } \sin \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \left( P _ { 0 } \cos \varepsilon _ { 0 } - P _ { g } \right) \sin \varepsilon _ { 0 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Prior to the adoption of the IAU 2ooO resolutions,the constant of precession was derived fromthe analysis of the proper motions of stars.In the solar neighborhood the starsare themselves revolvingabout the centerof the galaxy.Fricke (1977) thoroughly reviewed this method,and concluded that Newcomb's precession of the equator should be corrected by $+ 1 { ^ { \prime \prime } } 1 0$ per Julian century with an uncertainty of $\pm 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 1 5$ per Julian century.More recently, Mathews,Herring,and Buffett(2OO2) determined,based onVLBI $( \ S \ G . 2 )$ observations of distant objects such as galaxies and QSOs,that Fricke overcorrected the precession of the equator by $0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 2 9 9 6 5$ per Julian century.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/3699cc9788d8625f7a22c41164186ed3a8c7fcdff0b8c4b8eeb91f3686d2693a.jpg)  
Figure 6.5 TheLieske etal.(1977) general precession in longitude is the difference between the arcs $\hat { \Upsilon } \mathcal { R }$ and $\hat { \Upsilon _ { 0 } \mathcal { R } }$ while the Newcomb (1895) general precession in longitude is the difference between the arcs ${ \mathcal { Q } } { \mathcal { R } }$ and $\hat { \Upsilon _ { 0 } \mathcal { R } }$

There are several ways of parameterizing the precession.For example,the vector directions of the polar axisof the Earth and the polar axis of the ecliptic may be expressed in termsof polynomials involving timearguments (Fabri198o;Murray1983),which are then used to calculate the effects of precession.Williams (1994) presents four different methods in terms of various angles with respect to the ecliptic and rotational poles at a fiducial epoch. Fukushima (2Oo3) presents a scheme for determining the positions of the ecliptic and mean rotational poles of date with respect to the pole of the ICRS rather than the mean pole of epoch.Three of these parameterizations willbe discussed here.

# 6.6.2.1The Lieske etal.Parameterization

The Lieske etal.(1977) method of parameterization has been the standard used since the time of Newcomb.The IAU adopted it for rigorous calculations in1976.However,Williams (2002,private communication),Fukushima (2Oo3）and Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront (2003) have demonstrated that values of the angles $z _ { A }$ and $\zeta _ { A }$ near the fiducial epoch are highlysensitive,ina complementary way,to the values adopted for the offset between the mean equator and equinox of epoch and the GCRS $( \ S 6 . 2 )$ and,hence,cannot satisfactorily berepresented by polynomials in $t$ .Thus,if accuracy better than about 25 mas overall is required,either the frame bias has to be introduced asaseparate rotation,as inEq.6.12,or adifferent parameterization that does not suffer from this difficulty should be adopted.

The accumulated precession angles $\zeta _ { A }$ ， $z _ { A }$ ,and $\theta _ { A }$ are referred to a base epoch $t _ { 0 }$ .These anglesare shown with respect to the equator and ecliptic in Fig.6.3.Adetailed view of these angleswith respect to the poles of the equator and eclipticare shown inFig.6.4and with respect to the mean equator and equinox of epoch and the mean equator and equinox of date in Fig.6.4. The mean pole of the equator at $t _ { 0 }$ is the point $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ and the pole of the ecliptic ispoint $\mathcal { C } _ { 0 }$ At time $t$ , $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ hasmoved to $\mathcal { P }$ and $\mathcal { C } _ { 0 }$ to $c$ .Assumearight-handed setof threedimensional Cartesian coordinate axes with its geometric originat the center of the sphere, $x$ -axispointing to $\Upsilon _ { 0 }$ ,where the equator and ecliptic meet (the equinox) at epoch $t _ { 0 }$ .The $y$ -axisis $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ awayin an easterly direction along the equator,and the $z$ -axis points toward thepole $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ .Initially,precessionwill move $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ inadirection that isabout $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ to the east of the direction toward $\Upsilon _ { 0 }$ .Over time,the movement of the pole of the ecliptic will cause the pole of the equator to move in a somewhat different direction.The polynomial expressions forthese anglesare givenin Table 6.3.The coefficientsare fromthe Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront (2003) $P 0 3$ precession and are recommended by the IAU (Hilton et al.2Oo6).The coeficientsused prior to the 2Oo6 edition of The Astronomical Almanac are in Table 6.4. These older coefficientswere derived inLieske etal.(1977).

Table 6.3 IAU recommended coeficients (Hilton et al.20o6) describing the position of the ecliptic of date with respect to the ecliptic of J2000.0

Table 6.4 Lieske et al. (1977) coefficients describing the position of the ecliptic of date with respect to the ecliptic of J2Oo0.0.These values are obsolete.They are included for working with data priorto 2006.See Table 6.3 for the current recommended coefficients.   

<table><tr><td>Quantity</td><td>(&quot;cy-1)</td><td>(&quot;cy-2)</td><td>(&quot;cy-3)</td></tr><tr><td>5A</td><td>2306.2181</td><td>0.30188</td><td>0.017 998</td></tr><tr><td>ZA</td><td>2306.2181</td><td>1.094 68</td><td>0.018203</td></tr><tr><td>A</td><td>2004.3109</td><td>-0.426 65</td><td>-0.041833</td></tr></table>

Implementation of the Lieske et al. parameterization begins with a rotation of $- \zeta _ { A }$ about the $z$ -axis.The great circle from $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ to the $x ^ { \prime }$ -axis passes through the mean pole of date, $\mathcal { P }$ ,as well(see Fig.6.5).Thus,this great circle is perpendicular to both the mean equator of epoch and the mean equator of date (see Fig.6.5).Next,a rotation of $+ \theta _ { A }$ about the $y ^ { \prime }$ -axismoves from the mean pole of epoch, $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ to the mean pole of date, $\mathcal { P }$ (Fig.6.5).Equivalently, this rotation maps the mean equator of epoch onto the mean equator of date (Fig. 6.6).The $z ^ { \prime \prime }$ - axis now points to the mean pole of date, $_ { \mathcal { P } }$ and the $x ^ { \prime \prime }$ -axispointsto $_ { \mathcal { R } }$ inFig.6.6.Finally, there is a rotation of $- z _ { A }$ about the $z ^ { \prime \prime }$ -axis so that the $x ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ -axis points toward $\mathrm { ~  ~ r ~ } _ { M }$ ,the mean equinox of date.Thus,the precession matrix from the mean equator and equinox of epoch, $t _ { 0 }$ ,to the mean equator and equinox date, $t$ ,ismade up of these rotations

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/9dc17302fb6d8bd1fe8a35c3f06197564384faf810c7fde6975a0624213ba9dc.jpg)  
Figure 6.6 The Lieske et al.(1977) precession angles relate the mean equator and equinox of epoch, $\Upsilon _ { 0 }$ totheeanequatorandequofate $\Upsilon _ { M }$

$$
\mathbf { P } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - z _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( + \theta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - \zeta _ { A } ) .
$$

This equation expands to

$$
\mathbf { P } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos z _ { A } \cos \theta _ { A } \cos \zeta _ { A } - \sin z _ { A } \sin \zeta _ { A } } & { - \cos z _ { A } \cos \theta _ { A } \sin \zeta _ { A } - \sin z _ { A } \cos \zeta _ { A } } & { - \cos z _ { A } \sin \theta _ { A } } \\ { \sin z _ { A } \cos \theta _ { A } \cos \zeta _ { A } + \cos z _ { A } \sin \zeta _ { A } } & { - \sin z _ { A } \cos \theta _ { A } \sin \zeta _ { A } + \cos z _ { A } \cos \zeta _ { A } } & { - \sin z _ { A } \sin \theta _ { A } } \\ { \sin \theta _ { A } \cos \zeta _ { A } } & { - \sin \theta _ { A } \sin \zeta _ { A } } & { \cos \theta _ { A } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Having calculated the precession angles for the matrix $\mathbf { P }$ ,the inverse matrix $\mathbf { P } ^ { - 1 }$ can be calculated in variousways.For example,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { P } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ^ { - 1 } ( - \zeta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } ( + \theta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ^ { - 1 } ( - z _ { A } ) } \\ & { \quad = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ^ { T } ( - \zeta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ^ { T } ( + \theta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ^ { T } ( - z _ { A } ) } \\ & { \quad = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( + \zeta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( - \theta _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( + z _ { A } ) } \end{array}
$$

where use has been made of the property that the inverse of a rotation matrix is its transpose (i.e.,it is orthogonal).

Modern precessonanglesand ratesadopted.Beginning with the 2Oo9 edition of The Astronomical Almanac the precession anglesare those of the Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront (2003) $P 0 3$ solution.The values for the rate of precession, $\mathrm { ~ p ~ } ^ { 4 }$ ,obliquity at epoch, $\varepsilon _ { 0 }$ ,and rate of change in obliquity, $\Delta \varepsilon ^ { 5 }$ at epoch J2000.0 are:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm { p = 5 0 2 8 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 9 6 1 9 5 ~ J u l i a n ~ c y ^ { - 1 } } } } \\ { { \varepsilon _ { 0 } = 2 3 ^ { \circ } 2 6 ^ { \prime } 2 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 0 6 0 0 } } \\ { { \Delta \varepsilon = - 0 { \mathrm { : } } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 2 5 7 5 4 \mathrm { ~ J u l i a n ~ c y ^ { - 1 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

The geodesic precession, $\mathrm { P } _ { g }$ ,contribution to the precession is (Fukushima 2003)

$$
\mathrm { P } _ { g } = 1 . { } ^ { \prime \prime } 9 1 9 6 \pm 0 . { } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 3 \mathrm { J u l i a n c y ^ { - 1 } } .
$$

The expressions at J2Ooo.O for the rates per Julian century of general precession,in longitude, $P$ ；right ascension, $m$ ；and declination, $n$ ；and $\pi$ ,the rate of rotation of the ecliptic,are

$$
\begin{array} { c }  { { \begin{array} { c } { { a : } } \\ { { - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 1 3 8 2 6 8 T ^ { 3 } - 3 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 3 8 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } } } \end{array} } } \\  { { \begin{array} { c } { { { } } } \\ { { n = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } ( \theta _ { A } ) | _ { t = 0 } = 2 0 0 4 ^ { \prime \prime } 1 9 1 9 0 3 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 8 5 8 9 8 6 8 T - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 1 2 5 4 6 7 9 2 T ^ { 2 } } } \end{array} } } \end{array}
$$

$$
- 0 . 7 7 0 0 0 0 2 8 3 5 6 T ^ { 3 } - 6 . 3 7 \times 1 0 - 7 T ^ { 4 }
$$

$$
\pi = { \frac { d } { d t } } ( \pi _ { A } ) | _ { t = 0 } = 4 6 . 7 9 9 8 9 7 3 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 6 6 9 8 5 2 T - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 3 7 6 7 7 T ^ { 2 }
$$

$$
+ 4 . ^ { \prime \prime } 5 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } T ^ { 3 } - 1 . ^ { \prime \prime } 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 4 } .
$$

The2006 through 2oo8 editions of The Astronomical Almanac used the precession portion of the IAU 2OooA precession-nutation theory.However,the diference between the IAU 200oA precession and the $P 0 3$ precession during that interval is insignificant either at theprecision tabulated in The Astronomical Almanac orat the level ofaccuracy to which observations can currently be made.The IAU 2OooA precession only corrects the linear portion of the Lieske etal.(1977)precession theory.Thus,the higher ordercoefficientsare inconsistent with dynamical theory.Currently,the effect of the change to the higher order coeficients is too smallto be observed,and hence,has to be derived froma dynamical theory aswas done for $P 0 3$ .

Precession angles and rates adopted by the IAU(1976)． Prior to the 2O06 edition of The Astronomical Almanac the basis for precesson was Lieske etal. (1977)as modifiedbyLieske (1979).The following values were adopted constants at epoch J2000.0

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm { { \Delta p = 5 0 2 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 9 6 6 J u l i a n ~ c y ^ { - 1 } } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { { \Delta P _ { 1 } = - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 3 6 9 ~ J u l i a n ~ c y ^ { - 1 } } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { { \Delta P _ { g } = 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 9 2 ~ J u l i a n ~ c y ^ { - 1 } } } } } \\ { { \varepsilon _ { 0 } = 2 3 ^ { \circ } 2 6 ^ { \prime } 2 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 4 8 . } } \end{array}
$$

The expressions at epoch $t _ { 0 }$ for the rates per Julian century of general precession,inlongitude, $p$ ;right ascension, $m$ ; and declination, $n$ ,and $\pi$ the rate of rotation of the ecliptic,are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { p = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } ( p _ { A } ) \vert _ { t = 0 } = 5 0 2 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 9 6 6 + 2 ^ { \prime \prime } 2 2 2 2 6 T - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 0 4 2 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { m = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } ( \zeta _ { A } + z _ { A } ) \vert _ { t = 0 } = 4 6 1 2 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 3 6 2 + 2 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 9 3 1 2 T - 0 ^ { \prime } 0 0 0 2 7 8 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \ n = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } ( \theta _ { A } ) \vert _ { t = 0 } = 2 0 0 4 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 1 0 9 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 8 5 3 3 0 T - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 2 1 7 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \pi = \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } ( \pi _ { A } ) \vert _ { t = 0 } = 4 7 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 2 9 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 6 6 0 3 T + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 5 9 8 T ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

# 6.6.2.2 Other Representations of Precession

In addition to the set of angles used by Newcomb (1898)and Lieske et al. (1977)to parameterize the precession,Williams (1994) summarized four other methods of parameterizing the precession matrix using the various angles shown in Fig. 6.6.

# The Fukushima-Williams parameterization.

Fukushima (2oo3) parameterized the precession with the angles: $\gamma$ ,the angle between the ecliptic pole of epoch, $c _ { 0 }$ ,and the ecliptic pole of date, $c$ ,as viewed from the pole, $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ ,of the initial reference system; $\phi$ ,the arc from $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ to $c$ ; $\Psi$ ,theangle from $\mathcal { P } _ { 0 }$ to the mean pole of date, $\mathcal { P }$ ,asviewed from $c$ ;and $\varepsilon _ { A }$ ,the obliquity of the mean ecliptic of date.The precession matrix, $\mathbf { P }$ ,thusbecomes:

$$
{ \bf P } = { \bf R } _ { 1 } ( - \varepsilon _ { A } ) { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( - \Psi ) { \bf R } _ { 1 } ( \phi ) { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( \gamma ) .
$$

If the initial reference system is the mean equator and ecliptic of J2ooo.O,then the rotation matrices are the sameas those inEq.37of Williams (1994).Fukushima,however,chose the initial reference system to be the GCRS.In this case,

$$
{ \bf P } | _ { T = 0 } = { \bf B }
$$

where $\mathbf { B }$ is the frame bias matrix from the GCRS to the mean equator and equinox of J2000.0.   
Two sets of the coefficients for $\Psi$ ， $\phi$ ,and $\gamma$ asa function of time are given in Table 6.3.

Those with the subscript J2OoO use the mean equator and equinox of J2Ooo.O as the initial reference frame while those coefficients with the subscript GCRS use the GCRSas the initial referenceframe.

TheCapitaine,Wallace,and Chapront parameterization.The Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront (2O03) parameterization separates the precession of the equator, $\psi _ { A }$ and $\omega _ { A }$ (the precession in obliquity) from the precesson of the ecliptic $P _ { A }$ and $Q _ { A }$ （ $\ S$ 6.6.2). Capitaine et al.also recommend determining the precession matrix using Eq.35 of Williams (1994),

$$
\mathbf { P } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \chi _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - \omega _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - \psi _ { A } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( \varepsilon _ { 0 } )
$$

where $\chi _ { A }$ is the spherical angle from the ascending node of the ecliptic of epoch along the mean equator of date to the mean equinox of date shown inFig.6.1,or,equivalently,the angle from the ecliptic pole of epoch to the ecliptic pole of date with itsapex at the mean equatorial pole of date shown inFig.6.4.The angle $\chi _ { A }$ is determined from the already known parameters for the precession using:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \cos \varepsilon _ { A } = \cos \pi _ { A } \cos \omega _ { A } - \sin \pi _ { A } \sin \omega _ { A } \cos ( \Pi _ { A } + \psi _ { A } ) } \\ { \sin \chi _ { A } = \displaystyle \frac { \sin \pi _ { A } } { \sin \varepsilon _ { A } } \sin ( \Pi _ { A } + \psi _ { A } ) . } \end{array}
$$

6.6.2.3 Rigorous Reduction for Precession

The most convenient method to account for precession in transforming mean equatorial rectangularcoordinates is to use the precession matrix $\mathbf { P }$ as follows:

$$
\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { P } \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } \quad { \mathrm { ~ o r ~ } } \quad \mathbf { r } _ { 0 } = \mathbf { P } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { r }
$$

which transforms the position vector $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 }$ from the mean equator and equinox of epoch $\mathrm { t } _ { 0 }$ ,to the position vector $\mathbf { r }$ with respect to the mean equator and equinox of date,and vice versa. The matrix $\mathbf { P }$ is evaluated using one of the sets of precession angles given above.

To convert quickly from the right ascension and declination of J2000.0, $( \alpha _ { 0 } , \delta _ { 0 } )$ ,tothe right ascension and declination of date, $( \alpha , \delta )$ ,the precession may be rendered

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \sin ( \alpha - z _ { A } ) \cos \delta = \sin ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \cos \delta _ { 0 } } \\ & { \cos ( \alpha - z _ { A } ) \cos \delta = \cos ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \cos \theta _ { A } \cos \delta _ { 0 } - \sin \theta _ { A } \sin \delta _ { 0 } } \\ & { \hphantom { \cos ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \cos \delta = } \sin \delta = \cos ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \sin \theta _ { A } \cos \delta _ { 0 } + \cos \theta _ { A } \sin \delta _ { 0 } . } \end{array}
$$

The inverse of these equations are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sin ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \cos \delta _ { 0 } = + \sin ( \alpha - z _ { A } ) \cos \delta } \\ { \cos ( \alpha _ { 0 } + \zeta _ { A } ) \cos \delta _ { 0 } = + \cos ( \alpha - z _ { A } ) \cos \theta _ { A } \cos \delta + \sin \theta _ { A } \sin \delta } \\ { \sin \delta _ { 0 } = - \cos ( \alpha - z _ { A } ) \sin \theta _ { A } \cos \delta + \cos \theta _ { A } \sin \delta . } \end{array}
$$

# 6.6.2.4 Approximate Reduction for Precession

Approximate formulae,accurate to about 30 arcseconds,100 years fromJ2ooo.0,for the reduction of coordinates and orbital elements referred to the mean equinox and equator or ecliptic of date, $t$ ,are:

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l r l } & { \alpha _ { 0 } = \alpha - M - N \sin \alpha _ { m } \tan \delta _ { m } } & & { \alpha = \alpha _ { 0 } + M + N \sin \alpha _ { m } \tan \delta _ { m } } \\ & { \delta _ { 0 } = \delta - N \cos \alpha _ { m } } & & { \delta = \delta _ { 0 } + N \cos \alpha _ { m } } \\ & { { \boldsymbol { \lambda } } _ { 0 } = \lambda - a + b \cos ( \lambda + c ^ { \prime } ) \tan \beta _ { 0 } } & & { \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 } + a - b \cos ( \lambda _ { 0 } + c ) \tan \beta } \\ & { \beta _ { 0 } = \beta - b \sin ( \lambda + c ^ { \prime } ) } & & { \beta = \beta _ { 0 } + b \sin ( \lambda _ { 0 } + c ) } \\ & { \Omega _ { 0 } = \Omega - a + b \sin ( \Omega + c ^ { \prime } ) \cot i _ { 0 } } & & { \Omega = \Omega _ { 0 } + a - b \sin ( \Omega _ { 0 } + c ) \cot i } \\ & { i _ { 0 } = i - b \cos ( \Omega + c ^ { \prime } ) } & & { i = i _ { 0 } + b \cos ( \Omega _ { 0 } + c ) } \\ & { \omega _ { 0 } = \omega - b \sin ( \Omega + c ^ { \prime } ) \csc i _ { 0 } } & & { \omega = \omega _ { 0 } + b \sin ( \Omega _ { 0 } + c ) \csc i } \end{array} }
$$

where the subscript O refers to epoch J2000.0,and $\alpha _ { m }$ and $\delta _ { m }$ refer to the mean epoch;with sufficient accuracy

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \alpha _ { m } = \alpha - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( M + N \sin \alpha \tan \delta ) } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \delta _ { m } = \delta - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } N \cos \alpha _ { m } } \\ { \mathrm { o r } } \\ { \alpha _ { m } = \alpha _ { 0 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( M + N \sin \alpha _ { 0 } \tan \delta _ { 0 } ) } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \delta _ { m } = \delta _ { 0 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } N \cos \alpha _ { m } . } \end{array}
$$

The precession angles,to a precision of $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,fromexpressions in Table 6.3 are

$$
\begin{array} { l c l } { { { \cal M } = ( \zeta _ { A } + z _ { A } ) } } & { { = 1 \cdot 2 8 1 1 6 T + 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 3 9 T ^ { 2 } + 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 0 1 T ^ { 3 } } } \\ { { { \cal N } = \theta _ { A } } } & { { = 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 5 5 6 \cdot 7 2 T - 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 1 2 T ^ { 2 } - 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 0 1 T ^ { 3 } } } \\ { { { \cal a } = p _ { A } } } & { { = 1 \cdot 3 9 6 8 9 T + 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 3 1 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { \cal b } = \pi _ { A } } } & { { = 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 1 3 0 6 T - 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 0 1 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { \cal c } = 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } - \Pi _ { m } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \cal a } = 5 \cdot 1 2 5 8 9 + 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 9 3 9 5 4 T + 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 1 1 T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { \cal c } ^ { \prime } = 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } - \Pi _ { m } - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \cal a } = 5 \cdot 1 2 5 8 9 - 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 4 5 7 3 4 T - 0 \cdot ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 2 0 T ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\Pi _ { m }$ is the accumulated longitude of the node of the ecliptic of date on the mean of the ecliptic of date and the ecliptic of J2Ooo.0.This isadifferent definition of $\Pi _ { m }$ than was used with the previous Lieske et al. (1977) based precession constants.In the older system $\Pi _ { m } = \Pi _ { A } ( \tau = \frac { 1 } { 2 } t$ , $T = 0$ )，where $\tau$ isthe time from the epoch of J2ooo.0 to an arbitrary intermediate epoch and $T$ is the time from the intermediate epoch to the date of interest, in centuries.This transformation gives the longitude of the ecliptic of J2ooo.Oon the ecliptic ofthe intermediate epoch with respect to the equinox of the intermediate epoch.Unlike the Lieske et al.precession,the Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront (2oo3) precession was not formulated to transform to an intermediate epoch.Thus,the older Explanatory Supplement formulation for the $\Pi _ { m }$ isused(Nautical AlmanacOfficesofUKandUSA1961,pp.35- 38).This transformation gives the longitude of the ecliptic of J2ooo.O on the ecliptic of the intermediate epoch with respect to the node of the mean equator of J2ooo.0.To first order, the difference in the value $\Pi _ { m }$ determined in this manner is that precession of the equatorhas notbeen included.Thus,this formulation is inherently less accurate than the one used with the Lieske et al. precession.

Formulae,valid over short periods,for the reduction from the mean equinox and equator or ecliptic of epoch $t _ { 1 }$ (e.g.,the middle of the year) to date $\boldsymbol { t } = \boldsymbol { t } _ { 1 } + \boldsymbol { \tau } )$ ,where $\tau$ isa fraction ofayearand $| \tau | < 1$ ,are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \alpha = \alpha _ { 1 } + \tau ( m + n \sin \alpha _ { 1 } \tan \delta _ { 1 } ) } & & { \delta = \delta _ { 1 } + \tau n \cos \alpha _ { 1 } } \\ & { \lambda = \lambda _ { 1 } + \tau [ p - \pi \cos ( \lambda _ { 1 } + 6 ^ { \circ } ) \tan \beta ] } & & { \beta = \beta _ { 1 } + \tau \pi \sin ( \lambda _ { 1 } + 6 ^ { \circ } ) } \\ & { \Omega = \Omega _ { 1 } + \tau [ p - \pi \sin ( \Omega _ { 1 } + 6 ^ { \circ } ) \cot i ] } & & { i = i _ { 1 } + \tau \pi \cos ( \Omega _ { 1 } + 6 ^ { \circ } ) } \\ & { \omega = \omega _ { 1 } + \tau \pi \sin ( \Omega _ { 1 } + 6 ^ { \circ } ) \csc i } \end{array}
$$

where $p , m , n$ ,and $\pi$ are the annual rates,evaluated at $t _ { 1 }$

# 6.6.2.5 Newcomb's Precession

Newcomb's precession angles are included as they are relevant for the transformation of some old star catalogs.Since Newcomb's value of the precession constant (Newcomb 1895) came into general use,several representations have been used for the practical realization of the accumulated precession angles denoted by $\zeta _ { 0 }$ z,and $\theta$ ,to distinguish themfrom Lieske's precession angles $\zeta _ { A }$ , $z _ { A }$ ,and $\theta _ { A }$ .Formulationsare given in the The Explanatory Supplement (Nautical Almanac Offces of UK and USA1961).Thereare also developments by: Andoyer(1911),from which the discussion byWoolard and Clemence(1966) was drawn; the introduction to the SAOC(1966)；and a more recent discussion by Kinoshita (1975) repeated byAoki etal. (1983).The formulae inKinoshitaand Aoki etal.are carried to one more significant digit than those in Andoyer.

Among the various representations,differences in the accumulated precession angles of the orderof1 mas are found inas short atime as 3O years.Forexample,acomparison of results fromWoolard and Clemence (1966),Kinoshita (1975),and the Explanatory Supplement (Nautical Almanac Offices of UK and USA1961) for the accumulated precession angles $\zeta _ { 0 }$ , $z$ ,and $\theta$ between equinox and equator for the epoch B1950.0 (JD 243 3282.423 TT)and the equinox and equator of 1984 Jan $1 ^ { \mathrm { d } } 0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ is given in Table 6.5.

Table 6.5 Precession angles with respect to the mean equator and equinox of B1950.0 at $1 9 8 4 \mathrm { J a n }$ . $1 ^ { d } 0 ^ { h }$ for various precessiontheories   

<table><tr><td>Angle</td><td>Woolard&amp; Clemence (arcsec.)</td><td>Kinoshita (arcsec.)</td><td>Explanatory Supplement (arcsec.)</td><td>Adopted (arcsec.)</td></tr><tr><td>50</td><td>783.70925</td><td>783.70938</td><td>783.70798</td><td>783.7092</td></tr><tr><td>Z</td><td>783.800 93</td><td>783.80106</td><td>783.799 42</td><td>783.8009</td></tr><tr><td>θ</td><td>681.388 30</td><td>681.38849</td><td>681.387 32</td><td>681.3883</td></tr></table>

Theresultsof the Kinoshita formulation agree with those ofWoolard& Clemence at the O.1 mas level. The Explanatory Supplement disagrees with these results at the1 to2 mas level.This disagreement is caused by the neglect of higher-order terms in the Explanatory Supplement (p.30). The Andoyer (Woolard and Clemence 1966) expressions for the precession angles are

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \zeta _ { 0 } = ( 2 3 0 3 5 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 4 5 + 1 3 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 2 0 t + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 6 0 t ^ { 2 } ) \tau + ( 3 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 2 4 0 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 2 7 0 t ) \tau ^ { 2 } + 1 7 ^ { \prime \prime } 9 9 5 \tau ^ { 3 } } \\ & { \ z = ( 2 3 0 3 5 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 4 5 + 1 3 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 2 0 t + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 6 0 t ^ { 2 } ) \tau + ( 1 0 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 8 0 + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 9 0 t ) \tau ^ { 2 } + 1 8 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 2 5 \tau ^ { 3 } } \\ & { \theta = ( 2 0 0 5 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 1 2 - 8 5 ^ { \prime \prime } 2 9 t - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 7 t ^ { 2 } ) \tau + ( - 4 2 ^ { \prime \prime } 6 5 - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 7 t ) \tau ^ { 2 } - 4 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 8 0 \tau ^ { 3 } } \end{array} }
$$

where $t$ isthe interval in units of 1000 tropical years of 365242.198782 days between the initial fixed epoch and B1850.0,and $\tau$ is difference between the time from the epoch of date to B1850.0 and $t$ .

The rates of general precession in right ascension, $m _ { 0 }$ ,and declination, $n _ { 0 }$ ,are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { m _ { 0 } { = } \displaystyle { \frac { \partial } { \partial \tau } } ( \zeta _ { 0 } { + } z ) | _ { t { = } 0 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = { 4 } 6 0 7 1 ^ { \prime \prime } { 0 } 9 0 { + } 2 7 9 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } { 4 } 4 0 \tau { + } 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 1 2 0 \tau ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { n _ { 0 } { = } \displaystyle { \frac { \partial } { \partial \tau } } ( \theta _ { 0 } ) | _ { t { = } 0 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = 2 0 0 5 1 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 1 2 { - } 8 5 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 2 9 \tau { - } 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 3 7 \tau ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

# 6.6.2.6 Differential Precession and Nutation

Differential precession and nutation may be added to the observed difference in the right ascension and declination, $\Delta \alpha$ and $\Delta \delta$ ,of an object relative to a comparison star to obtain the differences in the mean place for a standard epoch (e.g.,J2ooo.0 or the beginning of the year).The differences $\Delta \alpha$ and $\Delta \delta$ arein the sense“object minus comparison star,”and the corrections are in the same units as $\Delta \alpha$ and $\Delta \delta$ . In the correction to right ascension the same units must be used for $\Delta \alpha$ and $\Delta \delta$ .

correction to right ascension $= e$ tan $\delta \Delta \alpha - f \sec ^ { 2 } \delta \Delta \delta .$ ，correction to declination $= f \Delta \alpha$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { f = + \sin \alpha ( n t + \sin \varepsilon \Delta \psi ) - \cos \alpha \Delta \varepsilon , } \\ { \varepsilon = 2 3 . 4 4 } \end{array}
$$

where $t$ isthe time from the standard epoch to the time ofobservation,and $n$ therate of precession in declination. If $t$ is in years,then $n$ is in radians per year,and $\Delta \psi$ and $\Delta \varepsilon$ are the nutations in longitude and obliquity at the time of observation,in radians.The errors in arc units caused by using these formulae are of order $1 0 ^ { - 8 } \ t ^ { 2 } \sec ^ { 2 } \delta$ multiplied by the displacement in arc from the companion star.

# 6.6.3 Nutation

The non-secular motion of the Earth's rotation axis with respect to a space-fixed coordinate system iscalled nutation,and consists of both forced and free components.The forced component of the nutation arises from the torque produced by the gravitational attraction of the Moon and,to a lesser extent,the Sun and planets on the equatorial bulge and any deformations of the Earth.Itis usually modeled as a series of quasi-periodic motions whose periods and amplitudes depend on the source of the torque and the motion of the celestial bodies with respect to the rotating Earth.Seting the external force to zero in the equations ofmotion gives theparticular integral,or freemotion.This free motion depends on the constitution of the Earth including properties such as elasticity of the mantle and the size and shapesof the inner and outer cores.Itis excited by internal processes,and can be determined only from observations.If the Earth were a rigid body,it would be possible to compute thecoefficients of the forced nutation terms.However,like the freemotion,some of the parameters of the non-rigid Earth can be determined only by observation of its motion in space.

The separation of the instantaneous axis of rotation and instantaneous axis of the principal moment of inertia gives rise to nutation componentswith periods that are approximately diurnal and amplitudesaslargeasafewmilliarcseconds.Inaddition,the figure of the Earth isslightly tri-axial,meaning that there isa small difference between the magnitudesof the two principal moments of inertia that lie nearly in the Earth's equatoras defined by the axis ofrotation.This difference in the equatorial principal moments of inertia also gives rise to nearly diurnal and semidiurnal nutation components with amplitudes of no more thana few microarcseconds.Although these short-period components of the nutation are properly part of the nutation,they cannot be separated observationally from notions due to the movement of theEarth'saxiswithin the body of the Earth.Consequently,the CIP( $\ S \ 6 . 2 . 3 )$ ischosen to be along the axis offigure for the mean surface of amodel of Earth in which the motions with periods greater than or equal to two days within a geocentric celestial reference frame have zero amplitude.This pole has no nearly diurnal components of motion with respect to either a space-fixed or an Earth-fixed coordinate system,and componentsof nutation with periods shorter than two days become part of the polar motion by definition.

The CIP is the axis about which the diurnal rotation of the Earth is applied in the transformation betweencelestial and terrestrial frames,so itisoften referred toastheaxis ofrotation in general discussions.It may also be referred to as the true celestial pole of date (IAU 1976).
