In the 1984 through 2oo6 editions of The Astronomical Almanac,the polar motion was measured with respect to the celestial ephemeris pole,chosen to be along theaxis of figure for the mean surface of a model of Earth in which the free motions have zero amplitude.Prior to1984,the ephemeris pole waschosen to be the instantaneous axis of rotation.Atkinson (1973); Atkinson (1975),however,pointed out that use of the instantaneous pole of rotation led to quasi-diurnal motions,and thus,it was not the best reference pole.

# 6.6.3.1The IAU 2000A Theory of Nutation

TheIAU 2OooA Theoryof Nutationwas computed for thecelestial intermediate pole by determining the nutations in longitude and obliquityof a rigid Earth (Souchay etal.1999),and making modifications for the non-rigid Earth (Mathews,Herring,and Buffett 2OO2) in such afashion that there are no nearly diurnal motions of this celestial pole with respect to either space-fixed or body-fixed (crust-fixed) coordinates.A table of the nutation amplitudes and thefundamental arguments is available in Kaplan (2Oo5).Software implementing the data in these tables are available on the world wide web from the International Earth Rotation andReference Systems Service (IERS) (URL[1]),from the USNO (URL[2]) NOVAS subroutines,and from SOFA(URL[3]).As explained in $\ S 6 . 6 . 1$ each of these software packages implements the IAU 2Oo nutation in slightly different ways.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/d301113b5a7d63fa287af1146318b8e3344fc2dd71e0a71487ebdbf644c2877e.jpg)  
Figure 6.7 The nutations in longitude, $\Delta \psi$ ,and latitude, $\Delta \varepsilon$ ， connect the mean pole of date, $\overline { { \mathcal { P } } }$ ,to the CIP, $\mathcal { P }$ ,via the ecliptic pole of date, $c$ .

Nutation describes the motion of the CIP, $\overline { { \mathcal { P } } }$ ,relative to the mean pole, $\mathcal { P }$ ,and may be resolved into the components $\Delta \psi$ in longitude and $\Delta \varepsilon$ in obliquity,shown in Fig.6.7.The nutation matrix, $\mathbf { N }$ ,isa sequence of three rotations which uses these anglesand the mean obliquity of the ecliptic of date, $\varepsilon _ { A }$ ,to transform equatorial coordinates referredto the Earth's mean pole of date to the CIP.

The rotations are a rotation of $\varepsilon _ { A }$ about the $x$ -axis,which transforms the $x y$ -plane from the mean equator of date to the mean ecliptic of date,followedbya rotation of $- \Delta \psi$ about the $Z$ -axis,which applies nutation in longitude and rotates the $x$ -axis,pointing toward the mean equinox of date $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon } _ { M }$ ,to the true equinox of date $\gamma _ { _ T }$ ,and lastlyarotation of $- \varepsilon$ ,the true obliquity of date,about the $x$ -axis,which rotates the $x y$ -plane to the true equator of date. The expressions for themean and true obliquity of date are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \varepsilon _ { A } = 2 3 ^ { \circ } 2 6 ^ { \prime } 2 1 . ^ { \prime \prime } 4 0 6 0 0 - 4 6 . _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 8 3 6 7 6 9 T - 0 . _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 1 8 3 1 T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad + 0 . 7 0 0 2 0 0 3 4 0 T ^ { 3 } - 5 . _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } T ^ { 4 } - 4 . \cdot 3 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { \varepsilon = \varepsilon _ { A } + \Delta \varepsilon } \end{array}
$$

where $T = \left( \mathrm { J D } - 2 4 5 1 5 4 5 . 0 \right) / 3 6 5 2 5 ,$ The nutation matrix (Emerson 1973) is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { \mathbf { N } } = { \mathbf { R } } _ { 1 } ( - \varepsilon ) ~ { \mathbf { R } } _ { 3 } ( - \Delta \psi ) ~ { \mathbf { R } } _ { 1 } ( + \varepsilon _ { A } ) } \\ & { \quad \quad = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \Delta \psi } & { - \sin \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon _ { A } } & { - \sin \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon _ { A } } \\ { \sin \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon } & { \cos \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon _ { A } + \sin \varepsilon \sin \varepsilon _ { A } } & { \cos \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon \sin \varepsilon _ { A } - \sin \varepsilon \cos \varepsilon _ { A } } \\ { \sin \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon } & { \cos \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon \cos \varepsilon _ { A } - \cos \varepsilon \sin \varepsilon _ { A } } & { \cos \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon \sin \varepsilon _ { A } + \cos \varepsilon \cos \varepsilon _ { A } } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Nutation may be rigorously applied to equatorial rectangular coordinates thus:

$$
\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { N } \mathbf { r } _ { 0 }
$$

where the position vector $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 }$ referred to the mean equinox of date is transformedto the position vectorr referred to the true equinox of date.

The nutation angles $\Delta \psi$ , $\Delta \varepsilon$ are evaluated using

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \Delta \psi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( S _ { \Delta \psi _ { i } } + { \dot { S } } _ { \Delta \psi _ { i } } T ) \sin A _ { i } + C _ { \Delta \psi _ { i } } \cos A _ { i } \quad \mathrm { a n d } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \Delta \varepsilon = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } S _ { \Delta \varepsilon _ { i } } \sin A _ { i } + ( C _ { \Delta \varepsilon _ { i } } + { \dot { C } } _ { \Delta \varepsilon _ { i } } T ) \cos A _ { i } } } \end{array}
$$

where $T$ is the time in Julian centuries and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { A _ { i } = a _ { i } l + b _ { i } l ^ { \prime } + c _ { i } F + d _ { i } D + e _ { i } \Omega + f _ { i } l _ { M e } + g _ { i } l _ { V } } } \\ { { \qquad + h _ { i } l _ { E } + j _ { i } l _ { M a } + k _ { i } l _ { J } + m _ { i } l _ { S } + n _ { i } l _ { U } + o _ { i } l _ { N } + r _ { i } p . } } \end{array}
$$

The critical arguments inEq. 6.44 are described in Table 6.6.The multipliers $a _ { i }$ through $r _ { i }$ ,and the coefficients amplitudes in seconds of arc for $S _ { \Delta \psi _ { i } }$ through $\dot { C } _ { \Delta \varepsilon _ { i } }$ are given in the IAU2000A Nutation Series table on pp.88-103 of Kaplan (2005).The terms of the series aregrouped according to their periods,and arranged roughly in order of magnitude of the coefficient of the nutation in longitude within each group.The values for the fundamental argumentsareevaluatedusingEqs.5.17through5.19ofKaplan.Valuesforthenutation anglesand the true obliquity of the ecliptic, $\varepsilon$ ,determined using these series are tabulated at daily intervalsonpagesB58 throughB65of TheAstronomical Almanac.

# 6.6.3.2 Approximate Nutation and Reduction

For applications that do not require an accuracy of better than one part in $1 0 ^ { 8 }$ ,the nutation matrix given inEq.6.41 may be simplified to

$$
\mathbf { N } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { - \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon } & { - \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon } \\ { + \Delta \psi \cos \varepsilon } & { 1 } & { - \Delta \varepsilon } \\ { + \Delta \psi \sin \varepsilon } & { + \Delta \varepsilon } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]
$$

where $\Delta \psi$ and $\Delta \varepsilon$ are expressed in radians.

Equatorial rectangular coordinates referred to the mean equinox can be converted to the true equinox by application of the corrections

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta x = - ( y \cos \varepsilon + z \sin \varepsilon ) \Delta \psi } \\ & { \Delta y = + x \cos \varepsilon \Delta \psi - z \Delta \varepsilon } \\ & { \Delta z = + x \sin \varepsilon \Delta \psi + y \Delta \varepsilon . } \end{array}
$$

Similarly the first-order corrections $( \Delta \alpha , \ \Delta \delta )$ to right ascension or celestial ephemeris longitude and declination may be calculated directly from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta \alpha = ( \cos \varepsilon + \sin \varepsilon \sin \alpha \tan \delta ) \Delta \psi - \cos \alpha \tan \delta \Delta \varepsilon } \\ & { } \\ & { \Delta \delta = \sin \varepsilon \cos \alpha \Delta \psi + \sin \alpha \Delta \varepsilon . } \end{array}
$$

Table 6.6 Critical arguments for the IAU 20o0A nutation   

<table><tr><td>Symbol</td><td>Argument</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>Themean anomaly of theMoon</td></tr><tr><td>l&#x27;</td><td>The mean anomaly of the Sun</td></tr><tr><td>F</td><td>The differenceL-Ω where L is the mean longitude of the Moon</td></tr><tr><td>D</td><td>The meananomaly of the Moon</td></tr><tr><td>Ω</td><td>The longitude of the ascending node of the Moon&#x27;s mean orbit</td></tr><tr><td>lMe</td><td>on the ecliptic,measured from the mean equinox of date</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>The mean heliocentric longitude of Mercury</td></tr><tr><td>lv</td><td>The mean heliocentric longitude of Venus</td></tr><tr><td>lE</td><td>Themeanheliocentriclongitudeof theEarth</td></tr><tr><td>lMa</td><td>The mean heliocentric longitude ofMars</td></tr><tr><td>l</td><td>Themeanheliocentriclongitude of Jupiter</td></tr><tr><td>ls</td><td>Themean heliocentric longitudeof Saturn</td></tr><tr><td>lu</td><td>Themeanheliocentriclongitude ofUranus</td></tr><tr><td>lN P</td><td>ThemeanheliocentriclongitudeofNeptune Anapproximation of thegeneral precession inlongitude</td></tr></table>

It is also possible to calculate the nutations in longitude and obliquity to about $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ by considering only the dominant terms of the series Kaplan (20o5,pp.88-103), thus

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \Delta { \psi } = - 0 ; 0 0 4 8 \sin ( 1 2 5 \mathrm { . 0 - 0 . 0 5 2 9 5 } d ) - 0 \mathrm { . 0 0 0 4 } \sin ( 2 0 0 \mathrm { ^ { \circ } } 9 + \mathrm { 1 . 9 } 7 1 2 9 d ) } \\ { \Delta { \varepsilon } = + 0 \mathrm { . 0 0 2 6 } \cos ( 1 2 5 \mathrm { . 0 - 0 . 0 5 2 9 5 } d ) + 0 \mathrm { . 0 0 0 2 } \cos ( 2 0 0 \mathrm { ^ { \circ } } 9 + \mathrm { 1 . 9 } 7 1 2 9 d ) } \end{array}
$$

where $d$ is the number of days from2451545.0.

# 6.6.3.3Differential Nutation

Forobjects within a small area of sky differential nutation is alwayscombined with differential precession; see $\ S 6 . 6 . 2 . 6$ .

# 6.7The Celestial Intermediate Origin (CIO)

Beginningon1 January 2Oo3,the IAU recommends the use of the Celestial Intermediate Origin(CIO) in place of the equinox as the origin of right ascension in the CRS $( \ S \ 6 . 2$ showninFig.6.1).The position ofthe CIO is determined kinematicallyand has the following properties:

1.It lies on the instantaneous celestial equator of date.   
2.As it moves,there is no motion around the $\mathbf { Z }$ -axis.Thatis,itsmotionisalways perpendicular to the instantaneous equator.This has been termed a non-rotating origin.   
3.The position of the CIO at J2000.0 (Julian date2451 545.0 TT) is defined to be that of the mean equinox of J2000.0.

Since its original definition it has been determined that there was aslight misalignment $( \sim 2 0 \mathrm { m a s } )$ betweenboth thepoleof theGCRS $( \ S \ G . 2 )$ and the CIP( $\ S \ 6 . 2 . 3 )$ ,and the CIO and the mean equinox of J2Ooo.0.The difference between these two reference systems is constant,and the conversion from one to the other is the purpose of the frame bias matrix, $\mathbf { B }$ (86.5).

The CIO is intended for use with a combined bias-precession-nutation matrix (Capitaine, Wallace,and Chapront 2Oo3).This parameterization is in terms of the two angles, $\mathcal { X }$ ,inthe direction toward the origin of right ascension of the GCRS (8 6.2),and $_ y$ ,in the direction $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ to the east of $x$ ),which give the coordinatesof the CIP unit vector in theGCRS.The third parameteris the CIO locator, $s$ .Exceptforatinyfixedoffset, $s$ is the difference between the length of the arcs from $\Sigma$ to $N ^ { \prime }$ and $o$ to $N ^ { \prime } .$ The point $\Sigma$ is the origin of right ascension of the GCRS, $\sigma$ is the CIO,and $N ^ { \prime }$ is the node of the equator of the CIP on the equator of the GCRS.This matrix includes precession, nutation,coupling between precession and nutation, and frame biases.The value of $\mathbf { \delta } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } } \mathbf { { \mathbf { { \mathcal { S } } } } }$ is given by

$$
s = - \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \frac { \chi \dot { y } - y \dot { \chi } } { 1 + \mathcal { Z } } d t - ( \Sigma N ^ { \prime } - \sigma _ { 0 } N ^ { \prime } )
$$

where $\sigma _ { 0 }$ is the position of $\sigma$ at JD 2 451545.0 and

$$
\mathcal { Z } = ( 1 - \chi ^ { 2 } - \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } .
$$

The bias-precession-nutation matrix7is:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathbf { N P B } _ { \mathrm { C I O } } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - s ) \mathbf { R } } & { } \\ { = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - s ) \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 - a \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } } & { - a \mathcal { X } \mathcal { Y } } & { \mathcal { X } } \\ { - a \mathcal { X } \mathcal { Y } } & { 1 - a \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } } & { \mathcal { Y } } \\ { - \mathcal { X } } & { - \mathcal { Y } } & { 1 - a ( \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } ) } \end{array} \right] } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle a \approx \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { \chi ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } { 8 } } \\ { \displaystyle \mathcal { X } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 5 } x _ { i } T ^ { i } + \sum _ { j } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { 3 } T ^ { k } ( b _ { s j k } \sin d _ { k } + b _ { c j k } \cos d _ { k } ) } \\ { \displaystyle y = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 5 } y _ { i } T ^ { i } + \sum _ { j } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { 3 } T ^ { k } ( e _ { s j k } \sin f _ { k } + e _ { c j k } \cos f _ { k } ) . } \end{array}
$$

$T$ is the time in Julian centuries from $J 2 0 0 0 . 0 \mathrm { T T } ,$ $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ are the coefficients for the frame bias and precession in $\mathcal { X }$ and $\mathcal { V }$ ,respectively, $b _ { s j k }$ , $b _ { c j k }$ , $e _ { s j k }$ ,and $e _ { c j k }$ are the coefficients for the nutation and coupling between precession and nutation,and $d _ { k }$ and $f _ { k }$ are the

fundamental angular arguments for the nutation and coupling terms.The parameter $s$ may also be represented byaseries,but itis more efcienttoderive $s$ from the series for $s + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \chi y$ The non-periodic parts of Eqs.6.52 and the series for $s + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \chi y$ are:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathcal { X } = - 0 . 7 ^ { \prime } 0 1 6 6 1 7 + 2 0 0 4 ^ { \prime \prime } 1 9 1 8 9 8 T - 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 4 2 9 7 8 2 9 T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad - 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 1 9 8 6 1 8 3 4 T ^ { 3 } + 7 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 7 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } + 5 ^ { \prime 9 } 2 8 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { \mathcal { Y } = - 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 6 9 5 1 - 0 . ^ { \prime 0 } 2 5 8 9 6 T - 2 2 . ^ { \prime \prime } 4 0 7 2 7 4 7 T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 1 9 0 0 5 9 T ^ { 3 } + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 1 1 1 2 5 2 6 T ^ { 4 } + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 1 3 5 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { s = - \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathcal { X } \mathcal { Y } + 9 . ^ { \prime } 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } + 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 3 8 0 8 6 5 T - 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 1 2 2 6 8 T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad - 0 . ^ { \prime \prime } 7 2 5 7 4 1 1 T ^ { 3 } + 2 . ^ { \prime \prime } 7 9 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } T ^ { 4 } + 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 6 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } T ^ { 5 } . } \end{array}
$$

Approximate formulae for the position of the CIP and CIO,accurate to about $0 \% 0 0 0 1$ over the period from1975 through 2025are available on pages B46andB47of The Astronomical Almanac.

An explanation of the theory and the sources for machine-readable tabulations of it are in McCarthy andPetit (2Oo4,p.44).The elements of the matrix inEq.6.51are derived from a second order expansion of cosine and sine functions,and are dimensionless.Thus,the values of $\mathcal { X } , \mathcal { Y } , s$ ,and the coefficients for the nutation and coupling between precession and nutation should be in radians.However,these values are normally given in arcseconds and must be converted.

# 6.8 Time and Earth Orientation

Time asmeasured by the Earth'srotation,may be thought of as the difference along the equator between the CRS(§ 6.2) and the projection of a terrestrial reference longitude onto the CRS.In the case of Universal Time,UT,the Earth'srotation is measured with respect to the mean fictitious anti-solardirection,midnight,and the terrestrial reference meridian is the origin of longitude of the TRS(S 6.2).For local meansolar time,the Earth's rotation is measured with respectto the mean fictitious anti-solar direction (midnight),and the reference meridian is the local meridian of longitude.For sidereal time theangle is measured with respectto the mean equinox for mean sidereal time andthe true equinox for apparent sidereal time.When thereference meridian for sidereal timeis the TRS,the sidereal time is either GreenwichMeanSidereal Time,GMST,orGreenwich ApparentSidereal Time,GAST.When thereference on the CRS is the CIO(§ 6.7)and the terrestrial reference meridian is the origin of longitude ofthe TRS,then theangulardifference is the Earth RotationAngle,ERA.

Until the nineteenth century the basic unit of time,the day,was based on the assumption that the Earth's rotation rate was constant,and,the determination of time depended on astronomical observations.There are seasonal differences in the apparent motion of the Sun that have been known since at least the time of Ptolemy.These differencesare not the result of variation in the Earth's rotation rate.They arise from the non-uniform motion of the Earth in its orbit about the Sun,and lead to the differentiation of apparent solar time and mean solar time.

The failure of successive lunar theories to represent the observed motion of the Moon eventuallyled to the suspicion that the rotation of the Earth was not uniform.In ephemerides calculated from gravitational theories,time isindependent of the variable rotation of the Earth.Consequently,the Earth's non-uniform rotation would cause the observed hour angle ofacelestial bodyto differfrom the position tabulated in the ephemeris.

Analysis of the discrepancies between observed and computed positions revealed the difference between the Earth's rotation and a uniform timescale.These discrepancies were most evident in the mean motion of the Moon,due to the accuracy with which the position can be observed and the rapidity of its motion.Similar discrepanciesin the motions ofother solar system bodies,in proportion to the magnitude of their mean motions,were also observed. These discrepanciesconfirmed the variable rateof theEarth'srotation.

The secular retardationof the rate of rotation due to tidal interaction between the Earth and Moon was the first variationto be recognized.Adams (1853) showed thatthe observed secular acceleration of the mean motionof theMooncould not totallybeproduced by gravitational perturbations.The validity of these results was hotly contested.The issue was resolved when Ferrell (1864) and Delaunay (1865) showed from dynamical principles that the tides exert a retarding action on the rotationof the Earth,accompaniedbyavariation ofthe orbital velocity of the Moon in accordance with the conservation of angular momentum.

Newcomb (1878)considered the possibility of irregular Variations of the Earth's rotation as an explanation oflunar residuals.He could not find confirmation from planetary data (Newcomb1912),however.Some of the difficulties faced by Newcomb,and prior astronomers, arose from inadequacies in the lunar theory.This situation was rectified when Brown (1919) introduced his new tables.A numberof studies offered increasing evidence of irregular variations inthe Earth's rotation.Most important were papersbyde Sitter(1927)and Spencer Jones (1939b),which correlated irregularities in the Moon's motion with irregularities of the inner planets.

Meanwhile,the accuracy of crystal-controlled clocks was becoming comparable with that of the rotation of the Earth.By comparing the observed rates of the clocks of national time services,Stoyko(1937) found a periodic seasonal variation in the rate of rotation.

TheEarth's rotationrate was eventually found to have secular,irregular,and periodic variations.Since mean solar time was determined directly from astronomical observations of the Earth's rotation,it could no longer be considered uniform.

Thus,a uniform timescale based on the motions of the bodies of the solar system, Ephemeris Time,ET,later followed by $\mathrm { T _ { e p h } }$ (the JPL realization of Barycentric Dynamical Time,TDB)replaced the rotationof the Earth as the independent variable for planetary ephemerides. ET and $\mathrm { T _ { e p h } }$ are discussed in Chapter 3.This section will discuss time as it relatestoEarth rotation thatis,solar time( $\left( \ S 6 . 8 . 1 \right)$ ,Universal Time(UT) see $\ S 6 . 8 . 3$ ,Earth Rotation Angle (ERA) see $\ S 6 . 8 . 4$ ,and sidereal time $( \ S 6 . 8 . 5 )$

# 6.8.1Apparent Solar Time,Mean Solar Time,and the Equationof Time

Apparent solar time is the measure of time defined by theactual diurnal motion of the Sun.If the Sun’s right ascension increased at a uniform rate and the Earth's rotation were uniform, solar days would be equal in length throughout the year,and apparent solar time would then provideauniform measure of time.However,like the Earth's rotation rate,the Sun's motion in right ascension is not uniform because the Sun moves along the ecliptic rather than the equator,and the Sun’s motionalong the ecliptic is not constant due to the Earth's orbit being elliptical,not circular.

The motion of an abstract fiducial point called the fictitious mean Sun,defines mean solar time.This point moves uniformlyin the equatorial plane atarate that is virtually equal to the mean rate of the true Sun’smotion in theecliptic.The difference between mean solar time and apparent solar time,called the equation of time,is never greater than 16 minutes.The link between meansolar time and sidereal time was based on the relation

$$
\mathrm { m e a n \ s o l a r \ t i m e \ a t { G r e e n w i c h = G M S T - R A _ { \odot } + 1 2 ^ { h } } }
$$

where GMST is the Greenwich Hour Angle of the mean equinox of date (i.e.,Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time)and $\mathbf { R A } _ { \odot }$ is the right ascension of the fictitious mean Sun.

Underlying the concept of mean solar time was the assumption that the rotation of the Earth was uniform.In the first halfof the twentieth century,the lunar ephemeris demonstrated thatthis assumption was incorrect( $\left( \ S 6 . 8 \right)$ .Thus,mean solar time was no longer used in precise timekeeping.It was replaced by two somewhat different concepts of time:Ephemeris Time, ET (see Chapter 3),was introduced to satisfy the desire for a uniform measure of time,and Universal Time,UT,tomeasure the Earth'srotation.Originally UTwas introducedto specify Greenwich Mean Time measured from noon instead of midnight (§ 6.8.3).

Over the centuries,the definition of mean solar time,and,hence,of the equation of time, evolvedas new tables for the motion of the Sun were introduced.Thus,care mustbe taken in analyzing historical observations that are expressed in mean time to determine whatdefinition wasused in the reductions.

# 6.8.2 Greenwich Mean Time

Prior to 1925,mean solar time was reckoned from noon in astronomical practice.The mean solar day beginning at noon, $1 2 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ after midnight at the beginning of the same civil date,was known as the astronomical day.Mean solar time reckoned from mean noon on the meridian ofGreenwichwasdesignatedGreenwichMean Time,GMT.Local MeanTime,LMT,was reckoned from mean noon on a local meridian.The national ephemerides for 1925,introduced a discontinuity of $1 2 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ at the beginning of the year in the tabular arguments,so the instant designated December 31.5 in the volumes for1924 was designated January 1.0 in the volumes for1925.

InTheNautical Almanac,the designation GreenwichMean Time,GMT,was still used for the new reckoning,together with local mean time where appropriate,whereas in The AmericanEphemeristhedesignationGreenwichCivilTime,GCT,wasadopted,together with local civil time,LCT.Thisconfusion in terminology was finallyremoved by dropping bothdesignationsand substitutingUniversal Time,UT(g 6.8.3).Careis necessary to avoid confusion.To distinguish between the two reckonings that have both been called Greenwich Mean Time,thedesignation Greenwich MeanAstronomical Time,GMAT,should be usedfor thereckoning fromnoon.The designation UTalways refers to time reckoned from Greenwich midnight,even for epochs before 1925.

IntheUnited Kingdom,GreenwichMean Timehasbeen identified with thecivil time or Coordinated Universal Time,UTC(§ 6.8.3.1).This connection,however,has never been formalized,sousing GMTto referto UTCshould be done with care.For navigation,however,

Greenwich Mean Time has meant UT1( 6.8.3).Thus,GMThas two meanings that can differ by as much as $0 . 9$ ,and the term GMT should not be used for precise purposes.

# 6.8.3 Universal Time (UT)

Universal Time,UT,is the de facto measure of time used as the basis for civil timekeeping.It conforms closely to the mean diurnal motion of the Sun.The apparent diurnal motion of the Sun involves both the non-uniform diurnal rotation of the Earth and the motion of the Earth in its orbit around the Sun.In practice UTis related to sidereal time by means of anumerical formula.It does not refer to the motion of the Earth and is not precisely related to the hour angle of the Sun.

UTis now derived from observations of the diurnal motion of radio sources.These observationsprovide the timescale designated UT1.UTO isalocal scale,which isacombination of UTand the effect of the variation of the local longitude due to polar motion.

Originally,Newcomb(1898) gave the expression for the right ascension of the fictitious mean Sun as:

$$
\mathbf { R A } _ { \odot } = 1 8 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 3 8 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 4 5 ^ { \mathrm { s } } 8 3 6 + 8 6 4 0 1 8 4 ^ { \mathrm { s } } 5 4 2 T + 0 ^ { \mathrm { s } } 0 9 2 9 T ^ { 2 }
$$

where $T$ is measured in Julian centuries from 19oo January O, $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ Greenwich mean noon. This expression differs from that of the mean longitude of the Sun by only a slight,progressively increasing excess of 00203 $T ^ { 2 }$ due to the secular acceleration of the Sun and to the different rates of general precession on the ecliptic and on the equator.

Inserting Eq.6.55 into the formula for mean solar time,Eq.6.54,and evaluating the result at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ mean solar time on the Greenwich meridian,gives the Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ mean solar time:

$$
\mathrm { G M S T _ { 0 } } = 6 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 3 8 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 4 5 ^ { \mathrm { s } } 8 3 6 + 8 6 4 0 1 8 4 ^ { \mathrm { s } } 5 4 2 T + 0 . 0 9 2 9 T ^ { 2 }
$$

where $T$ isa uniform timescale in units of Julian centuries $( 3 6 5 2 5 ^ { \mathrm { d } } )$ ).Butvaluescalculated fromthis expression are notconsistent with observed hour angles of stars because the rotation of the Earth is not uniform.

From the beginning of the century through 1959,these formulae were used in the British Nautical Almanac and in the American Ephemeristo define mean solar timeand to relate solar time and sidereal time.As givenby Newcomb,the independent variable in Eq.6.55 is consistent with that of his solar tables.

Asevidence accumulated that the Earth did not rotate uniformly,Newcomb's $T$ was interpreted as the independent variable of the Earth'sorbital revolution rather than its axial rotation.Since Newcomb'stables were the basis of the fundamental ephemerides of the inner planets, $T$ became associated with the concept of auniform measure of time,based on the laws of orbital dynamics.

$T _ { E }$ (expressed in Julian centuries of 36525 ephemeris days from 19o0 January 0, $1 2 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { E T }$ ) can be used inEq.6.55 to signifyET.Then $\mathrm { R A } _ { \odot } ( T _ { E } ) = R _ { E }$ defines a uniformly moving fiducial point that is independent of the Earth's rotation.

To make Eq.6.56 consistent with observed hourangles of the stars,Universal Time,UT, was institutedasameasure of the Earth's rotation.Then,Eq.6.55 becomesa definition of UT,with $T _ { U }$ (measured in Julian centuries of 36525 daysof UT from190o JanuaryO,12h

UT)serving as the independent variable: $\mathrm { R A } _ { \odot } ( T _ { U } ) = R _ { U }$ .The difference between $R _ { E }$ and $R _ { U }$ is given by

$$
R _ { E } - R _ { U } = 0 . 0 0 2 7 3 8 \Delta T
$$

where $\Delta T = \mathrm { E T - U T }$ .

# 6.8.3.1 Coordinated Universal Time

Beginning in 1962,an increasing number of broadcast time services cooperated to provide a consistent time standard.Most broadcast signals were synchronizedto the redefined CoordinatedUniversalTime,UTC,in1972.Priorto1972,broadcasttimesignalswere keptwithin 0.1 second of UT2by introducing step adjustments,normally of 0.1 second,and occasionally by changing the duration of the UTC second.The scale UT2 resulted fromapplying the following formula forthe seasonal variation in therate of the Earth'srotation to UT1:

$$
\mathrm { U T 2 } = \mathrm { U T 1 } + 0 . 0 2 2 \sin 2 \pi \tau - 0 . 0 1 2 \cos 2 \pi \tau - 0 . 0 0 6 \sin 4 \pi \tau + 0 . 0 0 7 \cos 4 \pi \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the fraction of the Besselian year of the observation.

The seasonal variation in the rotation of the Earth was first detected by means of crystal clocks atthe Physicalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt (Scheibe and Adelsberger1936).Stoyko (1936);Stoyko (1937);Finch (1950);Smith and Tucker(1953) confirmed these variations. Lunar tidal variations were found to bein reasonable agreement with the theory.McCarthy andPetit (2Oo4,Chapter8and references therein) provide more recent reports.Originally, the research onlong-term variations inthe rotation ofthe Earth was investigated using the motion of the Moon,which was the only process available with sufficient precision(Spencer Jones $1 9 3 9 \mathrm { a }$ ；MunkandMacDonald196O).Nowatomic timeisthebasisforresearch into the Earth's rotational variations.

Since1972,UTCdiffers from TAIby an integernumberof seconds.Itis maintained within 0.9 second of UT1 by the introduction of one-second steps (leap seconds) when necessary. Preferablya leap second is insertedat the end of June or December,but onemay occurat theend of anymonth.DUT1,anextrapolation of the difference UT1-UTC,accurate to approximately $\pm 0 . 1$ s,is transmitted in code on some broadcast time signals.

# 6.8.4 Earth Rotation Angle(ERA or $\theta$ ）

Therelationship between a conventionally defined direction in space and a conventionally defined directionon the Earth represents the rotation of the Earth in inertial space.There are several waysto measure this relationship.In thepast observationsweremadeinacelestial reference system defined by the directions to stars.Prior to 2OO3 measurements were made using as areference the equinox and its motion as realized by theIAU(1976) System of Astronomical Constants,the IAU(198O) Theory of Nutation,and the positions and proper motions of stars of theFK5catalog.Now radio observationsof quasarsareused.This quasiinertial framewas linked to the positions of quasarswith the adoption of the ICRS (IAU 1997).TheIAU (2Oolc) redefined UT1with respect to the Earth Rotation Angle (ERA or $\theta$ ） beginning on 2Oo3 January1as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \theta ( { \bf U } { \bf T } 1 ) = 2 \pi [ 0 . 7 7 9 0 5 7 2 7 3 2 6 4 0 + 1 . 0 0 2 7 3 7 8 1 1 9 1 1 3 5 4 4 8 } \\ { \times ( { \bf J } \mathrm { u l i a n U T } 1 \mathrm { d a t e } - 2 4 5 1 5 4 5 . 0 ) ] \quad \mathrm { r a d } . \quad } \end{array}
$$

# 6.8.5 Sidereal Time

Prior to 1960,thedesignation“Right Ascension of Mean $\mathrm { S u n } + 1 2 ^ { \mathrm { h } , . . }$ was sometimes applied to tabulations of Greenwich Sidereal Time at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ UT.This inexact terminology was dropped when the distinction between ETand UTwas introduced in national ephemerides in 1960. Eq.6.55,with $T _ { U }$ as the independent variable,was used during the period 196O-1983 to calculate tabularvalues ofGreenwichMean Sidereal Timeat $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ UT.

# 6.8.5.1Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time (GMST)

From1984 January 1 until 2O03 January 1 Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time(GMST) was related to UT1 by:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { G M S T ~ a t ~ } 0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { U T } 1 } & { { } = } & { 2 4 \ 1 1 0 . 5 4 8 \ 4 1 + 8 \ 6 4 0 \ 1 8 4 . 8 1 2 \ 8 6 6 T _ { U } } \\ { \mathrm { } } & { { } } & { } \\ { \qquad + \ 0 . 0 9 3 \ 1 0 4 T _ { U } ^ { 2 } - 6 . 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T _ { U } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { ~ s ~ } } \end{array}
$$

where $T _ { U } = d _ { U } / 3 6 5 2 5$ , $d _ { U }$ is the integer number of days $+ 0 . 5$ ofUT elapsed since JD 2 451545.0 UT1 (2000 January 1, $1 2 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ UT1)(Aoki et al.1982). This relation conforms to the position and motion of the equinox specified in theIAU(1976) System of Astronomical Constants,the IAU(198o) Theory of Nutation,and the positions and proper motions of stars in the FK5 catalog.

When it was established,Eq.6.6O wasconsidered to be the definitionof UT1,because at that time the observation of transits of stars with their positions and proper motions in theFK5 systemwas the best means to obtain UT1.As new techniques to determine UT1 became operational,the reference system modeling involved in their implementations was constrained to be consistent with this definition.

The constant term and the numerical coefficient of $T _ { U }$ in Eq.6.60 were chosen so that UT1 would be continuous in value and rate through the change from the old to the new system of constants (1984January1).The small differences due to the change in the theory of nutation were neglected.

The variation of UT1 with respect to uniform time truly represents the irregularities of the rotation of the Earth.The average duration, $\Delta$ ,of theday of UT1in SI seconds over an $n$ -dayintervalisgivenby

$$
\Delta = 8 6 4 0 0 - ( \psi _ { 2 } - \psi _ { 1 } ) / n \mathrm { ~ s ~ }
$$

where $\psi _ { 1 }$ and $\psi _ { 2 }$ are the valuesof UT1-TAIin secondsat $n$ -day intervals.The meanangular velocity of the Earth, $\omega$ ,is given by

$$
\omega = ( 8 6 4 0 0 / \Delta ) \times 7 2 . 9 2 1 1 5 1 4 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { r a d s } ^ { - 1 } .
$$

With the redefinitionofUT1inthe IAU200O resolutions (IAU 200ld;URL[4]),itbecame necessary to provide a new definition forGMSTin terms of the ERA.Beginning on1 January 2003thenewdefinitionofGMSThasthe form

$$
\mathrm { G M S T } = \theta ( D _ { U } ) + f ( T )
$$

where $D _ { U }$ is UT1 in days from J2000.0, $f$ is a polynomial function,and $T$ is the TT time in centuries from J2000.0.Using the $P 0 3$ solution for the precession,GMST in seconds to a

precision of $0 . 1 \mu \mathrm { s }$ is (Capitaine,Wallace,and Chapront 2005)

$$
\mathrm { G M S T } ( D _ { U } , T ) = 8 6 4 0 0 ^ { 8 } ( 0 . 7 7 9 0 5 7 2 7 3 2 6 4 0 + 0 . 0 0 2 7 3 7 8 1 1 9 1 1 3 5 4 4 8 D _ { U } + D _ { U } 
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \mathrm { + 0 ^ { 5 } . 0 0 0 9 6 7 0 7 + 3 0 7 ^ { s } 4 7 7 1 0 2 2 7 T + 0 ^ { s } 0 9 2 7 7 2 1 1 3 { \cal T } ^ { 2 } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { - 2 ^ { s } . 9 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } } { \cal T } ^ { 3 } - 1 ^ { 5 } 9 9 7 0 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } { \cal T } ^ { 4 } - 2 ^ { s } . 4 5 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 } { \cal T } ^ { 5 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Note that this equation for GMST now has two time arguments.Two time arguments are required since the angle between the TIO $( \ S \ 6 . 9 )$ ，(thatis, $0 ^ { \circ }$ terrestrial longitude) and the Sun depends on both the position of the Earth in its orbit about the Sun and the rotation angle of the Earth itself.Alternatively,making use of

$$
\Delta \mathrm { T } = \mathrm { T } \mathrm { T } - \mathrm { U } \mathrm { T } 1
$$

Eq.6.64 may be givenin terms of UTl and $\Delta \mathrm { T }$ in days.To first orderin△TEq.6.64 becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { \bf G M S T } ( D _ { U } , \Delta { \mathrm { T } } ) = ( 6 7 3 1 0 ^ { 5 } 5 4 8 3 7 7 0 8 + 8 6 6 3 6 ^ { 5 } 5 5 5 3 6 7 4 0 5 2 9 2 D _ { U } } \\ & { ~ + 6 ^ { 5 } 9 5 4 0 3 7 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } D _ { U } ^ { 2 } - 6 ^ { 5 } 0 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 4 } D _ { U } ^ { 3 } } \\ & { ~ - 1 ^ { 5 } 1 2 2 1 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 2 4 } D _ { U } ^ { 4 } - 3 ^ { 5 } 7 7 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 2 } D _ { U } ^ { 5 } } \\ & { ~ + 0 ^ { 5 } 0 0 8 4 1 8 2 6 4 2 6 5 \Delta { \mathrm { T } } ) { \mathrm { m o d } } 8 6 4 0 0 ^ { 5 } . } \end{array}
$$

As of 2011 Nov.1,the value of $\Delta \mathrm { T }$ was 6654.Ignoring the linear term in $\Delta \mathrm { T }$ decreases GMSTby approximately $6 . 4 \mu \mathrm { s } .$ Aslongas $| \Delta \mathrm { T } | \ge 1 2$ days the terms higher than first order are less than $0 . 1 \ \mu s$ and thus,are insignificant.

# 6.8.5.2 Greenwich Apparent Sidereal Time (GAST)

The expression for mean sidereal time accounts for precession but not nutation. That is, GMSTdefined with respect to the mean equinox of date.An additional correction called the equation of the equinoxes, $E _ { e }$ ,(§ 6.3) can be added to GMST to obtain Greenwich Apparent Sidereal Time,GAST,the sidereal time with respect to the true equinox.Thus,

$$
\mathrm { G A S T } = \mathrm { G M S T } + E _ { e } .
$$

The GAST may also be found by subtracting a correction,the equation of the origins, $E _ { o }$ ， $( \ S \ G . 4 )$ ,fromtheERA( $\ S 6 . 8 . 4 )$

$$
\mathrm { G A S T } = \mathrm { E R A } - E _ { o } .
$$

# 6.8.6 Conversions between Universal Time and Sidereal Time

The number of seconds of sidereal time in a Julian century is obtained by differentiation of Eq.6.64

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { s _ { J C } = \displaystyle \frac { \partial G M S T } { \partial T } + \frac { \partial G M S T } { \partial T _ { U } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } = 8 6 4 0 1 8 4 . 7 9 4 4 7 8 3 + 0 . 1 8 5 5 4 4 2 2 T - 8 . 7 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 } T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } - 0 . 0 0 0 0 0 0 7 9 0 8 3 T ^ { 3 } - 1 . 2 2 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 4 } \mathrm { s } . } } \end{array}
$$

This result depends only on TT.The independence from UT1 is a direct consequence of the redefinition of sidereal time.UT1 is now strictly proportional to the Earth's rotation,so the non-constant part of the relation comes purely from the precession of the equinox,which dependspurelyon TT,notUT1.Dividing $s _ { J C }$ by the number of days ina Julian century, 36525,and adding 86400 gives $s _ { d }$ ,the number of seconds of mean sidereal time in a day of UT1:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { s _ { d } = 8 6 6 3 6 . 5 5 5 3 6 7 4 0 5 2 9 2 + 5 . 0 7 9 9 2 3 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T - 2 . 4 0 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - 2 . 1 6 5 1 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 0 } T ^ { 3 } - 3 . 3 5 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T ^ { 4 } s } } \end{array}
$$

where $T$ is the number of Julian centuries elapsed since JD2451545.0.The quantity $s$ represents the number of sidereal seconds ina day of Universal Time.Dividing Eq.6.70 by 864Oo gives the ratio of mean sidereal time to UT1:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime } = 1 . 0 0 2 \ 7 3 7 9 0 9 \ 3 4 4 9 6 8 \ 6 6 + 5 . 8 7 9 5 4 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T - 2 . 7 8 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad - 2 . 5 0 5 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 5 } T ^ { 3 } - 3 . 8 8 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 8 } T ^ { 4 } \mathrm { d } . } \end{array}
$$

The inverse of this expresson gives the ratio of UT1 to mean sidereal time:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime } } = 0 . 9 9 7 2 6 9 5 6 6 3 3 4 8 7 8 8 9 - 5 . 8 4 7 4 7 8 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T + 2 . 7 6 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 6 } T ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime } } = } + 2 . 4 9 2 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 5 } T ^ { 3 } - 3 . 8 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 8 } T ^ { 4 } \mathrm { d } ^ { - 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

Roughly speaking,UT1 can be regarded as solar time.Eq.6.72 can then be thought of as giving the length of one siderealday in units of solardays.Disregarding the secular variations, the equivalent measures of the lengths of the days are:

$$
1 { \mathrm { ~ m e a n ~ s i d e r e a l ~ d a y } } = 2 3 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 5 6 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 0 4 { \mathrm { s } } 0 9 0 5 3 1 3 { \mathrm { ~ o f } }
$$

1 day of Universal $\mathrm { T i m e } = 2 4 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \ 0 3 ^ { \mathrm { m } } \ 5 6 . 5 5 5 3 6 7 4$ of mean sidereal time.

From Eq.6.59,the mean rotational periodof the Earth is 86164.098 903 690 3511 seconds of UT1or $2 3 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 5 6 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 0 4 5 0 9 8 9 0 3 6 9 0 3 5 1 1$ .The mean rate of rotation is 15.041 067 178 670 3172 seconds of arc per second of time.

6.8.6.1Previous Conversion between Universal Time and Sidereal Time

The number of seconds of sidereal time in a Julian century used prior to the 2OO6 edition of The Astronomical Almanac was

$$
s _ { J C } = 8 6 4 0 ~ 1 8 4 . 8 1 2 ~ 8 6 6 + 0 . 1 8 6 ~ 2 0 8 ~ T _ { U } - 1 . 8 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 } ~ { T _ { U } } ^ { 2 } ~ { \mathrm { s } } .
$$

The number of seconds of mean sidereal time in a day of UT1 was:

$$
s _ { d } = 8 6 6 3 6 . 5 5 5 3 6 7 9 0 8 7 2 + 5 . 0 9 8 0 9 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T _ { U } - 5 . 0 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 0 } { T _ { U } } ^ { 2 } { \mathrm { s } } .
$$

The ratio of mean sidereal time to UT1 was:

$$
r ^ { \prime } = 1 . 0 0 2 7 3 7 9 0 9 3 5 0 7 9 5 + 5 . 9 0 0 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T _ { U } - 5 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 5 } { T _ { U } } ^ { 2 } { \bf d } .
$$

And the ratio of UT1 to mean sidereal time was:

$$
\frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime } } = 0 . 9 9 7 2 6 9 5 6 6 3 2 9 0 8 4 - 5 . 8 6 8 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 1 } T _ { U } + 5 . 9 \times 1 0 ^ { - 1 5 } { T _ { U } } ^ { 2 } { \mathrm { d } } ^ { - 1 } .
$$

These older conversions use UT1 rather than TT for their time argument.The equivalent measures of the lengths of the days was:

1 mean sidereal $\mathrm { d a y } = 2 3 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 5 6 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 0 4 5 0 9 0 5 2 4$ of Universal Time

1 day of Universal $\mathrm { T i m e } = 2 4 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 0 3 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 5 6 _ { . } ^ { \mathrm { s } } 5 5 5 3 6 7 8$ of mean sidereal time.

Prior to the 2Oo6 edition of The Astronomical Almanac,the mean rotational period of the Earth was assumed to be 86164.098 903 697 32 seconds of UT1 or

$$
2 3 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 5 6 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 0 4 _ { \cdot } ^ { \mathrm { s } } 0 9 8 9 0 3 6 9 7 3 2 .
$$

This gavearatio of a UT1day to the mean period of rotation of 1.002737 811906.The mean rate of rotation used was 15.041 067178 669 10 seconds of arc per second of time.

# 6.8.7Conversion of Local Apparent Sidereal Time to UT1

The usual procedure to express the UT1 date of an event that occurred at some instant dated inlocal apparent sidereal time,LAST,is:

1.Expressthe date in local mean sidereal time,LMST, by

$$
\mathrm { L M S T } = \mathrm { L A S T } - E _ { e } ( T )
$$

where $E _ { e }$ is the equation of the equinoxes (§ 6.3).

2.Express the date in Grenwich Mean Sidereal Time by

$$
\mathrm { G M S T } = \mathrm { L M S T } - \lambda
$$

where $\lambda$ is the astronomical longitude of the event,positive toward the east.Since the pole of rotation is moving with respect to the surface of the Earth (polar motion), the local meridian is subject to small variations.The position of the rotational pole (x,y) with respect to the CIP（ $\ S \ : 6 . 2 . 3 )$ can be used to correct for this effect.This correction of $\lambda _ { 0 }$ (referred to the CIP) to $\lambda$ is accomplished by applying the expression

$$
\lambda = \lambda _ { 0 } - ( x \sin { \lambda _ { 0 } } + y \cos { \lambda _ { 0 } } ) \tan { \phi }
$$

where $\phi$ is the astronomical latitude of the event.

3.Compute the rotational angle $\phi$ between ${ 0 ^ { \mathrm { { h } } } \mathrm { { U T 1 } } }$ and the event, expressed in mean sidereal time by

$$
\phi = \mathrm { { G M S T } - \mathrm { { G M S T } \ o f 0 ^ { h } \ U T 1 . } }
$$

4.Convert $\phi$ ,expressed as an interval of sidereal time,to an interval of UT1:

$$
\mathrm { U T } 1 = r ^ { \prime } \phi
$$

where $\boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime }$ is the ratio of mean solar time to sidereal time intervals,given in Eq. 6.71.

For instance when the hour angle, $H$ ,of a star with apparent right ascension, $\alpha$ ,hasbeen measured,the corresponding UT1 value is

$$
\mathrm { U T 1 } = ( H + \alpha - E _ { e } - \lambda - \mathrm { G M S T } \operatorname { o f } 0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { U T 1 } ) r ^ { \prime } .
$$

# 6.8.8 Local Time and Hour Angle

The local hour angle, $h$ ,of an object is the angle between two planes.The first plane contains the geocenter,the CIP(§ 6.2.3),and the observer,and the second plane contains the geocenter, the CIP,and the object.The hour angle is positive when the object is west of the observer as viewed from the geocenter,and increases with time.

Another plane of importance is the plane containing the geocenter,the CIP,and the TIO $( \ S \ 6 . 9 )$ ,as this defines the astronomical zero meridian 8.An observer's longitude,λ, on theastronomical zero meridian is O.This plane is now called the TIO meridian and isafundamental plane of the Terrestrial IntermediateReference System.The Greenwich HourAngle,GHA,of an objectis the difference between the angle describing the Earth's orientation and the celestial meridian containing the object.For equinox-based coordinates those angles are the apparent right ascension, $\alpha _ { e }$ ,and the GAST,

$$
\mathrm { G H A } = \mathrm { G A S T } - \alpha _ { e }
$$

andfor CIO-based coordinates those angles are the intermediate right ascension, $\alpha _ { i }$ ,and $\theta$

$$
{ \mathrm { G H A } } = \theta - \alpha _ { i } .
$$

The local hour angle, $h$ ,is relatedto the GHAby

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { h = \mathrm { { G H A } } + \lambda } \\ & { \quad = \mathrm { { L A S T } } - \alpha _ { e } } \\ & { \quad = \theta - \alpha _ { i } + \lambda } \\ & { \quad = \theta - E _ { o } ( T ) - \alpha _ { e } + \lambda } \end{array}
$$

where $E _ { 0 } ( T )$ is the equation of the origins $( \ S 6 . 4 )$ and $\lambda$ is the observer's longitude measured positively towards east.

Alternatively,the relationship between the Greenwich Hour Angle and the TIO may be determined usingarotation matrix

$$
{ \bf r } = { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( \mathrm { L A S T } ) { \bf r } _ { e } = { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( \theta ) { \bf r } _ { i }
$$

where $\mathbf { r } _ { e }$ is the vector pointing to the body using the true equinox of date as the origin of right ascension and $\mathbf { r } _ { i }$ is the same vector using the CIO as the origin of right ascension.The GHA is then

$$
\mathrm { G H A } = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \mathrm { r } _ { y } } { \mathrm { r } _ { x } } }
$$

where $\mathbf { r } _ { x }$ and $\mathbf { r } _ { y }$ are the $x$ and $y$ components of the vectorr. The local hour angle, $h$ ,is found byadding $\lambda$ to the angle in the rotation matrix.Very precise observations require the user to applycorrections for the effect of polar motion ( $\left( \ S 6 . 9 . 2 \right)$ and for the separation of the TIO $( \ S \ : 6 . 9 )$ from theITRForigin oflongitudes( $\lbrace \ S 6 . 2 . 2 )$

Similarly,local meansolar time,LMSoT,isrelatedtoUTby the observer’seastlongitude

$$
\mathrm { L M S o T } = U T + \lambda .
$$

# 6.9The Terrestrial Intermediate Origin (TIO)

Inthe past,the most commonly used reference meridian was the meridian of Greenwich.With the adoption ofthe IAU 20o0 resolutions (IAU 2001d;URL[4]),the reference meridian is now that of the Terrestrial Intermediate Origin (TIO).Rather than being defined geographically, the TIO is defined kinematically such that there is no component of polar motion about the pole of rotation.This position moves over the geographic surface of the Earth.However,this motion is extremely small,about $5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 }$ arcseconds $\mathrm { y r } ^ { - 1 }$ (Lambert and Bizouard 2002), equivalent to a motion of $0 . 0 1 \ \mathrm { m m \ y r ^ { - 1 } }$ at the equator.

Analogous to the CIO $( \ S 6 . 7 )$ ,theTIO is defined by successive rotations through two small angles, $x$ and $y$ ,and the TIO locator, $s ^ { \prime }$ Theangles $x$ and $y$ are the polar motion ( 6.9.2), theangular coordinates of the CIPwith respect to the terrestrial pole measured along the meridians at longitude $0 ^ { \circ }$ and $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ ( $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ W). Similar to the CIO locator,the TIO locator is determined by

$$
s ^ { \prime } = - \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } { \frac { x { \dot { y } } - y { \dot { x } } } { 1 + z } } d t
$$

where $t _ { 0 } = \mathbf { J } \mathbf { D } 2 4 5 1 5 4 5 . 0$ and

$$
z = ( 1 - x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

# 6.9.1The Ephemeris Meridian

To facilitate practical calculations of phenomena that depend upon hour angle and geographic location,the concept of an auxiliary reference meridian,known as the ephemeris meridian, was introduced.

The ephemeris meridian was originally used in conjunction with ephemeris time; however,its name has been retained for use with calculations in TT.Its position is where the TIO would be if the Earth rotated uniformly at the rate implicit in the definition of TT; i.e., $1 . 0 0 2 7 3 7 9 0 9 3 5 0 7 9 5 \Delta T$ east of the actual TIO on the surface of the Earth.

Calculations in terms of TT may be referred to the ephemeris meridian using the same methods as those done in UT,when referred to the TIO.Once a sufficiently accurate value of $\Delta T = \mathrm { T T } - \mathrm { U T }$ is known or can be predicted,the longitudes and hour angles can be referred to the TIO,and the times in TTcanbe expressedin UT.This procedureis followed, for example,in predictions of the general circumstances of eclipses.

Forexample,Eq.6.64 gives the hour angle of the equinox at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ UT,referred to the actual TIO.However, if $_ \mathrm { T }$ is expressed in TTrather than Universal Time,Eq.6.64 gives the hour angle of the equinox at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { T T }$ ,referred to the ephemeris meridian.

TheUniversal Time of transit of the Sun,Moon,ora planet across the TIO can be found by subtracting $\Delta T$ fromthe TTof TIO transit.To determine the TTof TIO transit, the tabulated time of transit across the ephemeris meridian must be interpolated from the geographic longitude of the ephemeris meridian(1.002737 909 35△T east) to longitude $0 ^ { \circ }$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/1af1753637a962d39313748ee293ed9d5e194848b176dd51c823e7f668337f55.jpg)  
Figure6.8 Schematic representation of therelationships between the different fiducial meridians encountered in the celestial and terrestrial coordinate systems

The Universal TimeofTIOtransit is givenapproximately by

$$
\mathrm { U T ~ o f ~ T I O ~ t r a n s i t } = \mathrm { T T ~ o f ~ e p h e m e r i s ~ t r a n s i t } + \frac { \Delta T } { h } \Delta \alpha
$$

where $\Delta \alpha$ is the first difference in tabular right ascension and $h$ is the tabular interval.

Hourangles,sidereal time,and the equation of time may be defined with respect to the ephemeris meridian.But to relate suchasystem toobservations from the surface of the Earth, the value of $\Delta T$ isrequired. Thus,it ismore convenient and conventional to convert TT to UTby means of $\Delta T$ and to relate hour angles,longitudes,and times to the TIO.

The speed of rotation of the ephemeris meridian is such that it makes one complete revolution of $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,relative to the mean equinox,in $2 3 ^ { \mathrm { h } } 5 6 ^ { \mathrm { m } } 0 4 5 . 0 9 8 9 0 3 6 9 7 3 2$ of TT. The ephemeris meridian coincided with the TIO at some date between 190o and 1905.Figure 6.8 shows the relationship between the ephemeris meridian and the TIO.It also shows the relationships between the local meridian,the equinox,and the true and mean Sun.

# 6.9.2 Polar Motion

Therotationof the Earth isrepresentedbythediurnal rotationaround areference axis whose motion with respect to the inertial reference frame is represented by the theories of precession and nutation. This reference axis,the $\mathrm { C I P } ( \ S \ 6 . 2 . 3 )$ ,doesnot coincidewith the axisof figure (maximum principal moment of inertia) of the Earth,but moves slowly (in a terrestrial reference frame) inaquasi-circular path around theaxis of figure.The plane of the equator is normal to the CIP,and the motion of the terrestrial reference frame with respect tothe CIP is knownaspolar motion.The CIP is the axis about which the diurnal rotation oftheEarth isapplied in the transformation between celestial and terrestrial frames,and should not be confused with the instantaneous axis of rotation.The maximum amplitude of the polar motion is typically about $0 ^ { \prime \prime } 3$ ,corresponding to a displacement of about $9 \textrm { m }$ on the surface of the Earth,and the principal periods are about 365 (annual wobble) and 428 (Chandler wobble) days.Figure 6.9 shows the motion of the pole of rotation with respect to the CIP for the period from 1973.5 through 2009.5as observed from Very Long Baseline Interferometry,V $( \ S 6 . 2 )$ ,observations.Unpredictable geophysical forces affectthe exact period and amplitude of the polar motion.Thus,the polar motion must be determined from observations.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/5ee99ee7e4dbbcdbc1d49812522a7aa878af6082df8319562ca846942fe6d5df.jpg)  
Figure 6.9 Motion of the pole with respectto theConventional International Origin (the predecessor to the CIP),measured byVLBI(8 6.2) from 1973.0 through 2009.5.The filled circlesare at yearly intervals.

The adoption of the set of coordinates for the instruments used to determine UTand polar motion from astronomical observations implicitly define the pole and TIO of the intermediate terrestrial reference system.The International Earth Rotationand Reference Systems Service publishes current and predicted values of $x$ , $y$ ,and $s ^ { \prime }$ .Valuesof $x$ and $y$ ,in seconds of arc, from197O January1onwardare tabulated in SectionKofTheAstronomicalAlmanac.Polar motion causes variations in the zenith distance and azimuth of the CIP and hence in the values of the astronomical latitude, $\phi$ ,and longitude, $\lambda$ ,thatcan be determined fromdirect astronomical observations of latitude and time.

The rigorous transformation of a vector $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 }$ with respect to the frame of the true equator and equinox of date to the corresponding vector $\mathbf { r }$ with respectto the terrestrial frame is given by the formula

$$
\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( - x ) \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - y ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \theta ) \mathbf { r } _ { 0 }
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0007_pages_0241-0280/ocr/images/caff8d3137da9a156a60529d862bb2c8c98ebdc1798570c4213e499b5dd957c4.jpg)  
Figure 6.10 (a.) The polar motion in $x$ measured by VLBI(8 6.2) from 1973.0 through 2009.5.(b.) The polar motion in $y$ measured by VLBI from 1973.0 through 2009.5.

where $\theta$ is the Earth Rotation Angle.Replacing $\theta$ with the GAST( 6.8.5.2)inEq.6.92 gives the position of this terrestrial reference frame with respect to the true equator and equinox of date.Figure 6.1O shows the observed polar motion components $x$ and $y$ from1973.5 through 2009.5.The vector $\mathbf { r }$ could represent,for example,the coordinates of a point on the Earth's surface or of a satellite inorbit around the Earth.

Alternatively,the transformationcanbe expressedasavariation of the longitude and latitude of a point,and to first order, the departures from the mean values $\lambda _ { m }$ , $\phi _ { m }$ (corresponding to vector $\mathbf { r } _ { 0 }$ )aregivenby

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \Delta \phi = x \cos \lambda _ { m } - y \sin \lambda _ { m } } \\ { \Delta \lambda _ { m } = ( x \sin \lambda _ { m } + y \cos \lambda _ { m } ) \tan \phi _ { m } . } \end{array}
$$

The variation in longitude must be taken into account in the determination of Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time,and henceofUT,fromobservations.

# 6.9.2.1Rotational Deformation due to Polar Motion

The variation of station coordinates caused by the polar tide should be taken into account in the highest precision, $\sim 1$ mas,applications.Choose a terrestrial system with reference

axes $x$ , $y$ ,and $Z$ .The $Z$ -axisisoriented along the Earth'smean rotationaxis,the $x$ -axisisin the direction of the adopted origin of longitude,and the $y$ -axis is oriented along the $9 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { ~ E ~ }$ meridian.

The centrifugal potential caused by the Earth's rotation is

$$
V = \frac 1 2 [ r ^ { 2 } | \pmb { \Omega } | ^ { 2 } - ( \mathbf r \cdot \pmb { \Omega } ) ^ { 2 } ]
$$

where $\pmb { \Omega } = \omega [ m _ { 1 } \mathbf { x } + m _ { 2 } \mathbf { y } + ( 1 + m _ { 3 } ) \mathbf { z } ]$ $\omega$ is the mean angular velocity of rotation of the Earth; $m _ { i }$ are small dimensionless parameters, $m _ { 1 }$ ， $m _ { 2 }$ describing polar motion,and $m _ { 3 }$ describing variation in the rotation rate; $\mathbf { r }$ is the vector in the coordinate system rotating with the Earth from the geocenter to the station.Thus, $r$ is the radial distance to the station.

The $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ terms (calledwobble parameters in $\ S 5 . 3 . 5 . 2 $ givea first-orderperturbation in the potential $V$ (Wahr 1985)

$$
\Delta V ( r , \Theta , \lambda ) = - \frac { \omega ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \sin 2 \Theta ( m _ { 1 } \cos \lambda + m _ { 2 } \sin \lambda )
$$

where $\Theta$ is the co-latitude,and $\lambda$ is the eastward longitude.Variation in $m _ { 3 }$ is neglected because it induces displacements that are less than a millmeter.

The radial displacement $S _ { r }$ and the horizontal displacements $S _ { \Theta }$ ，and $S _ { \lambda }$ are positive upward,south,and east,respectively,in a horizon system at the station due to $\Delta V$ using theformulation of tidal Love numbers (Munk and MacDonald 1960,pp.24-25).

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { S _ { r } = h \displaystyle \frac { \Delta V } { g } } } \\ { { S _ { \Theta } = \displaystyle \frac { l } { g } \partial _ { \Theta } \Delta V } } \\ { { S _ { \lambda } = \displaystyle \frac { l } { g \sin \Theta } \partial _ { \lambda } \Delta V } } \end{array}
$$

where $g$ isthe gravitational accelerationattheEarth's surface,and $h$ and $l$ are the second-order body tide displacement Love numbers.

In general,these computed displacements have a non-zero average overany given time span because $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ ,used to find $\Delta V$ ,havea non-zero average. Consequently,theuse of theseresultswilllead toachangein the estimated mean station coordinates.When mean coordinates produced by different users are compared at the centimeter level, it is important to ensure that this effect has been handled consistently.It is recommended that $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ used inEq.6.95be replacedbyparametersdefined tobe zero for the ITRF( $\lbrace 6 . 2 . 2 )$ .

Thus,define

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x _ { p } = m _ { 1 } - \overline { { { x } } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { y _ { p } = - m _ { 2 } - \overline { { { y } } } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\overrightharpoon { x }$ and $\overline { { y } }$ are the values of $m _ { 1 }$ and $- m _ { 2 }$ for the ITRF.Then, using $h = 0 . 6 , l = 0 . 0 8 5$ , and $r = a = 6 . 4 \times 1 0 ^ { 6 } \mathrm { m }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { S _ { r } = - 3 2 \sin 2 \Theta ( x _ { p } \cos \lambda - y _ { p } \sin \lambda ) \mathrm { m m } } \\ & { S _ { \Theta } = - 9 \sin 2 \Theta ( x _ { p } \cos \lambda - y _ { p } \sin \lambda ) \mathrm { m m } } \\ & { S _ { \lambda } = 9 \sin \Theta ( x _ { p } \sin \lambda + y _ { p } \cos \lambda ) \mathrm { m m } } \end{array}
$$


<!-- chunk 0008: pages 281-320 -->
for $x _ { p }$ and $y _ { p }$ in seconds of arc.The values $x _ { p }$ and $y _ { p }$ varyby,at most, $0 . { } ^ { \prime \prime } 8$ ,so the maximum radial displacement is approximately $2 5 ~ \mathrm { m m }$ ,and the maximum horizontal displacement is about $7 \mathrm { m m }$ .

If $X$ ， $Y$ ,and $Z$ are the Cartesian coordinates of a station in a right-hand equatorial coordinate system,the displacements of coordinatesare

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { d X } \\ { d Y } \\ { d Z } \end{array} \right] = \mathbf { R } ^ { \mathrm { T } } \left[ \begin{array} { l } { S _ { \Theta } } \\ { S _ { \lambda } } \\ { S _ { r } } \end{array} \right]
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { R } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \Theta \cos \lambda } & { \cos \Theta \sin \lambda } & { - \sin \Theta } \\ { - \sin \lambda } & { \cos \lambda } & { 0 } \\ { \sin \Theta \cos \lambda } & { \sin \Theta \sin \lambda } & { \cos \Theta } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Equation 6.1OO can be used to determine corrections to station coordinates due to polar tide.

The deformation caused by the polar tide also leads to time-dependent perturbations in the $C _ { 2 , 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 , 1 }$ geopotential coeficients.The change in the external potential caused by this deformation is $k \Delta V$ ,where Eq.6.95 gives $\Delta V$ ,and $k$ is the degree-2 potential Love number. Using $k = 0 . 3 0$ gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \overline { { C } } _ { 2 , 1 } = - 1 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 } ( x _ { p } ) } \\ { \overline { { S } } _ { 2 , 1 } = - 1 . 3 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 } ( - y _ { p } ) } \end{array}
$$

where $x _ { p }$ and $y _ { p }$ are in seconds of arc and are used instead of $m _ { 1 }$ and $- m _ { 2 }$ so that no mean value is introduced into $C _ { 2 , 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 , 1 }$ while making this correction.

Toproperly compare UT1 results obtained at different epochs,the high-frequency tidal effects should be taken into account.Thus,a standard expression for the tidal variations in UT1isrequired.The IERS model is detailed in Bizouard,Eanes,andRay(2oo4).However, to avoid confusion among possble tidal models,it is recommended that the termsδUT1, $\delta \Delta$ , and $\delta \omega$ befollowed by themodel namein parentheses,e.g.,&UT1(IERS2004).

# Positions

# INTRODUCTION

This chapter provides for the reduction of celestial coordinates from one reference system to another.The coordinates are usually rectangular equatorial coordinates or equivalently right ascension,declination and hour angle.Inparticular the transformations fromaposition inthe Barycentric Celestial Reference System (BCRS)—or equivalently the International Celestial Reference System (ICRS)—to a geocentric apparent or intermediate position are described, but some other systemsand coordinates are discussed.

This chapter first summarizes the various reference systems,places,coordinates and notation used within the chapter. Then it describes the individual corrections of space motion, parallax,light-time,aberrationand gravitational light-deflection,which are required in the transformation from the BCRS to the Geocentric Celestial Reference System(GCRS).If the requirementis for precision better than milliarcseconds (mas),when General Relativity principlesareneeded,then thisis indicatedwhereappropriate.Finally,vector-matrixalgorithms are given for calculating various positions including,apparent,intermediate and topocentric places.Also analgorithm for calculating atmosphericrefraction isincluded.

Atthe current time all these procedures conform to IAU 2oo6 resolutions 1 and 2 as well as IAU 2000 resolutions B1.6,B1.7and B1.8 (IAU 2001,2008; URL[6]).

In simple terms givenaposition of an object $\mathbf { u _ { B } }$ with respect to the BCRS,then the transformation to $\mathbf { r }$ ，aposition of date in the International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS),may be considered thus

$$
{ \bf r } = { \bf W } \ { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( - \beta ) \mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma } \ [ f \left[ g \left[ h \left[ { \bf u } _ { \bf B } \right] \right] \right]
$$

where,asappropriate to the object, $h [ 1 , g [ ]$ and $f [ ]$ are schematicand representthe functions forspace motion,parallax,aberration and light-deflection,producinga vector in the GCRS, and are discussed in detail in $\ S 7 . 2$

The transformation $\mathscr { R } _ { \Sigma }$ ,which includes rotations for frame bias,precessionand nutation, transformsposition vectors from the GCRS to the of date system defined by the celestial intermediate pole(CIP).The origin for right ascension,and thus the paradigm,is chosen via the parameter $\beta$ and is described in CapitaineandWallace (2Oo6).Thischoicewill dictate whether right ascensions are measured from the equinox or from the celestial intermediate origin (CIO). Included in $\beta$ ,and depending on the users choice of right ascension origin, will be either,Greenwich Sidereal Time (apparent or mean) or the Earth Rotation Angle, respectively.The last transformation $( \mathbf { W } )$ ,which includes polar motion,transforms from the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System to the ITRS.The explanation of the theory behind the transformations from the GCRS to the ITRS,precession, nutation,polar motionand Earth rotation are discussed in detail in Chapter 6.

Equation 7.1 expresses the process in terms of the transformation from the celestial to the terrestrial,and this ordering is followed in much of this chapter.Other explanations,for example the International EarthRotationandReferenceSystems Service(IERS)Conventions (IERS 2O1O),adopt the reverse process of transforming from the terrestrial to the celestial. Thus,readers are advised to ensure that the transformations they select for their purpose produces the position in the appropriate reference system.

# 7.1 Summary of Systems, Places,and Coordinates

In order to transform a position from one coordinate system to another it is necessary to identify the systems,the location of the observer,and the type of coordinates,which,in general terms,are all discussed in this section.Table7.1 lists some frequently used systems, while Table7.2 gives variousplace namesand the implied systems.Finally,asummaryof the notations used in this chapter is given.

Table7.1 Summary of reference systems   

<table><tr><td>No.</td><td>System</td><td>Equator/Pole</td><td>Origin on the Equator</td><td>Epoch</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>BCRS (ICRS)</td><td>ICRS equator and pole</td><td>ICRS (RA)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>GCRS</td><td>ICRS</td><td>ICRS (RA)</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>3</td><td>J2000.0</td><td>mean equator</td><td>mean equinox (RA)</td><td>J2000.0</td></tr><tr><td>4</td><td>Mean</td><td>mean equator</td><td>mean equinox (RA)</td><td>of date</td></tr><tr><td>5</td><td>True</td><td>equator/CIP</td><td>true equinox (RA)</td><td>of date</td></tr><tr><td>6</td><td>Intermediate</td><td>equator/CIP</td><td>CIO (RA)</td><td>of date</td></tr><tr><td>7</td><td>Terrestrial</td><td>equator/CIP</td><td>TIO (GHA)</td><td>of date</td></tr><tr><td>8</td><td>ITRS</td><td>geodetic equator/pole</td><td>longitude (λrrs)</td><td>of date</td></tr></table>

Table7.2 Summary of places   

<table><tr><td>Place</td><td>Location of Observer</td><td>Systema</td></tr><tr><td>Proper</td><td>geocentre</td><td>GCRS (2)</td></tr><tr><td>Local</td><td>surface of Earth</td><td>GCRS</td></tr><tr><td>Astrometric</td><td>geocentre</td><td>Hybrid</td></tr><tr><td>Mean</td><td>barycentre</td><td>mean of date (4)</td></tr><tr><td>Apparent</td><td>geocentre</td><td>true (or mean) of date (4or 5)</td></tr><tr><td>Intermediate</td><td>geocentre</td><td>intermediate (6)</td></tr><tr><td>Topocentric</td><td>surface of the Earth</td><td>either (5) or (6)</td></tr><tr><td>Geodetic</td><td>surfaceof theEarth</td><td>of date (8)</td></tr></table>

äNumbers in parenthesesrefer to Table7.1and the numbered paragraphs within this section (§ 7.1.1).

# 7.1.1Reference Systems

Table 7.1 lists some of the frequently used systems giving their designation, equator, pole and origin on the equator. This is followed bya short summary about each system.Full details about these systems can be found in other chapters.

1.Barycentric Celestial Reference System (BCRS):a system of barycentric space-time coordinates for the solar system within the framework of General Relativity.For all practical applications,the BCRS is assumed to be oriented according to the ICRS axes,the directions of which are realized by the International Celestial Reference Frame (ICRF).The ICRSis not identical to the system defined by the dynamical mean equator and equinox of J2Ooo.O,although the difference in orientation is only about $0 . { } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 2$ .

2.The Geocentric Celestial Reference System (GCRS): is a system of geocentric spacetime coordinateswithin the framework of General Relativity.The directions of the GCRS axesare obtained from those of the BCRS (ICRS)by a relativistic transformation.Positions of stars obtained from ICRS reference data,corrected for proper motion,parallax,light-bending and aberration(fora geocentric observer)are with respect to the GCRS.The same is true for planetary positions,although the corrections are somewhat different.

3.The J2Ooo.O dynamical reference system;mean equator and equinox of J2O00.0:a geocentric system where the origin of right ascension is the intersection of the mean ecliptic and equator ofJ20oo.O; the system in which theIAU 20oO precession-nutation is defined.For precise applications asmall rotation (frame bias,see Chapter 6, $\ S 6 . 5$ ） should be made to GCRS positions before precession and nutation are applied. The J2000.O system may also be barycentric,for example as the reference system for catalogues.

4.The mean system of date; mean equator and equinox of date: both the origin (geocentre,heliocentre,or barycentre) and the axes should be spcified.The latter via the date (e.g.,J2013.5) orvia the context (J2000.0,ICRS,etc.).Nutation isignored.

5.The true system of date;true equator and equinox of date:a geocentric system of date,the pole of which is the celestial intermediate pole (CIP),with the originof right ascension at the equinox on the true equator of date (intermediate equator).It is a system“between”theGCRSand the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System that separates the components labelled precession-nutation and polar motion.

6.TheCelestial Intermediate Reference System; true equator and CIO of date: the IAU recommended geocentric system of date,thepole ofwhich is thecelestial intermediate pole (CIP),with the origin of right ascension at the celestial intermediate origin (CIO)which is located on the intermediate equator (true equator of date).It is a system‘between”(intermediate) theGCRSand the Terrestrial IntermediateReference System that separates the components labelled precession-nutation and polar motion.

7.The Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System: a rotating geocentric system of date, the pole of which is the celestial intermediate pole (CIP),with the originof longitude the terrestrial intermediate origin (TIO),which is located on the intermediate equator (trueequator of date).The plane containing the geocentre,the CIP and TIO is the fundamental plane of this system and is called the TIO meridian and corresponds to the astronomical zero meridian.

8.The International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS):a geodetic system realized bythe International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF2Oo8),see Chapter5.The CIPand TIO and positionsin the Terrestrial IntermediateReference Systemdiffer frompositiosinegedeticoledee $\lambda _ { \mathrm { { r I R S } } }$ on the geodetic equator by the effects of polar motion (see Chapter 6).

It is important to note that the true equator of date,the intermediate equator,the instantaneous equator are allterms for the plane orthogonal to the direction of the CIP,which in this chapter is also referred to as the“true equator of date".It is the equator of the true system of date,and the Celestial and Terrestrial Intermediate Systems.

Note the term Greenwich meridian isan inexact term for the zero-longitude,and thus it is important,if the precision warrants it,to establish the underlying system being used.

# 7.1.2 Places

There are various“names”that are used to specify different combinations of the system, the type of position and the location of the observer.These places together with the implied systemand location of the observerare shown in Table7.2.

Proper or virtual1,local and astrometric placesare all described in $\ S \ 7 . 3$ ，although astrometricplacesare consideredto be ina hybrid system.Theremainder are discussed in $\textrm { \scriptsize 8 7 . 4 }$ Itisimportant to stress that for the highest precision it is necessary to apply theappropriate transformations and effects in the correct sequence,evaluated using the appropriate timescale.

A mean place is used to label coordinates of objects outside the solar systemat a specific date.The coordinates represent the direction of the object as it would be observed from the solar system barycentre at the specified date if the masses of the Sun and other solar system bodies were negligible.Mean places are often used in star catalogues.

The terms apparent and intermediate place are used to label positions that are “geocentric of date".These positions include corrections for space motion,parallax,aberration,and lightdeflection,as appropriate to the object.They also include effects of precession.Anapparent place thatis“mean of date”ignoresnutation.The only difference betweenapparent and intermediate place is the location of the origin of right ascension.

Atopocentric place is an apparent or an intermediate place,where the location of the observer is the surface of the Earth and not the geocentre.A topocentric right ascension may be measured from the equinox or the CIO.However,usually, it is GHAorLHA that is being calculated.The algorithm for calculating topocentric places is given in $\ S 7 . 4 . 3$

The geometric place of an object isdefined bya straight line between the centre of the Earth (or the observer) and the object at a given time,without any other corrections.In many cases it is used for clarity as it confirms that no corrections have been applied.

# 7.1.3 Coordinates

The following gives various sets ofcoordinates together with some relevant information about themand their use in this chapter.

Note: ensure that the units of all quantities used are compatible.

# 7.1.3.1 Right Ascension and Declination

Right ascension is measured in the plane of the equator from an origin on that equator. This origin of the right ascension system may be one of six different locations;ICRS origin, J2000.O origin,GCRS origin,the mean equinox,the true equinox,or the CIO.When using the equator and equinox of date system (see item 5, $\ S \ 7 . 1 . 1$ ),rightascension ismeasured from the equinox and iscalled equinox right ascension; an apparent right ascension is always an equinox right ascension.When using the Celestial Intermediate Reference System (see item 6, $\ S \ 7 . 1 . 1$ ),rightascension ismeasured from the CIOand iscalled intermediate right ascension.Right ascensionand related quantities,such as Greenwich Apparent Sidereal Time (GAST),are often expressed in time-measure $1 ^ { \mathrm { h } } = 1 5 ^ { \circ }$ ,etc.）unless the contrary is indicated.

Declination is measured north and south of the equator.Declinations,apparent or intermediate,derived using either equinox-based or CIO-based methods,respectively,are identical. Declination and related quantitiesare expressed in angular measure.

Right ascension and declination $( \alpha , \delta )$ are transformed from an equatorial rectangular position vector $\mathbf { p } = ( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ thus

$$
\alpha = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \eta } { \xi } \qquad \delta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \zeta } { \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

The quadrant of $\alpha$ is determined by the signs of $\xi$ and $\eta$ .Most computers have a double argument function forinverse tangent (e.g.,ATAN2) which willprovide the correct quadrant if the numerator and denominator are entered as separated arguments.Equation 7.2 above assumes that $\mathbf { p }$ is not necessarily a unit vector,and the distance is given by

$$
| { \bf p } | = p = \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } } .
$$

# 7.1.3.2Hour Angle and Right Ascension

The local hour angle of an object is the angle between two planes: the plane containing the geocentre,the CIP and the observer;and the plane containing the geocentre,the CIP and the object.Hour angle increases with time and is positive when the object is west of the observer as viewed from the geocentre.

The plane defining the astronomical zero("Greenwich") meridian,from which Greenwich hour angles are measured,contains the geocentre,the CIPand the TIO;there,the observer's longitude $\lambda$ (not $\lambda _ { _ { \mathrm { I T R S } } } ) = 0$ .This planeiscalled the TIO meridian and it isa fundamental plane of the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System.

Figure 7.1 shows the relationships between the“zero-longitude”defined by the TIO,and the true equinox and the CIO,i.e.,the origins of right ascension.The diagram illustrates that the origin of Greenwich Hour Angle,the TIO,may be obtained from either GASTor the Earth Rotation Angle (ERA, $\theta$ ).Thequantity $s$ ,the CIO locator,positions the GCRSorigin $( \Sigma$ )at the CIO.Note that the planes of the intermediate equator and the true equator of date (the pole ofwhich is the CIP)are identical.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/713eb8fbe3743139d6ffd2e58f98f34cea89850ac4880c071407ad0f6189f1a1.jpg)  
Figure7.1 Relationships of origins

The following relationships are used to relate right ascensions of celestial objects to locations on the Earth and Universal Time (UT1):

Equinox-based

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { G A S T } = \mathrm { G M S T } + E _ { e } } \\ & { H = \mathrm { G H A } = \mathrm { G A S T } - \alpha _ { e } \qquad H = \theta - \alpha _ { i } } \\ & { h = \mathrm { L H A } = \mathrm { G A S T } + \lambda - \alpha _ { e } \quad h = \theta + \lambda - \alpha _ { i } = \theta + \lambda - ( E _ { o } + \alpha _ { e } ) } \end{array}
$$

where $\alpha _ { e }$ and $\alpha _ { i }$ are the apparent (i.e.,equinox right ascension) and intermediate right ascension,respectively. $E _ { e }$ and $E _ { o }$ are the equation of the equinoxes and equation of the origins,respectively,and are described inChapter6, $\ S 6 . 3$ and $\ S \ 6 . 4 .$ If the equation of the equinoxes isignored,then the right ascension,i.e.,the equinox right ascension,will be with respect to the mean equinox of date (i.e.,nutation has been ignored).The longitude $\lambda$ is measured positive to the east.

Alternatively,use the rotation matrix $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 }$ to rotate the equator and equinox of date systemor the Celestial Intermediate Reference System about the $z$ -axis (CIP)to the terrestrial system, resulting in either the TIO meridian and hour angle,or the local meridian and local hour angle.

Equinox-based

equator and equinox of date Intermediate Reference System

$$
