\begin{array} { r l } { \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \mathrm { G S T } ) \mathbf { r } _ { e } \qquad } & { \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \theta ) \mathbf { r } _ { i } \qquad \mathrm { o r } } \\ { \mathbf { \eta } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \mathrm { G S T } + \lambda ) \mathbf { r } _ { e } . \qquad } & { \mathbf { \eta } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \theta + \lambda ) \mathbf { r } _ { i } . } \end{array}
$$

Then,depending on whether the Greenwich ( $H$ orlocal $( h )$ hour angle is required,

$$
H { \mathrm { ~ o r ~ } } h = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( { \frac { - \eta } { \xi } } \right) \quad { \mathrm { p o s i t i v e ~ t o ~ t h e ~ w e s t } }
$$

where ${ \bf r } = ( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ ,and objects west ofthe meridian(seting) have positive hour angles.GST is either the Greenwich Mean (GMST) or Apparent (GAST) Sidereal Time,as appropriate, and $\theta$ isthe EarthRotation Angle.Greenwich Apparent andMean Sidereal Times,equation of the equinoxes $( E _ { e } )$ ,ERA $\mathbf { \Pi } ^ { ( \theta ) }$ and equation of the origins ( $\scriptstyle { E _ { o } } )$ are tabulated daily at ${ 0 ^ { \mathrm { { h } } } \mathrm { { U T 1 } } }$ inThe Astronomical Almanac.

It is important to note that when calculating hour angles it is essential that the time argumentsused for the object'sposition and that of GASTor GMST,or ERA are for the same instant.The effectof time on the rotationof the Earth via ERA,GASTorGMSTis large,whilethemovement ofcelestial objectsisnot as great.ERAisonlyafunctionof UT1,while GASTand GMST are functions of both UT1 and TT.The time argument of ephemerides of celestial objects is usually not UT.Timescales are discussed in Chapter 3.

The relationships above,which result in a position with respect to the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System,require corrections for polar motion when the reduction of very precise observations are made with respect to a standard geodetic system such as the International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS).These small correctionsare (i) thealignment of the TIOonto thelongitudeorigin $\lambda _ { \mathrm { { r I R S } } } = 0$ )oftheITRS,and(ii)forpositioningthepole (CIP)within theITRS see $\ S 7 . 2 . 6 . 2$ and Chapter 6.

Topocentric hour angle $h ^ { \prime }$ will be calculated similarly when the position vector $\mathbf { r }$ represents a topocentric position (see $\ S 7 . 2 . 2 . 1$ and $\ S \ 7 . 4 . 3 $ .Atuppermeridian transit $h ^ { \prime } = 0$ .

# 7.1.3.3 Ecliptic Longitude and Latitude

Ecliptic latitude $\beta$ ismeasured north or south of the ecliptic.Ecliptic longitude $\lambda$ is measured in the plane of the ecliptic froman origin on the ecliptic.This origin is the equinox,either the mean equinox or the true equinox.Thus equinox right ascension and declination $( \alpha _ { e } , \delta )$ may be converted to ecliptic coordinates $( \lambda , \beta )$ and conversely,using the following relationships:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \cos \beta \cos \lambda = } & { \cos \delta \cos \alpha _ { e } } \\ { \cos \beta \sin \lambda = } & { \cos \delta \sin \alpha _ { e } \cos \epsilon + \sin \delta \sin \epsilon } \\ & { \sin \beta = - \cos \delta \sin \alpha _ { e } \sin \epsilon + \sin \delta \cos \epsilon } \\ { \cos \delta \cos \alpha _ { e } = \cos \beta \cos \lambda } \\ { \cos \delta \sin \alpha _ { e } = \cos \beta \sin \lambda \cos \epsilon - \sin \beta \sin \epsilon } \\ & { \sin \delta = \cos \beta \sin \lambda \sin \epsilon + \sin \beta \cos \epsilon . } \end{array}
$$

However,when given the intermediate right ascension and declination $( \alpha _ { i } , \delta )$ ,calculate the equinox right ascension from

$$
\alpha _ { e } = \alpha _ { i } - E _ { o }
$$

where $E _ { o }$ is the equation of the origins.The angle $\epsilon$ in Eqs.7.10 and7.11 iseither the mean or the true obliquity of the ecliptic.The mean obliquity, $\epsilon _ { A }$ ,isgiveninEq.7.72,while the true obliquity is $\epsilon = \epsilon _ { A } + \Delta \epsilon$ ,where $\Delta \epsilon$ is the nutation in obliquity (see $\ S 7 . 2 . 5$ and Chapter 6). It is worth noting that if $\lambda$ is the true longitude,then

$$
\lambda = \lambda _ { m } + \Delta \psi
$$

where $\lambda _ { m }$ is the mean longitude and $\Delta \psi$ is the nutation in longitude.

Alternatively,an equatorial rectangularposition vector $\mathbf { r } _ { i }$ referred to the Celestial IntermediateReference System,not necessarilya unit vector,maybe transformed to ecliptic coordinates by first rotating the system about the CIP by the equation of the origins $E _ { o }$ followed by the rotation of the obliquity (∈) about the $x$ -axis,thus

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { e } = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( \epsilon ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( E _ { o } ) \mathbf { r } _ { i }
$$

where $\epsilon = \epsilon _ { A }$ or $\epsilon = \epsilon _ { A } + \Delta \epsilon$ depending whether the mean or true ecliptic is required. If the initial vector (i.e., $\mathbf { r } _ { i }$ )wasreferred to the equator and equinox,then ignore the rotation ${ \bf R } _ { 3 } ( - E _ { o } ) $ The ecliptic coordinates $( \lambda , \beta )$ are then

$$
\lambda = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \eta } { \xi } \qquad \beta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \zeta } { \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } }
$$

where the vector is $\mathbf { r } _ { e } = ( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ ,and as with right ascension,the quadrant of $\lambda$ is determined by the signs of $\xi$ and $\eta$

# 7.1.3.4 Altitude and Azimuth

For an observer on the Earth at longitude $\lambda$ and latitude $\phi$ thealtitude (a)and azimuth( $( A _ { z } )$ ofacelestial object may be calculated from its local hour angle $h = H + \lambda$ (see $\ S$ 7.1.3.2) and declination (δ) using the following relationships:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \cos a \sin A _ { \mathrm { z } } = - \cos \delta \sin h } & { { } } \\ { \cos a \cos A _ { \mathrm { z } } = { } } & { { } \sin \delta \cos \phi - \cos \delta \cos h \sin \phi } \\ { \sin a = { } } & { { } \sin \delta \sin \phi + \cos \delta \cos h \cos \phi } \end{array}
$$

where azimuth $( A _ { z } )$ is measured from the north through east in the plane of the horizon, altitude $( a )$ ismeasured perpendicular to the horizon.The plane of the horizon is defined to be perpendicular to the apparent direction of gravity.The coordinates of the observer $( \lambda , \phi )$ are the astronomical values (see $\ S 7 . 4 . 3 . 2$ and $\ S 5 . 2 . 4 )$ andeast longitudeand northlatitudes are positive.Zenith distance is given by $9 0 ^ { \circ } - a$ .

Alternatively,rotate the system so that the position vectorrthatis referred to the Terrestrial IntermediateReference Systemisrelative to the observer $( \lambda , \phi )$ ,viz.,

$$
\mathbf { p } = \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( 9 0 ^ { \circ } - \phi ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \lambda ) \mathbf { r } .
$$

The first rotation is about the CIP (the $z$ -axis)bythe longitude $\lambda$ ,thus the rotated $y$ -axisis then in the plane of the meridian.Then the system isrotated by $9 0 ^ { \circ } - \phi$ about the $y$ -axis, and the altitude and azimuth are thus given by

$$
a = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \zeta } { \sqrt { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) } } } \qquad A _ { \mathrm { z } } = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \eta } { - \xi } }
$$

where the position vector $\mathbf { p } = ( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ .Practically the rotationaboutthe $Z$ -axisof $\lambda$ should be included with the rotationto the TIO(zero-longitude),thus making rrelative to the observer's meridian.The quadrant of $A _ { \mathrm { z } }$ is determined by the signs of $\xi$ and $\eta$ .Most computers have a doubleargument function for inverse tangent (e.g.,ATAN2) which will provide the correct quadrant if the numerator and denominator are entered as separated arguments.

For most purposes the values of the geodetic longitude and latitude may be used, but in some cases the effects of local gravity anomalies and polar motion must be included.For full precision,the values of $\alpha$ ,δmust be corrected for diurnal parallax and diurnal aberration, i.e., the topocentric coordinates should be used.

The inverse formulae are:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \cos \delta \sin h = - \cos a \sin A _ { \mathrm { z } } } & { { } } \\ { \cos \delta \cos h = { } } & { { } \sin a \cos \phi - \cos a \cos A _ { \mathrm { z } } \sin \phi } \\ { \sin \delta = { } } & { { } \sin a \sin \phi + \cos a \cos A _ { \mathrm { z } } \cos \phi . } \end{array}
$$

# 7.1.4Summary of Notations

This section lists much of the matrix notation used in this chapter.

# Matrices

$\mathbf { R } _ { 1 }$ ， ${ \bf R } _ { 2 } , { \bf R } _ { 3 }$ .rotation matrices $\mathbf { R } _ { n } ( \phi )$ , $n = 1$ ,2,3,where the original systemis rotated about its $x$ , $y$ ,or $z$ -axisby theangle $\phi$ ,counterclockwise as viewed from the $+ x , + y$ or $+ z$ direction,respectively(seeChapter14forinformationonmatrices).   
$\mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma }$ Matrix transformation of the GCRS to the equator and GCRS origin of date. An intermediary matrix dependent on the CIP of date (see $\ S 7 . 2 . 5 . 2$ ,Eq.7.75and $\ S 6 . 2 . 3 )$   
$\mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - \beta )$ .Matrix transformationabout theCIP to the Terrestrial IntermediateReference System,where the origin of right ascension (equinox or CIO) is designated by the choice of $\beta$ which determines the angle of rotation and thus the paradigm used.   
W.Polar motion matrix:strictly the transformation from the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference Systemto the ITRS (origin λITRs $\lambda _ { \mathrm { { _ { I T R S } } } } = 0$ ).

Matrices for Equinox-Based Techniques

B.Frame bias matrix: transformation of the GCRS to J2ooo.0 system,mean equator and equinox of J2000.0,see Chapter 6, $\ S \ G . 5$ . P.Precession matrix:transformation of the J2Ooo.O system to the mean equator and equinox of date,see Chapter 6, $\ S \ O 6 . 6$ N.Nutation matrix: transformation of the mean equator and equinox of date to the true equator and equinox of date,see Chapter 6, $\ S \ O 6 . 6$ . $\mathbf { M } = \mathbf { N P B }$ CRS to equator and equinox of date matrix: transformation of the GCRS to the true equator and equinox of date,see Chapter 6. ${ \bf R } _ { 3 } ( { \bf G A S T } )$ .Earth rotation matrix: transformation of the true equator and equinox of date totheTerrestrial Intermediate Reference System (origin the TIO). Matricesfor ClO-Based Techniques C.CRS to Intermediate matrix: transformation of the GCRS to the Celestial Intermediate Reference System (equator and CIO of date).C includes frame biasand precessionnutation; see $\ S 6 . 7$ (but note it is referred to as $\mathbf { N P B } _ { \mathrm { C I O } }$ there). $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \theta )$ .Earth rotation matrix:transformation of the Celestial Intermediate Reference Systemto the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System (TIO origin).

Some Symbolsand Other terms $E _ { e }$ ．Equation of the equinoxes. $E _ { o }$ Equation of the origins.

T.an interval of time expressed in Julian centuries of 36 525 days;usually measured fromJ2000.0,i.e.,fromJD2451545.0 TT.   
$\mathbf { r _ { e } } , \mathbf { r _ { i } }$ ：column position vectors (see Chapter14),with respect to the true equinox,and intermediate system,respectively.   
$\alpha , \delta$ ：right ascension and declination;in this case neither equinox $( \alpha _ { e } )$ orintermediate $( \alpha _ { i } )$ right ascension is specified.   
$\mu _ { \alpha } , \mu _ { \delta }$ .components of proper motionin right ascension and declination.Check the unitscarefully.Modern cataloguesusually use mas/year,where the cos $\delta$ factor has been included in $\mu _ { \alpha }$ .   
$\lambda , \beta$ .ecliptic longitudeand latitude.   
$\lambda , \phi$ .terrestrial longitude and latitude.

# 7.2 Components Used in the Reduction of Positions

This section describes,from atheoretical basis,the individual corrections of space motion, parallax,light-time,aberrationand gravitational light-deflection,which are required for the transformation from the Barycentric Celestial Reference System (BCRS) to the Geocentric Celestial Reference System (GCRS).

Thisis followed by sections on the procedures for calculating the rotations for frame bias,precession and nutation,Earth rotationand polar motion that are required to transform positions referred to the GCRS to an“of date”system.Detailed information on precession, nutationand Earth rotation is given in Chapter6,while the frame biasis discussed in $\ S \ G . 5$ and $\ S 4 . 7$ .Polarmotionisalso discussed indetail inChapter6.

Lastly there isa section on astronomical refraction.Although refractionis not included in positions published in The Astronomical Almanac,except the standard amount included for rise and set phenomena,an accurate algorithm using numerical quadrature is given.Note, however,refraction—particularly at low altitudes-is very dependent on the atmospheric conditionsat the time of the observation.

# 7.2.1 Space Motion

The motion in space of stellar objects as a function of time must be considered since star catalogs give theposition of a star at an specified epoch and referred to achosen reference system. Currently the chosen reference system is usually the ICRSwhich isaligned to the BCRS.The epoch of the star positions is usually J2ooo.O,but not always.For example the USNO CCD Astrographic Catalogue (UCAC2) positions are given for epoch J20o0.0 and are referred to the ICRS.While Hipparcos Catalogue positions,although referred to the ICRS, areforepoch 1991.25.TheFK5 andFK6 reference systemsare referred to the mean equator and equinox at J2000.0.

Traditionally stellar motion has been divided into two parts: proper motion,which occurs perpendicular to the line of sight and is given as components in right ascension $( \mu _ { \alpha } )$ and declination $( \mu _ { \delta } )$ ；andradialvelocity $\dot { r }$ ，which occurs along the line of sight.Thus the correction to position consists of the product of the star's space motion vector (r) with the interval between the required epoch and the epoch of the star's position in the catalog.It should be noted that in some catalogs the secular variations of the proper motions are also included and in such cases the mean value of the proper motion during the interval must be used.In the cases of double stars the orbital motion of the components with respect to each other may also have to beincluded.

The position vector $\mathbf { r }$ of astar at the catalog epoch $t$ is given by

$$
\mathbf { r } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { r \cos \delta \cos \alpha } \\ { r \cos \delta \sin \alpha } \\ { \qquad r \sin \delta } \end{array} \right] }
$$

where $\alpha$ , $\delta$ are the catalog right ascension and declination and $r$ the barycentric distance to the star in au,which can be computed from

$$
r = 1 / \sin p
$$

where $p$ is the parallax of the star. If $P$ is unknown,unavailable,or zero to within the accuracy ofmeasurement,setitto somesmall but finite positive number in order to avoid mathematical indeterminacy;a choice of $1 ^ { \prime \prime } \times 1 0 ^ { - 7 }$ will effectively place objects of unknown parallax at a radius of $1 0 \mathrm { { M p c } }$ .

The space motion vector $\dot { \mathbf { r } }$ of thestarmaybeobtainedbydifferentiating Eq.7.20 giving

$$
\dot { \mathbf { r } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { - \cos \delta \sin \alpha } & { - \sin \delta \cos \alpha } & { \cos \delta \cos \alpha } \\ { \cos \delta \cos \alpha } & { - \sin \delta \sin \alpha } & { \cos \delta \sin \alpha } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \delta } & { \sin \delta } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 5 s r f \mu _ { \alpha } } \\ { s r f \mu _ { \delta } } \\ { k f \dot { r } } \end{array} \right]
$$

where $\mu _ { \alpha }$ and $\mu _ { \delta }$ are the proper motions in right ascension and declination in units of time and arc,respectively,and $\dot { r }$ is the radial velocity.The factors15, $s$ and $k$ ,convert $\mu _ { \alpha }$ , $\mu _ { \delta }$ and $\dot { r }$ ,into the required units for the space motion vector and the15assumes that isin $\mu _ { \alpha }$ units of time.The factor $f$ istherelativistic Doppler effect.Thisisa simple method used bytheUSNO NOVAS software (Kaplanetal.2OO9;URL[2]) to caterfor the fact that the proper motions are observables (see below).However,in most cases $f$ may be ignored (i.e., set $f = 1 )$ .

If $\mu _ { \alpha }$ and $\mu _ { \delta }$ are in seconds of time and seconds of arc per Julian century respectively, and $\dot { r }$ is in kilometres per second,then if $\dot { \mathbf { r } }$ is required in au per day,

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l r } { s = 2 \pi / ( 3 6 0 \times 3 6 0 0 \times 3 6 5 2 5 ) } & { \quad { \mathrm { c o n v e r t s ~ f r o m ~ } } ^ { \prime \prime } / { \mathrm { c y ~ t o ~ r a d i a n s } } / { \mathrm { d a y } } } \\ { k = 8 6 4 0 0 / c \tau _ { \mathrm { A } } } & { \quad { \mathrm { c o n v e r t s ~ f r o m ~ k m / s ~ t o ~ a u / d a y } } } \\ { = 8 6 4 0 0 \times 3 6 5 2 5 / c \tau _ { \mathrm { A } } } & { \quad { \mathrm { c o n v e r t s ~ f r o m ~ k m / s ~ t o ~ a u / c y } } } \end{array} }
$$

where $c$ is the speed of light and $c \tau _ { \mathrm { A } } = 1 . 4 9 5 9 7 8 7 0 7 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ { 8 } \ \mathrm { k m }$ is the unit distance consistent with theIAU 2009 system of constants.

However,great care needs to be taken with the proper motion in right ascension,since modern catalogues tend to tabulate $\mu _ { \alpha } = 1 5 \mu _ { \alpha }$ cos $\delta$ and $\mu _ { \delta }$ ,which are the measurements (in arc units) on the celestial sphere.In this case the relationship is

$$
\dot { \mathbf { r } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { - \sin \alpha } & { - \sin \delta \cos \alpha } & { \cos \delta \cos \alpha } \\ { \cos \alpha } & { - \sin \delta \sin \alpha } & { \cos \delta \sin \alpha } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \delta } & { \sin \delta } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { s r f \mu _ { \alpha } } \\ { s r f \mu _ { \delta } } \\ { k f \dot { r } } \end{array} \right]
$$

where the variables are defined above.Catalogues also use various units for proper motions, e.g.,arcseconds per century $\mathrm { ^ { \prime \prime } / / c y ) }$ ,or milliarcseconds per year (mas/yr).Use the factor1/10 to convert from mas/yr to ${ } ^ { \prime \prime } / \mathrm { c y } .$ The radial velocity $( \dot { r } )$ is usually given in $\mathrm { k m } / s$ and measured positivelyaway from the Earth.

It is worth considering the cases in which the available data are incomplete.At present less than 2O,OoO stars have known radial velocities,and of the order of about 1Oo,Ooo stars have known parallaxes,while there are tens of millions of stars with good precision proper motions.

If the proper motion components are zero,then the star has no known tangential velocity. Also,it may be the case that the proper motion components are known but not the parallax. Thereare millions of stars in this category and typically they have magnitudes fainter than 16th magnitude.In such acase,if a“reasonable guess”parallax valueis not used,then the computed velocity components could be greater than seemsphysicallyplausible.However, thisisacomputational curiosity with nophysical meaning or practical effect on theresults of the calculation.

The radial velocity isrequired only for relatively nearby stars for which foreshortening effects (second-order changes in the apparent motion of the stardue to the shifting aspect of itsmotion) are significant.However,the radial velocity is useless in this regard (and should be set to zero) if the parallax (distance) isunknown.If the star’s radial velocity is zero or unknown,or has been set to zero because the parallax is not known,then the above space motion is tangent to the celestial sphere at the star'scatalog position $\mathbf { r }$ .

The position $\mathbf { r } _ { 1 }$ of the star at epoch $t _ { 1 }$ is then calculated from

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { 1 } = \mathbf { r } + \left( t _ { 1 } - t \right) \dot { \mathbf { r } }
$$

where $t _ { 1 } - t$ is the interval between the required epoch and the epoch of the catalog.If $\dot { \mathbf { r } }$ is inau/day,then clearly $( t _ { 1 } - t )$ must be in days.By using the space motion vector given in Eqs.7.22 or7.24,asappropriate,both proper motion and foreshortening effects have been included in $\mathbf { r } _ { 1 }$

The errors that are being made in this method of calculating space motion have been investigated by Stumpff(1985).Basically proper motion and radial velocity are observables which should be converted to inertial quantities.Thus catalog proper motions include the effect of aberration,whilst radial velocities includearelativistic doppler effect.NOVAS (Kaplan et al.2oo9;URL[2]) multiplies each component of the space motion vector (see Eqs.7.22 or7.24) by the factor

$$
f = ( 1 - \dot { r } / c ) ^ { - 1 }
$$

where $\dot { r }$ is the radial velocity and $c$ is the speed of light,both in $\mathrm { k m } / \mathrm { s }$ TheIAU-SOFA routine (URL[3]) implements an iterative approach.Fortunately these effects are very small and are only important fora few nearby stars whose apparent motions are changing rapidly across the line of sight. Stumpff (ibid.) has shown that the errors in neglecting these effects can increase progressively with time so that after several decades they may become significant at the mas level.

There is no explicit correction for light-time in the stellar apparent place computation in TheAstronomical Almanac,nor in the methodsused in this chapter;it isassumed that the position and space motion vectors implicitly include the light-time and its time derivative.

A linear approximation to a star’s motion in space,which ignores the changes in proper motion due to the changes in right ascension and declination,and ignores foreshortening and radial velocity may be made when the highest accuracy is not needed,orif the time interval $( \tau )$ is small such as in the Day Numbers method (see $\ S 7 . 4 . 5 . 3 )$ when $\tau$ isafraction of a year. Corrections are made directly to the right ascension and declination,viz.

$$
\begin{array} { c c l } { { \alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha + \tau \mu _ { \alpha } } } & { { \mathrm { o r } } } & { { \alpha = \alpha _ { 0 } + \tau \mu _ { \alpha } / \cos \delta } } \\ { { \delta _ { 1 } = \delta + \tau \mu _ { \delta } } } & { { } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

where $\tau = t _ { 1 } - t$ is expressed in the appropriate units.

# 7.2.2 Parallax

Parallax is the term used to describe the difference in the apparent direction of an object as seen fromtwo different locations.For example,the Sun,Moon and planetsare observed from the surface of the Earth,and most almanacs publish their geocentric positions.The termisusually used in connection with a shift of origin from the centre of the Earth to the surface (geocentric,a.k.a.diurnal parallax),ora shift from the barycentre or heliocentre to thegeocentre (heliocentric,a.k.a.annualparallax).

The following is a simple and rigorous method of making corrections for both diurnal and annual parallax,and it is recommended that it be used in preference to approximate methods. The position vector of a body ${ \bf u } _ { E }$ ,with respect to the originE,is given by

$$
\mathbf { u } _ { \mathrm { E } } = \mathbf { u } _ { \mathrm { B } } - \mathbf { E } _ { \mathrm { B } }
$$

where $\mathbf { E } _ { B }$ and ${ \bf u } _ { B }$ are the position vectors of the new origin and the body,respectively,with respect to the old origin and referred to the same reference frame.

Approximate methods are given for calculating diurnal and annual parallax,which are useful when the appropriate data are not available; e.g.,the distance ofthe body is not known.

# 7.2.2.1 Diurnal Parallax

Diurnal parallax,also called geocentric parallax,isashift of origin from the centre of the Earth to the surface of the Earth.Coordinates with an origin at the surface of the Earth are called topocentric coordinates,and depend on the position of the observer. Using Eq.7.28, the topocentric rectangular equatorial coordinates of a body $\mathbf { r } ^ { \prime }$ ,are given by

$$
\mathbf { r } ^ { \prime } = \mathbf { r } - \mathbf { g } ( t )
$$

where r and ${ \bf g } ( t )$ are the geocentric position vectorsofthe body and the observer,respectively, referred to the same reference system.In terms of the $( \alpha , \delta )$ of the body,and ignoring polar motion gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x ^ { \prime } = r ^ { \prime } \cos \delta ^ { \prime } \cos \alpha ^ { \prime } = r \cos \delta \cos \alpha - \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos ( \beta + \lambda ) } } \\ { { y ^ { \prime } = r ^ { \prime } \cos \delta ^ { \prime } \sin \alpha ^ { \prime } = r \cos \delta \sin \alpha - \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin ( \beta + \lambda ) } } \\ { { z ^ { \prime } = r ^ { \prime } \sin \delta ^ { \prime } \qquad = r \sin \delta \qquad - \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } } \end{array}
$$

where the geocentric position of the observer ${ \bf g } ( t )$ is given here in terms of $\rho$ ,the distance of the observer from the centre of the Earth (in the same units as $r$ ),and the geocentric latitude $\phi ^ { \prime }$ and the longitude $\lambda$ (east longitudes are positive).If the origin of the right ascension system is the CIO then, $\beta = \theta$ is theEarthRotationAngle.On the other hand,if the body is referred to the equinox of date,then $\beta = \mathrm { G A S T }$ or $\beta = \mathrm { G M S T }$ ,where GASTisusedwith the true equinox of date and GMST with the mean equinox of date.

Section 7.4.3.2 gives the formulae for the position of the observer in theITRS,which is an Earth-fixed (of date) reference system,while $\ S 7 . 4 . 3 . 5$ gives the geocentric position of the observerin the standard reference systemof theICRS.

# 7.2.2.2Approximate Diurnal Parallax

Approximations to Eq.7.3O may be made in various ways.For objects relatively close to the Earth,such as the Moon,whose parallax is significant,it can be assumed that the Earth is spherical by setting $\rho = 1$ Earth radii.For the more distant bodies such as the Sun,the planets, orcomets,whose parallax amounts to only a few seconds of arc,itcan be assumed that the geocentric and topocentric distance are equal, i.e., $r ^ { \prime } = r .$ Thus for such bodies the topocentric right ascension and declination, $( \alpha ^ { \prime } , \delta ^ { \prime } )$ ,may be approximated using the geocentric position $( \alpha , \delta )$ ,tosufficient accuracy by the first-order approximation

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \alpha ^ { \prime } = \alpha - \pi \ { \frac { \rho } { a } } \ \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin h \sec \delta } } \\ { { \delta ^ { \prime } = \delta - \pi \ { \frac { \rho } { a } } ( \sin \phi ^ { \prime } \cos \delta - \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos h \sin \delta ) } } \end{array}
$$

where $\pi$ is the equatorial horizontal parallax, $a$ the equatorial radius of the Earth, $\rho$ the geocentric distance of the observer and $h$ the geocentric hour angle.

The solar parallax is given by

$$
\sin \pi _ { \odot } = a / A \quad { \mathrm { a n d } } \quad \pi _ { \odot } = 8 . 7 9 4 1 4 3
$$

where appropriate values of $^ { a }$ and $A$ (the unit distance）are given in Section $\mathrm { K }$ ofThe Astronomical Almanac.

The equatorial horizontal parallax (horizontal parallax)of any object may be calculated from

$$
\pi = \pi _ { \odot } / \Delta
$$

where $\Delta$ is the geocentric distance of the object in au.

In preliminary work on comets and minor planets，where the geocentric distance is unknown,itisconvenient tocalculateparallax factors $p _ { \alpha }$ and $p _ { \delta }$ for each observation; these maybe used,once the geocentric distancesare determined,to give the parallax corrections in the form

$$
\Delta \alpha = p _ { \alpha } / \Delta \qquad \Delta \delta = p _ { \delta } / \Delta
$$

where $\Delta \alpha = \alpha - \alpha ^ { \prime }$ and $\Delta \delta = \delta - \delta ^ { \prime }$ The parallax factors are calculated from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { p _ { \alpha } = 8 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 9 4 1 4 3 \rho \ \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin h \sec \delta = 0 ^ { 5 } . 5 8 6 2 7 6 2 \rho \ \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin h \sec \delta } \\ & { p _ { \delta } = 8 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 9 4 1 4 3 \rho ( \sin \phi ^ { \prime } \cos \delta - \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos h \sin \delta ) } \end{array}
$$

where $\rho$ is expressed in au.

The parallaxcorrection foraltitude,called parallax in altitude,is given approximately by $\pi$ cos $a ^ { \prime }$ ,where $a ^ { \prime }$ is thetopocentric altitude; it is added to the topocentric altitude to form the geocentric altitude to low precision (about $0 . 1$ );there is no correction for azimuth $( Z )$

This approximation does not allow for the oblateness of the Earth,which,for the Moon,can produce an error of up to $_ { 0 . 2 }$ .For an observer at latitude $\phi$ the oblateness of the Earth can be corrected forby adding the next term of the expansion which is

$$
- \pi f \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \cos a ^ { \prime } + \pi f \sin 2 \phi \cos Z \sin a ^ { \prime }
$$

where $Z$ is the azimuth and $f$ is the flattening (see $\ S 7 . 4 . 3 . 2$

# 7.2.2.3Approximate Annual Parallax

The reduction for annual parallax from the barycentric place $( \alpha , \delta )$ to the geocentric place $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \delta _ { 1 } )$ is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha + \pi ( X \sin \alpha - Y \cos \alpha ) / ( 1 5 \cos \delta ) } \\ & { \delta _ { 1 } = \delta + \pi ( X \cos \alpha \sin \delta + Y \sin \alpha \sin \delta - Z \cos \delta ) } \end{array}
$$

where $\pi$ is the parallax in arcseconds, $X$ ,Y, $Z$ are the barycentric coordinates of the Earth inau,and the right ascension is expressed in units of time.These expressions are used in the Day Number methods (se $\mathrm { ~ e ~ } \ S \ 7 . 4 . 5 . 3 )$ ..

The times of reception of periodic phenomena,such as pulsar signals,may be reduced to a common origin at the barycentre by adding the light-time corresponding to the component of theEarth's position vector along the directionof theobject,that isbyadding to the observed times( $X$ cos $\alpha$ cos $\delta + Y$ sin $\alpha$ cos $\delta + Z \sin \delta ) / c$ ,where $c = 1 7 3 . 1 4$ au/day,and the lighttime for 1au is $1 / c = 0 . 0 0 5 7 7 5 5$ .

# 7.2.3 Aberration

The velocity of light is finite,and so the apparent direction of a moving celestial object from amoving observer is not the same as the geometric direction of the object from the observer atthe same instant.This displacement of the apparent position from the geometric position may beattributed in part to the motionof the object,and in part to the motionof the observer, these motions being referred to an inertial frame of reference.The former part,independent ofthe motion of the observer,may be considered to bea correction for light-time and is termed light-time displacement;the latter part,independent of the motionor distance of the object,isreferred toas stellaraberration,since forthe stars (see $\ S 7 . 2 . 1 \_$ )the normal practice is to ignore light-time displacement.The sum of the two partsiscalled planetary aberration, since it is applicable to planets and other members of the solar system.

# 7.2.3.1 Light-Time

InFig.7.2,Eisastationary observer at time $t$ ,and $\mathrm { P }$ is the position of a celestial object also at time $t$ .The dotted curve represents the orbit ofP.The light which is received at $\mathbf { E }$ at time $t$ was emitted by the celestial object when it wasat $\mathbf { P } ^ { \prime }$ at time $( t - \tau )$ where $\tau$ is the light-time; i.e.,the time taken forthelightto travel from $\mathbf { P ^ { \prime } }$ to $\mathrm { E }$ .

Light-time iscalculated iteratively,witha first approximation $\tau _ { 1 }$ calculated from the geometric distance between the object and the observer at time $t$ ,and the next approximation $\tau _ { 2 }$ calculated fromthe distance between $\mathrm { \bf P } ^ { \prime }$ at $( t - \tau _ { 1 } )$ and the observerat timet.Noallowance has been made here forthe relativistic delay causedby the Sun’s gravitational field.However, theapparent and intermediate place algorithm includes this effect (see $\ S 7 . 4 . 1 . 3$ ),whichisof the order of1microarcsecond.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/0a93f169d74262615a7609eb47d7e33e8827cea03d2ddd911658f5d8a6fcfa09.jpg)  
Figure7.2 Light-time displacement

# 7.2.3.2 Stellar Aberration

In general,the observeratEwill be movingwith a velocity $\mathbf { V }$ .Theapparent change in the geometric directionof thecelestial object at $\mathrm { \bf P } ^ { \prime }$ dueto themotion of the observeriscalled stellaraberration.Themotionofan observeronthe Earth is theresultant of diurnal rotation of the Earth,the orbital motion of the Earth about the centre of mass of the solar system,and the motion of this centre of mass in space.The stellar aberration is,therefore,made up of threecomponents,whicharereferredtoasdiurnal aberration,annual aberrationandsecular aberration.The stars and the centre of mass of the solar system may each be considered to bein uniform rectilinear motion;in this case the correction for light-time and the secular aberration are indistinguishable,and the aberrational displacement due to the relative motion is merely equal to the proper motionof the star multipliedbythe light-time;itisconstant for eachstar,and ingeneral,isnotknown,andisthereforeignored(see $\ S 7 . 2 . 1$ on space motion forfurther discussion).The term“stellar aberration”is sometimes loosely used in contexts where“annual aberration”should strictly be used.

InFig.7.3, $\mathbf { p }$ is aunit vector in the geometric direction $\mathrm { E P ^ { \prime } }$ ,i.e.,inthedirectionof the body at time $t$ allowing forlight-time (but ignoring the effect of light-deflection).The observer is moving witha velocity $\mathbf { V }$ relative to thestationary frame,and at time $t$ will observe the body at $\mathbf { P } ^ { \prime \prime }$ in the direction $\mathbf { p } _ { 1 }$ ,where $\mathrm { P ^ { \prime } E B } = \theta$ is the angle between the direction of motion and $\mathbf { p }$ in the stationary frame,and $\mathrm { P } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { E } \mathrm { P } ^ { \prime \prime } = \Delta \theta$ is the displacement due to aberration in the moving frame,whichisalwaystowardsthedirectionofmotion.

The classical Newtonian expression for the direction of the source as seen by the moving observer is obtained by vector addition of velocitiesas follows:

$$
\mathbf { p } _ { 1 } = { \frac { \mathbf { p } + \mathbf { V } / c } { | \mathbf { p } + \mathbf { V } / c | } } .
$$

Taking the scalar part of the vector cross product of $\mathbf { p }$ with Eq.7.38, then

$$
\sin \Delta \theta = { \frac { ( V / c ) \sin \theta } { \sqrt { ( 1 + 2 ( V / c ) + ( V / c ) ^ { 2 } ) } } } = { \frac { V } { c } } \sin \theta - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( { \frac { V } { c } } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/aa609efe249c443fba9ff7dffaeddd46940deecd6b63949d470c0d04d88c0730.jpg)  
Figure7.3 Stellar aberration

since

$$
| \mathbf { p } \wedge \mathbf { p } _ { 1 } | = \sin \Delta \theta , | \mathbf { p } \wedge \mathbf { p } | = 0 , \mathrm { a n d } | \mathbf { p } \wedge \mathbf { V } / c | = { \frac { V } { c } } \sin \theta .
$$

The term of order $V / c$ is about 0.0001or $2 0 ^ { \prime \prime }$ ; the term $( V / c ) ^ { 2 }$ has a maximum value of about $0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 1$

In special relativity,the velocity of light is constant in the moving and stationary frame, and the Lorentz formula for the addition of velocitiesapplies.Hence

$$
\mathbf { p } _ { 1 } = \frac { \beta ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { p } + ( \mathbf { V } / c ) + ( \mathbf { p } \cdot \mathbf { V } / c ) ( \mathbf { V } / c ) / ( 1 + \beta ^ { - 1 } ) } { 1 + \mathbf { p } \cdot \mathbf { V } / c }
$$

where $\beta ^ { - 1 } = \sqrt { 1 - ( V / c ) ^ { 2 } }$ ,again taking the modulus of the vector cross product of Eq.7.40 with $\mathbf { p }$ ,then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \sin \Delta \theta = \frac { ( V / c ) \sin \theta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( V / c ) ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta / ( 1 + \beta ^ { - 1 } ) } { 1 + ( V / c ) \cos \theta } } \\ { = \displaystyle \frac { V } { c } \sin \theta - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( \frac { V } { c } \right) ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \theta + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

which shows that special relativistic aberration and classical Newtonian aberration agree to order $V / c$ (mas precision).However, it is recommended that special relativistic aberration (Eq.7.4O) be used,particularlywhen high precisionisrequired (see also apparent and intermediate place algorithm, $\ S 7 . 4 . 1 . 3$ and $\ S 7 . 4 . 1 . 5 )$ .

# 7.2.3.3 Classical Annual Aberration

Inaccordance with recommendations of the IAU(1952) the annual aberration is calculated as from196O from the actual motion of the Earth,referred to an inertial frame of reference and to the centre of mass of the solar system.The resulting aberrational displacement $\Delta \theta$ maybe resolved into corrections to the directional coordinates by standard methods.If,for example ${ \dot { X } } , { \dot { Y } }$ and $\dot { Z }$ arethe components of the Earth's velocity parallel to equatorial rectangularaxes, thecorrections to right ascension and declination,referred to the same equator and origin of right ascension,in the sense “apparent place minus mean place”are,to second order in $V / c$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \cos \delta \Delta \alpha = - \frac { \dot { X } } { c } \sin \alpha + \frac { \dot { Y } } { c } \cos \alpha } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 2 } } ( \dot { X } \sin \alpha - \dot { Y } \cos \alpha ) ( \dot { X } \cos \alpha + \dot { Y } \sin \alpha ) \sec \delta + \cdot \cdot \cos \alpha } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \Delta \delta = - \frac { \dot { X } } { c } \cos \alpha \sin \delta - \frac { \dot { Y } } { c } \sin \alpha \sin \delta + \frac { \dot { Z } } { c } \cos \delta } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad - \frac { 1 } { 2 c ^ { 2 } } ( \dot { X } \sin \alpha - \dot { Y } \cos \alpha ) ^ { 2 } \tan \delta } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \frac { 1 } { c ^ { 2 } } ( \dot { X } \cos \delta \cos \alpha + \dot { Y } \cos \delta \sin \alpha + \dot { Z } \sin \delta ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \times ( \dot { X } \sin \delta \cos \alpha + \dot { Y } \sin \delta \sin \alpha - \dot { Z } \cos \delta ) + \cdots } \end{array}
$$

These equations are usually used to first order in $( V / c )$ ,and they were also used in the Day Numbers methods (see $\ S 7 . 4 . 5 . 3 $ .

To a lower precision it is possible to use the expressions

$$
\dot { X } = + 0 . 0 1 7 2 \sin \lambda \qquad \dot { Y } = - 0 . 0 1 5 8 \cos \lambda \qquad \dot { Z } = - 0 . 0 0 6 8 \cos \lambda
$$

forthe barycentric velocity of the Earth with respect to the mean equator and equinox of J2000.0,where $\lambda$ is the apparent longitude of the Sun.

Also to first order in $V / c$ ,we obtain for the aberration in right ascension and declination due to the unperturbed elliptic component of the orbital motion of the Earth with respect to the Sun,obtained asa correction to be applied to the geometric place $\alpha$ , $\delta$ in order to obtain theapparent place $\alpha _ { 1 } , \delta _ { 1 }$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha = - ( \kappa \sin \odot + \kappa e \sin \Pi ) \sin \alpha \sec \delta } \\ { \qquad - ( \kappa \cos \odot \cos \epsilon + \kappa e \cos \Pi \cos \epsilon ) \cos \alpha \sec \delta } \\ { \qquad \delta _ { 1 } - \delta = - ( \kappa \sin \odot + \kappa e \sin \Pi ) \cos \alpha \sin \delta } \\ { \qquad - ( \kappa \cos \odot \cos \epsilon + \kappa e \cos \Pi \cos \epsilon ) ( \tan \epsilon \cos \delta - \sin \alpha \sin \delta ) } \end{array}
$$

where $\odot$ is the true geometric longitude of the Sun, $e$ ,Iare the eccentricity and longitude of perigee of the solar orbit, $\epsilon$ is the mean obliquity of the ecliptic and $\kappa$ is the constant of aberration (see Eq.7.46).The second term in each factor inEq.7.45depends explicitly on the eccentricity and represents the components of the displacement due to the departure of the elliptic orbital motion from a circle.The component of the aberration that depends on $e$ is known as elliptic aberration,and sometimes referred to as the $E$ -terms.

Theconstant of aberration $\kappa$ ,isthe ratio of themean orbital speed of theEarth to the speed of light, where perturbations and the motion of the Sun relative to the barycentre are neglected,and itisderivedfrom,

$$
\kappa = n a / ( c \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } )
$$

Table7.3 Coefficients (nma/c) for the planets   

<table><tr><td></td><td>Venus0&quot;0001</td><td></td><td>Jupiter0&quot;0086</td><td>Uranus </td><td>0&quot;0002</td></tr><tr><td>Earth</td><td>0.0001</td><td></td><td>Saturn 0&quot;0019</td><td>Neptune 0.0002</td><td></td></tr></table>

where $c$ is the speed of light, $^ a$ is the mean distance of the Earth from the Sun, $n$ is the mean motion and $e$ is the eccentricity of the orbit.

The annual aberration due to the barycentric motion of the Sun or to the action of any particular planet may be obtained,when the ecliptic latitude of the planet is neglected,from

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha = - \frac { n m a } { c } ( \sin \alpha \sin l + \cos \epsilon \cos \alpha \cos l ) \mathrm { s e c } \delta } \\ { \displaystyle \delta _ { 1 } - \delta = - \frac { n m a } { c } ( \cos \alpha \sin \delta \sin l + \cos l ( \sin \epsilon \cos \delta - \cos \epsilon \sin \alpha \sin \delta ) ) } \end{array}
$$

where $l$ is the heliocentric ecliptic longitude of the planet, $m$ is the ratio of mass of the planet to Sun, $a$ is the mean distance,and $n$ is the mean motion.The coefficients to be used are given in Table7.3.

The Sun's aberrationin longitude,assuming unperturbed elliptical motion,canbe given as

$$
\Delta \lambda = - \kappa \ \sec \beta ( 1 + \epsilon \cos v ) = \kappa a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) / R
$$

where $\kappa$ is the constant of aberration; $a , e$ and $v$ are the semimajor axis,eccentricity and true anomaly of the Earth'sorbit；and $\beta$ and $R$ are the Sun's latitude and true distance.The $e ^ { 2 }$ termaffects only the fifth significant figure; so until the constant of aberration was specified to five significant figuresand thelongitude of the Sun was given to three decimal figures,this term was ignored.

Measurements of radial velocity may be reduced to a common origin at the barycentre by adding thecomponentoftheEarth's velocityinthe directionofthe object,that is,by adding

$$
\dot { X } \cos \alpha \cos \delta + \dot { Y } \sin \alpha \cos \delta + \dot { Z } \sin \delta .
$$

# 7.2.3.4Diurnal Aberration

Therotation of the Earth on its axis carries the observer towards the east with a velocity $\omega \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime }$ ,where $\omega$ is the equatorial angular velocity of the Earth,(the nominal mean value of $\omega$ is $7 . 2 9 2 1 1 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ radians per second; if $^ { a }$ is the equatorialradius,then $a \omega = 0 . 4 6 5 \mathrm { k m / s }$ is the equatorial rotational velocity of the surface of the Earth),and $\rho$ and $\phi ^ { \prime }$ are the geocentric distance and latitude of the observer,respectively.The corresponding constant of diurnal aberration is

$$
{ \frac { a \omega } { c } } { \frac { \rho } { a } } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } = 0 \sp { \prime \prime } 3 2 0 0 { \frac { \rho } { a } } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } = 0 \sp { \mathrm { s } } 0 2 1 3 3 \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } .
$$

The aberrational displacement may be resolved into corrections (apparent-mean) in right ascension and declination:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Delta \alpha = 0 ; 0 2 1 3 3 \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos h \sec \delta } } \\ { { \qquad \Delta \delta = 0 ; 7 3 2 0 0 \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin h \sin \delta } } \end{array}
$$

where $h$ isthe hour angle.The effect issmall but is of importance in meridian observations. Fora star at transit, $h = 0 ^ { \circ }$ or $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,so $\Delta \delta$ is zero, but

$$
\Delta \alpha = \pm 0 \displaystyle \mathrm { 5 0 } 2 1 \displaystyle 3 3 \displaystyle \frac { \rho } { a } \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sec \delta
$$

where the plus and minus signs are used for upper and lower transits,respectively;this may be regarded as a correction to the time of transit.

Alternatively,the effect may be computed in rectangular coordinates using the following expression for the geocentric velocity $( \dot { \bf g } ( t ) )$ vector of the observer,ignoring polar motion, with respect to the celestial equatorial reference system of date:

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { \mathbf { g } } ( t ) = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - \dot { \beta } \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin ( \beta + \lambda ) } \\ { \dot { \beta } \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos ( \beta + \lambda ) } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] } \end{array}
$$

where $\beta$ iseither the Greenwich Sidereal Time (mean or apparent) or the Earth Rotation Angle,as appropriate,and $\lambda$ is the longitude (east longitudes are positive). The geocentric velocity vector of the observer is added to the barycentric velocity of the Earth'scentre,to obtain the corresponding barycentric velocity vector of the observer ( $\sec \ S \ 7 . 4 . 3 . 3 - 7 . 4 . 3 . 5$ .

# 7.2.3.5Planetary Aberration

Planetary aberration is the apparent displacement of the observed position of acelestial body producedby both the motion of the body and the motion of the Earth.It is often calculated by adding the correction for stellar aberration $\lbrace \lbrace \lbrace . 7 . 2 . 3 . 2 )$ to the geometric position corrected forlight-time (g7.2.3.1).On the other hand it may be calculated directly,eitherby using the barycentric positions of the bodyand the Earth at time $( t - \tau )$ ,orbyusing the barycentric positions and velocitiesof the body and the Earth at time $t$ as described below.However, although these latter two methodsare simple and widelyused,the first method may be preferred since it brings out the principle that aberration depends upon the relative velocity of observer and object.

If we denote the barycentric position of the Earth at time $t$ by ${ \bf E } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t )$ and the barycentric position of the planet at time $t$ by ${ \bf u } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t )$ ,then the geometric geocentric position of the planet at time $t$ ,allowingforlight-time,isgiven by

$$
{ \bf P } ( t ) = { \bf u } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t - \tau ) - { \bf E } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t )
$$

where $\tau = P / c$ is the light-time and $P = | \mathbf { P } |$ .If $\mathbf { P } _ { 1 }$ is the geocentric position of the planet allowing for planetary aberration,then using classical stellar aberration (Eq.7.38),we get

$$
\mathbf { P } _ { 1 } = \mathbf { P } + \tau \dot { \mathbf { E } } _ { \mathrm { B } } .
$$

If we assume that the velocity of the Earth $\dot { \bf E } _ { \mathrm { B } }$ and the velocity of the planet $\dot { \bf u } _ { \mathrm { B } }$ are constant during the light-time,thenEq.7.54 maybe written as

$$
{ \bf P } _ { 1 } = { \bf u } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t - \tau ) - { \bf E } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t - \tau )
$$

or

$$
\mathbf { P } _ { 1 } = \mathbf { u } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t ) - \mathbf { E } _ { \mathrm { B } } ( t ) - \tau ( \dot { \mathbf { u } } _ { \mathrm { B } } - \dot { \mathbf { E } } _ { \mathrm { B } } ) .
$$

Since Newtonian aberration and special relativistic aberration agree to order $V / c$ ,it is only when mas precision is required that it becomes necessary to use a rigorous formula (see $\ S 7 . 2 . 3 . 2 $

# 7.2.3.6Differential Annual Aberration

The differential coordinates of an object with respect to a“fixed”star will be affected by differential aberration;if $\Delta \alpha$ , $\Delta \delta$ are the observed differences of the coordinates in the sense objectminusstar, then the corrections fordifferential annual aberration are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta ( \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha ) = ( - \dot { X } \cos \alpha - \dot { Y } \sin \alpha ) \sec \delta \Delta \alpha + ( - \dot { X } \sin \alpha + \dot { Y } \cos \alpha ) \sec \delta \tan \delta \Delta \delta } \\ & { \qquad \Delta ( \delta _ { 1 } - \delta ) = - ( - \dot { X } \sin \alpha + \dot { Y } \cos \alpha ) \sin \delta \Delta \alpha + ( - \dot { X } \cos \alpha - \dot { Y } \sin \alpha ) \cos \delta \Delta \delta \ ( 7 . 5 \cos \alpha - \dot { Y } \cos \alpha ) } \\ & { \qquad - \dot { Y } \tan \epsilon _ { 0 } \sin \delta \Delta \delta } \end{array}
$$

where $\dot { X }$ and $\dot { Y }$ are the Earth's barycentric velocity components with respect to the mean equinox of date and the units of each term are consistent. (Note: $+ \dot { Y }$ and $- \dot { X }$ are the defunct $c$ and $_ { D }$ BesselianDay Numbers,respectively.) The corrections shouldbe applied with those for differential precession and nutation to give mean positions referred to the same equator and origin of right ascension as those of the stars.

Alternatively the corrections for differentialannual aberrationto beadded tothe observed differences (in the sense moving object minus star)of right ascension and declination to give true differences are:

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { \mathrm { i n ~ r i g h t ~ a s c e n s i o n } } } & { { a \Delta \alpha + b \Delta \delta } } & { { \mathrm { i n ~ u n i t s ~ o f ~ } 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \mathrm { s } } 0 0 1 } } \\ { { \mathrm { i n ~ d e c l i n a t i o n } } } & { { c \Delta \alpha + d \Delta \delta } } & { { \mathrm { i n ~ u n i t s ~ o f ~ } 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 1 } } \end{array}
$$

where $\Delta \alpha$ , $\Delta \delta$ are the observed differences in units of $1 ^ { \mathrm { m } }$ and $1 ^ { \prime }$ respectively,and where $^ a$ $b , c , d$ are coefficients defined by:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a = - 5 . 7 0 1 \cos ( H + \alpha ) \sec \delta } } \\ { { b = - 0 . 3 8 0 \sin ( H + \alpha ) \sec \delta \tan \delta } } \\ { { c = + 8 . 5 5 2 \sin ( H + \alpha ) \sin \delta } } \\ { { d = - 0 . 5 7 0 \cos ( H + \alpha ) \cos \delta } } \\ { { H ^ { \mathrm { h } } = 2 3 . 4 - \mathrm { d a y ~ o f ~ y e a r } / 1 5 . 2 . } } \end{array}
$$

# 7.2.3.7Diferential Planetary Aberration

Aberration,because of itsdependence on therelative motionsanddistances,sometimes has complex effects where two or more bodies areinvolved,as,e.g.,in eclipses,transits,and the phenomena of satellite systems;and on some past occasions,the determination of these effects has presented an intricate problem.

Inatransit of a planet across the disk of the Sun,e.g.,the external contacts occur when the observer is on the conical surface that circumscribes the Sun and the planet and has its vertex between the Sun and the planet.The internal contacts occur when the observer is on the cone circumscribing the planet and the Sun having its vertex between the planet and the Earth.The observed contacts are at the instants when the apparent positions of a point on the limb of the planet and a point on the limb of the Sun are the same,i.e.,the ray of light from the Sun that reaches the geometric position of the observer at the instant $T$ of contact hasgrazed theplanet on theway.Thisrayleft the Sunataprevious time $T - \tau _ { 2 }$ and reached the planet at time $( T - \tau _ { 2 } ) + \tau _ { 1 }$ .The circumscribing cones are formed by the grazing rays; hence,the points on the Earth and the planet that lie in the same straight line on one of the cones at the instant of a contact are the geometric position of the observer at the time $T$ , and the geometricposition of the point on the planet at time $T - \tau _ { 2 } + \tau _ { 1 }$ .Therefore,in the formulae of the theory of transits,for any value of the time $T$ ,all quantities depending on the time must be derived from the values of the geometric coordinates $( r , l , b )$ of the planet at $T - \tau _ { 2 } + \tau _ { 1 }$ and the geometric coordinates $( \boldsymbol r ^ { \prime } , l ^ { \prime } , b ^ { \prime } )$ of the Earth at $T$

Similarly,in comparing observed positions of objects in the solar system with one another or with reference stars,in order to determine the coordinates of abody,great care is required in correcting the observations for aberration,according to the means of observation used,and the method of comparison.

In eclipsing binary systems,an apparent variation of the period may be produced by the variation in light-time with changing distance from the observer due to an orbital motion of the eclipsing pair with respect to a distant third component (Irwin 1959).

# 7.2.4 Gravitational Light-Deflection

Gravitational light-deflection was predictedby Einstein and first confirmed photographically atthe eclipse of 1919 May by expeditions from Greenwich and Cambridge,England(Dyson, Eddington and Davidson 192O).It has been measured many times and most recent high precision measurements have been made with radio interferometers,which can observe sourcesvery close to the Sun.Fomalont and Sramek(1975) have confirmed the deflection predicted by Einstein's theory withanaccuracy of $1 \%$ ,which isan order of magnitude more accurate than that achieved optically from eclipse observations.The deflection increases the closer the light path is to the Sun;ataheliocentric elongation of $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,however,thedeflection hasdecreased to $0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 4$ .

The algorithm for the deflection of light is that of Yallop (1984)as given in The Astronomical Almanac 1984 (Nautical Almanac Offices1983),which isanadaption of Murray's (1981) formulae.The isotropic metric has been assumed. Only the Sun's gravitational field has been included;each of the planets causesasimilar effect that is smaller byafactor equal to the ratio of the planet's mass to that of the Sun (1/1O47 for Jupiter).The gravitational field of the Earth,also ignored here,candeflect lightbyafew tenths ofamilliarcsecond for ground-based observers.Itmayalso have to beallowed for in precise astrometry with Earth satellites.

InFig.7.4,Sisthe Sun,Pthe body thatisbeing observed,andEistheEarth.Theunit vectors e and $\mathbf { q }$ represent the heliocentric directions of the Earth and the body,respectively. The heliocentric elongation of the Earth from $\mathrm { P }$ is $\psi$ ,where cos $\psi = { \bf q } \cdot { \bf e }$ .Thegeocentric direction to the bodyPwhen the light left it is givenby the unit vectorp.The dotted arc AEB represents the light-pathasitpassestheEarth.The tangent tothe light-pathat $\mathrm { E }$ isXEY. As the light,which was emitted atP,travels along thepath AB,it isalways deflected towards the Sun.At E the direction between $\mathbf { p }$ and the tangent to the light-path is $\Delta \phi$ (as shown in thefigure).Einstein'sgeneral relativity theory predicts that

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac { 2 \mu } { c ^ { 2 } E } \ \frac { \sin \psi } { 1 + \cos \psi }
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/b298bc10314bcb295ae3163c4c4c92f862acdf6f30eb0951248d786f57d84a9d.jpg)  
Figure7.4 Gravitational light-deflection

where $E$ is the distance of the Earth from the Sun, $\mu$ is the heliocentric gravitational constant, and $c$ is the speed of light.

The apparent directionof $\mathrm { \bf P }$ is along thetangenttothelight-path $\mathbf { p _ { 1 } }$ ,andbyvector addition

$$
\mathbf { p 1 } = \mathbf { p } + \frac { \left( \mathbf { q } \wedge \mathbf { e } \right) \wedge \mathbf { p } } { \sin \psi } \Delta \phi
$$

since $( \mathbf { q } \wedge \mathbf { e } ) \wedge \mathbf { p } / \sin \psi$ is a unit vector in the plane PES,at right angles to $\mathbf { p }$ and pointing away from the Sun.This equation may be written in the form which is more useful for computation,

$$
{ \bf p } _ { 1 } = { \bf p } + \frac { g _ { 1 } } { g _ { 2 } } \left[ ( { \bf p } \cdot { \bf q } ) { \bf e } - ( { \bf e } \cdot { \bf p } ) { \bf q } \right] .
$$

The dimensionless scalar quantities $g _ { 1 }$ and $g _ { 2 }$ are

$$
g _ { 1 } = { \frac { 2 \mu } { c ^ { 2 } E } } \quad { \mathrm { a n d } } \quad g _ { 2 } = 1 + \mathbf { q } \cdot \mathbf { e }
$$

where $\mu / c ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } \tau _ { A } ^ { 2 } / 8 6 4 0 0 ^ { 2 }$ , $k$ is the Gaussian gravitational constant,and $\tau _ { A }$ is the light-time forunit distance (1 au) appropriate for the ephemeris being used (see $\ S \ 7 . 4 . 1 . 3$ ,Eq.7.111). These constantsare published in Section Kof The Astronomical Almanac.The value of $g _ { 1 }$ is always close to $2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 }$ ,and $g _ { 2 }$ varies between O and $+ 2$ Thevector $\mathbf { p _ { 1 } }$ is a unit vector to order $\mu / c ^ { 2 }$ .

Stars represent the asymptotic case when $\mathbf p = \mathbf q$ ,andEq.7.63 becomes

$$
{ \bf p _ { 1 } } = { \bf p } + g _ { 1 } \frac { { \bf e } - ( { \bf p } \cdot { \bf e } ) { \bf p } } { 1 + { \bf p } \cdot { \bf e } } .
$$

When one is applying the light-deflection correction in the algorithm for apparent and intermediate places (see $\ S \ 7 . 4 . 1 . 4 )$ ,using thevector $\mathbf { E _ { H } }$ in the relativistic deflection computation introduces a minor approximation resulting from the use of the barycentric position of the Sunat the epoch of observation.The resulting error cannot exceed O.1 mas in the worst case (object observed at the limb of the Sun with barycentric motionof the Sun orthogonal to the line of sight)and is generally much less.Furthermore,the deflection algorithm itself results froma first-order development that assumes small deviations of the photon track from a straight line in Euclidian space;the errorin neglecting second-order effects can reach about 0.5 mas for an object observed at the Sun's limb (Kammeyer 1988,private communication).

Table7.4 Apparent deflection angles   

<table><tr><td>D</td><td>0.25</td><td>0.5</td><td>1°</td><td>2°</td><td>5°</td><td>10°</td><td>20°</td><td>50°</td><td>90°</td></tr><tr><td>△</td><td>1:&#x27;866</td><td>0.933</td><td>0&#x27;466</td><td>0233</td><td>0093</td><td>0047</td><td>0&#x27;023</td><td>0009</td><td>0004</td></tr></table>

Table7.4 tabulates the deflection angles $\Delta \phi = g _ { 1 }$ tan $( \psi / 2 )$ (Eq.7.61) for various values of $D = 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } - \psi$ (for stars $D$ approximates the geocentric elongation).The body disappears behind the Sun when $D$ is less than the limiting grazing value of about $0 ^ { \circ } 2 5$

Table 7.4 was produced assuming that the Earth-Sun distance $E = 1$ ，and thus $g _ { 1 } =$ $0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 4 0 7$ .Thevariation of the Earth-Sun distance modulates the value of $g _ { 1 }$ by less than $2 \%$ .This simple form of the gravitational deflection formula has been noted previously by Shapiro(1967)and by Fukushima(1982,private communication).In this form,the deflection isnot explicitly dependent on the distance of the emitting body from the Sun or the Earth. Therefore,to an observer on the Earth,the apparent gravitational deflection is the same for all objects that lieanywhere ona given line that extends radially outward from the Sun.This result holdsregardless of the orientation of the line with respect to the Earth.

Figure7.5 shows the magnitude of the deflection of light,as viewed fromtheEarth,for planetsand stars as a function of the geocentric angular separation of the observed body from the centre of the Sun.Maximum deflection occurs for bodies that are about to be occulted by the Sun.Minimum deflection for Mercury and Venus occurs when they are about to transit the Sun.For the other bodies minimum deflection occurs at $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ elongation from the Sun, thatis,near opposition.The figure was produced fromEq.7.61 assuming circular orbits.

The equations for light-deflection have an indeterminacy for light-paths starting beyond theSun on the extension of the Sun-Earth line;bodies there are hidden bythe Sun's disk and unobservablein any event.For these bodies orthe Sun itself,the deflection can be considered to be zero.

# 7.2.4.1Approximate Light-Deflection for Stars

The increments to be added to thecalculated right ascension and declination of the star may be evaluated approximately from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { ~ \beta _ { s } ~ } D = \sin \delta \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } + \cos \delta \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } \cos ( \alpha - \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } ) } \\ & { \Delta \alpha = 0 \mathrm { s } 0 0 0 2 7 1 \frac { \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } \sin { ( \alpha - \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } ) } } { ( 1 - \cos D ) \cos \delta } } \\ & { \Delta \delta = 0 \mathrm { \beta _ { \mathrm { \cdot } } } 0 0 4 0 7 \frac { \sin \delta \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } \cos { ( \alpha - \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } ) } - \cos \delta \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } } { 1 - \cos D } } \end{array}
$$

where $\alpha , \delta , \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } , \delta _ { \mathrm { s } }$ are the geocentric right ascensions and declinations of the star and the Sun, respectively.Forstars, $D$ ,thegeocentric elongation,approximates $1 8 0 ^ { \circ } - \psi$ ,where $\psi$ is the heliocentric elongation.

# 7.2.5 Celestial "Of Date"Positions

This section gives the rotations required to transform positions from the GCRS to acelestial “of date”system (CRS)that are used inthis chapter. Simply,the of date systems are those that have precession and nutation included. Celestially they are the true equator and equinox of date or the Celestial Intermediate Reference System,which is the (true) equator and CIO of date.The transformation for the former,is designated $\mathbf { M } = \mathbf { N P B }$ where the origin of rightascension is the equinox.While the transformation matrix for the latter is designated $\mathbf { C } = \mathbf { C } ( \mathcal { X } , \mathcal { Y } , s )$ ,where the IAU recommended origin for right ascensionis the CIO (see $\ S \ 7 . 1 . 1 )$ .The three concepts involved are frame bias,and precession and nutation，and practically are usually combined into a single matrix transformation.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0008_pages_0281-0320/ocr/images/73b7e2b3de0c7491886c818ca7d2ac553be82aa11655cda897c8dea8ac88a74e.jpg)  
Figure7.5 Light from the planets and stars deflected by the Sun

The frame bias consist of the three offsets of the mean equator and (dynamical) mean equinox of J2oo0.0,provided by the current model,with respect to the GCRS;the first two offsets are the mean pole offsets at J2ooo.O and the third is the offset in right ascension of the mean dynamical equinox of J2Ooo.O.There are different offsets depending whether,for example,the object is on theFK5or Hipparcos reference systems,which areall discussed in Chapter 4.

A practical definition of precession-nutation in the case of the Earth,consistent with the IAU2000/2006 resolutions (IAU 2001,2008;URL[6]),is thatit is the motion of theCIPin the GCRS,including free core nutation and other corrections to the standard models: precession is the secular part of this motion plus the term of 26ooO-year period and nutation is that part of the CIP motion not classed as precession. Precession is discussed in Chapter 6.

Nutation isalso described in Chapter6and Web links for the IAU 2OoOA seriesmay be found viaThe AstronomicalAlmanac Onlineat URL[1].IAU-SOFAalso provides routines for calculating $\Delta \psi$ and $\Delta \epsilon$ (NUTOoAandNUTO6A,URL[3]).Thelatterroutinealsoimplements theIAU 2OooA nutation but it also includes the adjustments for use with the IAU 2006 precession; the combined precession-nutation model is termed IAU $2 0 0 6 / 2 0 0 0 \mathrm { A _ { R } }$ in this book.Kaplan (2Oo5) has published all the terms of the IAU 2OooA nutation series,and

USNO's NOVAS subroutine package (Kaplan et al. 20o9;URL[2])also implements them (NU2000A Fortran-version;iau2o00a C-version).

Detailed information on the individual matricesB, $\mathbf { P }$ and $\mathbf { N }$ is given in Chapter 6, $\ S 6 . 5$ and $\ S 6 . 6$ .Both the combined matricesMandC,the $\mathcal { X }$ , $_ { \mathcal { V } }$ position of the CIP,the CIO locator $s$ ,the true obliquity of the ecliptic (ε),and the nutations inlongitude and obliquity $( \Delta \psi , \Delta \epsilon )$ are all tabulated dailyat $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { T T }$ in various tablesin SectionBof The Astronomical Almanac.

The procedures described in this chapter are those used by The Astronomical Almanac and do not include free core nutation or any of the additional effects that are obtained by observation.

# 7.2.5.1 Equinox-Based Transformation

The traditional transformation combining frame bias,precession and nutation may be expressed thus

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { M } = \mathbf { N } \mathbf { P } \mathbf { B } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - s + E _ { o } ) \mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma } } \\ & { \quad = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - [ \epsilon _ { \mathrm { A } } + \Delta \epsilon ] ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - [ \bar { \psi } + \Delta \psi ] ) \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( \bar { \phi } ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \bar { \gamma } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Nutation is included via $( \Delta \psi , \Delta \epsilon )$ ,the nutations in longitude and obliquity,respectively (see Chapter 6).Frame bias has been included with precession (IAU 20o6,Hilton 2006)and represented by the angles $\bar { \gamma }$ $, \bar { \phi } , \bar { \psi }$ ,and $\epsilon _ { \mathrm { A } }$ the obliquity of the ecliptic with respect to the mean equator of date,and are given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \bar { \gamma } = - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 5 2 9 2 8 + 1 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 5 6 3 7 8 T + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 9 3 2 0 4 4 T ^ { 2 } - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 3 1 2 3 8 T ^ { 3 } } \\ & { } & { - 2 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 8 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } + 2 ^ { \prime } 6 0 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { } & { \bar { \phi } = 8 4 3 8 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 1 2 8 1 9 - 4 6 ^ { \prime \prime } 8 1 1 0 1 6 T + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 5 1 1 2 6 8 T ^ { 2 } + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 5 3 2 8 9 T ^ { 3 } } \\ & { } & { - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 4 0 8 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } - 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { } & { \bar { \psi } = - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 4 1 7 7 5 + 5 0 3 8 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 8 1 4 8 4 T + 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 5 8 4 1 7 5 T ^ { 2 } - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 1 8 5 2 2 T ^ { 3 } } \\ & { } & { - 2 6 ^ { \prime } 4 5 2 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } - 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 8 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 5 } } \\ & { } & { \epsilon _ { \mathrm { A } } = 2 3 ^ { 0 } 2 6 ^ { \prime } 2 1 ^ { \prime \prime } 4 0 6 - 4 6 ^ { \prime \prime } 8 3 6 7 6 9 T - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 1 8 3 1 T ^ { 2 } + 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 2 0 0 3 4 0 T ^ { 3 } } \\ & { } & { - 0 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 7 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 6 } T ^ { 4 } - 4 ^ { \prime \prime } 3 4 \times 1 0 ^ { - 8 } T ^ { 5 } } \end{array}
$$

where $T$ is the TT time interval in Julian centuries from J2000.0 (2451545.0 TT).Strictly speaking precession angles should be a function of TDB,but this makes no significant difference.

The formulation of $\mathbf { M }$ inEq.7.67above,in terms of $\mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma }$ (see Eq.7.75), $s$ the CIO locator and $E _ { o }$ the equation of the origins demonstrates (Capitaine 2Oo6) that the equinox and CIObased (g7.2.5.2) paradigms are the same,except for a rotation of $E _ { o }$ about the CIP.This results in the different origins for measuringRA.The equation of the origins $E _ { o }$ is discussed in Chapter6(S 6.4)and is tabulated dailyat $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } }$ UT1inSectionBof TheAstronomical Almanac. IAU-SOFA(URL[3]) provide software routines for all the quantities in this section.These concepts are discussed in full in Chapter 6.

# 7.2.5.2ClO-Based Transformation

The matrix that includes frame bias,precession and nutation,and transforms positions referred to the GCRS to the Celestial Intermediate Reference System where the origin of right ascension is the CIO,may be expressed in terms of the position of the CIP $( \mathcal { X } , \mathcal { Y } )$ and location of the CIO, thus

$$
\mathbf { C } = \mathbf { C } ( \mathcal { X } , \mathcal { Y } , s ) = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - [ E + s ] ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( d ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( E ) = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - s ) \mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma }
$$

where $\mathbf { \delta } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } } } } } } } } }$ locates the CIO (see Chapter 6)on the equator of the CIP and

$$
E = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \mathcal { V } } { \mathcal { X } } , \qquad d = \tan ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { \frac { \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } } { \mathcal { Z } ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad \mathcal { Z } ^ { 2 } = 1 - \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } - \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } .
$$

The most practical way of calculating $\mathbf { c }$ is to use $\mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma }$ (IERS 2010),which is givenby

$$
\mathcal { R } _ { \Sigma } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 - a \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } } & { - a \mathcal { X } \mathcal { Y } } & { - \mathcal { X } } \\ { - a \mathcal { X } \mathcal { Y } } & { 1 - a \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } } & { - \mathcal { Y } } \\ { \mathcal { X } } & { \mathcal { Y } } & { 1 - a ( \mathcal { X } ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { Y } ^ { 2 } ) } \end{array} \right]
$$

where $a = 1 / ( 1 + \mathcal { Z } ) .$ The $( { \mathcal { X } } , { \mathcal { Y } } )$ coordinates of the CIP and $s$ the CIO locator are given and discussed in Chapter $6 ^ { 2 } ( \ S 6 . 7 ) $ IAU-SOFA provides software for calculating Cand the CIO locator $\mathbf { \delta } _ { \mathbf { { \delta } } } \mathbf { { \mathbf { { \mathcal { S } } } } }$ as well as the IAU 2006 series for $\mathcal { X }$ , $_ { y }$ (URL[3]).

Alternatively, the $( { \mathcal { X } } , { \mathcal { Y } } )$ coordinates of the CIP and $\mathcal { Z }$ ,maybecalculated directly from the frame bias and precession-nutation angles (Capitaine 2Oo6),and then used in Eq.7.75. Thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mathcal { X } = \sin \epsilon \sin \psi \cos \bar { \gamma } - ( \sin \epsilon \cos \psi \cos \bar { \phi } - \cos \epsilon \sin \bar { \phi } ) \sin \bar { \gamma } } } \\ { { \mathcal { Y } = \sin \epsilon \sin \psi \sin \bar { \gamma } + ( \sin \epsilon \cos \psi \cos \bar { \phi } - \cos \epsilon \sin \bar { \phi } ) \cos \bar { \gamma } } } \\ { { \mathcal { Z } = \sin \epsilon \cos \psi \cos \bar { \phi } + \cos \epsilon \cos \bar { \phi } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\epsilon = \epsilon _ { \mathrm { A } } + \Delta \epsilon$ and $\psi = \bar { \psi } + \Delta \psi$ include the effects of nutation (see Chapter 6, $\ S 6 . 6 . 3$ while the frame bias and precession are incorporated by the angles $\bar { \gamma } , \bar { \phi } , \bar { \psi }$ ,and $\epsilon _ { \mathrm { A } }$ ,and are givenabove in Eqs.7.69 to 7.72.

# 7.2.6Terrestrial "Of Date"Positions

This section touches on the rotations required to transform positions referred to the celestial “of date”system to one of the terrestrial systems.These systems are the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference Systemand the International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS).The former system isachieved when the celestial system is rotated about the CIPso that the origin on the equator isno longer the equinox or the CIO,but is the TIO.While the later system istheresult of positioning of the TIO via the TIO locator $( s ^ { \prime } )$ and includes the effects of polar motion.Often this is just referred to as polar motion since $s ^ { \prime }$ is insignificant.However, strictly,an ITRS position will need to include allthe observable corrections such as free core nutation and the other parameters of the IERS (URL[5]).

# 7.2.6.1The TIOand Earth Rotation

An“of date”position in the celestial system,where the origin of right ascension is either the equinox or the CIO,may be transformed to the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System, where the originis the TIO.Thus GHA,oriflongitude is includedLHA,which are referred to the TIO(Greenwich")meridian or the observers meridian,respectively, may be calculated. This change in origin to the TIO isachieved bya rotation of $\beta$ aboutthe $z$ -axis,i.e.,arotation of $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \beta )$ about the CIP, where $\beta = \mathrm { G A S T }$ or $\beta = \theta =$ ERA and depends on the origin of right ascension and paradigm being used.

The equations for GMST,GASTand ERA are given in Chapter6(§ 6.8) where the topic is fully discussed (see also $\ S \ 7 . 1 . 3 . 2 )$ . It should be remembered that GAST depends on the nutation angles (equation of the equinoxes $E _ { e }$ ),while ERAand GMSTdo not.Further more, theseparametersofEarth rotation,GAST(UT1,TT)andERA(UT1),are basically functions of UT1,while precession,nutation and the solar system ephemerides are not.

# 7.2.6.2 Transformation to the ITRS

Thissection touches on the transformation (W,see Eq.7.1) from the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System to the International Terrestrial Reference System (ITRS).Simply this includes the effects of polar motion.Polar motionand the TIO locator are discussed in detail in Chapter 6 $( \ S \ : 6 . 9 )$ .

Polar motion is defined as the motion of the Earth'spole with respect to theITRS.The main components are the Chandlerian free motion with a period of approximately 430 days, andanannual motion.It alsoincludes sub-daily variations causedby ocean tidesand periodic motions driven by gravitationaltorques with periods lessthan two days.Sub-daily variations are notincludedinthe values distributedbytheIERS(URL[5]),andare therefore to beadded, afterinterpolation to the date of interest,using a model provided by the IERS Conventions.

Thusthe transformation matrix from the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference Systemto the ITRS is

$$
{ \bf W } = { \bf R } _ { 1 } ( - y _ { p } ) { \bf R } _ { 2 } ( - x _ { p } ) { \bf R } _ { 3 } ( s ^ { \prime } )
$$

where $s ^ { \prime }$ ,the TIO locator,is $s ^ { \prime } = - 0 . 0 0 0 0 4 7 T$ and $T$ is measured in Julian centuries of 36525days from 2451545.0 TT.The TIO locator isa measure of the secular drift of the TIO,with respect to geodetic zero longitude,that is the very slow systematic rotation of the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System with respect to the ITRS(due to polar motion).The coordinates $x _ { p }$ ，, $y _ { p }$ correspond to the position of the CIP with respect to the ITRS,measured along the meridians at longitudes $0 ^ { \circ }$ and $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ ( $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ west). Current values of the coordinates, $x _ { p } , y _ { p }$ ,of the pole forusein the reduction of observations are published by theCentral Bureau of the IERS (see The Astronomical Almanac Onlineat URL[1] for web links).

The longitudeand latitude of aterrestrial observer, $\lambda$ and $\phi$ ,used in astronomical formulae (e.g.,for hour angle or the determination of astronomical time),should be expressed in the Terrestrial Intermediate Reference System,thatis,corrected for polar motion,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \lambda = \lambda _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } + \left( x _ { p } \sin \lambda _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } + y _ { p } \cos \lambda _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } \right) \tan \phi _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } } \\ & { } \\ & { \phi = \phi _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } + \left( x _ { p } \cos \lambda _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } - y _ { p } \sin \lambda _ { _ \mathrm { I T R S } } \right) } \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda _ { \mathrm { { r m s } } }$ and $\phi _ { \mathrm { { \pi R S } } }$ aretheITRS (geodetic)longitudeadlatudeoftheobed $x _ { p }$ $y _ { p }$ ）are the ITRS coordinatesof the CIP,in the same units as $\lambda$ and $\phi$ .These formulae are approximations and should not be used for places at polar latitudes.

# 7.2.7 Refraction

Atmosphericrefractionisincluded in TheAstronomical Almanac in onlyafewtopocentric phenomena,such as the times of rising and settingof the Sun and Moon,and in theory in the predictions of local circumstances of eclipses.However,for observational reductions the effect of refraction must be included.Inthis section we give several low-precision formulae, validat the horizon,that are particularly useful for navigationaswellas formulae valid for zenith distances less than $7 0 ^ { \circ }$ ，which takes into account the amount ofwatervapour intheatmosphere.However,we start by givinganalgorithm for evaluating the integral in the classical formulation using quadrature,which allows for the principal change of refraction due to variations of temperature,pressure,humidity,and even the temperature lapserate that resultsinawel-defined reference value.Thus,forexample,residualsof the observed values from the reference values could be used to model the local variation ofrefraction.

# 7.2.7.1 Refraction-Numerical Integration

In this section we give a precise algorithm for calculating refraction using numerical integration.The real accuracyis very dependent on the conditions,particularly forlarge zenith distances,and on the atmospheric model.

The atmosphere is assumed to be spherically symmetric and in hydrostatic equilibrium, and to obey the perfect gas law for the combined mixture of dry air and water vapour,and also for the dry air and water vapour separately. The two layers of the atmosphere are the troposphere and the stratosphere.The troposphere,the lowest portion,extends from the Earth to the tropopause,which isassumed to beat $1 1 \mathrm { k m } ( h _ { t } )$ .Inthisregion the temperature decreases at aconstant rate ( $\alpha = 0 . 0 0 6 5$ degrees Kelvin per meter) and the relative humidity is constant and equal to its value at the observer.In the stratosphere the temperature remains constant and equal to the temperature at the tropopause $( T _ { t } )$ and there is no pressure due to watervapour.Theupper height $( h _ { s } )$ of the stratosphere is taken as $8 0 \mathrm { k m }$ ,sinceabove this height refraction becomes negligible.

The totalbendingof aray is givenby

$$
\xi = - \int _ { 0 } ^ { z _ { 0 } } \frac { r d n / d r } { n + r d n / d r } d z .
$$

This integral is atransformation of the usual refraction integral.It has been recommended by Auerand Standish (1979,2Ooo) because it is more suitable for numerical integration; it is a more slowly varying function over the whole range of $Z$ and removes the problem at $z = 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ However,there isa discontinuity in the function $d n / d r$ at the tropopause,and so the integral isevaluated in two parts,from $z = z _ { s }$ to $z _ { t s }$ in the stratosphere,and from $z = z _ { t }$ to $z _ { 0 }$ in the troposphere.

The following procedure gives the steps required to calculate the total refraction, $\xi$ ,i.e., the sum of the refraction caused by the troposphere ( $\xi _ { t }$ )and the stratosphere( $\xi _ { s }$ ),from

$$
\xi = \xi _ { t } + \xi _ { s } = \int _ { z _ { 0 } } ^ { z _ { t } } f d z + \int _ { z _ { t s } } ^ { z _ { s } } f d z
$$

to a specified tolerance $\epsilon$ ,where $f$ is the integrand given in Eq.7.80.
