# 10.4 The Apparent Disk of a Solar System Body

Most bodies with diameters $\gtrsim 5 0 0 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ ,suchasaplanet,dwarf planet,or large satellite,are essentially oblate spheroids inshape with asemimajor axis, $a$ ,andasemiminor axis, $^ b$ The apparent disk of such abody is an ellipse with an apparent flattening, $f ^ { \prime }$ ,dependent on the apparent tilt of the object's axis.The apparent flattening of the object is always less than or equal to its true flattening. The apparent polar radius and the apparent flattening of an object are derived from the parametric equation for an ellipse:

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/142ab5354c7d6ef4a5c02efeb5a404adb2d58087f03e6081f4f449e22f3b96c5.jpg)  
Figure 10.4 The geometric appearance of the apparent polar radius, $b ^ { \prime }$

$$
\scriptstyle r = { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } }
$$

where $x$ is the length of the projection of the point along the major axis from the center of the ellipse, $y$ is the length of the projection of the point along the minor axis from the center, and $\phi$ is the planetocentric latitude of the sub-Earth point,the angle between the line segment connecting the point with the center of the ellipse and the semimajor axis (Fig.10.4).The apparent polar semiminor axis for a geocentric observer is the length of the line segment, $b ^ { \prime }$ Using Eqs.10.8 the apparent semiminor axis, $b ^ { \prime }$ ,is

$$
b ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi + b ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } .
$$

Rearranging Eq.10.1,gives

$$
b = a ( 1 - f )
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { b ^ { \prime } { = } \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi + a ^ { 2 } ( 1 - f ) ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } \\ & { \quad { = } a \sqrt { 1 + ( f ^ { 2 } - 2 f ) \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } \\ & { \quad { = } a \sqrt { 1 + ( f ^ { 2 } - 2 f ) \left( \frac { \mathbf { b } ^ { \prime } \cdot \mathbf { b } } { | \mathbf { b } ^ { \prime } | | \mathbf { b } | } \right) ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

where $ { \mathbf { b } } ^ { \prime }$ isthe vector from the planetocenter to the apparent polar axis and $\mathbf { b }$ is the vector from the planetocenter to the true polar axis.The apparent semiminoraxis, $b ^ { \prime }$ is,to first order in $f$

$$
b ^ { \prime } = a \left[ 1 - f \left( { \frac { \mathbf { b } ^ { \prime } \cdot \mathbf { b } } { | \mathbf { b } ^ { \prime } | | \mathbf { b } | } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] .
$$

Thus,to first order,theapparent fattening, $f ^ { \prime }$ ,of the body is

$$
f ^ { \prime } = { \frac { a - b ^ { \prime } } { a } } .
$$

The maximum error arising from using a first order expansion is $0 . 1 3 \%$ or $0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 1 4$ for Saturn. Formore accurate applications

$$
f ^ { \prime } = f \sin ^ { 2 } \phi + \frac { f ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \left( \sin ^ { 4 } \phi - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \right)
$$

to second order in $f$

# 10.4.1Sub-Earth Pointsand Sub-Solar Points

The tabulated quantities for the sub-Earth point,sub-solar point,and the greatest defect of illumination on the apparent disk of an object as it is seen fromanobserver on the Earth are illustrated inFig.10.5.At the centerof the apparent disk is the sub-Earth point, $e$ .Thetwo otherpoints of reference are the sub-solar point, $s$ ,and thenorth pole of thebody, $n$ .The apparent position of $\mathbf { \sigma } _ { \mathbf { { \mathcal { S } } } }$ with respect to $e$ is designated by the apparent distance $d _ { s }$ and position angle, $P A _ { s }$ ,of $s$ .Similarly,theapparent position of $n$ is designated by $d _ { n }$ and $P A _ { n }$ .The position anglesare measured eastward from north, $N$ ,onthecelestial sphere.Thedirection north is defined by the great circle on the celestial sphere passing through the center of the object's apparent disk and the Celestial Intermediate Pole $( \ S \ 6 . 2 . 3 )$ of date.The apparent distance of a tabulated point is listed as positive if the point lieson the visible hemisphere of the object and negative if thepoint is on the far side of the object.Thus,as point $n$ or $\mathbf { \Delta } _ { \mathbf { { \boldsymbol { \mathscr { S } } } } }$ passes from the visible hemisphere to the far side,or vice versa,the sign of the distance changesabruptly,but the position angle varies continuously.On the other hand,when the point passes close to the center of the disk,the sign of the distance remains unchanged,but both the distance and positionangle may vary rapidly and may appear to be discontinuous in afixed interval tabulation.

The planetographic coordinates of the sub-Earth and sub-solar points are determined as follows:First,the position ephemerides are used to calculate the heliocentric equatorial rectangularcoordinates forthe body, $\mathbf { r }$ ,andtheEarth, $\mathbf { r } _ { e }$ at a given JulianDate in TT[JD(TT)]. The distance vector, $\Delta \mathbf { r }$ ,from the Earth to the object is

$$
\Delta \mathbf { r } = \mathbf { r } - \mathbf { r } _ { e } .
$$

The light-time correction, $\delta t$ , is found from $\Delta \mathbf { r }$ by dividing bythe speedoflight, $c$

$$
\delta t = \frac { | \Delta \mathbf { r } | } { c } .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/2f2e83ae1a19520ecec107ddd747221436bfdb951d39befdf35802815df49ba7.jpg)  
Figure10.5 The disk of a planet as seen from an observer on the Earth

The difference $\mathrm { J D } ( \mathrm { T T } ) - \delta t$ is calculated to approximate the time at which the image of the body,asobservedattheEarthatJD(TT),leftthebody.Thus, ${ \bf r } _ { \mathrm { J D ( T T ) } - \delta t }$ ,is the approximate position of the body as observed from the Earth attime JD(TT).This process may be iterated, if desired,to producea more accurate body position; however,body motions are slow enough that to theprecision of The Astronomical Almanac no more than a single iteration is ever required.The position of the Earth, $\mathbf { r } _ { e }$ ,and the apparent position of the body, $\mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { J D ( T T ) } - t }$ ,are then transformed from the mean equator and ecliptic of J2ooo.O to the true equatorand ecliptic of date using the frame bias,precession and nutation routines described in $\ S \ G . 5$ and $\ S \ G . 6$ . The transformed position vectors are designated $\mathbf { r } _ { e d }$ for the Earth and $\mathbf { r } _ { d }$ for the body. The distance vector, $\Delta \mathbf { r }$ ,is recomputed by using $\mathbf { r } _ { e d }$ and $\mathbf { r } _ { d }$ as in Eq.10.15and then corrected for aberration in position as described in $\ S 7 . 2 . 3$ .The unit vector along the body-Sun line,

$$
s = \frac { - \mathbf { r } _ { d } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { d } | }
$$

is the planetocentric unit vector denoting the direction of the Sun from the body.Prior to the 2013 edition the position vector from the body to the Sun did not correct for the aberration of light.However,the difference between the directions of the aberrated and the unaberatted

vector isalways less than $0 \% 1$ forall tabulated bodies.Similarly,the planetocentric unit vector,includingaberration,pointing from the body to the Earth is

$$
\mathbf { e } = { \frac { - \Delta \mathbf { r } } { | \Delta \mathbf { r } | } } .
$$

The position of the north pole of date of the body in the coordinates of the mean equator and equinox of J2000.0, $( \alpha _ { 0 } , \delta _ { 0 } )$ ,isfound using the appropriate expression from Table10.1. These coordinates are transformedto the equator and equinox of date, $( \alpha _ { d } , \delta _ { d } )$ .The unit vector $\mathbf { n }$ is the planetocentric unit vector of the body's north pole.In terms of the right ascension anddeclination $\mathbf { n }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { n } = ( \cos \delta _ { d } \cos \alpha _ { d } , \cos \delta _ { d } \sin \alpha _ { d } , \sin \delta _ { d } ) } \\ { = \mathbf { N P B } ( \cos \delta _ { 0 } \cos \alpha _ { 0 } , \cos \delta _ { 0 } \sin \alpha _ { 0 } , \sin \delta _ { 0 } ) } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf { P }$ , $\mathbf { N }$ ,and $\mathbf { B }$ are the precession, nutation,and frame bias matrices,respectively (see $\textrm { \AA } 6 . 5$ and $\ S \ 6 . 6 $ .Theplanetocentric unit vector for the intersection of the planet’s prime meridian and equator, ${ \bf w } _ { d }$ ,isalso required.Its declination, $\delta _ { w }$ ,is

$$
\sin \delta _ { w } = \sin W \cos \delta _ { 0 }
$$

where $W$ isthe rotation angleof the body'sprime meridian;refertoFig.1O.1.The current values of $W$ for the planets,Ceres,and Pluto (Archinal etal.2O1la;Archinal etal.2011b) aregivenin Table 1O.1.Theangle $\delta _ { w }$ liesbetween $+ 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ and $- 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,so there is no loss of generality.

Let the arc, $\psi$ ,run from the node of the planet's equator of date along the equator of the BCRS to the arc representing $\delta _ { w }$ as shown in Fig.10.1. Then

$$
\sin \psi = { \frac { \sin W \sin \delta _ { 0 } } { \cos \delta _ { w } } } \quad { \mathrm { a n d } } \quad \cos \psi = { \frac { \cos W } { \cos \delta _ { w } } } .
$$

The right ascension of the planet's north pole of date is $6 ^ { \mathrm { h } } ~ ( 9 0 ^ { \circ } )$ less than the position of its node.Thus,the right ascension of $\mathbf { w }$ is

$$
\alpha _ { w } = \alpha _ { 0 } + 9 0 ^ { \circ } + \psi .
$$

The vector $\mathbf { w }$ is thus

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { w } = \mathbf { N P B } ( \cos \delta _ { w } \cos \alpha _ { w } , \cos \delta _ { w } \sin \alpha _ { w } , \sin \delta _ { w } ) . } \end{array}
$$

The coordinates for the sub-Earth and sub-solar points are computed using the planetary polar vector, $\mathbf { n }$ ，and $\mathbf { w }$ to determine the rotation from the equatorial coordinate system to determine the planetocentric latitudeand theplanetographic longitude(from thedefinition of $W$ ).Theflatteningof theplanet, $f$ ,isused to convert theplanetocentric latitude to the planetographic latitude.The process of determining the sub-Earth and sub-solar points by settingupa coordinate system where the planetary polar vector, $\mathbf { n }$ ,isthe $z$ -axis, $\mathbf { w }$ isthe $x$ -axis,and the $y$ -axisof the coordinatesystem completesa left-handed coordinate system, that is

$$
\mathbf { y } = \mathbf { w } \times \mathbf { n } .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/61f41baae86a0d33369055ef880e7428f58ba7ddc28713527c89ecb5a48e6cc2.jpg)  
Figure 10.6 Planetocentric unit vectors for points of interest on the disk of a planet,and the angles giving the position of the pole and sub-solarpointwith respectto the Earth'smeanequatorand pole of date

The components of the unit vector for the sub-Earth point, e,are

$$
\mathbf { e } = ( \mathbf { e } \cdot \mathbf { w } , \mathbf { e } \cdot \mathbf { y } , \mathbf { e } \cdot \mathbf { n } ) .
$$

The planetocentric and planetographic latitudes and planetographic longitude of the sub-Earth point are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \phi _ { e } = \sin ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { e } _ { z } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \phi _ { e } ^ { \prime } = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \tan \phi _ { e } } { 1 - f ^ { 2 } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \lambda _ { e } ^ { \prime } = \tan ^ { - 1 } \frac { \mathbf { e } _ { y } } { \mathbf { e } _ { x } } } . } \end{array}
$$

For retrograde rotation, $\lambda _ { e } ^ { \prime } = 3 6 0 ^ { \circ } - \lambda _ { e } ^ { \prime }$ as found in Eq.10.26.

The planetographic latitudeand longitude of the sub-solar point are found by replacing the position for the Sun, $( \alpha _ { s } , \delta _ { s } )$ ,in place of the sub-Earth point $( \alpha _ { d } , \delta _ { d } )$ in Eqs.10.19 through 10.26.

Determining the apparent distance and position angle of the body's north pole starts by specifyinga coordinate system in which theyz-planeisthe plane of the sky,the $y$ -axis points toward the west as seen on the plane of the sky,and the $x$ -axispointstoward the Earth.In the equatorial coordinatesof date,the direction for the north pole of the Earth is $\mathsf { N } = ( 0 , 0 , 1 )$ .Thus,the directions for axes, $( \mathbf { u } _ { i } , \mathbf { u } _ { j } , \mathbf { u } _ { k } )$ ,of the coordinate system in the

equatorial coordinates of date are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \mathbf { u } _ { i } = \mathbf { e } } \\ { \mathbf { u } _ { j } = \mathbf { e } \times \mathbf { N } } \\ { \mathbf { u } _ { k } = \mathbf { N } . } \end{array}
$$

The coordinates for the north pole vector in this coordinate system are

$$
P ( \mathbf n \cdot \mathbf u _ { i } , \mathbf n \cdot \mathbf u _ { j } , \mathbf n \cdot \mathbf u _ { k } )
$$

where $P$ is the apparent polar diameter ofthe body.The position angle, $p _ { n }$ of the north pole is

$$
p _ { n } = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \mathbf { n } _ { y } } { \mathbf { n } _ { z } } } .
$$

The length of the projection from the center of mass to the north pole on the body is

$$
r = \sqrt { \mathbf { n } _ { x } ^ { 2 } + \mathbf { n } _ { z } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

The apparent distance, $d _ { n }$ ,in arcseconds,is

$$
d _ { n } = 2 0 6 2 6 4 . 8 0 6 2 \frac { r } { \left| \Delta \mathbf { r } \right| - \mathbf { n } _ { x } } .
$$

The position angle, $p _ { s }$ ,and apparent distance, $d _ { s }$ ,of the sub-solar point are calculated similarly using the unit vector for the Sun,s,and the apparent radius of the body at the latitude of the sub-solar point rather than $\mathbf { n }$ and the apparent polar radius. The apparent radius of the body atthe sub-solar point is derived using Eq.1O.8 and substituting the planetocentric latitude of the sub-solar point, $\phi _ { s }$ for $\phi$ .

The phase angle,

$$
i = \cos \mathbf { s } \cdot \mathbf { e }
$$

ofabody is the planetocentric elongation of the Earth from the Sun.The phase, $k$ ,isthe ratio of the illuminated area of the disk to the total area of the disk as seen from the Earth. Assuming that the apparent disk of the body is circular,

$$
k = 0 . 5 ( 1 + \cos i ) .
$$

The planetographic longitude and latitude of the sub-Earth point and the planetographic longitude of the sub-solar point along with the distance and position angle of $s$ forMercury aretabulated in SectionEof The Astronomical Almanac.The pole of rotation of Mercury is nearly perpendicular to the plane of its orbit (Margot etal.2Oo7).Thus,the planetographic latitude of the sub-solar point does not differ significantly from $0 ^ { \circ }$ ,and isnot tabulated.

The planetographic longitudes and latitudes of the sub-Earth and sub-solar points along with the distance and position angle of $s$ aretabulated forVenus,Mars,Jupiter,andSaturn inSectionEof TheAstronomicalAlmanac.

The planetographic latitudes of the sub-Earth point forUranus and Neptune are tabulated in SectionE.For Uranusand Neptune the differencein position between the sub-Earth and sub-solar points is never greaterthan $0 \%$ Thus,values for the sub-solarpoint are not tabulated for these bodies.

The planetographic longitude and latitude of the sub-Earth point forCeres and Pluto are tabulated on in Section G.Like Uranusand Neptune the difference in position between the sub-Earth and sub-solar points is never greater than $0 { } ^ { \prime \prime } 1 .$ Thus,values for the sub-solar point arenot tabulated for these bodies either.

The position anglesand apparent distances of the north poles of Mercury,Venus,Mars, Jupiterand Saturn are tabulated in SectionE of The Astronomical Almanac.Theposition angle and apparent distance of the north pole of Uranus and Neptune are tabulated as well.

# 10.4.2 The Greatest Defect of Ilumination

The geometric terminator,the plane orthogonal to the direction of the Sun passing through the center of mass of the object (Fig.1O.5),is used to compute the phase and the maximum geometric defect of illumination.The maximum geometric defect of illumination, $q$ ,isthe lengthof the portion of the line segment across the face of the body passng through the sub-Earth and sub-solar points, $s$ and $e$ ,thatis not illuminated (Fig.10.6).The defect of illumination does not include non-geometric effects such as the refraction of light through the body'satmosphere.

The apparent equatorial semidiameter, $r _ { e q }$ of the body,inseconds ofarc,is

$$
r _ { e q } = 2 0 6 2 6 4 . 8 0 6 2 \frac { a } { | \Delta \mathbf { r } | } .
$$

The ratio of the apparent polar diameter to the equatorial diameter depends on the planetocentric latitude, $\phi$ ,of the sub-Earth point (Fig.10.5),and is found by rearrangingEq.10.11

$$
{ \frac { b ^ { \prime } } { a } } = { \sqrt { 1 + ( f ^ { 2 } - 2 f ) \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } .
$$

The maximum geometric defect of illumination is

$$
q = 2 r _ { e q } \left[ 1 - \left( 1 - \frac { b ^ { \prime } } { a } \right) \sin ^ { 2 } \left( p _ { s } - p _ { n } + 9 0 ^ { \circ } \right) \right] ( 1 - k ) .
$$

Its position angle, $p _ { q }$ ,measured from celestial north,is:

$$
p _ { q } = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { p _ { s } + 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } } & { p _ { s } < 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } } \\ { p _ { s } - 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } } & { p _ { s } \geq 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } . } \end{array} \right.
$$

Thegreatest defects of illuminationforMercury,Venus,Mars,Jupiter,and Saturnare tabulated onthe even numbered pagesofE54 through E76 of The Astronomical Almanac. Owing to both the small phase angles and small apparent diameters,the greatest defects of illumination of Uranusand Neptune arealways less than $0 . { } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 1$ ,and,hence,not tabulated.

# 10.4.3The Apparent Magnitudes of the Minor Planets

InSection G The Astronomical Almanac tabulates the apparent magnitude for 92 minor planets(90 asteroids and the probable dwarf planets (2)Pallas and (4) Vesta) at opposition. These comprise the set of minor planets forwhich theapparent magnitude is $\leq 1 1 ( \leq 1 2$ if the minor planet'smean diameter $\geq 2 0 0 \mathrm { k m } .$ )atmean opposition.Inaddition,The Astronomical

Almanac tabulates the apparent magnitudes daily for 6O days either side of opposition for the fourteen minor planets (2) Pallas,(3) Juno,(4) Vesta,(6)Hebe,(7) Iris,(8)Flora, (9)Metis,(10)Hygiea,(15)Eunomia,(16)Psyche,(52)Europa,(65)Cybele,(511)Davida, and (704) Interamnia.

Commission 2O of the IAU adopted the apparent magnitude system for minor planets termed the H-G magnitude systemin 1985(Marsden 1986).This system is based on Bowell et al.(1989).The mean visual magnitude, $V ( i )$ ,of a minor planet as a function of phase, $i$ , is calculated using the relation.

$$
V ( i ) = H - 2 . 5 \log [ ( 1 - G ) \Phi _ { 1 } ( i ) + G \Phi _ { 2 } ( i ) ]
$$

where

$$
H \equiv V ( 1 , 0 )
$$

is the visual magnitude of the minor planet at1aufrom the Earth,1au from the Sun,and a phase angle of $0 ^ { \circ }$ , $^ { \sc G }$ is the slope parameter,and $\Phi _ { 1 } ( i )$ and $\Phi _ { 2 } ( i )$ are empirically determined phase functions.Thus,the second term on the right hand side of Eq.1O.38 constitutes the phase function, $\Delta m ( i )$

The slope parameter, $^ { G }$ ,isameasure of the gradientof the phasecurve.It has been scaled so that $G \approx 0$ forlow-albedo bodies and $G \approx 1$ forhigh-albedo bodies.However,itis possible for $^ { G }$ to havevaluesless thanO or greater than1.Thevalues for $G$ foreach of the93 selected minor planets along with the values for $\mathbf { H }$ are given in Table 10.8 and on pages G2 and G3 ofThe Astronomical Almanac for thoseminor planets tabulated.

The phase functions, $\Phi _ { 1 } ( i )$ and $\Phi _ { 2 } ( i )$ ,are normalized to unity at $\mathrm { i } = 0$ .The relations used for these functions are:

$$
\Phi _ { 1 } ( i ) = \exp \left[ - 3 . 3 3 \left( \tan \frac { i } { 2 } \right) ^ { 0 . 6 3 } \right] \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \Phi _ { 2 } ( i ) = \exp \left[ - 1 . 8 7 \left( \tan \frac { i } { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 . 2 2 } \right] .
$$

These equations are valid for phase angles of $0 ^ { \circ } \leq i \leq 1 2 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Equation A4 of Bowell et al. (1989) gives a somewhat more accurate set of expressions for $\Phi _ { 1 } ( i )$ and $\Phi _ { 2 } ( i )$

The apparent visual magnitude, $V$ ,of the minor planet is then

$$
V = V ( i ) + 5 \log r d
$$

where $r$ is the heliocentric distance of the minor planet and $^ d$ is its geocentric distance in au's.

Inaddition to the apparent magnitudeat opposition,The Astronomical Almanac tabulates themean diameters, $d _ { m }$ ,for each of the93minor planets listed on pagesG2andG3.The mean diameter is specifically the one that would produce the same volumeas an ellipsoidal bodywith diameters, $a , b$ ,and $c$ ,thatis

$$
d _ { m } = { \sqrt [ 3 ] { a b c } } .
$$

The diameters used for the minor planets come from several sources and are available from TheAstronomical Almanac Onlineat URL[1].

# 10.5Physical Ephemeris of the Sun

Thephysical ephemeris of the Sun tabulated in The Astronomical Almanac consistsof the position angle of the solar axis,the heliographic sub-Earth latitude and longitude,the horizontal parallax,and the apparent semidiameter of the Sun.These quantities along with the ephemeris transit time are tabulated on the odd numbered pages C7 though C21.

Forthe sake of continuity as requested by Commission 10 of the IAU (IAU 1986),the elements used to calculate the physical ephemeris of the Sun are from Carrington (1863). The values of the physical constants for the ephemeris are:

1.adopted mean sidereal rotation rate at $1 4 ^ { \circ }$ latitude, $8 5 1 ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d a y } ^ { - 1 }$ ;   
2.inclination of solar equator to the ecliptic, $I = 7 \% 2 5$ ;   
3.longitude of the ascending node of the solar equator on the ecliptic, $\Omega = 7 5 . 7 6 +$   
$0 ^ { \circ } 0 1 3 9 7 \ : T$ ,where $T$ isthe time,in years,from J2000.0 (JD2451545.0);and   
4.the equatorial semidiameter of the Sun, $d _ { S } = 6 9 6$ , $0 0 0 \mathrm { k m }$

Carrington's elements do not include the motion of the ecliptic.That is,the rotation of the Sun is given with respect to the ecliptic of 1850.However, Carrington's elements do includeprecession,representedbythe linear termin therelationfor $\Omega$ ,whichistherate of precession since 185O as determined by Carrington.The uncertainty in both the inclination of the solar equator to the ecliptic, $\pm 0 . 0 0 2$ ,and the position of the node, $\pm 0 \% 1$ (Beck and Giles 2oo5),are large enough that any error introduced arising from neglecting the motion of theecliptic,differences between Carrington'srate of precessionand the currently accepted value,or higher order terms in the precessionis insignificant.The flatteningof the Sun is assumed to be extremely small $( f \approx 0$ ).Because theSunisa gaseousbody,it doesnotrotate rigidly.The sidereal period given is for the meanrotationrate of the Sunata latitude of $1 4 ^ { \circ }$ on either side of its equator.

The position of the north pole of the Sun derived from the inclination of the Sun and the longitude of the ascending node with respect to the mean equator and equinox of J2Oo0.O is:

$$
\alpha _ { 0 } = 2 8 6 ^ { \circ } . 1 3 0 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \delta _ { 0 } = 6 3 ^ { \circ } . 8 7 0 0 .
$$

The position of the prime meridian derived from the mean sidereal period of the Sun is6:

$$
W = 8 4 \small { : } 1 0 + 1 4 \small { : } 1 8 4 4 0 0 0 t
$$

where $t$ is the time,indays,fromJ2000.0(JD2451545.0).

Unlike other solar system bodies,the position for the prime meridian of the Sun has traditionally included both the light-time correction and aberration.This situation isarelic of Carrington's method forreducing his observations.However,both these quantities are nearly constant for the Sun.Thus,prior to the advent of fast computers these corrections were simply included in the position of the prime meridian to reduce the number of calculations needed to determine the physical ephemeris of the Sun.Archinal etal.(2O11a) have removed the lighttime correction and aberration from the position of the prime meridian.Thus,beginning with the 2009 edition of The Astronomical Almanac the relation forthe prime meridian of the Sun, adjusted for light-time and aberration becomes

$$
W = 8 4 _ { \cdot } ^ { \circ } 1 7 6 + 1 4 _ { \cdot } ^ { \circ } 1 8 4 4 0 0 0 t .
$$

Anadditional digit in $W$ is included so that the physical ephemeris of the Sunmay be treated in the same manner as the other planets and ensures consistency with previous calculations. The additional digit does not represent an increase in the accuracy of the position of the prime meridianat J2ooo.0.Henceforward,the Sun willbe treatedlike every other solar system body in regard to the position of its prime meridian.

The horizontal parallax of an object is the difference in direction between the topocentric coordinates of an object when the center of the object is on the astronomical horizon and its geocentric coordinates.Thus,the horizontal parallax, $H P$ ,of the Sun is

$$
H P = \sin ^ { - 1 } { \frac { R _ { e } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { e } | } } .
$$

where $R _ { e } = 6 3 7 8 . 1 4 \mathrm { k m }$ ,theIAUequatorial radiusof theEarth(Archinaletal.2O11a)and $\mathbf { r } _ { e }$ is the Earth-Sun distance.

Similarly,the semidiameter, $s D$ ,of the Sun,in arcseconds,is

$$
S D = 2 0 6 2 6 4 . 8 0 6 2 \frac { d _ { S } } { 2 | \mathbf { r } _ { e } | } .
$$

The tabulated semidiameter is then converted to arcminutesand seconds.

# 10.6Physical Ephemeris of the Moon

In The Astronomical Almanac data for the physical ephemeris of the Moon,the sub-Earth longitude,latitude,the physical libration,andthe position angle of the lunar pole are tabulated to an accuracy of $0 \% 0 0 1$ .The Sun's selenographic latitude and co-longitude and the position angle ofthe bright limb are tabulated toanaccuracyof O:O1.The datatabulatedforthe Moon consist of the apparent position,the true distance,horizontal parallax,and semidiameter tabulated on even numbered pages D6 through D2o.The Earth'sselenographic longitude and latitude;physicallibrations inlongitude,latitude,and position angle;the Sun's selenographic co-longitude and latitude,the positionangles of the axisand bright limb,and the fraction illuminated are tabulated on theodd pages D7through D21.Beginning with the 2O13 edition, thephysical librationswill notbe tabulated.

The coordinate system used for the physical ephemeris of the Moon is the mean Earthmean polar axis coordinate system.In this coordinate system, the $z$ -axisis the mean lunarpole of rotation and the $x$ -axis isthe mean direction from the center of the Moon to the geocenter. Another commonly used coordinate system is that of the lunar principal moments of inertia.

Ontheaverage,the same hemisphere of the Moon isalwaysturned toward the Earth,but there are periodic oscillations,or librations,of the apparent position of the lunar surface that allows about $59 \%$ of the surface to be seen from the Earth.The librations are due partly to physical librations-oscillations of the actual rate of rotation of the Moon with respect to its mean rotation rate.A second,much larger component of the librations are the geocentric optical librations,which are the result of the nonuniform motion of revolution of the Moon on its elliptical path about the Earth-Moon barycenter.Both of these effects are taken into account in the computation of the Earth's selenographic longitude, $\lambda _ { e }$ ,and latitude, $\beta _ { e }$ ,and of the position angle, $c$ ,ofthe axis of rotation.The contributions from the physical librations were tabulated separately in The Astronomical Almanac through the2O12edition.Athird componentto the librations,the topocentric optical librations,is the result of the difference between the viewpoints of an observeron the surface of the Earth and ahypothetical observer at the geocenter.The topocentric optical librations may be as large as $1 ^ { \circ }$ and have important effectsonthe apparent contour of the limb.The topocentric optical librationsare not tabulated in TheAstronomical Almanac because the values depend on the observer's position.Instead, amethod for estimating the topocentric optical librations is given on page D5of The Astronomical Almanac and atthe end of this section.MICA,the Multiyear Interactive Computer Almanac,computes the topocentric optical librations from rigorous formulae.The difference between MICA and the method given for estimating the topocentric optical librations is,at most, $1 ^ { \prime \prime } \left( 0 . ^ { \circ } 0 0 0 3 \right)$ .

The selenographic coordinates of the Earth and Sun specify the point on the lunar surface where the Earth and Sun,respectively,are in the selenographic zenith.The tabulated selenographic longitude and latitude of the Earth include the total geocentric (both optical and physical) librations in longitudeand latitude,respectively.When the libration inselenographic longitude of the Earth ispositive,the mean center of the lunar disk is displaced eastward on the celestial sphere,exposing to viewa region of the western limb.When the libration inselenographic latitudeof theEarth ispositive,themeancenter of the lunardisk is displaced toward the south,and a region of the north limb is exposed to view.

The offset matrix, $\mathbf { U }$ ,from the principal moment of inertia of the Moon coordinates to the meandirection of the Earth-meanrotationaxesLE4O3is

$$
\mathbf { U } = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - 0 . 7 7 1 4 6 ) \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } ( - 7 9 . 7 0 7 7 ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( - 6 3 . 7 8 9 9 )
$$

where $\mathbf { R } _ { 1 } , \mathbf { R } _ { 2 }$ ,and $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 }$ are the Eulerian rotation matrices around the first,second,and third axes,respectively,i.e.,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] , \qquad \mathbf { R } _ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { 0 } & { \sin \theta } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { 0 } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] , } \\ & { \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \theta } & { \sin \theta } & { 0 } \\ { - \sin \theta } & { \cos \theta } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Let $\mathbf { M }$ be the selenocentric unit vector for the selenocentric longitude, $\lambda _ { M }$ , and latitude, $\beta _ { M }$ ， reckoned from the mean Earth direction-mean pole of rotation,i.e.,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { M } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \lambda _ { M } \sin \beta _ { M } } \\ { \sin \lambda _ { M } \sin \beta _ { M } } \\ { \cos \beta _ { M } } \end{array} \right] } \end{array}
$$

and $\mathbf { P }$ is the selenocentric unit vector for the selenocentric longitude, $\lambda _ { M }$ ,and latitude, $\beta _ { M }$ , reckoned from the principal moments of inertia.The flattening of the Moon is assumed to be zero (Archinal etal.2O1la),so the selenographic latitude is identical to the selenocentric latitude.The selenographicco-longitude of theSun tabulated inTheAstronomicalAlmanac is the east selenographic longitude of the morning geometric terminator. Itis determined by subtracting the selenographic longitude of the Sun from $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ and,if the result is negative, adding $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Co-longitudes of $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ , $0 ^ { \circ }$ , $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,and $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ correspond,approximately,to New Moon,First Quarter,Full Moon,and Last Quarter,respectively.The age of the Moon is computed as the time elapsed,in days,since the most recent moment at which the selenographic co-longitude of the Sun was $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ .

The position angle of the midpoint of the bright limb is the same as the position angle of the sub-solar point,and is computed in the manner described for the sub-solar point in $\ S \ 1 0 . 4 . 1$ .Like the other position angles,the position angle of the midpoint of the bright limb is measured eastward around the disk from north.The fraction of the Moon illuminated, $k$ , is the same as the phase.This quantity is computed as described in $\ S \ 1 0 . 4 . 1$ .

# 10.6.1 Calculating Librations

# 10.6.1.1Calculating Physical Librations

The physical libration of the Moon is determined from the lunar rotation angles included in LE403 (Standish et al.1995). These angles,described in Newhall and Williams (2000),are with respect to the mean equator and equinox of J2ooo.0.Thus,they must first be rotated into the reference frame of the mean equinox and ecliptic of date.

The rotationangles stored in the JPL lunar ephemerides,Fig.10.7,are: $\phi$ ,theanglealong the Earth's mean equator of J2ooo.O from the mean equinox of $\mathbf { J } 2 0 0 0 . 0 ^ { 7 }$ to the ascending node of the true lunar equator of date on the Earth's mean equator of J2000.0; $\theta$ ,the inclination of the lunar true equator of date on the Earth's mean equator of J2ooo.O;and $\psi$ ,theangle along thelunar true equator of date from the node with the Earth's mean equator of J2ooo.O to the selenographicorigin.These three anglesarequite different fromthe traditional angles $\phi _ { C }$ ,the angle along the Earth's true equator of date from the true equinox of date to the descending node of the true lunar equator of date on the Earth's true equator of date; $\theta _ { C }$ ,the inclination of the true lunar equator of date to the Earth's equator of date; and $\psi _ { C } = \tau - \sigma + F + 1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ , the anglealong the lunar true equator of date from the ecliptic of date to the selenographic prime meridian (see Fig.10.7). $F$ is the difference $L - \Omega$ ,where $L$ is the mean longitude of the Moon on the ecliptic of date.Thus,the JPL rotation angles need to be transformed from angles on the mean equator and equinox of J2Ooo.O to angles on the mean ecliptic and equinox of date.

The rotation matrix,A,from the selenographic coordinate system to the mean equator and equinox of J2000.0 is:

$$
\mathbf { A } = \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \phi ) \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( \theta ) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } ( \psi ) .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/eae13d3d6536850f910098bf927d27db55514ab203e23259b319ec3a135e65c4.jpg)  
Figure 10.7 The angles for the position of the lunar prime meridian and equator with respect to the equator and equinox of J2ooo.O as given in the JPL ephemerides and the traditional angles given with respect to the ecliptic and equinox of date

The complete rotation matrix is

$$
\mathbf { A } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { \cos \phi \cos \psi - \sin \phi \cos \theta \sin \psi } & { - \cos \phi \sin \psi - \sin \phi \cos \theta \cos \psi } & { \sin \psi \sin \theta } \\ { \sin \phi \cos \psi + \cos \phi \cos \theta \sin \psi } & { - \sin \phi \sin \psi + \cos \phi \cos \theta \cos \psi } & { - \cos \psi \sin \theta } \\ { \sin \theta \sin \psi } & { \sin \theta \cos \psi } & { \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Let the vector $\mathbf { x }$ pointtoward the intersection of the lunar equator and selenographic prime meridian,and the vector $\mathbf { z }$ point toward the lunar pole.In the principal moments of inertia, selenographic reference frame,these vectors are

$$
\mathbf { x } ^ { s e l } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] } \quad \mathbf { a n d } \quad \mathbf { z } ^ { s e l } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 0 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] } ~ .
$$

These vectors in the reference frame of the mean equator and equinox of J200o.O are

$$
\mathbf { x } ^ { J 2 0 0 0 } = \mathbf { A } \mathbf { U } \mathbf { x } ^ { P M I } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathbf { z } ^ { J 2 0 0 0 } = \mathbf { A } \mathbf { U } \mathbf { z } ^ { P M I } .
$$

The vectors are then precessed and nutated to the system of date:

$$
\mathbf { x } ^ { d a t e } = \mathbf { N } \mathbf { P } \mathbf { x } ^ { J 2 0 0 0 } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathbf { z } ^ { d a t e } = \mathbf { N } \mathbf { P } \mathbf { z } ^ { J 2 0 0 0 } .
$$

where $\mathbf { N }$ is the nutation matrix and $\mathbf { P }$ is the precession matrix.

The inclination of the lunar equator on the ecliptic of date, $\theta _ { c }$ ,is the dot product between the lunar pole and the pole of the ecliptic of date, $\mathbf { z } ^ { e c l i p }$ ,

$$
\cos \theta _ { c } = \mathbf { z } ^ { d a t e } \cdot \mathbf { z } ^ { e c l i p } = z _ { 3 } ^ { d a t e }
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { z } ^ { e c l i p } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 0 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] } ~ .
$$

The quanties $z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e }$ $z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e }$ and $z _ { 3 } ^ { d a t e }$ are the rectaga compoets f ${ \pmb x } ^ { d a t e }$ ,th selen0 le $z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e }$ pointstowards the equinox of date, $z _ { 3 } ^ { d a t e }$ pointstowardstheclipticpoleofated $z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e }$ points along the third coordinate of the right handed rectangular coordinate system defined by the other two coordinatedirections.

The angle from the mean equinox of date to the node of the lunar equator on the ecliptic is $\phi _ { c }$ .The node, $\mathbf { a }$ ,is the unit vector of the cross product of the lunar pole with the ecliptic pole,

$$
\pmb { \Omega } = \frac { \pmb { z } ^ { d a t e } \times \mathbf { z } ^ { e c l i p } } { | \pmb { z } ^ { d a t e } \times \mathbf { z } ^ { e c l i p } | } = \frac { z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e } \mathbf { i } - z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e } \mathbf { j } } { \sqrt { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } + ( z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } } }
$$

where i and $\mathbf { j }$ are the unit vectors in the ecliptic reference system of date and point towards theequinox of date and the direction at right angles to the equinox in the plane of the ecliptic, respectively. The quantity cos $\phi _ { c }$ is the dot product of the node with the unit vector pointing to the equinox,

$$
\cos \phi _ { c } = \pmb { \Omega } \cdot \mathbf { x } ^ { e q } = \pmb { \Omega } _ { 1 } = \frac { z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e } } { \sqrt { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } + ( z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } } }
$$

and sin $\phi _ { c }$ is the magnitude of the cross product,

$$
\sin \phi _ { c } = \operatorname * { d e t } \left( \pmb { \Omega } \times \mathbf { x } ^ { e q } \right) = \Omega _ { 2 } = \frac { - z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e } } { \sqrt { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } + ( z _ { 2 } ^ { d a t e } ) ^ { 2 } } }
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { x } ^ { e q } = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Finally, $\psi _ { c }$ ,the angulardistance along the lunar equator from the node of the lunar equator on the ecliptic of date to the lunar prime meridian,is determined from

$$
\cos \psi _ { c } = \pmb { \Omega } \cdot \mathbf { x } ^ { d a t e } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \sin \psi _ { c } = ( \mathbf { z } ^ { d a t e } \times \pmb { \Omega } ) \cdot \mathbf { x } ^ { d a t e } .
$$

The apparent positions of the Sun and Moon are used in the calculations to determining the physical librations of the Moon.Thus,except for the inappreciable difference between the light-time from the Sunto the Moon and from the Sunto the Earth,aberration is already included.

Priorto The Astronomical Almanac for 2O11,the analytic theory for the libration of the Moon used was that of Eckhardt (1981);Eckhardt (1982) for the physical libration of theMoon；however,the IAU value for the inclination of the mean lunar equator to the ecliptic, $1 ^ { \circ } \ 3 2 ^ { \prime } \ 3 2 . 7$ ,isused instead of Eckhardt's value.Inaddition,there are printing errors in Eckhardt's published values,so any attempt to use that theory must be done with care.Although the values of Eckhardt’s constants differ slightly from those of the IAU,the difference is ofno consequence for the precision of the tabulation,except for the inclination of the lunar equator.Included in the calculations were perturbations for all terms greater than $0 ^ { \circ } . 0 0 0 1$

# 10.6.1.2 Calculating Geocentric Optical Librations

The optical librations are determined assuming that the Moon is in a Cassini state,that is:

1.It is rotating at a uniform rate,   
2.Its mean axis of rotation is perpendicular to the mean plane of its orbit,and   
3.The mean axis pointing towards the Earth is perpendicular to both the mean plane of its orbit and the ecliptic.

Thus,given the ecliptic longitude and latitude of the Moon, $( \lambda _ { M E } , \beta _ { M E } )$ ,the position angle, $P _ { N }$ ,(Fig.10.8) of the Moon with respect to its mean node, $\Omega _ { O }$ ,(Simon et al.1994) is

$$
P _ { N } = \lambda _ { M E } - \Omega _ { O } - \Delta \psi
$$

where $\Delta \psi$ is the Earth's nutationin longitude( $\ S 6 . 6 . 3 )$ ; that is, $P _ { N }$ is measured from the node of the Moon’s orbit on the ecliptic.Rotating the unit vector denoted by $( P _ { N } , \beta _ { M E } )$ about the node of the mean plane of the lunar orbit on the ecliptic by the mean inclination of the lunar

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0012_pages_0441-0480/ocr/images/5a1db84b9c9fc7903d49d510118668573386a1e9425a8eb3f0a7686b9f3fa591.jpg)  
Figure10.8 The angles of the lunar orbit used in the determination of the apparent librations of theMoon

orbit gives $( \lambda _ { M M } , \beta _ { M M } )$ ,the longitudeand latitude of the Moon in the coordinate system of the mean plane of the lunar orbit where the origin of longitude is the node of the mean plane of the lunar orbit on the ecliptic,

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \beta _ { M M } \cos \lambda _ { M M } } \\ { \cos \beta _ { M M } \sin \lambda _ { M M } } \\ { \sin \beta _ { M M } } \end{array} \right] = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - I ) \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \beta _ { M E } \cos P _ { N } } \\ { \cos \beta _ { M E } \sin P _ { N } } \\ { \sin \beta _ { M E } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

The mean selenographic longitude and latitude $( \lambda _ { O } , \beta _ { O } )$ of the sub-Earth point are then

$$
\lambda _ { O } = \lambda _ { M M } + \Omega _ { O } - \lambda _ { M } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \beta _ { O } = - \beta _ { M M }
$$

where $\lambda _ { M }$ is the mean orbital longitude of the Moon (Simon et al.1994).

The inclination, $i$ ,and longitude of the descending node, $\Omega _ { M }$ ,of the lunar orbit on the true equator of date are:

$$
\cos i = \cos I \cos \epsilon + \sin I \sin \epsilon \cos ( \Omega + \Delta \psi )
$$

$$
\tan \Omega _ { M } = \frac { - \sin I \sin ( \Omega + \Delta \psi ) } { \cos I \sin \epsilon + \sin I \cos \epsilon \cos ( \Omega + \Delta \psi ) }
$$

where $I = 1 ^ { \circ } 3 2 ^ { \prime } 3 3 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 6$ ,the mean inclination of the lunar orbit on the ecliptic (Newhall and Williams 2000),and $\epsilon$ is the true obliquity of date.

The latitude of the lunar pole, $\beta _ { P }$ ,as seen byan observer at the Earth's geocenter is

$$
\sin \beta _ { P } = - \sin P _ { N } \cos \beta _ { M } \sin I - \sin \beta _ { M } \cos I
$$

where $\beta _ { M }$ is the lunar ecliptic latitude in the coordinates of the true equinox and ecliptic of date.

The mean position angle, $P A _ { O }$ ,of the lunar pole as seen by an observer at the Earth's geocenter is

$$
\sin { P A _ { O } } = { \frac { - \sin i \cos ( \alpha - \Omega _ { M } ) } { \cos \beta _ { P } } }
$$

where $\alpha$ is the Moon's right ascension with respect to the true equator and equinox of date.

The angles $\lambda _ { O }$ , $\beta _ { O }$ ,and $P A _ { O }$ constitute the optical librations of the Moon.Repeating Eqs.10.64through 10.69and substituting $\phi _ { c }$ for $\lambda _ { M E } , \theta _ { c }$ for $\beta _ { M E }$ ，

$$
\Omega _ { T } = ( \phi _ { c } + \psi ) c - 1 8 0 ^ { \circ }
$$

for $\Omega _ { O }$ ,and

$$
\lambda _ { T } = 1 8 0 ^ { \circ } - \psi _ { c }
$$

for $\lambda _ { M }$ gives the true librations, $\lambda _ { T } , \beta _ { T }$ ,and $P A _ { T }$

The physical librations, $\lambda _ { P } , \beta _ { P }$ ,and $P A _ { P }$ ,are then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \lambda _ { P } = \lambda _ { T } - \lambda _ { O } } \\ { \beta _ { P } = \beta _ { T } - \beta _ { O } } \\ { P A _ { P } = P A _ { T } - P A _ { O } . } \end{array}
$$

$\lambda _ { P }$ , $\beta _ { P }$ ,and $P A _ { P }$ will not be tabulated after the 2012 edition.

# 10.6.1.3 Calculating Topocentric Optical Librations

The differential corrections to be applied to the tabular geocentric librations to form the topocentric librationsare derived in Atkinson (1951).The formulae for the topocentric corrections are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta \lambda _ { m } = - H P ^ { \prime } \frac { \sin ( Q _ { m } - C _ { m } ) } { \cos b } } \\ & { \Delta \beta _ { m } = + H P ^ { \prime } \cos ( Q _ { m } - C _ { m } ) } \\ & { \Delta C _ { m } = + \sin ( \beta _ { m } - \Delta \beta _ { m } ) l - H P ^ { \prime } \sin Q \tan b } \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda _ { m }$ is the selenographic longitude, $\beta _ { m }$ is the selenographic latitude, $C _ { m }$ is the position angle of the axis of rotation, $Q _ { m }$ is the geocentric parallactic angle subtended by the Moon, and $H P ^ { \prime }$ istheparallax between the point of observation on the Earth's surface and the geocenter, commonly called the diurnal parallax.

The horizontal parallax, $H P$ ,occasionally called the geocentric horizontal parallax,is found from the Earth-Moon distance, $| \mathbf { r } _ { m } |$ ,and the equatorial radius of the Earth, $R _ { e } = { \ }$ 6378.137 km.

$$
\sin H P = { \frac { R _ { e } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { m } | } } .
$$

The geocentric (a.k.a.diurnal) parallax, $H P ^ { \prime }$ ,is computed from thehorizontal parallaxusing

$$
\sin H P ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \sin z \sin H P } { 1 - \sin z \sin H P } }
$$

where $Z$ is the geocentric zenith angle of the Moon. The values of $\boldsymbol { Q }$ and $z$ are calculated from the geocentric right ascension, $\alpha _ { m }$ ,and declination, $\delta _ { m }$ ,of theMoon:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \sin z \sin Q = \cos \Phi \sin h } \\ { \sin z \cos Q = \cos \delta _ { m } \sin \Phi - \sin \delta _ { m } \cos \Phi \cos h } \\ { \cos z = \sin \delta _ { m } \sin \Phi + \cos \delta _ { m } \cos \Phi \cos h } \end{array}
$$

where $\Phi$ is the geocentric latitude of the observer and $\mathbf { h }$ is the local hour angle of the Moon,

$$
h = \mathrm { L S T } - \alpha _ { m }
$$

whereLST isthe apparent local sidereal time.

In the interpolation of the tabular geocentric librations to the time of observation,second differences must be taken into account.The semidiameter, $S D$ ,of theMoon,in radians,is

$$
S D = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { R _ { m } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { m } | } }
$$

where $R _ { m } = 1 7 3 7 . 4 \mathrm { k m }$ is the radius of the Moon and $| \mathbf { r } _ { m } |$ is the Earth-Moon distance.

# 10.7Physical Ephemerides of the Planets

In addition to the quantities described in $\ S 1 0 . 3$ and10.4,the planetocentric orbital longitude of the Sun, $L _ { S }$ ,istabulatedforMars,Jupiter,Saturn,Uranus,andNeptuneinTheAstronomicalAlmanac,and forallof the planetsandPlutoinMICA.Thisangle ismeasured eastward

inthe body’sorbital plane from its instantaneous vernalequinox.The planet’s vernal equinox is the point where the ascending node of the planet's equator crosses the plane of its orbit about the Sun. Thus,

$$
L _ { S } = \cos ^ { - 1 } { \frac { \mathbf { r } _ { p } \cdot \mathbf { r } _ { v e } } { | \mathbf { r } _ { p } | | \mathbf { r } _ { v e } | } }
$$

where $\mathbf { r } _ { p }$ is the vector in ecliptic rectangular coordinates of the planet and $\mathbf { r } _ { v e }$ is the vector in ecliptic rectangular coordinates of the planet's instantaneous vernal equinox.The correct quadrant is determined from the sign of the third component of

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { p } \times \mathbf { r } _ { v e } .
$$

Values for $L _ { S }$ of $0 ^ { \circ }$ ， $9 0 ^ { \circ }$ ， $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,and $2 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ correspond to the beginning of spring,summer, autumn,and winter,respectively,in the body's northern hemisphere.

Unless otherwise noted,the positions of the planetary poles and the prime meridians andtheplanetaryradiiand flatteningsare thosein Archinal etal.(2O1la).Thevalues for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ ,the magnitude of the planets at a distance of 1 au and at a phase angle of $0 ^ { \circ }$ ,are from de Vaucouleurs (197O),and the values for the phase coeficient, $\Delta m ( i )$ ,are from Harris (1961).

# 10.7.1 Mercury

SinceMercury’sorbit about the Sun is interior to the orbit of the Earth,Mercury is seen to go through the entire range of phase angles.Prior to the edition for 2Oo5,thevalues for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ and $\Delta m ( i )$ arefrom de Vaucouleurs(197O) and Harris (1961),respectively.For the 2005 and 2oo6 editions of The Astronomical Almanac the phase coeficient was replaced by a quadratic expression determined by fiting the photometric observations of Danjon (1949); Danjon(1954) using the de Vaucouleurs (1970) correction from $m$ to $V$ forMercury (Hilton 2005,private communication).The expression for $\Delta m ( i )$ is valid for phaseangles inthe range $3 ^ { \circ } < i < 1 2 3 ^ { \circ }$ .For $i < 3 ^ { \circ }$ and $i > 1 2 3 ^ { \circ }$ ,Mercury was too close to the Sun to be observed by Danjon.The uncertainty in the predicted visual magnitudeis about O.12 mag.Beginning with the edition for 2Oo7,the values for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ and $\Delta m ( i )$ are determined from an analysis of theMallama etal. (2Oo3)LASCO/SOHO and ground-based observations of Mercury (Hilton 2005).For these data thevalid range in phase for $\Delta m ( i )$ is $2 ^ { \circ }$ to $1 7 0 ^ { \circ }$ ,and the uncertainty in the predicted visual magnitude isabout O.1O mag.Magnitudes are not printedin The Astronomical Almanac when Mercury's phase is outside the valid range.The cartographic prime meridian ofMercury is defined so that the $2 0 ^ { \circ }$ meridian passes through the center of the crater Hun Kal.

# 10.7.2 Venus

Venus rotates in a retrograde fashion,thus the sign for the motion of the prime meridian is negative.LikeMercury,the flattening of Venus is negligible; so the equatorial and polar radii of Venus are identical.

Also likeMercury,Venus'orbit isinterior to the Earth'sorbit,soit hasaphase angle that varies from $0 ^ { \circ }$ to $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Priorto the edition for 2Oo5,the values for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ and $\Delta m ( i )$ are from deVaucouleurs (1970)and Harris (1961),respectively.For the 2005and 2006 editions of The

Astronomical Almanac the phase coefficient was replaced by aquadratic expression determined by fitting the photometric observations of Danjon (1949) using the de Vaucouleurs (1970) correction from $m$ to $V$ forVenus (Hilton 2Oo5,private communication). The expression for $\Delta m ( i )$ is valid for phase angles in the range $0 \% 9 < i < 1 7 0 \%$ For $i < 0 . 9$ and $i > 1 7 0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \circ } 7$ ,Venusis too close to the Sun to be observed photometrically.The uncertainty in the predicted visual magnitude is about $0 . 0 5 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ .Beginning with the edition for 2007, the values for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ and $\Delta m ( i )$ aredetermined from an analysisof the Mallama (2005) ground-based observations of Venus (Hilton 2Oo5).For these data the valid range in phase for $\Delta m ( i )$ is $2 ^ { \circ } . 2 < i < 1 6 3 ^ { \circ } . 6$ .Theuncertaintyinthepredictedvisual magnitudeisabout 0.05 mag.Magnitudes are not printed in The Astronomical Almanac when Venus'sphaseis outside the valid range.

# 10.7.3 Mars

Although the phase angle of Mars can be as large as $4 7 ^ { \circ }$ ,the current phase relation for its magnitude contains only a single linear term.Mars’surface contains alarge numberof visible markings.These markingscause thebrilliancy of Mars to vary about O.15 mag.over its period of rotation.Theamplitudeof thevariationisnot fixed,however,andismodified by other factors such as the transparency of the atmosphere due to planet-wide sandstorms. There appears to be a variation over the Martian year as well.All these additional factors can cause variations in the visual magnitude of up to $0 . 1 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ .These are not accounted for in the tabulated magnitudes.Thecartographic primemeridianofMarsis defined so that it passes through the center of the crater Airy-O.

# 10.7.4 Jupiter

Multiple longitude systemsare defined forJupiter.Each systemcorrespondstoa different apparent rate of motion.System II, which applies to the origin of radio emissions on Jupiter, is the preferred system.System Iapplies to the mean atmospheric equatorial rotation of the planet.System II applies to the mean atmospheric rotation north of the south component of the north equatorial belt and south of the north component of the south equatorial belt of Jupiter. The rotation rates for both System Iand System II are uncertain and subject to change.The sub-Earth and sub-solarlongitudes tabulated in The Astronomical Almanac are based on System III.

Jupiter is the most luminous of the planets.The value used for $V ( 1 , 0 )$ istaken from Irvine et al.(1968).Variations in its brilliancy with rotation are small, $\sim 0 . 0 1$ mag. and irregular. Also,there are long-period variations correlated with changes in the surface features of the planet.

# 10.7.5 Saturn

For Saturn only System II [observed rotation of radio emissions;Desch and Kaiser(1981)] rotations are defined.Galopeau and Lecacheux (2Ooo) noted that the apparent period of Saturn’sradio emissions vary on the order of a percent over periods of years.The angular momentum of the planet is orders of magnitude too large to allow for such large changes in the rotation period over such a short period of time.Gurnett et al.(2OO7) conclude that there is a slippage between the System II rotation rate arising in Saturn's plasma disk,and that its internal rotation is the source forthis discrepancy.Thus,the useof Saturn'sradio emissions as an indicator for its rotation rate isin question.

Like Jupiter,the mean rotation rate of the atmosphere is uncertain.The previously defined System Irotation system has been found to be of litte use since the Voyager encounters with Saturn,and is no longer supported by the IAU.

Because of the high brilliance of Saturn's rings,the brilliance of the planet alone is not well known.Thus,the combined planet-ring magnitude and surface brightness are tabulated. The phase coefficient for Saturn includes the rings.The visual magnitude in Table 10.6 is the extrapolated value for the planet alone at1au and $0 ^ { \circ }$ phase angle.There is,however,an additional term for variation in the brightness of the planet with the aspect of the rings:

$$
V _ { r } = - 2 . 6 0 | \sin e | + 1 . 2 5 \sin ^ { 2 } e
$$

where $V _ { r }$ is the visual magnitude of the rings and $e$ istheSaturnocentric sub-Earth latitude. There is litle evidence for variations of the magnitude in the brightness of Saturn with rotation or over extended periods of time.

# 10.7.6 Uranus

Like Venus,Uranus’rotation is retrograde.The latitude of Uranus’north polewith respect to the invariable plane is $6 \% 9 2$ ,butits declination with respect to the equator is-15:18.Thus, Uranus’north pole is in the south celestial hemisphere.There is evidencefor variation of the brightness of Uranuswith rotation,but it has not been confirmed.A smallterm with a period of84 years isexpected in Uranus’magnitude due to the flattening of the planet and thehigh inclination of its equator to its orbital path.Itis also possble that thereare changes in Uranus’albedo with time.For example,Rages,Hammel,and Freidson (2oo4) document changes in the appearance of Uranus’south pole over the period from 1994 to 2oo2.Just as forSaturn,only the rotation rate for radio emissions (System III) is defined for Uranus.

# 10.7.7 Neptune

The phase angle of Neptune at greatest elongation is only19,so the variation in brightness of the planet from its phase angle is insignificant.There is some evidence of variation in the brightness of Neptune with its rotation.There is no evidence of long-period variations in its brightness.Only the System II rotation system for radio sources is defined forNeptune.

# 10.8Physical Ephemerides of the Dwarf Planets

The apparent visual magnitudes of Ceres,Pluto,and Eris are found along with their geocentric astrometric positions beginning on page G5 on years when there is an opposition. In addition,physical ephemerides forCeresandPlutoat tenday intervals throughout the year arefound in Section G immediately after the positional ephemerides of the dwarf planets. These ephemerides tabulate the apparent visual magnitude,phase angle,planetocentric orbital longitude of the Sun, $L _ { S }$ ,sub-Earth longitudeand latitude and position angle of the positive pole.

The positions for the positive pole and the prime meridian of Ceres and Pluto are derived fromArchinal etal.(201la);Archinal et al. (2O11b).The motion of the prime meridian and the position of the pole for Pluto are based on the assumptions that Charon's orbital plane is coincidental with Pluto's equatorial plane and that the orbital motion of Charon is synchronous with Pluto's rotation.Pluto's flattening is unknown due to its great distance from the Sun and small size,but is presumed to be small because of its slow rotation rate.

The apparent magnitudes of Ceres and Eris are determined using the Tholen (2009) $H$ and $G$ parameters in thealgorithmdescribed in $\ S 1 0 . 4 . 3$ Thevaluesof $H$ and $G$ are tabulated on page G3.The apparent magnitudes of Pluto are determined using the algorithm described in $\ S \ 1 0 . 3 . 1$ .Thevaluesof $V _ { 0 }$ and $\Delta m$ are tabulated in Table10.6.

The givenbrilliancy at a distance of1auand a phase angle of $0 ^ { \circ }$ is the combined magnitude ofPluto and its satellite,Charon.The brilliancy inHarris (1961) is that of the combined Pluto-Charon system.Inactuality,it has changed over timeas different albedo features ofthe planet become visible (see Marcialis 1988).Binzel and Mulholland (1984) determined the phase coefficient.The combined magnitude of the Pluto-Charon system changes with rotation due tothe differingalbedosand surface markings of the two bodies.The rotational change in magnitude,like the mean magnitude,hasalso been changingwith timeas different albedo features of the planet and satelliteare exposed to the Earth in Pluto's orbit around the Sun. The rotational difference between the minimum and maximum brilliancy was $0 . 1 1 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ . in 1954(Harris1961) and $0 . 2 9 1 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ .in1981(Marcialis1988).Theperiod of therotational variations is 6.3867 days.During the eclipse season the Pluto-Charon mutual events changes in the magnitude as much as $0 . 5 0 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ .(Tholen,Buie,andSwift1987) were observed.The prime meridian of Pluto is defined to fall along the mean vector connecting Pluto to Charon.

# 10.9Physical Ephemerides of the Satellites

Aside from the Moon,the disks of all of the satellites have apparent diameters of less than $2 ^ { \prime \prime }$ when viewed from the Earth or nearby space.They are so small that they are difficult toobserved directly,except from interplanetary probes.Thus,the amount of information tabulated for the physicalephemerides is lessthan thatfor the planets.These dataare limited to the satellite's dimensions,geometric albedo, $V ( 1 , 0 )$ ,and the $B - V$ and $U - B$ colors, if available.These data are found on pagesF3andF5of The Astronomical Almanac and in Tables10.3and 1O.7 here.Unless otherwise stated,the values for the sidereal period of the satelitesare from Archinal etal.(2O1la),and the value for the phase coefficients are from Harris (1961).

The recent proliferation of known satellites associated with the gas giant planets has lead to the development of selection criteria to determine which satelites are tobe included in the datatables presented on pagesF2-F5.The mean apparent visual magnitude of the tabulated satellites must not be fainter than 23.0,and the satelite must have anIAU approved Roman numeral and name.Satellites that have not received IAU approval are considered“works in progress.”A satellitemay be included atalater time,provided itsmeanapparent visual magnitude is not fainter than 23.O.A more complete version of these tables is found at The Astronomical Almanac Online at URL[1].

# 10.9.1 Satelltes of Mars

Mars'two naturalsatellites,Phobos andDeimos,are both triaxial ellipsoids,with theirlongest axispointed toward the planet.The source for the reference surfaces of these satellites is Pollack etal.(1973)whousedMariner9measurements.

Table 10.9 The rotation parameters for Mars’satellites   

<table><tr><td>Satellite</td><td>Parameterand Value</td></tr><tr><td>IPhobos</td><td>α0=317:68-0:108T+179 sin M1</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>δ0=52:90-0:061T-1:08 cosM1</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>W=35:06+1128:844 5850t+8864T²-1:42 sin M1-078 sin M2</td></tr><tr><td>I Deimos</td><td>α0=316:65-0:108T+2:98 sin M3</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>δ0=5352-0:061T-178cosM3</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>W=79:41+2851618970t-0:520T²-2:58 sin M3+019 cos M3</td></tr><tr><td colspan="2">The values for Mn are:</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>M=169:51-0:4357640 t</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>M=192:93+1128:409 6700t +8864 T²</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>M3=53:47-0:0181510t</td></tr></table>

Both satelites have rotation periods that are synchronous with their orbital periods.Both also have secular accelerations in their rotation rates,which appearas quadratic terms in the motion of their prime meridians.These acceleration terms arise from secular changes inthe semimajor axes of their orbits.The differing signs for the acceleration terms in the motion of the satelites’prime meridians indicate that Phobos’orbit is contractingwith time while Deimos'orbit is expanding.The rotation parameters for the Martian satellites are given in Table10.9.

The masses of Phobos and Deimos were determination by Sjogren(1983） from the perturbations of the Viking Orbiters during their close flybys of the satellites.Information on the magnitudes of Phobosand Deimos,their albedos,and their colors are derived from Vikingphotometryof the satelites that has been analyzedbyKlaasen,Duxbury,and Veverka (1979).Because the satelltes are very near Mars,information on the phase coeficients for both satellites and the $( U - B )$ color for Phobos are useless for ground-based observations and are not given.

# 10.9.2 Satellites of Jupiter

Therotation parameters for the Galilean satelites [Io (JI),Europa (JII),Ganymede (JII), and Callisto (JIV)],alongwith Amalthea (JV),Thebe (JXIV),Adrastea (JXV),andMetis (JXVI)] are given in Table10.10.All these satellites have mean periods of rotation that are synchronous with their orbital periods.

The values for the visual magnitude at1 au and $0 ^ { \circ }$ phaseangle,thealbedos,and the colors of theJovian satellitesare fromMorrison (1982).The Galilean sateliteshave been observed well enough that well determined phase relations,in Table10.6,have been derived for them.

The reference surfaces for Amalthea (Smith etal.1979);the Galilean satellites (Davies etal.1979);andJXIV,JXV,and JXVI;Burns (1986) were derived from measurementsof theVoyager spacecraft pictures of the satelites.Estimates of the sizes of the outer Jovian satelitesare from Tholen and Zellner(1984)and are based on the albedos ofJVIand JVII (Himalia and Elara).

Galilean satellite masses are calculated from the perturbation in the paths of the Pioneer and Voyager spacecraft flybys (Campbell and Synott 1985).The masses of the rest of the satellites are based upon their individual sizesand the mean density of the compositions inferredfrom theiralbedos.

Three of the Galilean satellites have cartographic coordinates defined by surface craters. The cartographic definitions are given in Table 10.11.

# 10.9.3Satellites of Saturn

Rotation parameters for Saturn's major satellites are given in Table 10.12.

Wisdom et al.(1984) have shown that one of the major Saturnian satelites,Hyperion, maybe tumbling chaotically rather than being in synchronous rotation.This tumblingis a result of Hyperion’s triaxial shape and its rather elliptical orbit with a semimajor axis near the4:3 resonancewith Titan.

The reference surfaces for the larger satellites are derived from Smith et al. (1982).The sizes of the smaller satellitesare derived from their geometric albedos (Thomas etal.1983).

Morrison,Owen,and Sonderblom(1986) determined the magnitudes,albedos,and colors ofSaturn's satellites.Iapetus is especially interesting,because the leading hemisphere is over two magnitudes dimmer than its trailing hemisphere.Windorn(195O) givesa parametric equation for the brightness (not the magnitude) of the satellite of

$$
B = 0 . 5 7 1 - 0 . 4 2 9 \sin { k }
$$

where $k$ is its orbital longitude,with the maximum occurring at greatest western elongation. More recent papers (e.g.,Morrison et al.1975) have shown that this expression isa good first order expression of Iapetus'brightness.

Tyleretal.(1981);Tyler et al.(1982) determined the tabulated masses for Mimas, Enceladus,Tethys,Dione,Rhea,Titan,and Iapetus from the deflections of the Voyager trajectories.The masses of Enceladus and Mimas are of lower accuracy and are contradicted byresonance theories (e.g.,Greenberg 1984).Six of the major Saturnian satelltes have cartographic coordinates defined by surface craters.The cartographic definitions are given in Table 10.13.

# 10.9.4 Satellites of Uranus

The rotation parameters for15of the known satelites of Uranus are given in Table 10.14.

Theradii for the Uranian satellites (Smith etal.1986)are derived from measurements of Voyager photographs.The masses forthe five large satelites,Viellet(1983) are derived from mutual orbital perturbations.Smith et al.(1986)also determined the magnitudes,albedos, and colors for the large Uranian satelltes from Voyager2 photometry.

# 10.9.5 Satellites of Neptune

The physical ephemerides of six of the known satellites of Neptune are included in The Astronomical Almanac.The rotation parameters for those satelites are given in Table 10.15.

The mass,radius,and colors ofTritonare determined from Voyager2data.Littleis known of Nereid except its magnitude at 1 au and $0 ^ { \circ }$ phase angle (Morrison and Cruikshank 1974).

The diameter is based on an assumed albedo of O.4.Recent studies by Williams et al.(1991) of Nereid indicate that it may have a non-synchronous rotation.

# 10.9.6 Satellites of Pluto

Charon,Pluto’sonly large satellite,isassumed to orbit in the plane of Pluto's equator and is in synchronous rotation.The position of its north pole and prime meridian are given in Table10.16.

The mass,diameter,and magnitude of Charon are based on observations of Charon-Pluto eclipses (Dunbar and Tedesco 1986).

# Eclipses of the Sun and Moon

ALAND.FIALA,JOHNA.BANGERT&STEVENA.BELL

# 11.1 Introduction

All bodies orbiting in the solar system cast a shadow away from the Sun.In the most general terms,an eclipse occurs when the shadow of one body falls upon another and temporarily blocks out a portion of the solar illumination.An eclipse is distinct from an occultation, whichis ablock inthe line of sight.Before this distinction was understood,however,a long historyof observation from Earth created different namesfor thephenomena,dependingon what objects were involved.The shadow of the Moon falling upon the Earth was calld a solar eclipse;the shadow of the Earth falling upon theMoon was called a lunar eclipse;and the shadow ofa planet falling upon the Earth was called a transit (across the Sun).By strict application of these definitions,a solar eclipse is reallyan occultation.

The basic methods by which eclipses of the Sun and Moon and transits of Venus and Mercury are predicted and described were introduced by Bessel and extensively developed by Chauvenet (1891).The discussion here will be limited to describing the calculation of datathat are necessaryto observeany of these phenomena from the Earth.

Bessel's method of calculating eclipse predictions is based on the following concept:A body casts ashadow,the axis of which isaline extended fromthe centerof the Sun through the center of the body.Observations,by necessity,are constrained to the topocentric coordinate system.However,thegeometryand kinematicsare easiertodescribeina systemoriented to the cone of the shadow—that is,one in which the $Z$ -axisis eitherparallel to,orcoincident with,the shadow axis,and the $x y$ -plane passes through the center of the body on which the shadow falls.This plane is also known as the Besselian plane.A description of the progress of the eclipse iscalculated in this coordinate system,and then is transformed to a topocentric systemasnecessary.Elementsdescribe thegeometryof theconfigurationasa function of time; circumstances describe distinct, observable events by time and place.

Diagrams in treatises on eclipses by necessity are exaggerated to illustrate points.Hence, thediagrams tend to disguise thefactthat,inreality,the bodiesinvolved arevery small, farapart,and movingrelatively slowly.Thus,the shadowscast arelongand slender,there are many small angles,and therelative short-term motionsare very nearly linear. These qualities were used to advantageby people equipped only with tablesand a calculator,as it was necessary and adequate to use approximations and intermediate trigonometric constructs. In the eraof computers,the use of approximations and simplifying constructs is reduced but not eliminated.Also,itis helpful to newer generations of astronomers to discuss spherical trigonometry by incorporating more vector and matrix notation instead of direction cosines.

# 11.1.1Eclipse Data Available from the Nautical Almanac Offices

# 11.1.1.1 International Agreements

Eversince astronomical ephemeridesand almanacs have been published,eclipse information has been included.This was a natural action because the extensive calculations required arebased on predicted ephemerides.In modern times,the situationis much eased, but the Nautical Almanac Offices of the U.S.Naval Observatory and the United Kingdom continue to provide information on every eclipse of each year.The informationconforms to International Astronomical Union (IAU) standards and is not changed without adequate notice to the IAU. This may cause some misunderstandings (e.g.,in the value of $k$ ,describedin $\ S \ 1 1 . 1 . 2 \}$ ,or occasional diffculties when a convention changes (for instance,longitude is now considered positive to the east of Greenwich).Some changes that improve production without changing the information are adopted by decisions within the Nautical Almanac Ofices.Other special publications for individual eclipses are designed to assist the scientific observer as much as possible.Since 1949,at the requestof the appropriate Commissions of theIAU,the principal circumstances of all solar eclipses have been routinely calculated and made available several yearsin advance of the publication of the annual almanac.In the spirit of this agreement,a web site,EclipsesOnline found at URL[2],has been created to provide eclipse information covering the period A.D.1501-21oo.Further information can be found in $\ S 1 1 . 5$

# 11.1.1.2 Data Providedin The Astronomical Almanac

Elementsand general circumstancesaregiven in TheAstronomical Almanac forall solar and lunar eclipses,including the penumbral lunar eclipses that occur during the year.For the solar eclipses,maps are given from which approximate local circumstances may be obtained forany particular place,and the Besselian elements are presented in polynomial form for the calculation of accurate local circumstances for any point on or above the surface of the Earth.For total and annular solar eclipses,thelatitudes and longitudes of points on the central line and on the northern and southern limits,the duration of the total or annular phase,and thealtitude and azimuth of the Sun on the central line are tabulated.These are presented in longitude order at intervalsof one degree.Forlunareclipses,times ofthe major circumstances are presented on a diagram showing the Moon's path with respect to the Earth's penumbral andumbral shadows.Additionally,a map showing the eclipse's visibility on the surface of theEarth is given.Since the circumstances and their times are the same for all parts of the Earth,any particular phase is visiblefromany point at which the Moonisabove the horizon. Furtherdetailsaredescribed in $\ S 1 1 . 5 . 2$ .

For years in which atransitof Mercury or Venus occurs,general circumstances and a map aregiven,by which approximate local circumstancesmay easily be estimated.Formulaeare also provided for the computation of more accurate local circumstances.The presented data aremore fullydescribed in $\ S \ 1 1 . 6$ .

The information that appears in The Astronomical Almanac is prepared approximately three years before the event.A provisional value of $\Delta T$ to the nearest whole second is assigned,so that the time argument is provisional Universal Time (UT) or,more precisely, provisional UT1 (see $\ S \ 3 . 2 . 2$ ).Moredetailsare given in $\ S 1 1 . 1 . 2$ and $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 6 . 3$

Thepredictions given in The Astronomical Almanac takeno account of the effects of refraction,although refraction may be significant for the reduction of observations intended to give precise positions.The Besselian elements,however,are rigorously independent of refraction.

Table11.1 Selected USNO eclipse circulars   

<table><tr><td>Cir.No.</td><td>Date</td><td>Title</td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>1949 July 18</td><td>Tracks of total solar eclipses in 1952,1953,1954</td></tr><tr><td>2</td><td>1949 September 19</td><td>Tracks of total solar eclipses in 1955,1956,1957</td></tr><tr><td>16</td><td>1950May 4</td><td>Tracksof total solar eclipses in 1958,1959,1960</td></tr><tr><td>40</td><td>1952December8</td><td>Annular and partial solar eclipses in 1955,1956,and 1957.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td>Non-central total eclipses of 1957</td></tr><tr><td>53</td><td>1954 August 20</td><td>Annular eclipses in 1958,1959</td></tr><tr><td>59</td><td>1955June 28</td><td>Solar eclipses,1960-1963</td></tr><tr><td>85</td><td>1958 April 16</td><td>Solareclipses,1963-1967</td></tr><tr><td>89</td><td>1960 June 15</td><td>Solar eclipses,1968-1970</td></tr><tr><td>101</td><td>1964July 29</td><td>Solareclipses,1971-1975</td></tr><tr><td>113</td><td>1966December 15</td><td>Solar eclipses,1976-1980</td></tr><tr><td>142</td><td>1973 April 20</td><td>Solareclipses,1981-1990</td></tr><tr><td>170</td><td>1986November7</td><td>Solareclipses,1991-2000</td></tr></table>

# 11.1.1.3 Data Provided in Other Publications

Material from the section of The Astronomical Almanac on solar and lunar eclipses and transits is also published,inadvance of publication of TheAstronomical Almanac itself,in thesmall bookletAstronomical Phenomena.Descriptions of eclipses and solar eclipse maps arealso published in The Nautical Almanac.Both publicationsare issued annually by the U.S.Naval Observatory and HerMajesty'sNautical Almanac Office.

In1949,the U.S.Naval Observatory began a publication series called the U.S.Naval Observatory Circulars.The series transmited astronomical data resulting from continuing programs but not available in any other publications.Two of the continuing programs that contributed many issues to this series are solar eclipse predictions and sunspot counts.There were two kinds of solar eclipse circulars.First,there were circulars issued well in advance that gave justthe principal circumstances ofall solar eclipses inamultiple-year period.These are listed inTable11.1.

Second,the Observatory issued circulars for specific solar eclipses;these circulars contained extensive information for observers to choose a site and calculate accurate local circumstances.Before the circulars began in 1949,this informationwas issued in Supplements totheannual AmericanEphemeris.Until1991,itwasthepolicyof theU.S.Naval Observatory to issue acircular for each total solar eclipse,with very few exceptions,and for selected annular solar eclipses that had some observationally interesting features.Typically these circulars contained general information and circumstances,Besselian elements,a discussion of anticipated meteorological conditions along the eclipse path,local circumstances for selected locations,the lunar limb profileand limb corrections,the path of the central phase tabulated by time and longitude at sea level andat flying altitudes,and detailed mapsof the portion of the path overland.The U.S.Naval Observatory planned to release each circular12 to18 months in advance of a particular eclipse and to incorporate the best value of $\Delta T$ available at the time of preparation.However,the lead time and contents were subject to regular review with respect to meeting the reported needs of the scientific community.In 1992,the USNO eclipse circularswere discontinued.NASA has produced aseries of Eclipse Bulletins covering eclipses from 1994 to 2010 containing predictions and meteorological information for theprofessional and lay community.Material isalso availableat URL[2] and URL[3],the formerusing techniques based on those used in The Astronomical Almanac.

# 11.1.2 Corrections to the Ephemerides

The basic quantities for calculations of solar and lunar eclipses are:the apparent right ascension $( \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } )$ ,declination $( \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } )$ ,anddistance $( R _ { \mathrm { s } } )$ oftheSun,and theapparent right ascension $( \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } )$ ,declination $( \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } )$ ,anddistance( $( R _ { \mathrm { m } } )$ of the Moon,for everyhourof Terrestrial Time(TT) during the eclipse;and the ephemeris sidereal time at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } ~ \mathrm { T T }$ for the day of the eclipse and for the following day.The apparent places of the Sun and the Moon are computed rigorously using the same ephemerides for the Sunand theMoon asare used for The Astronomical Almanac,and using the reduction methods described elsewhere in this book (see Chapter 7). Atthe time of this writing,the ephemerides are based upon the DE405/LE4O5 produced by the JetPropulsionLaboratory(see Chapter 8).The ephemeris sidereal time at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { T T }$ ,whichis the local apparent sidereal time on the ephemeris meridian,is virtually the same numericallyas the (Greenwich) apparent siderealtime at $0 ^ { \mathrm { h } } \mathrm { U T }$ ,astabulated in TheAstronomicalAlmanac.
