Gravitational ephemerides refer to the positions of the centers of mass of the bodies concerned.Eclipses,however,are governed by the positions of the centers offigures of the Sun and the Moon.The center of figure ofthe Moon does not coincide with its center of mass; toallow forthis difference,empirical corrections are made to the mean equatorial coordinates of the Moon,referred to the center of mass of the Earth.Unpublished investigations have shown that the corrections depend upon the lunar librations at the time of an eclipse.The range of the corrections is less than $0 { : } 5$ ,and for thecalculationsin TheAstronomical Almanac, only the minimum corrections are applied.These are $\Delta \lambda = + 0 . 7 7 5 0$ and $\Delta \beta = - 0 . 7 2 5$ for the LE405 lunar ephemeris. The corrections are made during the apparent place computation by rotating the mean equatorial geocentric coordinates of the Moon into the ecliptic system, applying the corrections in the ecliptic system,and rotating back into the equatorial system. The corrections are applied as constants for each eclipse.

The semidiameters of the Sun and the Moon used in the calculation of eclipses do not include irradiation.The adopted semidiameter of the Sun $( s _ { \mathrm { s } } )$ is computed directly from the ephemeris of the Sun,thereby using the ephemeris constants.However,the apparent semidiameter of theMoon is calculated by putting its sine equal to $k$ sin $\pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ,where $\pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ is the horizontal parallaxand $k$ ,theratioof theMoon’sradiustothe equatorialradiusof theEarth,is anadopted constant.The IAU adopted a value of $k$ $( k = 0 . 2 7 2 5 0 7 6 )$ inAugust1982.Before that time,two different values of $k$ were used in computing a solar eclipse.The value taken forthe lunar ephemeris was used for the most part,but in practice it was too large forthe total phase in total solar eclipses because of the effects of the rough lunar limb.As an approximate correction for this effect,a smaller value of $k$ Was adopted solely for calculating the duration on the central line of total solar eclipses.This smaller value caused numerical inconsistencies aswell as misunderstandings on the part of users of the information.Therefore,when the new value was adopted in 1982 to conform to the value used in occultation predictions,it wasagreed implicitly that limb effectsare no longer accounted for,but areaveraged,and if an observer considers them to be important,then corrections must be calculated and applied separately.Itispossible for these effects toadvance orretard predicted second-orthirdcontact times on the central line by as much as two seconds apiece.The same limb corrections can be applied in the case of annular solar eclipses,or even lunar eclipses,but the effects will notbe observable.It is only in the case of a total solar eclipse that the presence or absence of anylight from the solar disk is easily detected and is hence critical to delicate observations.

The calculation of the occurrence of eclipses does not depend upon the timescale for the portions restricted to the spatial relationships of the Earth,Moon,and Sun.However,for calculation of phenomenaas they are related to the surface of the Earth for time and place of visibility,itis necessary touse Universal Time(UT1) in the best approximationpossible. This approximation is achieved byadjusting the ephemerides at the start of the calculations. The predictions in The Astronomical Almanac were given in ephemeris time (ET) until1981, when $\Delta T$ had become large enough that it was deemed more usefulto publish the predictions in provisional Universal Time (see $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 6 . 3$ for details).

# 11.2 The Occurrence of Lunar and Solar Eclipses

# 11.2.1 Overview

The purpose of this section is to develop conditions that can be used to determine whether an eclipse will occur at a particular conjunction or opposition of the Sun and Moon.The approach is,first,to develop a general expression for the geocentric least angular separation of the Moon and either the Sun or the antisolar point,when one of the latter is in the vicinity of one of the nodes of the Moon's orbit. Second,expressions for the apparent sizes of the shadowsare found.Finally,discrimination testsare established.Specifically,todevelop the conditions foralunar eclipse,the apparent sizes ofthe umbral and penumbral shadows of the Earth are compared to the geocentric least angular separation of the Moon and the antisolar point.Todevelop the conditions fora solar eclipse,expressions forthe geocentric least angular separation of the Sun and the Moon are derived from the geometry of each particular type of solareclipse and are compared with the geocentricapparent angular semidiameters.

# 11.2.2 Geocentric Least Angular Separation

Consider the Sun and the Moon ata time ofconjunction or opposition in longitude.In order for an eclipse to occur,the Sun (or the antisolar point)and the Moon must each be near the same one of the nodes of the Moon’s orbit.Thus,the ecliptic latitude of the Moon ( $\beta _ { \mathrm { m } }$ )will besmall,and plane geometrymaybeused.

This situation is illustrated in Fig.11.1.This isa general representation; $\beta _ { \mathrm { m } }$ may be positive (above the ecliptic) or negative (below the ecliptic).Correspondingly, $N$ may be either the ascending or descending node of the lunar orbit.For a lunar eclipse, $s$ represents theantisolarpoint at opposition.Forasolar eclipse, $s$ represents the center of the Sun at conjunction.For either case, $M$ represents the center of the Moon.Take the absolute value of $\beta _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ,which is the latitude of the Moon at opposition fora lunar eclipse,or at conjunction for a solar eclipse.The primed values, $M ^ { \prime }$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ ,represent positions at a time when angle $\boldsymbol { S } \boldsymbol { M } \boldsymbol { S } ^ { \prime } = \boldsymbol { \gamma }$ ,and theangular separation of $M ^ { \prime }$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ is $\sigma$ $I$ is the inclination of the Moon’s orbit to the ecliptic.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/1168154695d71baa2cb658e9c54b880bf51a1faba3031068c3873b1f0c703262.jpg)  
Figure11.1 Geometric construct for determining whether any kind of eclipse will occur when the Moon is near one of the nodes of its orbit on the ecliptic.In this figure, $N$ is the descending node as seen from outside the orbit,looking toward Earth.Let $S$ represent the Sun or its antisolar point,and $M$ the Moon,at conjunction or opposition,respectively, when the separation is the lunar latitude $\beta _ { \mathrm { m } }$ .At some later time when the two points have moved to $S ^ { \prime } , M ^ { \prime }$ ,then the instantaneous separation is $\sigma$

We wish now to derive an expression for $\sigma$ in terms of known quantities.Define a quantity $\lambda$ ,aratio of the longitudinal motions:

$$
\lambda = \frac { S P } { S S ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \mathrm { M o o n ~ ^ { \prime } s ~ m o t i o n ~ i n ~ l o n g i t u d e } } { \mathrm { S u n ~ ^ { \prime } s ~ m o t i o n ~ i n ~ l o n g i t u d e } } .
$$

From the geometry:

$$
\sigma ^ { 2 } = ( S ^ { \prime } P ) ^ { 2 } + ( M ^ { \prime } P ) ^ { 2 }
$$

where

$$
S ^ { \prime } P = S P - S S ^ { \prime } = ( \lambda - 1 ) \beta _ { \mathrm { { m } } } \tan \gamma .
$$

Thus,

$$
\sigma ^ { 2 } = \beta _ { \mathrm { { m } } } ^ { 2 } [ ( \lambda - 1 ) ^ { 2 } \tan ^ { 2 } \gamma + ( 1 - \lambda \tan I \tan \gamma ) ^ { 2 } ] .
$$

In order to find the value of $\gamma$ at which $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is a minimum,take the derivative of Eq.11.5 with respect to $\gamma$ and set it equal to zero.After some algebra,the result is:

$$
\tan \gamma = \frac { \lambda \tan I } { ( \lambda - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } \tan ^ { 2 } I } .
$$

Substitute Eq.11.6 into Eq.11.5 to obtain:

$$
\sigma = \beta _ { \mathrm { m } } ( \lambda - 1 ) [ ( \lambda - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } \tan ^ { 2 } I ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

This equation may be simplified by the following change of variables.Let

$$
\tan { \cal I ^ { \prime } } \equiv \frac { \lambda } { \lambda - 1 } \tan { \cal I } .
$$

$I ^ { \prime }$ is the angle at which the moving Moon would appearto intersectthe ecliptic from the point of view of an observer on the moving point $s$ .Then

$$
\sigma = \beta _ { \mathrm { { m } } } \cos { I ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Equations 11.7 and11.9 give expressions for the geocentric least angular separation between the Moon and the Sun at conjunction,or the geocentric least angular separation of the Moon and the antisolar point at opposition of the Sun and Moon.

# 11.2.3Occurrence of Lunar Eclipses

Now that we have an expression fortheleast angular separation of the Moon andthe antisolar point near opposition,let us examine how to use it to test for the occurrence of alunar eclipse. From the geometry of the shadow cone,we shallfind a relationship between the size of the cone and other well-known quantities.

# 11.2.3.1 Apparent Semidiameters of the Shadow Cones

The penumbral shadow cone of the Earth is defined by the interior tangents between the Sun and the Earth.Figure11.2 illustrates the geometry.In the diagram, $s$ is the center of the Sun, $E$ is the center of the Earth,and $M$ is a point on the penumbral cone at the distance of theMoon.The line $L M$ isthe semidiameter of the coneat thedistanceof theMoon. The known quantities are angle $G M E$ ,which is the lunarhorizontal parallax $( \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } )$ based on the mean radius of the Earth;angle $S E T$ ,which is the apparent semidiameterof the Sun $( s _ { \mathrm { s } } )$ ；andangle $E T G$ ，whichistheparallax of the Sun $( \pi _ { \mathrm { { s } } } )$ .Wewish to find $L M$ asthe angularquantity $L E M$ seenfrom theEarth,designated $f _ { 1 }$ Angle $E V M$ isan intermediate angle, so that angle $E V M =$ angle $E T G +$ angle $S E T$ .Wewant $f _ { 1 } =$ angle $L E M =$ angle $G M E +$ angle $E V M$ .Thus,

$$
f _ { 1 } = \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } .
$$

The cross-section of the shadow is not a true circle,because the Earth is not a true sphere. To compensate,a meanradius for the Earth is substituted,which is equivalentto substituting for $\pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ a parallax $\pi _ { 1 }$ reduced to latitude $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ ,so that $\pi _ { 1 } { = } 0 . 9 9 8 3 4 0 \pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ Moreover,observation has shown that the atmosphere of the Earth increases the apparent semidiameterof the shadow by approximately one-fiftieth.Hence,

$$
f _ { 1 } = 1 . 0 2 ( \pi _ { 1 } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } ) .
$$

Theumbral shadow cone of the Earth is defined by the exterior tangents between the Sunand the Earth.The vertex falls beyond the Earth and beyond the Moon's distance as well.Figure 11.3illustrates the geometry.In this diagram, $S$ and $E$ represent the centers of the Sunand theEarth,respectively,and $M$ isa point on the umbral cone at the distance of the Moon.All angles are as described in the previous section,except that angle $V E M$ ， the apparent semidiameter of the umbral cone $( f _ { 2 } )$ ,replacesangle $f _ { 1 }$ From triangle $V E M$ , $f _ { 2 } =$ angleGME-angle $E V M$ ,and from triangle $T E V$ ,angle $E V T =$ angle $E V M =$ angle $S E T -$ angle $_ { E T G }$ .Thus,

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/139a1d4871bed153fc299a105a4b02492ebf18dad045ef3517687965c4febfd0.jpg)  
Figure11.2 Geometric parametersof the Earth'spenumbral shadow,relating its angular size to observed quantities.In this diagram, $\angle G M E = \pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ， $\angle E T G = \pi _ { s }$ ， $\angle S E T = s _ { s } ,$ and $\angle L E M = f _ { 1 }$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/9445965757bf04e0197221083dc58307c8004be7d554db3e2bc5bf8ab264257e.jpg)  
Figure11.3 Geometric parameters of the Earth's umbral shadow,relating its angular size to observed quantities.In this diagram, $\angle G M E = \pi _ { \mathrm { m } }$ $\angle E T G = \pi _ { s }$ $\angle S E T = s _ { s } ,$ and $\angle V E M = f _ { 2 }$

$$
f _ { 2 } = 1 . 0 2 ( \pi _ { 1 } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } - s _ { \mathrm { s } } )
$$

aftercompensation forthe effect of the Earth's atmosphere on the semidiameter ofthe shadow.

# 11.2.3.2 Conditions fora Lunar Eclipse

There are several types of lunar eclipse,depending upon what portion of the Earth'sshadow the Moon passes through.We will consider the conditions for each.Allconditions should be tested to find the maximum possible eclipse.

Conditions for a penumbral lunar eclipse.A penumbral lunar eclipse occurs when the Moon enters partially or completely into the penumbral cone,but not the umbral cone.The right side ofFig.11.4illustrates the limiting case forapenumbrallunareclipse,where $f = f _ { 1 }$ In this diagram, $s$ represents the antisolar point and $M ^ { \prime }$ represents the center of the Moon. Let $S M ^ { \prime }$ be the geocentric least angular separation $( \sigma$ ）givenbyEq.11.9; $M ^ { \prime } T ^ { \prime }$ is $s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ .A penumbral eclipse will occur if $\sigma$ is less than $f _ { 1 } + s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ .Thus,the condition for apenumbral lunar eclipse is

$$
\sigma < 1 . 0 2 ( \pi _ { 1 } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } ) + s _ { \mathrm { m } } .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/f5d209566ad1825460a97beeba6211809539151619f35e168fe72f9ab4c8812d.jpg)  
Figure11.4 Composite diagram showing limiting conditions for different types of lunar eclipses,not to scale.Limiting conditions that separate types of eclipses occur when the separation of the center of the Moon and the shadow axis is equal to the sum or difference of theapparent radius of the Moon and the radius of the cross-section of one of the shadow cones at the distance of the Moon.Let the small circle represent theMoon,with radius $s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ,theshaded circlerepresent either theumbra,withradius $f _ { 2 }$ , or the penumbra,with radius $f _ { 1 }$ ,and $\sigma$ represent the distance between centers $s$ and $M$ or $M ^ { \prime }$ .Onthe left side, if $\sigma \le f - s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ,theMoon is totallyimmersed in theshadow.Ontherightside,if $\sigma \geq f + s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ , theMoon is just out of the shadow.If theMoon lies between these two limits,it is partially eclipsed in the shadow.The type of eclipse is named for its maximum phase—total(umbral),partial (umbral), total penumbral, or partial penumbral.

Conditions fora partial lunar eclipse.A partial lunar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes through the penumbral cone and partly,but not completely,into and through the umbra.Theright side ofFig.11.4also illustrates the limitingcasefora partial (umbral) lunar eclipse,except this time, $f = f _ { 2 }$ .A partial eclipse will occur if $\sigma$ is less than $f _ { 2 } + s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ Thus,the condition for apartial lunar eclipse is

$$
\sigma < 1 . 0 2 ( \pi _ { 1 } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } - s _ { \mathrm { s } } ) + s _ { \mathrm { m } } .
$$

Conditions for a total lunar eclipse.A total lunar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes through the penumbral cone and completely into and through the umbral cone.The left side of Fig.11.4 illustrates the limiting case fora total (umbral) lunar eclipse,where $f = f _ { 2 }$ . In this diagram, $S$ represents the antisolar point and $M$ represents the center of the Moon. Let $_ { S M }$ be the geocentric least angular separation ( $\sigma$ )givenbyEq.11.9; $M T$ is $s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ Atotal eclipse will occur if $o$ is less than $f _ { 2 } - s _ { \mathrm { m } }$ .Thus,the condition fora total lunar eclipse is

$$
\sigma < 1 . 0 2 ( \pi _ { 1 } + \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } - s _ { \mathrm { s } } ) - s _ { \mathrm { m } } .
$$

# 11.2.4Occurrence of Solar Eclipses

The geometry to test for the occurrence of a solar eclipse is similar to that fora lunar eclipse. In this situation,we are examining the shadow of the Moon instead of that of the Earth.The shadowis,ofcourse,smaller and shorter,the vertex beingatabout the same distance from theMoonas the Earth.Moreover,the Earth ismuch larger,so that itcan never be completely within the Moon's shadow.In searching for the occurrence of a solar eclipse,we use the center of the Earth as the reference point of observation.The expression for least angular separation of the centers of the Sun and Moon still applies.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/a8a655b22f4a783cc52b9ed3bc8b2dea50fcb3e9e793807b5079088493b9fa08.jpg)  
Figure11.5 Known geometric parameters inthe limiting configuration forapartial solar eclipse,when the Moon is externally tangent.As seen by anobserverat $o$ ,theapparent disks of the Sunand the Moon areexternally tangent along the line $o T$ In this diagram, $\angle Z O M = z _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime }$ , $\angle Z E M = z _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ， $\angle Z O S = z _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime }$ , $\angle Z E S = z _ { s }$ $\angle M O N = s _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime }$ ,and $\angle S O T = { s } _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime }$

# 11.2.4.1 Conditions fora Solar Eclipse

There are also several types of solar eclipse,depending upon what portion of the shadow reaches the Earth's surface.We willexamine the conditions for each.It is not necessary to correct for atmospheric augmentation of the Moon's shadow;the Moon has no atmosphere. It is also not necessry to correct the parallax for shape;this is done by limb corrections,if needed (Herald1983).

Conditions forapartial solareclipse.Apartial solar eclipse occurs when the Earth passes through the penumbra of the Moon's shadow,but not the umbra.(Also,partial phase is seen byan observer in the penumbra during any type of solar eclipse.) Figure11.5 illustrates the limitingcase fora partial solar eclipse.In this figure, $s$ represents the center of the Sun, $M$ the center of the Moon,and $E$ the center of the Earth.Point $o$ ,on the Earth,lies on the edge of thepenumbral cone,depictedby line segment $o T$ ,whichalso servesasthe horizonat $o$ ; point $Z$ isat the zenith foran observerat $^ { o }$ .The topocentric zenith distances of the Moon and the Sun at $o$ are:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { z _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - \angle M O N = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - s _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime } } \\ & { z _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - \angle T O S = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

where $s _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime }$ and $s _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime }$ are the topocentric semidiameters of the Moon and the Sun,respectively. The geocentric angular separation between the Moon and the Sun is angle $M E S$ ,whichis equal to the difference between the geocentric zenith distances of the Sun and the Moon, $z _ { \mathrm { s } } - z _ { \mathrm { m } }$ .The geocentric zenith distances $( z )$ are related to their corresponding topocentric values $( z ^ { \prime } )$ by

$$
z = z ^ { \prime } - \pi ^ { \prime }
$$

where $\pi ^ { \prime }$ is the geocentric parallax.Assuming a spherical Earth, $\pi ^ { \prime }$ is given in terms of the horizontal parallax $( \pi )$ by

$$
\pi ^ { \prime } = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin \pi \sin z ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/7b03244f220371082ef5906e1440c3f62d7163faf7a0431ed51801ea5679ca87.jpg)  
Figure11.6 Known geometric parameters in the limiting configuration for a partial solar eclipse,when the Moon is internally tangent.As seen by an observerat $o$ ,theapparent disks of the Sunand the Moonare internally tangent along the line $o T$ . In this diagram, $\angle Z O M = z _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime }$ , $\angle Z E M = z _ { \mathrm { m } }$ , $\angle Z O S = z _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime }$ , $\angle Z E S = z _ { s }$ , $\angle M O N = s _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime }$ ,and $\angle S O T = s _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime } .$

Substituting Eqs.11.16 and11.18 into Eq.11.17,neglecting theaugmentation of the semidiameters,and setting cos $s ^ { \prime }$ equal to unity yields

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { z _ { \mathrm { m } } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } - s _ { \mathrm { m } } - \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } } \\ { { \phantom { z _ { \mathrm { s } } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } - \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } . } } } \end{array}
$$

Setting angle $M E S$ equal to the geocentric least angular separation $( \sigma )$ yields

$$
\sigma = z _ { \mathrm { s } } - z _ { \mathrm { m } } = s _ { \mathrm { s } } + s _ { \mathrm { m } } + \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } - \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } .
$$

A solar eclipse will occur when the apparent least angular separation of the centers of the Sun and the Moon is less than the sum of their apparent semidiameters.Thus,the condition for a partial solar eclipse as seen from the geocenter is

$$
\sigma < s _ { \mathrm { s } } + s _ { \mathrm { m } } + \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } - \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } .
$$

Conditions foracentral solar eclipse. If the observeron Earth passes through the penumbra of theMoon's shadow and into the umbra,he or she experiences acentral solar eclipse.If thereference observing point is in the umbra on the inside of the vertex,the eclipse is total. Ifthe reference point is in the umbra on the outside of the vertex,the eclipse is annular.If the reference point is in theumbraand passes through the vertex,it isanannular-total eclipse, also called a hybrid eclipse.Inthe rare event that an observer near apole sees atotal eclipse, butthe shadowaxis does not pass through the surface of the Earth,the eclipse is a noncentral total eclipse.A central solar eclipse will occur when the apparent separation of the centers of the Sun and the Moon is less than the absolute value of the difference of the apparent semidiameters.The condition for the occurrence of an annular solar eclipse is specifically derived in this section,but the same condition may be used to differentiate between the occurrence of any type of central solar eclipse (total,annular,or annular-total） and the occurrence of apartial solar eclipse.Figure 11.6 illustratesthe geometry.The definitions inthis figureare identical to the definitions ofFig.11.5,except that point $o$ now lies on the edge of the umbral cone.The topocentric zenith distances at $o$ are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + \angle M O N = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + s _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { \prime } } \\ { z _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime } = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + \angle T O S = 9 0 ^ { \circ } + s _ { \mathrm { s } } ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Just as in the preceding section,the geocentric angular separation between the Moon and the Sun is angle $M E S$ ,which is equal to $| z _ { \mathrm { s } } - z _ { \mathrm { m } } |$ ,the difference between the geocentric zenith distances of the Sun and the Moon.Employing the same procedure and approximations as in the preceding section,the condition for an annular solar eclipse is

$$
\sigma < | s _ { \mathrm { s } } - s _ { \mathrm { m } } + \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } - \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } |
$$

which,as stated before,may be usedas a general condition for any central solar eclipse.If $s _ { \mathrm { m } } > s _ { \mathrm { s } }$ ,the eclipse is total;if $s _ { \mathrm { m } } < s _ { \mathrm { s } }$ ,it is annular. If $o$ is near zero,the eclipse may be annular-total,but that determination requires further calculation.

# 11.3 Solar Eclipses

Section 11.1 described considerations for ephemerides to use in calculating eclipses.Section11.2 described how to find approximately when an eclipse of any kind may occur.This section describes how to calculate the details of a solar eclipse.

# 11.3.1Fundamental Equations: Introduction

Bessel developed the method used to calculate and describe precisely any eclipse,based on using a coordinate system oriented to the shadow axis.The basic steps are:elements arecalculated in the Besseliansystem to describe geometrical quantities;the observer's positionis transformed to Bessel's coordinate system;equationsofcondition are formed; circumstances are derived that describe the time and place of observable events or conditions in the Besselian system; the circumstances are transformed back to topocentric or geocentric coordinates.

# 11.3.2 Besselian Elements

# 11.3.2.1 The Fundamental Coordinate System: Overview

To use Bessel's method for solar eclipses,the geometric position of the shadow of the Moon with respect to the Earth is described ina coordinatesystem (Fig.11.7)wherein the origin of coordinates is the geocenter,the $z$ -axisisparallel to theaxisof the shadowand positive toward the Moon, the $x$ -axispasses through the equator and is positive toward the east,and the $y$ -axisispositive toward thenorth.Thisisthefundamentalcoordinatesystem,and the xyplaneis the fundamentalplane.The intersectionof the shadow with the fundamental plane is two circles concentric about the axis.The surface of the Earth moves with respect to this plane,because the Earth is rotating on itsaxisand moving in orbit.

# 11.3.2.2 Calculationof the Besselian Elements:Transformation to the Fundamental Coordinate System

In the geocentric equatorial coordinate system,let $\mathbf { R } _ { \mathrm { s } }$ be the position vectorof the Sun,and $\mathbf { R } _ { \mathrm { m } }$ that ofthe Moon.Let $\mathbf { G } = \mathbf { R } _ { \mathrm { s } } - \mathbf { R } _ { \mathrm { m } }$ beavector collinear with the shadow axis.In astronomical units,

$$
\left. \hat { \mathbf { R } } _ { \mathrm { s } } \right. = r _ { \mathrm { s } } = 1
$$

$$
r _ { \mathrm { m } } = { \frac { R _ { \mathrm { m } } } { R _ { \mathrm { s } } } } = { \frac { \sin \pi _ { 0 } } { R _ { \mathrm { s } } \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } }
$$

$$
g = { \frac { G } { R _ { \mathrm { s } } } }
$$

$\pi _ { 0 } =$ horizontal parallax of the Sun at mean distance

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { s } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } } \\ { \sin \alpha _ { \mathrm { s } } \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } } \\ { \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { s } } } \end{array} \right] } \\ & { \mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { m } } = \frac { \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { 0 } } } { R _ { \mathrm { s } } \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } } \\ { \sin \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } } \\ { \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

By definition,

$$
\mathbf { g } = \mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { { s } } } - \mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { { m } } } = g { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \cos d \cos a } \\ { \cos d \sin a } \\ { \sin d \ } \end{array} \right] }
$$

where $( a , d )$ is the geocentric equatorial right ascension and declination of the intersection of the shadow's axis with the celestial sphere (point $Z$ ).

Recall from $\ S \ 1 1 . 3 . 2 . 1$ the fundamental coordinate system $( x , y , z )$ whose $z$ -axisis parallel to the shadow axis,or to $\mathbf { g }$ Theplane $z = 0$ is the fundamental plane,the $x$ -axis

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/5144ee207a16c413951b00d1ad9bd8c707a2f4cc0bd636f7caf397aeeaa135fb.jpg)  
Figure11.7 Transformation of geocentric equatorial coordinates to the fundamental plane. Take the $x$ -axispointed at $\Upsilon$ and rotate it around the $z$ -axis (toward NCP) to $a + \pi / 2$ ；then rotate around the new $x$ -axisby $\pi / 2 - d$ to make the new $z$ -axisparallel to the shadowaxis.

isthe intersection of the fundamental plane with the equator (positive to the east),and the $y$ -axis completes the triad (positive to the north).

Hence,

$$
{ \bf r } _ { \mathrm { m F u n d . } } = \frac { 1 } { \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } { \bf R } _ { 1 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - d \right) { \bf R } _ { 3 } \left( a + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) { \bf r } _ { \mathrm { m G e o c . } }
$$

in unitsof Earth radii.( ${ \bf R } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 }$ are standard rotation matrices, positive direction,about the $x$ -axisand $Z$ -axis,respectively.See $\ S 1 4 . 4$ for further details.)

In the $( x , y , z )$ system,letthe unit vectors bei,j,and $\mathbf { k }$ .Then

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \mathbf { \vec { y } } \times \mathbf { k } = 0 } \\ & { \mathbf { \vec { g } } \cdot \mathbf { \vec { i } } = 0 } \\ & { \mathbf { \vec { g } } \cdot \mathbf { \vec { j } } = 0 } \\ & { \qquad \mathbf { \vec { k } } = { \frac { \mathbf { g } } { g } } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \cos d \cos a } \\ { \cos d \sin a } \\ { \sin d } \end{array} \right] } } \end{array} }
$$

and the orthogonal unit vectors are

$$
\mathbf { i } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - \sin a } \\ { \cos a } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] } , \qquad \mathbf { j } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - \cos a \sin d } \\ { - \sin a \sin d } \\ { \phantom { - } \cos d } \end{array} \right] } .
$$

By working out Eq.11.26 or by resolving the lunar position vector into components using Eq.11.28,

$$
\mathbf { r } _ { \mathrm { m F u n d } } = \frac { 1 } { \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } \sin ( \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } - a ) } \\ { \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } \cos d - \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } \sin d \cos ( \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } - a ) } \\ { \sin \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } \sin d + \cos \delta _ { \mathrm { m } } \cos d \cos ( \alpha _ { \mathrm { m } } - a ) } \end{array} \right] .
$$

$( x , y )$ are the coordinates of the intersection of the axis of the shadow with the fundamental plane.

In presenting Besselian elements of eclipses for practical use,the right ascension $^ a$ of the point $Z$ is conventionally replaced by the Greenwich Hour Angle $\mu$ of that point, given by

$$
\mu = { \mathrm { G r e e n w i c h ~ A p p a r e n t ~ S i d e r e a l ~ T i m e } } - a .
$$

In practical calculation,the Greenwich Sidereal Time is evaluated according to the precepts given in Chapter 3 (see $\ S \ 3 . 2 . 1 $ ).

# 11.3.2.3 Parameters of the Shadow Cone

The radii of theumbra and penumbra on the fundamental plane can be easily calculated given the generating angle of each cone,and the perpendicular distance of each vertex from thefundamental plane.RefertoFigs.11.8and11.9for theplanegeometryofacross-section of the cone.This geometry relates the desired quantities to the known size and separation of the bodies.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/a4c35357eb40e9b475396121796943b87c4264842498a658bfa0637dac42c667.jpg)  
Figure11.8 Components of shadow cones in the Besselian fundamentalreference system.The Sun is centered at $s$ ,with apparent semidiameter $d _ { s } ,$ at height $R$ above the fundamental plane. The Moon is centered at $M$ ,withsemidiameter $d _ { \mathrm { m } }$ ,atheight $r = z$ above the fundamental plane. The fundamental plane,through the Earth's center,and the observer's plane are perpendicular to the axis through $S M$ ,the separation being $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ .The penumbra has itsvertex $V _ { 1 }$ at height $c _ { 1 }$ above the fundamental plane,radius $l _ { 1 }$ in the fundamental plane, $L _ { 1 }$ in the observer's plane, vertex angle $f _ { 1 }$ Similarly for the umbra.(Subscript1refers to penumbra,2 to umbra.)

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/31c32635bebcd042f563896294d3d320eabec61c625b13d6753e431730060a9a.jpg)  
Figure11.9 Relationshipsamong angular semidiameter $s$ ,apparent semidiameter $d$ ,and parallax $\pi$ $R _ { s }$ is expressed in Earth radii.

From Fig.11.8,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sin f _ { \mathrm { 1 } } = \frac { d _ { \mathrm { s } } + d _ { \mathrm { m } } } { g R _ { \mathrm { s } } } } \\ { \displaystyle \sin f _ { \mathrm { 2 } } = \frac { d _ { \mathrm { s } } - d _ { \mathrm { m } } } { g R _ { \mathrm { s } } } . } \end{array}
$$

To transform to units of Earth equatorial radi (seeFig.11.9),

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { d _ { \mathrm { e } } \equiv 1 = R _ { \mathrm { s } } \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } } \\ & { d _ { \mathrm { s } } \quad = R _ { \mathrm { s } } \sin s _ { \mathrm { s } } = { \frac { \sin s _ { \mathrm { s } } } { \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { s } } } } = { \frac { \sin s _ { 0 } } { \sin \pi _ { 0 } } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\pi _ { \mathrm { s } } = \pi _ { 0 } / R _ { \mathrm { s } }$ , $s _ { \mathrm { s } } = s _ { 0 } / R _ { \mathrm { s } }$ For the Moon,set

$$
d _ { \mathrm { m } } = k \operatorname { E a r t h } \mathrm { r a d i i } , \quad { \mathrm { w h e r e ~ } } k = d _ { \mathrm { m } } / d _ { \mathrm { e } } .
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { d _ { \mathrm { e } } \equiv 1 = R _ { \mathrm { m } } \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } \\ & { d _ { \mathrm { m } } = k = R _ { \mathrm { m } } \sin s _ { \mathrm { m } } = \frac { \sin s _ { \mathrm { m } } } { \sin \pi _ { \mathrm { m } } } . } \end{array}
$$

Let $R = { R _ { \mathrm { s } } }$ sin $\pi _ { 0 }$ be the distance to the Sun in astronomical units.Substituting Eqs.11.31 through 11.33 into Eq.11.30 and reducing yields

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \sin f _ { 1 } = ( \sin s _ { 0 } + k \sin \pi _ { 0 } ) / g R } \\ { \sin f _ { 2 } = ( \sin s _ { 0 } - k \sin \pi _ { 0 } ) / g R . } \end{array}
$$

The numerators are evaluated using adopted values for $k , \ s _ { 0 } .$ and $\pi _ { 0 }$ .Equations 11.24 and11.25give $g$ ,and $R$ is from the solar ephemeris.

The distances $c _ { 1 } , \ c _ { 2 }$ ,of the vertices of the penumbral and umbral cones above the fundamental plane are showninFig.11.8.Figures11.10 and11.11 show that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { c _ { 1 } = z + k \mathrm { \ c o s e c \ } f _ { 1 } } } \\ { { c _ { 2 } = z - k \mathrm { \ c o s e c \ } f _ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/f5bde8a225a299bdce6be9f34cdf8f14b7c2819e3ce7abfc72b398d1b958dc4b.jpg)  
Figure11.10 Vertex angle of the penumbra, detail fromFig.11.8.sin $f _ { 1 } { = } d _ { \mathrm { m } } / ( c _ { 1 } { - } z )$   
Figure11.11 Vertexangleof the umbra,detail from Fig. 11.8. sin $f _ { 2 } = d _ { \mathrm { m } } / ( z - c _ { 2 } )$

and hence the radii $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 }$ of the penumbra and umbra on the fundamental plane are obtained from

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { l _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } \tan f _ { 1 } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { l _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } \tan f _ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The quantities $z$ ， $k$ ， $c _ { 1 } , \ : c _ { 2 } ,$ $l _ { 1 }$ ,and $l _ { 2 }$ are in units of Earth radii. The convention of signs introduced inEq.11.35makes $l _ { 2 }$ negative fortotal eclipses-i.e.,thevertex is below the fundamental plane.For annular eclipses, $l _ { 2 }$ is positive,and of course $l _ { 1 }$ is always positive.

# 11.3.2.4 Summary of Besselian Elements

The quantities $x , y$ ,sin $d$ ,cosd, $\mu , l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 }$ ,tan $f _ { 1 }$ ,tan $f _ { 2 } , \dot { \mu }$ ,and $\dot { d }$ areconventionallydesignated asBesselian elements.(N.B.Itis conventional in almanacs to use the notation $\mu ^ { \prime } , d ^ { \prime }$ for the derivatives with respect to time.) In the publications,the first seven quantities are presented as low-order polynomialsas functions of time.The remaining four quantities,to the precision required,are constant for the entire eclipse and are givenat the conjunction value.Using all these quantities,the sizeand orientationof the shadow cone with respect to the surface of the Earth and the fundamental plane may be calculated for any instant during the eclipse.

# 11.3.2.5ANote on Practical Calculation

In practice,using a computer,one begins the computation by constructing short ephemerides of the Sun and the Moon from the desired source,at a convenient interval,say,one hour, and then subtabulates to smaller intervals as desired.However,itis possible with some ephemerides storedinpolynomial form totake the beginning ephemeris directlyatthedesired interval.This directness is especially helpfulincalculating points ofcurves nearthe beginning and end of a central solar eclipse.

Initially,for each eclipse,calculations are carried out using aten-minute time interval, but for some instances additional calculations at an interval of 3O seconds orless are necessary. In all instances,the Besselian elements,derivatives,and auxiliary quantities are calculated from the ephemeris entries and carried as an array; none is assumed to be constant.Derivatives are taken numerically.Most quantities change so slowly that onlyalow-order scheme is required.

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/25590906ad9f92a1939ed78d45d5e91b3b72dd0dcef233e510027edd8983a73a.jpg)  
Figure11.12 Angular quantities in the geocentric equatorial plane

# 11.3.3 Coordinates ofthe Observer

# 11.3.3.1 Geocentric Position

Consider an observerlocated on the surface of the Earth in longitude $\lambda$ ,at geocentric latitude $\phi ^ { \prime }$ ,and at a distance $\rho$ from the center of the terrestrial spheroid.The longitude is not now corrected for $\Delta T$ and is considered positive east (right-handed system).Careful attention must be paid to algebraic signs (see Fig.11.12). In the $x \cdot$ ， $y \cdot$ ,z-axes of the fundamental plane system,inunitsof the Earth'sequatorial radius,let the observer’sposition vector be

$$
\rho _ { \mathrm { F } } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \xi } \\ { \eta } \\ { \zeta } \end{array} \right] } ~ .
$$

In the geocentric equatorial system,let the observer's position be

$$
\rho _ { \mathrm { G } } = \rho \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos \lambda } \\ { \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin \lambda } \\ { \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Thetransformation to the fundamental system is very similar to that of Eq.11.26:

$$
\rho _ { \mathrm { F } } = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } - d \right) \mathbf { R } _ { 3 } \left( - \left( \mu - { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \right) \right) \rho _ { \mathrm { G } } .
$$

It is convenient to use the local hour angle in further calculations.Because by current convention the Greenwich Hour Angle $( \mu )$ and longitude $( \lambda )$ are measured in opposite directions,the local hour angle $\mathbf { \Pi } ^ { ( \theta ) }$ is

$$
\theta = \mu + \lambda .
$$

Applying Eq.11.39 toEq.11.38,combining termsby multiple-angle formulae,substituting Eq.11.40,and resolving the components along the unit vectors gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \xi = \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin \theta } \\ { \eta = \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } \cos d - \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \sin d \cos \theta } \\ { \zeta = \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } \sin d + \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } \cos d \cos \theta . } \end{array}
$$

# 11.3.3.2 Geocentric Velocity

It will also be necessary to know how the observer is moving with respectto the fundamental plane.The components of the motion are rotations about the same two axes used in the coordinate transformation:rotation about the polar axis of the Earth because of both the rotation of the Earth and the motion of the Moon,and about the axis pointing to the equinox because of the motion of the Moon.In the geocentric rectangular equatorial system,the rotation vectoris

$$
\omega _ { \mathrm { G } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \dot { d } } \\ { 0 } \\ { \dot { \mu } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Transforming to the fundamental plane,

$$
\omega _ { \mathrm { { F } } } = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } - d \right) \omega _ { \mathrm { { G } } } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { { \dot { d } } } \\ { { \dot { \mu } } \cos d } \\ { { \dot { \mu } } \sin d } \end{array} \right] } .
$$

The velocity of the observer with respect to the fundamental plane is

$$
\dot { \boldsymbol { \rho } } _ { \mathrm { F } } = \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \mathrm { F } } \times \boldsymbol { \rho } _ { \mathrm { F } } = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - d \right) \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \mathrm { G } } \times \boldsymbol { \rho } _ { \mathrm { F } } .
$$

The operation $\omega _ { \mathrm { { F } } } \times$ may be expressed as a matrix premultiplier:

$$
\Omega = [ \omega _ { x } , \omega _ { y } , \omega _ { z } ] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { { 0 } } & { { - \dot { \mu } \sin d } } & { { \dot { \mu } \cos d } } \\ { { \dot { \mu } \sin d } } & { { 0 } } & { { - \dot { d } } } \\ { { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } } & { { \dot { d } } } & { { 0 } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Substitution of Eq.11.45 intoEq.11.44 yields

$$
\dot { \rho } _ { \mathrm { F } } = \pmb { \Omega } \rho _ { \mathrm { F } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \dot { \mu } ( - \eta \sin d + \zeta \cos d ) } \\ { \dot { \mu } \xi \sin d - \dot { d } \zeta } \\ { - \dot { \mu } \xi \cos d + \dot { d } \eta } \end{array} \right] .
$$

# 11.3.3.3 Coordinates of Observer with Respectto the Fundamental Plane, Corrected for Flattening

Considera spheroid of ellipticity $e$ .Geodetic positions are specified on the surface.Calculations of eclipse tracks in the fundamental plane are no problem,nor are local circumstances for a given point.However,in translating any pointfrom the fundamental plane to the surface of the spheroid,evaluationof $\zeta$ requires knowing $\rho$ ,the distance of any point from the center of the Earth.This distance is a function only of latitude,so that $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ could be determined by successive approximation.

However,Bessel devised a procedure to providea directcomputation of $\zeta$ from $\xi , \eta$ .This procedure requires a set of auxiliary Besselian elements,based on the Besselian element $^ d$ and the ellipticity $e$ of the spheroid.

Let $a$ and $^ b$ be the equatorial and polar radius,respectively,of the spheroid. Then

$$
\mathrm { f l a t t e n i n g , } f = { \frac { a - b } { a } } , \quad \mathrm { a n d }
$$

$$
{ \mathrm { e l l i p t i c i t y } } , e = ( 1 - b ^ { 2 } / a ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

Define

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , \qquad S = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) C ; } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { C = ( \cos ^ { 2 } \phi + ( 1 - f ) ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , \qquad S = ( 1 - f ) ^ { 2 } C . } } \end{array}
$$

The relations between geocentric latitude $\phi ^ { \prime }$ and geocentric distance of observer $\rho$ and geodetic latitude $\phi$ are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } = S \sin \phi } } \\ { { \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } = C \cos \phi . } } \end{array}
$$

To eliminate $\rho$ and $\phi ^ { \prime }$ ,transform toaparametric latitude $\phi _ { 1 }$ ,defined by:

$$
\cos \phi _ { 1 } = \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } = \frac { \cos \phi } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \equiv C \cos \phi .
$$

Then,from Eqs.11.48,11.49,and11.50,

$$
\sin \phi _ { 1 } = ( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 1 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } = \frac { \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } .
$$

Inversely,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \rho \cos \phi ^ { \prime } } & { = \cos \phi _ { 1 } } & { \equiv C \cos \phi } \\ { \rho \sin \phi ^ { \prime } } & { = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin \phi _ { 1 } } & { \equiv S \sin \phi . } \end{array}
$$

Substituting into Eq. 11.41,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \xi = \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin \theta } \\ { \eta = \sin \phi _ { 1 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos d - \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin d \cos \theta } \\ { \zeta = \sin \phi _ { 1 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin d + \cos \phi _ { 1 } \cos d \sin \theta . } \end{array}
$$

Nowto eliminate the terms in $d$ and $e$ ,define

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \operatorname { i n } \eta : } & { \quad \sin d = \rho _ { 1 } \sin d _ { 1 } } \\ & { } & { \quad ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos d = \rho _ { 1 } \cos d _ { 1 } } \\ & { \operatorname { i n } \zeta : } & { \quad ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin d = \rho _ { 2 } \sin d _ { 2 } } \\ & { } & { \quad \cos d = \rho _ { 2 } \cos d _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \xi = \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin \theta } \\ { \eta _ { 1 } = \eta / \rho _ { 1 } = \sin \phi _ { 1 } \cos d _ { 1 } - \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin d _ { 1 } \cos \theta } \\ { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } ( \sin \phi _ { 1 } \sin d _ { 2 } + \cos \phi _ { 1 } \cos d _ { 2 } \cos \theta ) . } \end{array}
$$

Define

$$
\zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = 1 - \zeta ^ { 2 } - \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

After extensive algebraic manipulation and reduction,this becomes

$$
\zeta _ { 1 } = \sin \phi _ { 1 } \sin d _ { 1 } + \cos \phi _ { 1 } \cos d _ { 1 } \cos \theta .
$$

Itis desirabletoeliminate $\phi _ { 1 }$ and $\theta$ .Then Eq.11.55 combined withEq.11.57 may be written

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \xi } \\ { \eta _ { 1 } } \\ { \zeta _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right] = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( - d _ { 1 } ) \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin \theta } \\ { \sin \phi _ { 1 } } \\ { \cos \phi _ { 1 } \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sin \theta } \\ { \sin \phi _ { 1 } } \\ { \cos \phi _ { 1 } \cos \theta } \end{array} \right] = \mathbf { R } _ { 1 } ( d _ { 1 } ) \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \xi } \\ { \eta _ { 1 } } \\ { \zeta _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Apply components of Eq.11.59 to the expression for $\zeta$ in Eq.11.55 and reduce to obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } \left[ \zeta _ { 1 } \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) - \eta _ { 1 } \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) \right] . } \end{array}
$$

# 11.3.3.4Auxiliary Besselian Elementsand Summary

The following defined quantities are part of a set known as auxiliary elements;more are defined in $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 4 . 2$ .These elements are used to introduce the flattening of the Earth into the eclipse calculations.All quantities are in units of Earth's equatorial radius.

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \rho _ { 1 } = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } d ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \\ & { } & { \rho _ { 2 } = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } d ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \\ & { } & { \sin d _ { 1 } = \sin d / \rho _ { 1 } } \\ & { } & { \cos d _ { 1 } = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos d / \rho _ { 1 } } \\ & { } & { \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) = e ^ { 2 } \sin d \cos d / \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } } \\ & { } & { \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) = ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } / \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

(The subscripts do not referto the umbra and penumbra.) In computation,all these quantities are carried with the regular elements at the same interval,even though $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 }$ ,and $( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } )$ are nearly constant for the duration of the eclipse.Once the elements are available and a point $( \zeta , \eta , 0 )$ isgiven in the fundamental plane,then $\phi$ and $\zeta$ are obtained as follows: point $( \xi , \eta _ { 1 } , \zeta _ { 1 } )$ is calculated from the definitions of $\eta _ { 1 }$ and $\zeta _ { 1 }$ inEqs.11.55 and11.56.Next, Eq. 11.59 gives $\phi _ { 1 }$ and $\theta$ ;Eq. 11.52 gives $\phi$ ;and Eq.11.60 or11.53 gives $\zeta$ .

# 11.3.4 Conditional and Variational Equations

# 11.3.4.1 Introduction

This section establishes conditions by which eclipse phenomena may be distinguished and calculated.

# 11.3.4.2 Conditions Defining General Circumstances and Curves

Consider now the relation between observer and shadow.We have,in the fundamental plane reference system,the geocentric position of the observer $\rho = ( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ .The geocentric position of the intersection of the shadow axis with a plane through the observer parallel to the fundamental plane is

$$
\mathbf { r } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { \zeta } \end{array} \right] } .
$$

Now define the vectorin the observer's plane from observer to shadow axis as

$$
\Delta = \mathbf { r } - { \boldsymbol { \rho } } .
$$

In the plane through the observer,let $\boldsymbol { Q }$ be the position angle of the axis, measured from north through east,so that the unit vector

$$
\hat { \mathbf { A } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \sin Q } \\ { \cos Q } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] .
$$

The radius $( L )$ of the shadow at height $\zeta$ above the fundamental plane is

$$
L = l - \zeta \tan f
$$

subscripted for umbra or penumbra,and $L$ may be positive or negative.

Equations of condition.We may now impose geometric and dynamic conditions in order to calculate eclipse phenomena.We knowr.We may considertwo cases fromEq.11.63:one, in which $\pmb { \rho }$ is not known but we wish to find the locus of all points satisfying a condition as afunction of time (curve); the other,in which $\pmb \rho$ is fixed and we want the times of significant configurations (circumstances).

Much of the following discussion applies to either case,the difference being the choice of knowns and unknowns.First,consider the more general cases,in which $\pmb \rho$ is not known. The discussion applies to both umbra and penumbra, so the subscripts are omited.

If the observer is onthe edge of the shadow (penumbra or umbra) or,more generally,the surface of the shadow cone,the eclipse is either beginning or ending.This surface location may be defined by imposing the geometric condition

$$
| \Delta | = | L | , \quad { \mathrm { o r } } \quad \Delta \cdot \Delta - L ^ { 2 } = 0 .
$$

Consider Eq.11.66 now as a general expression that can be evaluated at any instant.The equation has two roots.If they are imaginary,then the observer is outside the shadow.If they are equal,then the eclipse is simultaneously beginning and ending-i.e.,grazing-and the observer is on either the northern or southern limit of the shadow.This limit may be defined byimposing the dynamic condition

$$
\frac { d } { d t } ( \pmb { \Delta } \cdot \pmb { \Delta } - L ^ { 2 } ) = 0
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Delta \cdot \dot { \Delta } - L \dot { L } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
| \Delta | = | { \cal L } | , \mathrm { t h i s r e d u c e s t o } \hat { \Delta } \cdot \dot { \Delta } - \dot { \cal L } = 0 .
$$

It is always the case that $d f / d t \approx 0$ for $f _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ ,so it is sufficiently accurate to use

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \dot { L } = \dot { l } - \dot { \zeta } \tan f } \\ { \dot { \zeta } = \omega _ { z } \cdot \rho . } \end{array}
$$

In Eq.11.68,substitute

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \dot { \pmb { \Delta } } = \dot { \bf r } - \dot { \pmb { \rho } } } \\ & { \dot { \pmb { \rho } } = \omega _ { \mathrm { F } } \times \rho _ { \mathrm { F } } = \omega _ { \mathrm { F } } \times ( { \bf r } - { \bf \Delta } \mathbf { \Delta } ) } \end{array}
$$

$$
\hat { \bf A } \cdot \dot { \bf r } - \hat { \bf A } \cdot ( \omega _ { \mathrm { F } } \times ( { \bf r } - { \bf A } ) ) + \tan f \omega _ { z } \cdot ( { \bf r } \cdot { \bf A } ) - \dot { l } = 0 .
$$

In the second term,

$$
\hat { \mathbf { A } } \cdot ( \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \mathrm { F } } \times ( \mathbf { r } - \mathbf { A } ) ) = \hat { \mathbf { A } } \cdot ( \boldsymbol { \omega } _ { \mathrm { F } } \times \mathbf { r } ) .
$$

From Eq.11.45:

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \omega \times \mathbf { r } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { - { \dot { \mu } } \sin d } & { { \dot { \mu } } \cos d } \\ { { \dot { \mu } } \sin d } & { 0 } & { - { \dot { d } } } \\ { - { \dot { \mu } } \cos d } & { { \dot { d } } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { y } \\ { \zeta } \end{array} \right] } } \\ & { \qquad = { \dot { \mu } } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - y \sin d + \zeta \cos d } \\ { x \sin d - \zeta { \dot { d } } { \dot { \mu } } } \\ { - x \cos d + y { \dot { d } } { \dot { \mu } } } \\ { - y \sin d } \end{array} \right] } } \\ & { \qquad = { \dot { \mu } } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - y \sin d } \\ { { \dot { x } } \sin d } \\ { - x \cos d } \end{array} \right] } - \zeta { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - { \dot { \mu } } \cos d } \\ { { \dot { d } } } \\ { - y { \dot { d } } { \zeta } } \\ { - y { \dot { d } } { \zeta } } \end{array} \right] } . } \end{array} }
$$

FromEqs.11.43,11.45,and11.66:

$$
\omega _ { \mathrm { { F } } } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \dot { d } } \\ { \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { \dot { \mu } \sin d } \end{array} \right] , \qquad \omega _ { z } = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] , \qquad \mathbf { A } = \hat { \mathbf { A } } ( l - \zeta \tan f ) .
$$

Substitute theseand Eqs.11.66,11.71,11.72 into Eq.11.70 and,through algebraic manipulation,collect terms as factors of $\pmb { \Delta } \cdot$ and $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ to obtain

$$
\begin{array}{c} \Delta \cdot \left\{ \dot { \bf r } - \dot { \mu } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - y \sin d } \\ { x \sin d } \\ { - x \cos d } \end{array} \right] - l \tan f \omega _ { z } \right\} + \zeta \{ \sec ^ { 2 } f \hat { \bf A } \cdot \omega _ { z } \}  \\ { - i + \tan f \omega _ { z } \cdot { \bf r } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Equation 11.74 may be written in component form as:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin Q } \\ { \cos Q } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left\{ \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \dot { x } } \\ { \dot { y } } \\ { \dot { z } } \end{array} \right] + \dot { \mu } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { y \sin d } \\ { - x \sin d } \\ { x \cos d } \end{array} \right] - l \tan f \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { \dot { d } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] \right\} } \\ & { + \zeta \sec ^ { 2 } f \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \sin Q } \\ { \cos Q } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { \dot { d } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] - \dot { l } + \tan f \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { \dot { d } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x } \\ { y } \\ { \zeta } \end{array} \right] = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Additional auxiliaryelements.Usingacomputer,Eq.11.75may beused in this form to find combinations of $\zeta$ ， $\boldsymbol { Q }$ that satisfy it,and hence the limiting curves.For efficiency, however,it is conventional to define auxiliary quantities $a , b , c$ with derivatives ${ \dot { a } } , { \dot { b } } , { \dot { c } }$ ,there being a separate set for umbra and penumbraas distinguished by subscripting and use of the correspondingangle $f$ ,such that

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \sin Q } \\ { \cos Q } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \dot { c } } \\ { - \dot { b } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] + \zeta \sec ^ { 2 } f \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \sin Q } \\ { \cos Q } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] ^ { T } \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - \dot { \mu } \cos d } \\ { \dot { d } } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] + \dot { a } = 0 .
$$

Thus,to make Eq.11.75 identical to Eq.11.76,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } { \dot { a } = - \dot { l } } & { { } - \dot { \mu } x \cos d \tan f } & { { } + y \dot { d } \tan f } \\ { \dot { b } = - \dot { y } } & { { } + \dot { \mu } x \sin d } & { } & { { } + l \dot { d } \tan f } \\ { \dot { c } = \dot { x } } & { { } + \dot { \mu } y \sin d } & { } & { { } + l \dot { \mu } \tan f \cos d . } \end{array}
$$

In older references,the approximations $\dot { d }$ tan $f _ { i } = 0$ , $\sec ^ { 2 } f _ { i } = 1$ are used,which are reasonable and adequate for limited computer resources.

Now collect terms in sin $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ,cos $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ：

$$
\dot { a } + \sin Q ( \dot { c } - \dot { \mu } \zeta \cos d \sec ^ { 2 } f ) + \cos Q ( - \dot { b } + \zeta \dot { d } \sec ^ { 2 } f ) = 0 .
$$

Multiply bysec $\mathcal { Q }$ to obtain

$$
\tan Q = { \frac { \dot { b } - \dot { d } \zeta \sec ^ { 2 } f - \dot { a } \sec Q } { \dot { c } - \dot { \mu } \zeta \sec ^ { 2 } f \cos d } } .
$$

Theapplication of this fundamental equation to calculate limits of the shadows will be described in $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 5 . 5$ Incalculations forTheAstronomicalAlmanac,the auxiliary quantities are calculated along with the Besselian elements and carried in an array as a function of time.

# 11.3.4.3 A Noteon Practical Calculation

The Besselian elements,both regular and auxiliary,are nearly linear in behavior during an eclipse,such that integration or numerical differentiation seldom requires more than a thirdorderLagrangian scheme,and linear interpolation is often adequate.In preparing computer programs and carrying elements in an array,however,it isalways necessary to be sure that angular quantities change monotonically,especially when passing through $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Itisalso necessary to carry enough entries atthe beginningand end ofthe eclipse to allowinterpolation, butnot so many thatthe behavior of the tabulated elementsaccelerates or reversesand potentially creates specious extrema.

Even though incalculations for The Astronomical Almanac all quantitiesare carried in double precision (15-16 digits),the published data should be considered accurate only to the number of significant figurespresented.

# 11.3.5 Calculation of General Solar Eclipse Phenomena

# 11.3.5.1 Introduction

The Besselian and auxiliary elements are the basis of calculating all further phenomena associated with each eclipse,utilizing,of course,the necessary ephemeris data given in the eclipse section or elsewhere in The Astronomical Almanac.The eclipse section also gives positions on the surface of the Earth as a function of time,where well-defined phases or phenomena occur according to certain geometric or dynamical conditions—e.g.,geocentric beginningand end of each phase.These data are given in tabular and graphical form,and are knownasgeneral circumstances,elements of theeclipse,andcurvesof several kinds.The independent variable,unless stated otherwise,is Universal Time.Conversely,fora given imposed condition,a time may be either deduced or assumed,and the coordinates $( \xi , \eta , 0 )$ determined.Using the auxiliary elements $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 } , d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 }$ ,thequantities $\zeta$ and $( \xi , \eta _ { 1 } , \zeta _ { 1 } )$ maybe calculated and then transformed to $\lambda$ , $\theta , \phi _ { 1 }$ , $\phi$ .General circumstancesare usuallycalculated before the curves,butfor purposes of exposition,calculation of the curves is described first.

Eclipse curves are the loci of points on the spheroid of the Earth at some specified radius, usually the surface.These curves delineate setboundary conditions,and,hence,define areas within which certain eclipse phenomena are visible;they also show times of occurrence.They are produced by going through atable of Besselian elements as afunction of time,calculating atable of derived discriminants,and evaluating whetherimposed criteria are satisfied.

General circumstances (as opposedto local circumstances) are individual points and times atwhich extrema of the curves occur on the spheroid of reference.

# 11.3.5.2 Basic Geometry

In general, eclipse phenomena are treated and calculated using the fundamental triangle in the fundamental plane shown in Fig.11.13.

Byimposing various conditions and finding corresponding points in the fundamental plane,it is then but arelatively easy step to project the point to the surface and convert to latitude and longitudeas described in $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 3$ .

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0013_pages_0481-0520/ocr/images/e4f43710185beb8afb02cb6523e6d3fe91d73d5fc1ebc74f1ca476e6ad34c800.jpg)  
Figure11.13 Relationship between rectangularand polar reference systems with the origins at the geocenter or the observer's projected point,in the fundamental reference plane.Anglesare measured from the north,or $y$ -axis.In the geocentric system,the observer's projected point may be specified as $( \xi , \eta )$ or $( \rho , \gamma )$ ; the shadowaxis interceptmay be specified as $( x , y )$ or $( m , M )$ .In thereference system centered at the observer's projected point, the position of the shadow axis intercept is $( x - \xi , y - \eta )$ or $( \Delta , Q )$

# 11.3.5.3Relationships

The fundamental relationship of the geocenter $( 0 , 0 )$ ,the projected axis of the shadow $( x , y )$ , and the projected position of the observer $( \xi , \eta )$ is:

$$
\pmb { \rho } = \mathbf { m } - \pmb { \Delta } .
$$

Asderived in $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 4 . 2$ ,if the point of the observeris“tangent”to the path of the shadow, such that the eclipse is beginning and ending simultaneously,then the second fundamental relation,which is a condition on $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ,is (from Eq.11.78)

$$
a _ { i } - b _ { i } \cos Q + c _ { i } \sin Q + \zeta ( 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } f _ { i } ) ( \dot { d } \cos Q - \dot { \mu } \cos d \sin Q ) = 0 ,
$$

$\rho$ is usually not unity; it contains flatteningand height above thegeoid.

These relationshipsare also used:

$$
{ \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \xi } \\ { \eta } \end{array} \right] } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x - \Delta \sin Q } \\ { y - \Delta \cos Q } \end{array} \right] } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \rho \sin \gamma } \\ { \rho \cos \gamma } \end{array} \right] } .
$$

Furthermore,the cosine law for plane triangles (dot-product) gives

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \Delta \cdot \Delta = ( \mathbf { m } - \rho ) \cdot ( \mathbf { m } - \rho ) \Leftrightarrow \cos ( \gamma - M ) = { \frac { m ^ { 2 } + \rho ^ { 2 } - \Delta ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \rho } } } \\ & { } \\ & { \rho \cdot \rho = ( \mathbf { m } - \Delta ) \cdot ( \mathbf { m } - \Delta ) \Leftrightarrow \cos ( Q - M ) = { \frac { m ^ { 2 } + \Delta ^ { 2 } - \rho ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \Delta } } } \\ & { } \\ & { \mathbf { m } \cdot \mathbf { m } = ( \rho + \Delta ) \cdot ( \rho + \Delta ) \Leftrightarrow \sin ( \gamma - Q ) = { \frac { \Delta ^ { 2 } + \rho ^ { 2 } - m ^ { 2 } } { 2 \Delta \rho } } . } \end{array} }
$$

The sine law for planar trigonometry (cross-product) gives

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \bf m } \times { \bf \delta } \Delta = \hat { \bf k } \Delta ( x \cos Q - y \sin Q ) } } \\ { { { \bf \omega } } } \\ { { { \bf \omega } = \hat { \bf k } \Delta m \sin ( Q - M ) \quad \mathrm { a n d } } } \\ { { { \boldsymbol \rho } \times { \bf \delta } \Delta = \bf ( m - \Delta ) \times \Delta \Delta = m \times { \bf \delta } \Delta } } \\ { { { \bf \omega } } } \\ { { { \bf \omega } = \hat { \bf k } \rho \Delta \sin ( \gamma - Q ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Another useful relation is

$$
2 \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \gamma - M ) = 1 - \cos ( \gamma - M ) = { \frac { ( l - m + \rho ) ( l + m - \rho ) } { 2 m \rho } } .
$$

# 11.3.5.4 Flattening

Flatteningis taken into account by altering $\rho$ and $\gamma$ slightly to $r \rho$ and $\gamma ^ { \prime }$ .Assume that $\zeta ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } .$ Nowif $\zeta _ { 1 } = 0$ ,then $\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 }$ Let

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \xi = r \rho \sin \gamma = r \sin \gamma ^ { \prime } } } \\ { {  } } \\ { { \eta = r \rho \cos \gamma = r \rho _ { 1 } \cos \gamma ^ { \prime } } } \\ { {  } } \\ { { \eta _ { 1 } = \eta / \rho _ { 1 }    } } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\tan \gamma ^ { \prime } = \xi / \eta _ { 1 } = \rho _ { 1 } \xi / \eta = \rho _ { 1 } \tan \gamma
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { \sin \gamma ^ { \prime } } { \sin \gamma } } = \rho = \rho _ { 1 } { \frac { \cos \gamma ^ { \prime } } { \cos \gamma } } .
$$

The following iterative procedure accounts for flattening.Ordinarily,three iterations are sufficient,for each of $\pm ( \gamma - m )$ .

To start,assume $| \pmb { \rho } | = 1$ ,where $\rho = | \xi , \eta |$ from Eq.11.82 andFig.11.13

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \sin \gamma = \xi / \rho , \quad \cos \gamma = \eta / \rho } } \\ { { \tan \gamma ^ { \prime } = \rho _ { 1 } \sin \gamma / \cos \gamma } } \\ { { \gamma ^ { \prime } = \tan ^ { - 1 } ( \tan \gamma ^ { \prime } ) } } \\ { { \rho = \sin \gamma ^ { \prime } / \sin \gamma . } } \end{array}
$$

Repeat the steps of Eq.11.89 three or more times,until convergence. Then $\xi = \sin \gamma ^ { \prime }$ ， $\eta _ { 1 } = \cos \gamma ^ { \prime }$ (and $\zeta _ { 1 } = 0$ ).

# 11.3.5.5 Curves

The order in which curves are computed is not important; computations are all done independently from the tabulated elements.However,it helps to leave computation of the outline curves until last.

Risingand settng curves.An observer who is at a point of intersection of the penumbral cone with the fundamental plane is also on the sunrise/sunset terminator and may,weather permitting,see a partial eclipse beginning or ending at sunrise or sunset.The locus of all such points on the Earth's surface,known as the rising and seting curve,isshown graphically on eclipsemaps inTheAstronomical Almanac with thelabel Eclipsebeginsatsunrise,Eclipse ends at sunset,and so on.

The rising and setting curve is the locus of end points of the outline curves for the penumbra as it intersects the terminator.At aselected instant,there are two points on the curve or no points.If there are two points,each belongs to one of two series.Forasuccession of instants,the two series of points trace different branches of the same curve and are joined at first contact(penumbra emerges from the terminatorand intersects the Earth's surface)and lastcontact (penumbra merges completely into the terminator and leaves the Earth's surface). The track of the penumbra always generates at least one limit curve (see subtopic“Central line,duration of central eclipse,and width of path,”below),either northern or southern,but if both the northern and southern limits exist for an interval of time (i.e.,the central path is in equatorial regions),then during that interval the rising and setting curve does not exist-the penumbral shadowis completely emerged fromthe terminator.The resultisthat the rising and setting curveforms two separate loops likeelongated teardrops,each loop being generated in two segments.Otherwise,if there is only one limiting curve,then the loops are joined,and the curve of risingand setting forms a distorted figure-eight.In either case,one loop or lobe is for rising,the otherfor setting.In a series of similar eclipses,both forms occur.When the first separation of the loops occurs in the series,the break into separate loops does not occur at the apparent node,but near it.

In the fundamental plane,the circle of the penumbral cone,at any instant,intersects the surface of the Earth in no points or in two points (which may occasionally be coincident).In the fundamental triangle,

$$
\zeta = 0 , \qquad \Delta = l _ { 1 } \qquad \mathrm { a r e ~ i m p o s e d ~ c o n d i t i o n s . }
$$

If $\mathbf { R } _ { 3 }$ is the matrix for positive rotation about the $z$ -axis,then

$$
\rho \equiv \frac { \rho } { m } { \bf R } _ { 3 } [ \pm ( \gamma - M ) ] { \bf m } .
$$

i.e.,

$$
{ \bf R } _ { 3 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { { \cos [ \pm ( \gamma - M ) ] } } & { { \pm \sin [ \pm ( \gamma - M ) ] } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { \mp \sin [ \pm ( \gamma - M ) ] } } & { { \cos [ \pm ( \gamma - M ) ] } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { 0 } } & { { 1 } } \end{array} \right]
$$

where one uses all top signs or all bottom signs.

If, by the cosine law (Eq.11.83), $\cos ( \gamma - M ) < 0$ ,then there are no points on the curve for the selected instant.

Curves of maximum eclipse in the horizon.Any locus of points that satisfies the fundamental relation givenin Eq.11.81 (a dynamic constraint) is a curve of maximum eclipse. If the geometric conditions are also imposed that $\zeta = 0$ , $\Delta = l _ { 1 }$ ,then we have the curve of maximum eclipse at sunrise or sunset,or maximum eclipse in the horizon. On the map this curve approximately bisects the lobes of the rising and setting curves.It is continuous if the rising and setting curves are joined,otherwise not.It is also generated in two branches,as are the rising and seting curves.

From Eq. 11.81,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \zeta = 0 } \\ { a _ { 1 } - b _ { 1 } \cos Q + c _ { 1 } \sin Q = 0 } \end{array}
$$

and from Eq. 11.84,

$$
\rho \times \Delta = { \bf m } \times \Delta \Rightarrow \rho \sin ( \gamma - Q ) = x \cos Q - y \sin Q .
$$

From Eq.11.93 find two values of $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ,then for each findavalueof $\gamma$ ,then $\gamma ^ { \prime }$ asbefore (Eq. 11.89).

Points exist on these curves only if both these conditions are satisfied:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \sin ( \gamma - Q ) \leq 1 \quad \mathrm { a n d } } \\ { ( x - \xi ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - \eta _ { 1 } \rho _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } \leq l _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Central line,duration of central eclipse,and width of path．The central line is the locus of the points of intersection of the axis of the shadow with the surface of the Earth.The following conditions must be satisfied:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \rho \cdot \mathbf { m } = \rho m } \\ { \rho \times \mathbf { m } = 0 } \\ { \xi = x , \eta = y , \eta _ { 1 } = y / \rho _ { 1 } } \\ { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { 2 } } \\ { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

From Eqs.11.97 and11.60:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \zeta _ { 1 } = + ( r ^ { 2 } - \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } } \\ { { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } [ \zeta _ { 1 } \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) - \eta _ { 1 } \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Pointsonthe oppositesideof thespheroid maybe foundbytaking the negative values of $\zeta _ { 1 }$

The duration of central eclipse may be estimated by disregarding vertical motion and considering only the instantaneous size and velocity of the intersection of the shadow with the plane through the observing point and parallel to the fundamental plane.

From the section on conditional and variational equations (Eq.11.70):

$$
{ \dot { \pmb { \rho } } } _ { \mathrm { F } } = { \pmb { \omega } } _ { \mathrm { F } } \times { \pmb { \rho } } _ { \mathrm { F } }
$$

has components

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \dot { \boldsymbol { \xi } } = \dot { \mu } ( - y \sin d + \zeta \cos d ) } \\ { \dot { \eta } = \dot { \mu } x \sin d - \dot { d } \zeta . } \end{array}
$$

Using the definitions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { n ^ { 2 } = ( \dot { x } - \dot { \xi } ) ^ { 2 } + ( \dot { y } - \dot { \eta } ) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { L _ { 2 } = l _ { 2 } - \zeta \tan f _ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

the duration is

$$
2 L _ { 2 } / n \qquad \mathrm { ( w i t h ~ a p p r o p r i a t e ~ s i g n ) . }
$$

The units must be converted as necessary.Normally the conversion is from hours to seconds of time.Before 1982, $L _ { 2 }$ had to be adjusted for different values of $k$ .

In local circumstances,however,the accurate duration is the difference between appropriate pairs of contact times.

The width of the path,perpendicular to the direction of motion,may be estimated by a formula derived by Mikhailov (1931):

$$
\mathrm { w i d t h } = 2 L _ { 2 } \left\{ \zeta ^ { 2 } + \left[ \frac { \xi } { n } ( \dot { x } - \dot { \xi } ) + \frac { \eta } { n } ( \dot { y } - \dot { \eta } ) \right] ^ { 2 } \right\} ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

Northern and southern limits ofumbra or penumbra. Each curve limiting a shadow path is the locus of points on the surface of the Earth's spheroid tangent to the path of the shadow, such that first andlast contacts are simultaneous,orelse maximum occurs in the horizon.The umbra nearlyalwayshas both limitson the surface because it is so small,but the penumbra often has only one.The northern and southern limitsof the umbral track define the central path;the northern and/or southern limits of the penumbral track,plus the curve of rise and set,define the limits of the eclipse.

Impose the condition that Eq.11.81 must be satisfied.Choose a value of $\zeta$ .If acomplete set of limits is being generated,it is usual to begin with $\zeta = 0$ .Then the coefficients of sin $\mathcal { Q }$ and cos $\boldsymbol { Q }$ are evaluated and the left-hand side ofEq.11.81isused asa discriminant.This discriminantis evaluated fora run of $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ,startingfromafirst guess obtainedby seting $\dot { a } _ { i } = 0$ , and proceedinga degree ata timeuntil two zero pointsare found (by inverse interpolation when the discriminant changes sign).Then for each $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ,iterate on $\zeta$ as follows,using Besselian elements:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { L _ { i } = l _ { i } - \zeta \tan f _ { i } } \\ & { \xi = x - L _ { i } \sin Q } \\ & { \eta _ { 1 } = ( y - L _ { i } \sin Q ) / \rho _ { 1 } } \\ & { \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } - \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( \mathrm { I f } \ \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } < 0 , \mathrm { t h e r e \ e x i s t s \ n o \ p o i n t . } ) } \end{array}
$$

Normally choose $\zeta _ { 1 } \geq 0$ to continue. $\zeta _ { 1 } < 0$ may be used to continue the curve below the horizon.

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } [ \zeta _ { 1 } \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) - \eta _ { 1 } \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) ] } } \\ { { \phantom { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } [ \zeta _ { 1 } \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) - \eta _ { 1 } \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) ] } } } \\ { { \phantom { \zeta = \rho _ { 2 } [ \zeta _ { 1 } \cos ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) - \eta _ { 1 } \sin ( d _ { 1 } - d _ { 2 } ) ] } } } \\ { { i = \mathrm { u m b r a ~ o r ~ p e n u m b r a } . } } \end{array}
$$

Put this value of $\zeta$ into Eq.11.81,and repeat until it converges to the required tolerance.In TheAstronomical Almanac,thatis $1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ Earth radii.In extremes of these curves,the iteration may begin oscillating about some mean value,in which case the mean is taken to continue the iteration.

When the iteration converges,then the pointis assgned toa limit by the discriminant:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { L _ { i } \cos Q < 0 \mathrm { f o r } \mathrm { n o r t h e r n } \mathrm { l i m i t } } } \\ { { L _ { i } \cos Q > 0 \mathrm { f o r } \mathrm { s o u t h e r n } \mathrm { l i m i t } . } } \end{array}
$$

The coordinates $( \xi , \eta _ { 1 } , \zeta _ { 1 } )$ are then easily converted to $( \phi , \lambda )$ .

Outline curves.An outline curve of the shadow (umbra or penumbra) on the spheroid is the locus of all points at which the corresponding central or partial eclipse is beginning or ending atagiven time.Outline curves for the penumbra,showing the places where the partial phase is beginning or ending at stated times,are plotted on the maps presented in The Astronomical Almanacand otherpublications.(See also $\ S \ 1 1 . 3 . 6 . 1 .$ )Outline curves of the umbra are not published.Forall practical purposes,it is acceptable to neglect the flatening of the Earth in thesecalculations,butforthemapsinTheAstronomical Almanac,itisnot neglected.

The angle $\boldsymbol { Q }$ becomes the independent variable.Atthe instant for which the curve is calculated,if both the northern and southern limits of the penumbra exist,then $\boldsymbol { Q }$ has the complete range of $0 ^ { \circ }$ to $3 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ .If not,the extreme values must be found;they are two points on the curve for which $\zeta = 0$ .Because these points are on the limiting curves,in the computer program aninitial test is made for the availability of pointsalready computed for the limiting curves.

Those points are then verified from the equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \xi = x - L _ { i } \sin Q } \\ { \eta _ { 1 } \rho _ { 1 } = y - L _ { i } \cos Q } \\ { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = 1 } \end{array}
$$

inwhich $\zeta _ { 1 } = 0$ .Once the end points are found and the correct portion of the arc which intersects the iluminated surface is established,points are calculated at one-degree steps of $Q$ ,iterating as described for the northern/southern limit curvesand requiring $\zeta _ { 1 } > 0$ If desired for special purposes,points below the horizon may be found by using the solutions for $\zeta _ { 1 } < 0$ .

Othercurves.For 1960-1980,mapsin the American Ephemerisand Nautical Almanac showed curves of equal middle and equal semiduration of eclipse.These curves were constructed graphically from the intersections ofanetwork of outline curves,as there is no way to calculate them directly.

Curves of maximum eclipse are not prepared for regular publications,but may be readily calculated. Such calculations are an extension of the procedure for the curve of maximum eclipse in the horizon,in which the condition $\zeta = 0$ isremoved,and $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ becomes the independent variable-i.e.,

Choose $\zeta$ ;   
Find $\mathcal { Q }$ (two values) from Eq.11.81;   
Find $\gamma$ from $\sin ( \gamma - Q ) = ( x $ Cos $\boldsymbol Q - \boldsymbol y$ sin $Q ) / r \rho$ (fundamental cross-product).

If neither value of $\boldsymbol { Q }$ gave an imaginary result,only one may be an eclipsed point in most cases.It is also required that

$$
| \Delta | \leq | L | , \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad ( x - \xi ) ^ { 2 } + ( y - \eta ) ^ { 2 } > L _ { i } ^ { 2 } .
$$

If this test is met, iteration may proceed as usual.


<!-- chunk 0014: pages 521-560 -->
![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0014_pages_0521-0560/ocr/images/0751fe21fdbd10758257ce0cd0439c64d7b4a5e339992f748509280cd0f7defc.jpg)  
Figure11.14 Definition of auxiliary quantity $\psi$ . $L$ isradius of the shadow in the plane of the observer, $\Delta$ isdistance of observer from the axis, $n$ isspeed of separation, $s$ issemiduration.

From maximum eclipse,semiduration of the partial phase may be estimated as follows. Define $\psi$ in Fig.11.14,where $s$ is the semiduration and $n$ is the speed of the shadow. The respective units are hours and equatorial radii per hour. Then

$$
s = ( L _ { i } \cos \psi ) / n ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { n ^ { 2 } = ( \dot { x } - \dot { \xi } ) ^ { 2 } + ( \dot { y } - \dot { \eta } ) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \dot { \xi } = \dot { \mu } \cos \psi \cos \theta } } \\ { { \dot { \eta } = \dot { \mu } \xi \sin d . } } \end{array}
$$

The exact durationis found from the difference in times offirst andlast contacts,as described in $\ S 1 1 . 3 . 6 . 2$ .

The magnitude of the eclipse is found from

$$
{ \frac { L _ { 1 } - \Delta } { L _ { 1 } + L _ { 2 } } } .
$$

If $L _ { 2 }$ is not available,the approximation $L _ { 2 } = L _ { 1 } - 0 . 5 4 5 9$ is used.(Subscript 1forpenumbra, 2 for umbra.)

Curves of equal semiduration or equal magnitude must be found by inverse interpolation on curves of maximum eclipse.(See $\ S \ 1 1 . 3 . 6$ on local circumstances for further discussion of magnitude.)

# 11.3.5.6 General Circumstances
