# Stars and Stellar Systems

SUSANG.STEWART

# 13.1 Introduction

The Starsand Stellar Systems section of TheAstronomical Almanac is acollection of tables containing coordinates and associated data for a wide variety of sources.The sources span the entire wavelength regime fromradio,visible,X-ray and gammaray and types fromsingle starsto stellar groupings.The tables arerepresentative of thewide variety of astronomical research and aimto present acomprehensive sample of sources for each.Tablesare selected based on their usefulnessand the availability of reliable data.We work closely with the International Astronomical Union (IAU） and its working groups to ensure that the data presented in the tablesare the most reliable and currently relevant available.

Sources are often chosento serve as finding lists and,hence,are distributed isotropically where possible.This is especially the case of the stellar tables where there are seemingly an unlimited number of sources to choose from.Other selection criteria,such as magnitude, may limit the selection further. Some of the tables serve a specific purpose such as standards forcalibration.Selectioncriteria for each tableis listed intheNotesandReferences section ofTheAstronomical Almanacand discussed in furtherdetail below.

Severalof the more popularlistsare provided in downloadable form on The Astronomical Almanac Online website in theform of ASCII and PDF tables.Searchable databases of bright starsand UBVRILandolt photometric standard stars,selected on position and magnitude,are also available on the website.

# 13.1.1 Data Presentation

The tablesare a combination of commonly used designations,coordinates,data and notes. The data and associated properties such as magnitudes,spectral types,velocities,fluxes,etc. are chosen on the advice of experts in their respective fields and aim to be the most useful to the observer.These data are generally static,whileanew set of coordinates,obtained for thecurrent editionviaa precession routine,are generated annually.Positions for sources are accurate to the precision listed in tables and,in general,are given to be sufficient for identification using a telescope.

The tables ofICRF2 radio source positions,radio flux calibrators,pulsars and gamma ray sources do not have their positions updated annually,instead,the ICRS J2Ooo.O positions are given.Given that the ICRF radio sources actually define the ICRF whose axes are fixed in time(see Chapter 4),the table is naturally fixed as well.The decision to present the radio flux calibratorsand pulsars inasimilarmannerwas based on the factthat both the fluxcalibrators and pulsars are often observed similarly and simultaneously with the ICRF objects.

Datagoing into each table are checked annually for updates.In addition to the positional data,some tables are revised more frequently than others fora few reasons,typically due to data changing quickly or to newly published data.For example,the double star orbital parametersare generated annually due to their changing orientation,whereas the table of open clusters,pulsars and exoplanets are updated frequently due to on-going research which isreadilyavailable.

# 13.1.2 Sources of Data

Given the wide array of data provided in the Stars and Stellar Systemssection of The Astronomical Almanac,there are various sources from which they originate.The tables are either compiled by USNO astronomers from recommended catalogs or provided entirely by aperson or group in the astronomical community.The stellar data tend to be compiled from catalogs with recommended selection criteria,while the shorter wavelength data come froma compilation of published results augmented by personal research.The tables of photometric andradial velocity standardsare selected and preparedby theIAU Committees responsible formaintaining them.The specific sources for data in each tableare discussed below aswell aslisted in theNotesand References section of The Astronomical Almanac.

# 13.1.3Sources of Position

Positional dataare included in each table in thissectionof The Astronomical Almanac.Often the original data sources for a table contain imprecise or inaccurate positions.In these cases, the positions have likely been updated using an astrometric quality catalog.Specifics for each tableare given inthe Notes and Reference section of The Astronomical Almanac.For many of the tables,the U.S.Naval ObservatoryMerged Astrometric Database,NOMAD,was used.

# 13.1.3.1 NOMAD

Quite often users of star catalogs want the best astrometry available.Although existing data servicesprovide a wealth of astrometry-the best example being the Centre de Donnees astronomiques de Strasbourg (CDS; URL[2])—yet none give users the single most accurate result for any particular object.During the 199Os the U.S.Naval Observatory undertook a project to provide this information.The result of this project, named Naval Observatory Merged Astrometric Database,orNOMAD,became publicly available in2Oo4(Zacharias et al. 2004a); see URL[3].

NOMAD isa database containing information on over1 billion stars,including all the bright stars.The faintlimitingmagnitudeisabout $V = 2 0 .$ Itprovides the best astrometry (and often the best photometry) for any set of stars.For each star,only one high-quality position and proper motion is listed,regardless of the number ofcatalogs in which the star is listed. Several photometric bandsare given,including 2MASS near IR photometry.Additionally, there are cross references to other catalogs.The database is accessed through a Graphical User Interface.

Astrometry inNOMADistaken from six inputcatalogs:TheHipparcosCatalogue (Perryman andESA 1997); Tycho-2 (Hog et al.2000),UCAC2 (Zacharias et al. 2004b), YB6 (an unpublished re-measurement of the Lick and Yale proper motion plates,Monet 2004),2MASS point source catalog (Cutri et al.2003; URL[4]),and USNO-B (Monet et al. 2003).Due to varying magnitude-dependent accuracies of the input catalogs,the following precedence isused for the astrometry found in NOMAD.For stars brighter than $V = 8 . 0$ ： Hipparcos,Tycho-2,thenUCAC2.For $V = 8 . 0$ and fainter,the order of precedence is Hipparcos,UCAC2,Tycho-2,USNO-B,YB6,then 2MAsS.As for photometry,the optical dataare taken in thefollowing order:Tycho-2,YB6,USNO-B,then UCAC2.For near IR photometry,only 2MASS wasused.

Since NOMAD provides high quality astrometry for any star regardless of magnitude,it has been used as the basis of positions and motions in many tables found in The Astronomical Almanac.

# 13.2 Stellar Data

# 13.2.1 Bright Stars

One of themost used partsof The Astronomical Almanac is the tableof Bright Stars.It is used for a wide array of purposes,most notably to set telescope coordinates,focus cameras, and calibrate optics.For thisreason,youwill often find acopy of the book ina telescope dome.It is useful in preparing finding charts for observing runs or for training students in observational techniques.The list isalso used for nautical,air,and land navigation.Due to its popularity to a wide range of users,the list is constructed to be an isotropic,dense group of stars to the visual magnitude limit of 5.5.

Theoriginal selection wastaken from the Basic Fifth Fundamental Catalogue (FK5, Frickeetal.1988)and the fifth editionof theYale Bright Star Catalog (BSC5,Hoffleit and Warren1991).All starsbrighterthanvisual magnitude5.5 in theFK5and4.5 intheBSC were included.Also integrated into thetablewasa listofMKAtlas standardsand anchorpoints fortheMKspectral classificationsystem (Morgan,Abt,and Tapschot1978;Keenanand McNeil 1976). Since many of these standard stars overlap with stars already included in the above selection,a separate tablein The Astronomical Almanac was not necessary.When the coordinates of the bright stars were updated to the International Celestial Reference System (ICRS),it was decided to keep the selection of stars the same as in previous years since it was complete.Thus,the criteriaforinclusionin the list of Bright Starsare those mentioned above,plus being listedin the Hipparcos Catalogue.Only14 starsfrom the originalFK5/BSC selectedlistwere excluded due to this finalcriterion.

Positions for the list of Bright Stars are taken from the Hipparcos Catalog.Theproper motion,parallaxand radial velocity,whenavailable,are included in theroutineswhich produce geocentric stellar positions for the mean equator and equinox of the middle of the editionyear.From the 20o3 edition through the20o8 edition,the Bright Star list typically used Hipparcos positions with Tycho-2 Catalogue proper motions.In the preparation of the 2009 edition,Tycho-2 proper motions were replaced,so thatall stars now use Hipparcos astrometry;the effects were minimal.If astaris listed in the HipparcosDoubleand Multiple

Systemannex (DMS),then the astrometry in the DMS takes precedence.An exception is for stars marked with an“o”flag in the table.These arewide orbit stars:positions of each star arecalculated using center-of-lightFK5 astrometryand orbital data fromthe Sixth Catalog ofOrbitsofVisual Binary Starsat URL[5].

The apparent visual magnitudes and colors are taken from the BSC5,since these have been carefully scrutinized over the years.For several stars with large variability,a magnitude rangeis determined ona case bycase basis.Spectral types originate fromW.P.Bidelman who maintained a card catalog of stellar spectral types,compiled bya lifetime of personal research.Hegraciouslyprovided typesforthestars listed here,and since then,theyhave been updated byR.F.Garrison.The MK standards define the spectral classification system and types are taken from the MK atlas.The anchor pointsare a subset of standard stars which have survived through the years with no change,and which,therefore,represent the most stable points in the system.A note is given in the table when either the magnitudes or spectral typesare from more than one stellar source (i.e.,a blended image).

# 13.2.2 Double Stars

Double star systems permit the determination of the stellar mass,hence their importance in astrophysics,given that the structure and evolution of a star are basically dependent on two parameters:(1) the initial chemical content,and (2) the initial mass.While most of the main sequence iswell determined,the two endsare still poorlycharacterized,asare stars off the main sequence.Even for stellar typeswhich are well known,additional mass determinations can probe more subtle differences inchemical properties as well as intra-and inter-cluster variations.The numbers and distribution of binary stars also provide insights into the process of star formation.

Doubleand multiple stars constitute an important fraction of the stellar population.The term double star includes optical pairs formed by chance juxtaposition of unrelated stars, aswell as true binaries that are physically related.For many double stars there is not enough information to determine whether the pairis physical; therefore,catalogs also include optical doubles.Historically,double stars havealso been differentiatedby theobservational meansused to discover and study them.Thus,there are not only visual doubles,but also spectroscopic,astrometric,photometric,interferometric,and occultation pairs.With the development of modern observing techniques,however,these distinctions are increasingly blurred.

Multiple systems,containing more than two components,generallyare classedas either hierarchical or nonhierarchical.In the first instance,a typical triple will contain aclose pair andathird component,located at a distance many times the separation of the close pair.These hierarchical multiples,despite beingquite small,are usually virialized.Inthe second instance, the separations of all the components are roughly the same.Examples of these classes are Epsilon Hydrae (hierarchical) and the Trapezium (nonhierarchical).

Nomenclature for double stars is extremely mixed.Visual pairs are usually designated by the discoverer'sabbreviation and serial number,although some observers have neglected tonumberalloraportionof theirpairs.Eclipsingpairsare designatedby the variable star nomenclature.Spectroscopic binaries are usually designated by their Bayer/Flamsteed designation or HD number. Occultation binaries are often denoted by their SAO or ZC number,although thisis byno meansuniversal.There appears to be no standard nomenclature whatsoever for astrometric or other more exotic binaries,which are given a multitude of designations taken from various catalogs.An effort is underway to unify the various nomenclature schemes.For further details on this effort,see the Washington Multiplicity Catalog (WMC) webpage at URL[6].

Withafew exceptions,the primary (A component) of a visual double is the brighter star; the secondary (B component)is fainter.There are systems of very small magnitude difference, however,where this distinction is arbitrary.Further,there are cases where the distinction of which component is brighter depends on the observed wavelength.Additional components in amultiple systemare designated C,D,and so on,in order of discovery;simultaneous discoveries are ordered by increasing separation.It sometimes happens that a new,close, companion is discovered in an already defined system.The conventionis then to designate it with lower case letters.For example,if theAcomponent of a wide pair is split into a close pair,then starAisredesignatedasthe systemAa,Ab.However,these rulesof component designation have not always been historically followed. The WMC is striving to correct all these errors.

Thetableof SelectedDouble Starsin TheAstronomical Almanac givesorbital data for doublestarsinthe tableofBright Stars.Positionsare of the primary starand taken from the Bright Star table.As indicated by the epoch,the orbital data are either the most recent measure(a date inthe past) or calculatedforthe epoch of the edition.The criteria forinclusion in the table is that the pair(1) is listed in the table of Bright Stars;(2) includes the primary; (3)has a separation greater than3arcseconds;and(4) hasamagnitude difference of less than3magnitudes.A few other systems of recent interest are also present.Therefore,the systems that are included are those that would be seen by most observers with a moderatesized telescope.

Thedata listed in this tableare taken from the Washington Double StarCatalog (WDS; URL[7]),also issued in CD form.It is an update of earlier editions of the WDS(1984,1996 and2001) andis thereplacementforthe IndexCatalog ofVisual DoubleStars,1961.0,issued bythe Lick Observatory,and commonlycalled the IDS.While the format of the WDS was initially the same as the IDS,the catalog has evolved to include other parameters such as precise (arcsecond) coordinates of the primary,proper motion of the secondary,and codes to indicate the object is in one of the other USNO double star catalogs.As of this writing (2012),the WDS contains orbital data and other information for components of 108,628 systems.The“current”versionof theWDS isregenerated each dayand made available on thewebsite.This site also includes lists of“neglected"doubles (those needing observation or confirmation),verified single stars (for calibration purposes),cross-references to historical double star catalogs and modern astrometric catalogs,as well as various statistical and historicalfiles.Thecompanion catalog to theWDS is thePhotometric MagnitudeDifference Catalogat URL[8].Thiscatalog contains the differential measures of each pair.

# 13.2.2.1 Other Double Star Data

TheU.S.Naval Observatory also maintains the Visual Orbit Catalog (currently in its 6th version；URL[5])，whichcontainsorbital elements,ephemerides,notes,orbitplots,and objective grades for2,236 orbitsof2,127 systems (asofJanuary 2011) as wellasanadditional subset of orbits considered of sufficient quality to be used for scale calibration.A listof1,165 systems whose motion can be characterized as rectilinearis available from USNO at URL[9]. These optical systems can also be quite effective for scale calibration purposes as well as differential proper motion determinations.

The USNO also maintainsthe MagnitudeDifference Catalog (54,436 systems,165,058 measures as of November 2O11) of this quantity and the Interferometric Measures Catalog, which contains many of the same measures of this type common to the WDs, but provides additional information such as the wavelength of observation.

Eclipsing and cataclysmic binaries are listed by Downes et al.(2oo1),Ritter and Kolb (1998),van Paradijs (1995),and Svechnikov and Bessonova(1984).These catalogs contain a variety of parameters whose enumeration is not appropriate for this discussion.

The venerable catalog of orbits of spectroscopic binaries by Batten,Fletcher,and Mc-Carthy (1989) is in the process of being updated by IAU Commission 30 (Radial Velocities) underthe guidance ofD.Pourbaix.Forfurtherinformation see URL[1O].Unfortunately,there is no modern catalog for known or suspected spectroscopic binaries that have no calculated orbit.

# 13.2.3 Photometric Standards

Photometric systemsare defined by the filters used to measure flux of objects in different wavelength passbands.The various photometric systems in use are summarized in Table 15.5ofAllen's Astrophysical Quantities(Allen andCox 2Ooo).Widely used atthis time is the UBVRI Johnson-Kron-Cousins system which is a combination of the Johnson-Morgan UBV system(Johnson and Morgan 1953)and the Kron-Cousins $R I$ (Kron,White,andGascoigne 1953;Cousins 1976).This system has broad passbands $7 0 0 { - } 1 0 0 0 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }$ wide,with effective wavelengths of 3600A,4400 A,5500 A, $6 4 0 0 \mathring { \mathrm { A } }$ and $7 9 0 0 \mathring { \mathrm { A } }$ ,respectively.

Standard stars are used to define photometric systems.They are needed to calibrate telescopes and reduce raw observations to produce stellar magnitudes.The standards provided byLandolt are widely used to define the UBVRI system.These standards are located in a narrow band surrounding the celestial equator and,therefore,are convenient to astronomers worldwide.At their inception,theyserved tofill the need forafainter listof standard stars giventhe improvement of telescope optics.Landolt's first list was of 642 faint UBV standard stars $1 0 . 5 < V < 1 2 . 5$ withina1 degree band of the celestial equator(Landolt 1973),followed byalistof 223 faint UBVRI standards stars $7 < V < 1 2 . 5$ and $- 0 . 3 < ( B - V ) < + 2 . 0$ within a2degree band of the celestial equator(Landolt1983).The second list had starscalibrated in the $U B V$ by the first list and $R I$ ofCousins (1976).The observations are made in what is termed selected areas with findingcharts listed in the publication.

Beginning with the2O13editionof TheAstronomicalAlmanac,the printedlistis based on Landolt(2Oo9) which provides new UBVRI observations of 202 stars centered on the celestial equator,augmenting the 526 stars fromLandolt (1992) which had been the source in earlier editions.The almanac list iscomprisedof the combinedlist givenin Table 2ofLandolt (2009) and provides standard star observations in the magnitude range $8 . 9 0 < V < 1 6 . 3 0$ and color range $- 0 . 3 5 < ( B - V ) < + 2 . 3 0$ .The list serves to provide standards for intermediate and large size telescopesin both hemispheres.

For the list in The Astronomical Almanac,many of the positions of the stars given in Table2 of Landolt (2Oo9)were replaced with those from the Naval Observatory Merged

Astrometric Database (NOMAD;URL[3]),which provides the best positions on the ICRF from the following sourcesin the order given:Hipparcos Catalogue,Tycho-2,UCAC2,or USNO-B(see $\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { ~ } } & { { } } & { \ S \ 1 3 . 1 . 3 . 1 . 3 . 1 } \end{array}$ ).Proper motions are also included in thecalculation of the positions when provided in the database.However,for positions of stars new to the 2O13 edition from yearsprior,theirpositions are taken fromLandolt (2Oo9);their accuracy has yettobe verified and may be off by several arcseconds.

TheLandolt standards replaced the bright UBVRI standards from Johnson beginning with the 2003 edition of The Astronomical Almanac.Given that the Johnson standards are stars about $V$ magnitude6.Oand brighter,itwasdeemed that theywere toobrightformuch of the work being done at the time—especially observations using CCD photometers-and probably only useful to small telescope users.There are also known variables on the list and ratherlarge known random errors,given that for many stars there are less than 1Oobservations. TheJohnson standardsare nowprovided on The Astronomical Almanac Online for epoch J2000.0.The list was selected by H.L.Johnson from those given in Johnson et al.(1966), who selected an evenly distributed group of 1O7 stars,with 45 stars being primary standards of the UBVor UBVRI systems,and the rest of the stars were those that were observed many times (those starswith more than9 observations).The star names,positions,and spectral typesare taken fromtheBright Star list.

Also included in The Astronomical Almanac are standards for the Stromgren uvby intermediate-band photometric system and the $\mathrm { H } \beta$ narrow-band system,providing an excellent tool for supplementing the wide-band photometric systems.Wide-band systems give onlyafew parameters that can be used to get information on stellar (and other) characteristics,such as surface temperatures,absolute magnitudes,and interstellar reddening (and absorption).The intermediate and narrow-band photometric indices offer more potential and are designed also to be able to separate parameters.For example,indices are responsive not only to temperature,but toa varietyof otherparameters,such as heavy element abundance and surface gravity.Because they are seen in most stars-they are strongest in stars with a surface temperatures of about10,Ooo kelvin (stellar spectral type A)—the hydrogen Balmer seriesare often used to determine stellar radial velocities by analyzing the Doppler shift of these lines.Photometric standards for these systems are,therefore,importanttoolsin insuring both accurate and precise photometry that can be used for these purposes.

The Stromgren photometric system was pioneered by Stromgren (1956)and upgraded by Crawford (1958),hence it is sometimes referred to as the Stromgrem-Crawford photometric system. The passbandsare180-300Awide centered at 3500A,4110A,4670A,and $5 4 7 0 \mathrm { \AA }$ ,with apair of passbands at $4 6 8 0 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }$ associated with the hydrogen Balmer lines.

The uvby and $\mathrm { H } \beta$ standards widely used by photometric observers and listed in The Astronomical Almanac are those of Perry,Olsen,and Crawford (1987).Only the 319 stars which have four-colordataare included.Star namesand numbers in the list are taken from theBSC;spectral typesare those from the Bright Star list (pp.H2-H31).Visual magnitudes are taken from the original reference and,therefore,may disagree with those given elsewhere in this section.

The third setof photometricstandards includedin The Astronomical Almanacare suitable forthereduction of astronomical spectroscopic observations in the optical and ultraviolet wavelengths.Spectroscopy pertains to the dispersion of an object’s light into its component colors (i.e.,energies)and probes the interaction between radiation and matteras a function of wavelength. Spectroscopy ispossible by appropriate techniques inall wavelength regimes; spectrophotometry refers to spectroscopy with visible,near-ultraviolet,and near-infrared light.Applications of spectroscopy are the backbone of astrophysics,probing both the conditions of emissionof radiation and its modification en route to the observer.By dispersing andanalyzinganobject’s light,astronomers can infer thephysical propertiesof that object (such as temperature,mass,luminosity and composition).The standards provide absolute spectral energy distributions with which to calibrate observations using these techniques.

The data for the spectrophotometric standard stars listed in The Astronomical Almanac were recommended by the IAU Standard Stars Working Group and taken from the European Southern Observatory's (ESO) site at URL[11] except for the positions which are taken from the NOMAD database as described in $\ S \ 1 3 . 1 . 3 . 1$ (URL[3]).Finding charts for the sources and plots of the magnitudeand flux against wavelength are found on the website.

The standards on theESO listare from four sources.Theultraviolet standardsare from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) ultraviolet spectrophotometric standards which are based on International Ultraviolet Explorer (IUE)and optical spectra and calibrated by the primary white dwarf standards(Turnshek etal.199O；Bohlin etal.199O).The optical standards are based on Hale $5 \mathrm { m }$ observations in the7to 16 magnitude range (Oke 199O) and CTIO observations of southern hemisphere secondary and tertiary standard stars (Hamuyetal. 1992,1994).Data for four white dwarf primary spectrophotometric standards in the 11-13 magnitude range based on model atmospheres and HSTFaint Object Spectrograph (FOS) observations in $1 0 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ to 3microns are also included (Bohlin,Colina,and Finley 1995).

# 13.2.4Radial Velocity Standards

The motion of an object along the line of sight from an observer can be obtained directly from spectroscopic measurements.Radialvelocity observations are used for a wide range of applications (clusterdynamics,galaxy dynamics,stellar pulsations,mass and orbital element determinations in spectroscopic binary systems,etc.),butthe instrumentationused,reduction of data,and standardization of the results are roughly similar.Areliable set of radial velocity standardsare crucial in monitoring instrument stability and zero-points.The dificulty with creating a list of standards has been that they must be non-variable sources in magnitude with a sufficient history of observations.

In the late 198Os,IAU Commission 30on Radial Velocities established a Working Group onStandard Stars,tasked to createasystem of standards.Since this time,the listof standards inTheAstronomical Almanachas been mirroring therecommendationswhich haveresulted from their efforts in establishing a system of standards.The list was initially based on two reports from Commission30 (IAU1957,1973) which combined represented theIAU radial velocity starswith late spectral types at that time.At the IAU General Assemblies in both Kyoto (1997)and Manchester (2Oo0) it was the policy of the reestablished Working Group to remove stars from this provisional list that showed velocity variations greater than about $1 \mathrm { k m / s }$ ,whilecontinuing the work of three observatories developing an officially sanctioned list of standards.Geneva Observatory,Dominion Astrophysical Observatory (DAO),and the Centerfor Astrophysics(CfA) have each been working on establishing the list.The work of the three observatories is summarized ina report ofIAU Commission 30 (IAU1992).

Geneva Observatory has observed potential radial velocity standard stars for over 2O years using the CORAVEL instrument. These include many giants known to possess an intrinsic velocityvariabilityatthelevel ofafewhundredsof $\mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }$ orless.This list is useful for low- and medium-precision work.Using the high-precision velocities from the Elodie spectrometer,it is possible to correct the small systematic color terms in the CORAVEL velocities.A set of 350 dwarf stars is being followed with this instrument with a precision of $1 0 \mathrm { m / s }$ and a zeropoint stability of a few $\mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }$ ,to form in the future anew set of high-precision standards.Both lists are found at URL[12]; see also Udry et al.(1999).

The results of photographic standard star measurements at DAO have a precision of $1 5 0 \mathrm { m / s }$ overa time span of almost 2O years,with an absolute zero-point linked to asteroids with a precision of $1 0 0 \mathrm { m / s }$ or better.The comparison with Elodie velocities shows anabsence of any color terms.

In the past, the comparison of the Geneva velocities and the combined DAO and CfA velocities showed acolor term,the source ofwhich is still not clear.Work continues on understanding this issue.Until itisresolved,Commission 3O cannot recommendarevised list.There has not been a great amount ofresolution on this issue since the Manchester IAU General Assembly.The emergence of the higher precision radial velocity requirements associated with the study of exoplanets certainly has reinforced the need to continue work to thisend.

Inthe2Oo2 editionof The Astronomical Almanac,a listoffaint stars with radial velocity data (Evans 1968)was added to extend the list to the Southern Hemisphere.There was some trepidation from Commission 3O as to adding these 14 stars,but it was decided they were suitable,since they had been observed for more than 2O years.

The three lists provided by the IAU references were upgraded forthe almanac by adding positions and proper motions from the Hipparcos Catalogue; $V$ magnitudes from the BSC, theHipparcosCatalogue,or SIMBAD;and spectral types from the listofBright Stars (pp.H2-H31),the BSC,the Hipparcos Catalogue,or the original IAU list.

# 13.2.5 Variable Stars

Starswith variable light output are classified based on the type of variability exhibited and aredivided in two maincategories,intrinsic andextrinsic.Theintrinsic variablesare stars undergoing internal physical changes due to pulsations or eruptions. Intrinsic variable stars include pulsating stars (Cepheid variables,RRLyrae stars,RV Tauri Stars,and long period variables such as Mira type stars)and erupting stars (supernovae,novae,recurrent novae,and dwarf novae,symbiotic stars,andRCoronae Borealis stars).Extrinsic variablesare due to external factorsand include stars such as eclipsing binariesand rotating variables.Variable star data can provide a wealth of information about stellar properties that can be used to understand other stars such as mass,radius,luminosity,and temperature.Cepheid variables allowfor determining distances to far away galaxies and the age of the universe.

The variability of magnitude in stars can range from periods of a fraction of a second to years,depending upon the type.Therefore,variable stars need a long history of observations to be correctly classified and understood.The American Association of Variable Star Observers(AAVSO;URL[13]) is an established organizationof professional and amateur astronomers which serves tocoordinate,collect,evaluate,analyze,publish,and archivevariable star observations.

Thelist in The Astronomical Almanac was selected and updated by astronomersat the AAVSO.Itis based on data provided by the AAVsO and,since the 2Ooo edition,information containedwithin the fourth editionof the General CatalogueofVariable Stars(GCVS, Kholopov etal.1996),the Sky Catalogue2000.0,Volume2(Hirshfeld and Sinnott1997), andACatalogueandAtlasofCataclysmicVariables,2ndEdition(Downes,Webbink,and Shara 1997).The selection of these sources is based on having the brightest stars from each classificationrepresented.Specific criteria forinclusion are givenin the Notes and References section of The Astronomical Almanac.Positions and proper motions were updated for the list in TheAstronomical Almanac,starting with the 2006 edition,using theNOMAD database which is consistent with the ICRS (see $\ S \ 1 3 . 1 . 3 . 1$ and URL[3]).

The nomenclature used for variable stars was developed byF.W.A.Argelanderin the mid-1800s.According to this method,the first star discovered inaconstellation is given the letter R,followed by the genitive form of the Latin name of the constellation.Subsequent variables in the same constellation are named S,T,and so on to Z,followed byRR to RZ; then SS to SZ,and so on to ZZ,after which follow AA to AZ,and so on to QZ,the 334th variable in aconstellation.The next variablefound inaconstellation is named V335 plus the genitive. Stars which have been assigned Greek letters priorto the start ofthis system,such as Omicron Ceti,or small Roman letters,such as $^ \mathrm { g }$ Herculis,continue to keep those names.

# 13.2.6 Exoplanets and Host Stars

An extrasolar planet,or exoplanet for short,isa planet that orbits a star other than the Sunand that is beyond the solar system.Most have been detected through radial velocity observations-the Doppler method—and otherindirect methods rather than actual imaging. Given the increased interest and research in exoplanets,adding them to The Astronomical Almanac,starting with the 2O11 version,was considered to be valuable to the observing community.

The table of exoplanets and their host stars is based upon updated versions of the Catalog ofNearby Exoplanets (Butler 2Oo6).The almanac draws from the most currently available version at the time of publicationwhich can be found at URL[14] wherea table of host star characteristics is also available.It contains all the known exoplanets with masses and orbits established by Doppler measurements of stars within 2OO parsecs.Given the number of exoplanets continues to grow rapidly,selection criteria must be applied to keep the table inthe almanac to a manageable size.For example,for the2Ol3 edition of The Astronomical Almanac,a subset from the data set was chosen usinga host star magnitude limit of $V < 8$ Also incorporated into the table are all transiting exoplanets,which pushes the limiting magnitude of the table inthealmanac to about $V = 1 0$ .Thecatalog'sauthor and exoplanet pioneer,P.Butler,suggested that useful propertiesof the exoplanets and host stars from the catalog would fall into two categories.For those most interested in the properties of theplanets,orbital period,eccentricity,and time of periastron are the most helpful.This forinstanceiswhatisneeded to calculate time of transit for the transitingplanets.Those most interested in the host stars of exoplanets would want $B - V$ magnitude,parallax,and metallicity. The $V$ magnitude of the star would be useful in both instances.These data are takenfrom the online catalogand includedin The Astronomical Almanac table.

Positions,proper motionsand parallaxes (where available) for the host stars were taken independently from theNOMAD database(§13.1.3.1; URL[3]).The mid-year positions for the host stars given in The Astronomical Almanac table are improved from those positions listedin the original version of thecatalogas they areboth more accurate and precise;several inconsistencies in the catalog were corrected.

# 13.2.7 Pulsars

Radio pulsarsare rapidly rotating neutron stars having strong magnetic fields.Because of their smallphysical size,pulsars are unresolved by even the largest radio interferometers,and arevaluable as astrometric reference sources.Because of theirlarge moments of inertia,the rotation period of pulsars is extremely regular,in most cases showing only a linear increase in the period with time as the pulsar loses energy.The oldest,coolest pulsars have longtermspin stabilities comparable to the best terrestrial clocksand are being studied foruseas navigational beacons,similar to GPS.The discovery of pulsars twice resulted in the award of theNobel Prize in Physics:in 1974 to Antony Hewish for the discovery of the firstpulsars, and in1993 to Joseph Taylor andRussell Hulse for the discovery of the first binary pulsar, $\mathbf { B } 1 9 1 3 + 1 6$ .

Thelist of pulsars in The Astronomical Almanac dates from the 2oo7 edition.It is derived from Manchester et al. (2oo5; URL[15]) and updated annually.The positions are,in nearly all cases,timing positions which are measures of arrival times on various dates spread over a time period.These are accurate wellunder an arcsecond for“normal”pulsars and under the milliarcsecond level for millisecond pulsars.Positions are in the coordinate system defined by the JPL planetary ephemeris.(The particular ephemeris used may vary from source to source,depending on how recently theanalysis was done.) Ina few cases,interferometric positionswith comparable accuracy have been obtained.

# 13.3 Clusters and Galaxies

# 13.3.1 Open Star Clusters

Open clusters are irregularly shaped groups of stars moving together with roughly the same space motion.They generally liein the galactic plane and contain prominent young stars or star forming regions.Unlike associations which are often dynamically unbound groups of stars less than 1 million years old,open clusters are dynamically bound groups of stars ranging in ages fromless than1million years to more than1billion years.It is theorized that open clusters are those dynamically bound associations which survived being dispersed into smaller groups of stars.Open clusters are distinctly different from globular clusters,which areseveral billion yearsold and found inthe galactic halo.

Open clusters are a crucial element in understanding the structure and history of our Galaxy,given that distances and ages of open clusters can be determined with beter accuracy than forisolated field stars.Opencluster systemsarekey tounderstanding galactic dynamics, because they spanarelativelywide range ofageswhichcan be determinedwith more precision thanany other spiral arm tracer.They are used to better understand the motion of spiral arms to derive the galactic rotation curve.They also provide a means to map the chemical composition in our Galaxy.In addition,open clusters providea set of coeval stars with different masses that canbe used toderiveand test theories of starformation,suchas the initial mass function,rate of matter transferred into stars,the rate of density of cloud,and rate of binary stars.

The table of open clusters was selected by GostaLyngä based upon the 5th(1987) edition of the Lund-Strasbourg catalog(original edition described by G.Lynga 1981).The clusters were selected on the basis of the availability of dataat the timeand,therefore,comprise the most observed open clusters.The selection of the clustersremained while the data were updated over the years.Forthe2Oo5 edition ofThe AstronomicalAlmanac,cluster data were updated using theNew Catalog ofOptically Visible Open Clustersand Candidates (Dias et al.2002).The online version of thecatalog isavailable at URL[16],where additional data parameters are given and updated annually.The Astronomical Almanac for 2O13 uses version 3.1of this catalog.Another useful resource for open cluster datais John-Claude Mermiliod's WEBDAopenclusterdatabaseatURL[17].

Listed in the table of open clusters are a number of associations that might not develop into physically bound clusters.These are the Trapezium cluster and some of the Collinder objects. TheBlancoand Upgrenclustersare considered physical entities,because their members have similarmotionsand physicalproperties,although theyare onlyasmallpartof the total stellar population inside the volume they occupy. Clusters that are only the apparent result of the distribution of obscuring clouds have been excluded from the list.

Two identifications are given for each cluster.The first is the designation adopted by theIAU,while the secondis the traditional name (Alter etal.197O).The International Astronomical Union decided in 1979 ona nomenclature consisting of a“C" for cluster,four digits for hours and minutes of right ascension (195o.O) and a plus or minus sign plus three digits for degrees and tenths of degrees of declination (1950.0).

All data parameters for the open clusters are taken from the New Catalogof Optically Visible Open Clusters and Candidates (Dias et al. 2oo2) including positions and proper motions.Positions are consistent with the ICRS and referred to the mean equator and equinox of the middle of the Julian year.

The tabulated angular diameter of a cluster refers to the cluster's nucleus.The published values of angular diameters of clusters sometimes differ bya large factor due to different limiting magnitudes for different studies and to different opinions about the extent of each cluster.

Distances refer to the open cluster's distance from the Sun.Its determination depends on the chemical composition which is not known for many clusters.Assuming a solar composition,asis usually done,introduces some uncertainty.The most reliable way to determine the distance of an open cluster is to observe its color-magnitude diagram and fit the diagram to the main sequence of nearby stars.When a cluster includes a number of main sequence stars,this method of determining distances is superior to other methods used for distant stars or star systems.For the distance of anearby cluster with a long main sequence, a precision as low as $10 \%$ can be achieved.

Trumpler classification is defined byR.S.Trumpler(1930).The classification scheme contains the following classes:

·Concentration of cluster stars ·Idetached,strong central concentration ·I detached,little central concentration ·III detached, no noticeable concentration ·IV not well detached,appearing like a star-field concentration

·Range of stellar brightness ·1most stars of nearly the same brightness ■2 medium range in brightness ·3bright and faint stars in thecluster

·Richness of cluster(number of stars in the cluster)

·ppoor( $( < 5 0$ stars)   
·m moderately rich (50-100 stars)   
·rrich( $> 1 0 0$ stars)

·Nebulosity

·n cluster is involved in nebulosity

The definition of the metallicity ratio is the logarithmic ratio of the abundances of iron and hydrogen divided by the corresponding ratio for the solar spectrum.Log (Fe/H) is mostly determined from photometric narrow band or intermediate band studies.In practice,metal lines other than those of iron influence the parameter.

Thelogarithm to the base 1Oof the cluster age in years is determined fromthe turnoff point on the main sequence.To have a homogeneous set of cluster ranges,Janes, Tilley,and Lyngä derived a calibration of the logarithm of age as a function of the turn-off color. The listed values of around 6.O (one million years) can be considered as rough upper limits in most cases.Clusters of that age group might still be in the processof star formation and thus have a large spread in age between member stars.Clusters with logarithmic ages higher than 7.0 maybe in error of up to a few tenths of a unit.

Interstellar extinction in front of the cluster in the visual band is tabulated using the assumption that $\mathrm { A _ { V } } = 3 \mathrm { E _ { ( B - V ) } }$ .Thecolorexcess $ { \mathrm { E _ { ( B - V ) } } }$ is derived fromsome of the brightest cluster members.

An expected update to the open clusters in coming editions will include the distance from the galactic plane.By knowing the galactic latitude and the distance,one can easily compute the distance from the galactic plane,usually noted $\mathbf { Z }$ .

# 13.3.2 Globular Star Clusters

Formore than acentury,globularclustershave provided astronomers with testbed laboratories forall kinds of astrophysical processes.First noticed for their variable star populations (RR Lyraestars,Population IICepheids,red-giant variables,novae),they laterbecame essential proving grounds for the development of the theory of stellar structure and stellar evolution, andthey remain the single most important way to measure the age of our Galaxy.Globular clustersarevery tightly bound,giving themspherical shapesand relatively high stellar densities toward their centers,and contain more stars than the less dense open clusters.Found inthe halo ofa galaxy,globular clustersare fairly common.Itis thought that globular clusters contain some of the oldestand first stars to be produced in agalaxy and formed with the parent galaxyrather than as distinctly separate objects.The 197Os and198Ossaw the discoveries of newhigh-energy sourceswithin globular clusters (X-ray binaries and millisecond pulsars), and computer modeling of the internal dynamical evolution of clusters allthe way through to core collapse.Newer observations are concentrating on tracing the faintest stars within the clusters (the bottom ends of the ancient white-dwarf sequence),and on the characteristics of globular clusters in other galaxies.

Thelistof globular clustersin The Astronomical Almanac isprovidedbyWilliamE. Harris,who maintains a database ofMilky Way globular clusters at URL[18].The online list isupdated regularlyand provides dataon distances,velocities,metallicities,luminosities, colors,and dynamical parameters.

# 13.3.3Bright Galaxies

The bright galaxies are used by many groups doing high-resolution work on giant and supergiant stars,star formation and stellar populations,star clusters and associations,HI regions,novae and other bright variables,planetary nebulae,gas and dust distribution,spiral armmodeling,dark matter searches,dwarf companions,the nearby velocity field,and as fundamental or secondary calibrators for the extragalactic distance scale.The two hundred or so galaxiesin The Astronomical Almanac form the backbone forallof these studies,so they constitute a finding list of the most important galaxies in the nearby universe.These are generally the most photogenic extragalactic objectsas well,so they appear often in popular publications.Therefore,the list isalso useful for the non-specialist asa handy reference to the basic properties of the galaxies.

The198 galaxies selected for inclusion in The Astronomical Almanac are generally brighter than B-band magnitude11.5and larger than 5.O arcminutes.These galaxieswere extractedfromanupdated versionoftheThirdReferenceCatalogueofBrightGalaxies (RC3, de Vaucouleurs et al.1991)or,in the case of two dwarf spheroidal galaxies (in Sextans and Sagitarius) not included in RC3,from the literature(see IrwinandHatzidimitriou1995; Ibata et al. 1997).

Inmost cases,theRC3 equatorial position has been replaced withamore accurate weighted mean position based on measurements from many different sources,some unpublished.Nominally,all positionsare on the ICRS,explained elsewhere in this volume.Where positions for unresolved nuclearradio sources from high-resolution interferometry (usually at 6-or $2 0 \mathrm { - c m }$ are known to coincide with the position of the optical nucleus,we have adopted the radio positions.Similarly,we have adopted positions from the 2-Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS,see e.g.,Jarrett et al.2Oo3) where these coincide with the optical nucleus.Positions for magellanic irregular galaxies without nuclei (e.g.,LMC,NGC 6822,IC1613) are for the centersof the bars inthese galaxies.Positions for the dwarf spheroidal galaxies(e.g.,Fornax, Sculptor, Carina)referto the peaks of the optical luminosity distributions.The precision with which the position is listed reflects the accuracy with which it is known.The mean errors in the listed positionsare 2-3 digits in the last place given.

Agalaxy'smorphological type is based on the revised Hubble system described byde Vaucouleurs (1959a,1963).The system includes four classes:Ellipticals (E),Lenticulars (L), Spirals(S),and Irregulars (I).In theLand Sclasses (disk galaxies),two families,ordinary (A) and barred (B),are distinguished,with transition types denotedAB.Each family can exist in two varieties,depending on the absence (s)or presence (r) of an inner ring structure, with transition types denoted (rs).IntheLclass,the relative importance of the two major components of a galaxy—the spheroid(or bulge) and the disk—is denoted by the superscript “-"(early,ie.,mostsimilartoE),“O”(intermediate),d $\cdot _ { + } \cdot \mathbf { \vec { \tau } }$ (late,i.e.,moresimilar toS).

The stage along the Hubble sequence of spirals is denoted by a,b,c,and d in order of decreasing bulge-to-disk ratio and increasing development of the arms.The transition type between Land Sis denoted $\mathrm { S 0 / a }$ ；betweenSand magellanic irregulars (Im),bySm.Thus, theLarge Magellanic Cloud is the prototype of $\mathrm { S B } ( \mathrm { s } ) \mathrm { m }$ ,and the Andromeda galaxy (M31) of SA(s)b.An outer ring structure is designated by the symbol (R) preceding the Hubblestage designation.The puzzling nonmagellanic irregulars, of which M82 is the prototype,are designated IO.Edge-onsystemsare signaled by the sufix“sp”(for“spindle").Peculiarities are noted by the suffix “pec.”For more detailed discussions of galaxy morphology,see Sandage (1975) and Buta (1989).

Because physical properties,such as color index,hydrogen index,bulge-to-disk ratio, and so on,are closely correlated with stage along the Hubble sequence,it is convenient to attach to the stagea numerical scale,T,from $^ { - 5 }$ atE,through O at $\mathrm { S 0 / a }$ ,to10 atIm.The luminosityclass,L,introducedbyvandenBergh( $1 9 6 0 { \mathrm { a } } ,$ ,b)—orbetter,the luminosityindex $ \Lambda = ( \mathrm { T } + \mathrm { L } ) / 1 0$ (deVaucouleurs1979)—iscorrelated withabsolute magnitude.The $L$ scale, listed in The Astronomical Almanac,corresponds to van den Bergh luminosity class.At its inception,Lranfrom1 fora bright giant galaxy,to9fora faint dwarfsystem(luminosity classesI-V).In theRC3,thesystem was extended to include 10 and11on the numerical scale (classesV-VIand VI).For details,see Buta et al. (1994).

The isophotal apparent major diameter of galaxies,denoted Log $\mathrm { D } _ { 2 5 }$ ,is given in the table for the isophote level $2 5 . 0 \mathrm { B }$ -magnitude per square arcsecond $\mathbf { ( B { \mathrm { - } } m / s s ) }$ .The level corresponds roughly to the visible sizeof the galaxy image seen onthe Palomar Observatory Sky Survey paperprints (blue light),and to the diameters listed in the Uppsala General Catalog(Nilson 1973) for northern galaxies.Note that isophotal diametersare not metric diameters.The former depend on the surface brightness (specific intensity)of the galaxy image.The latter do notdepend on surface brightness,and while they are better indications of the true size of galaxies,theyare more difficult to measureandare not yetavailable for many galaxies.Note that to avoid negative logarithms,itis given in the table in units of O.1 arcminute.

The ratio of the major and minor axes at the isotope level 25.O blue mag/arcsec2 $( \mathbf { R } _ { 2 5 } = \mathbf { D } _ { 2 5 } / \mathbf { d } _ { 2 5 } )$ isalso listed in logarithmic form.The precision isusually better than O.1. Theisophotal axisratios given in the tableare corrected forthe systematic errors thataffect visual measurements of photographic plates (de Vaucouleurs 1959b).(For more details on systematic and random errors in diameter and axis ratios,seePaturel etal.1987and Paturel et al. 1991.)

The total apparent B-band magnitudes, $\mathbf { B } _ { T } ^ { w }$ aregenerally derived from photoelectric measurements or detailed photographic surface photometry in the Johnson B system.These magnitudes are weighted means of the best available magnitudes extrapolated to infinity to allow for the generally smallfraction of theluminosity contributed by the outermost regions ofagalaxy,as explained in the Introduction to RC3.The precision is generally $0 . 1 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ or better.(For details,see Buta etal.1995.)For some applications,the observed magnitudes need to be corrected for galactic and internal extinction,which are both controversial and uncertain by 0.1 to $0 . 2 \mathrm { \ m a g }$ or more in extreme cases (e.g.,an edge-on spiral galaxy near the galactic plane).The total color indices, $( B - V ) _ { T }$ and $( U - B ) _ { T }$ ,in the Johnson UBV systemsareasobserved;i.e.,uncorrected forgalacticand internal extinction.Therange is roughly from $+ 3 . 0 > ( B - V ) _ { T } > + 0 . 1$ and $- 0 . 3 > ( U - B ) _ { T } > + 0 . 6$ as one moves along the Hubble sequence from E(yellow-orange) to Im (whitish) through $\mathrm { L }$ and S types. By an oversimplification,the extreme typesare often described as“red”and“blue”galaxies.The colors give crude indications of the energy distribution in the continuum and,hence,of the stellarcomposition of a galaxy.However,the colors are often modified by line emission fromHII regions (which decreases the color index,or increases the blueness) and internal dust extinction (which increases the color index,or increases the redness).

The heliocentric radial velocity,derived from optical observations and 21-cm neutral hydrogen radio emission,is given in $\mathrm { k m } / \mathrm { s }$ and calculated with the optical convention that $\Delta \lambda { = } \mathbf { v } / \mathbf { c }$ Forthe small redshifts involved, this convention is entirely adequate.Fora discussion of systematicand accidental errors in radial velocities,see the Introduction to RC3.For cosmological applications,it is better to replace the conventional solar motion of $3 0 0 \mathrm { k m / s }$ toward $1 = 9 0 ^ { \circ }$ , $\mathbf b = 0 ^ { \circ }$ (seeIAU1977),with the total velocity vector of $3 7 1 . 0 \mathrm { k m / s }$ toward $1 = 2 6 4 . 1 4 ^ { \circ }$ , $\mathsf { b } = + 4 8 . 2 6 ^ { \circ }$ relative to the cosmic background radiation (see Fixsen et al.1996).

Data for other galaxiescan be found in two online databases:NED,theNASA/IPAC Extragalactic Databaseat URL[19];andLEDA,theLyon-Meudon Extragalactic Database at URL[20].

# 13.3.4Quasi-Stellar Objects

A quasar (the name is actually a contraction of “quasi-stellar radio source")is an extremely bright and distant active galactic nucleus.There was a great deal of initial controversy over the nature of quasars,but there is now a general consensus that they are a compact halo of mattersurroundinga supermassive black hole.They are observed as high redshift sources in the infarared,optical,ultraviolet,radio,X-Ray and gamma ray frequencies.They seem ratherstellar (point-like),rather than extended sources similar to galaxies.They display rapid changes in luminosity and are believed to be powered by the accretion of material into their nuclei,making these luminous versions of active galaxies.No other currently known mechanism appears able to explain the vast energy output and rapid variability.

At present,more than 1Oo,ooo quasars are known，all with redshifts ranging from $0 . 0 6 > \mathbf { z } > 6 . 4$ ；redshift isdefinedas $\mathrm { z } = \left( \lambda - \lambda _ { 0 } \right) / \lambda _ { 0 }$ ,where $\lambda _ { 0 }$ is the laboratory wavelength for the spectral line being observed.Quasar redshiftsare almost always measured with the broad-emission lines of hydrogen,helium,and other heavy elements that are characteristic of quasars.Most quasars are brightest in their rest-frame near-ultraviolet (near the $1 2 1 6 { \mathring \mathrm { A } }$ (Lyman-alpha line of hydrogen),but due to the tremendous redshifts of these sources, that peak luminosity hasbeen observed as far as $9 0 0 0 \mathring { \mathrm { A } }$ in the near infrared.The redshifts correspond to distances between 78O million and 28 billion light-years.Due to these great distances,quasars offer a glimpse into the very early universe.At this time,the Sloan Digital Sky Survey is in the process of measuring quasar properties; once complete,it will provide redshift values for approximately 10o,ooo quasars.

Thelistof quasars is originally fromA Catalogue of Quasars and Active Galactic Nuclei, lst ed.(Veron-Cetty and Veron 1984) using selection parameters suggested by Tim Heckman. These selection parameters did not have any physical significance other than allowing a list ofquasars with a variety ofcharacteristics.Since this catalog has been updated many times, TheAstronomicalAlmanac for 2Oo9 used the12th edition of thecatalog(Veron-Cetty and Veron 20o6;URL[21]).When updated,the aim for the list was to be a subsample of those types that are popular in current research interests:the optically brightest,strongest in radio and X-ray,highest known redshifts,gravitational lenses,brightest quasars with interesting absorptionsystems (e.g.,PHL 52oo),high redshift quasars suitable forHe IIGunn-Peterson observations (e.g., $\mathrm { H } 1 7 0 0 { + } 6 4$ ,QSO 0302-003).

# 13.4 Wavelength Specific Sources

# 13.4.1Radio-Source Positional Calibrators

The International Celestial Reference Frame (ICRF) sources are used forcalibration of many radio observationsmade using the VLBA and the European VLBI Network.However,all astronomers use the ICRFindirectly,as the Hipparcos Celestial ReferenceFrame(HCRF)is linkeddirectly to the ICRF.The ICRFis theIAU recommended fundamental astronomical reference frame and the positions in the ICRF form the underlying basis for all astrometry. Basically,theICRFis the frame to which all other reference frames,atall wavelengths,are referred (see Chapter 4).

TheICRF isa space-fixed frame based on high accuracy radio positions measured by VLBI.They are accurate for pointingand calibration ofradio telescopes.The list of radio source positions which originally defined the ICRF was introduced in the 1999 edition of TheAstronomical Almanac following itsadoption bytheIAUas the fundamental reference frameinJanuary1998 (Maetal.1998);onlythe212defining sourcesof the ICRFwere included due to space considerations.The ICRF defining sources were the best and most stable positions based on the knowledge of the sources at the time the ICRF was defined.

Anupdate to the ICRF occured in August of 2Oo9 in conjunction with the XXVII IAU General Assembly (IAU 2010,Res.B3;IERS2009).This second realization of the ICRF, termed the ICRF2,contains precise positions of 3414 compact radio sources.Maintenance ofICRF2 is made using a set of 295 new defining sources selected on the basis of positional stability,lack of extensive intrinsic source structure,and spatial distribution. These 295 defining sourcesare presented in The Astronomical Almanac.Positions of all ICRF2 sources areavailableatURL[22].

Information on the known physical characteristics of the ICRF2 radio sources includes, where known,the object type,8.4Ghzand2.3Ghzflux,spectral index,Vmagnitude,redshift, and aclassification of spectrum.Notes for many ICRF2 defining sources are also presented.

Thistable is compiled by A.-M.Gontier and is derived by sequentially assembling the data from the following primary sources:

1.Large Quasar Astrometric Catalog (LQAC),acompilation of 12 largest quasar catalogues containing113666 quasars,which provided informationwhen available on photometry,redshift,and radio fluxes(Souchayetal.2oo9,availableatURL[2] as catalogue J/A+A/494/799).This sourcewasused to provide information on flux at $8 . 4 \ : \mathrm { G H z }$ and $2 . 3 \ : \mathrm { G H z }$ and initial information for the redshift and the magnitude. 2.Optical Characteristics of Astrometric Radio Sources which includes 4261 radio sources with J2000.O coordinates,redshift, $V$ magnitude,object type and comments (Malkin and Titov2008;URL[23]). 3.CatalogueofQuasars andActiveGalacticNuclei,12th Edition which includes85221 quasars,1122BLLac objects and 21737 active galaxies togetherwith known lensed

quasarsand double quasars (Véron-Cettyand Véron 2OO6,availableat URL[2] as catalogue VII/248,also used for the table of quasars in $\ S 1 3 . 3 . 4 _ { \scriptscriptstyle }$ ).   
4.AnAll-Sky Survey ofFlat-Spectrum Radio Sourcesproviding precise positions,subarcsecond structures,and spectral indices for some 11ooO sources (Healey et al.2007, available at URL[2] ascatalogue J/ApJS/171/61).   
5.TheOptical Spectroscopy of1Jy,S4andS5Radio Source Identifications which gives position,magnitude,type of the optical identification,flux at 5GHz and two-point spectral index between $2 . 7 \operatorname { G H z }$ and5GHz (Stickel and Kuehr 1994;Stickel,Fried, and Kuehr1989,available at URL[2] as catalogue II/175).

# 13.4.2 Radio-FluxCalibrators

Calibration isa necessary step in the reduction of radio flux observations for research purposes.Absolute calibration of radio flux data is required to delete systematic effects such as those from the receiver,the antenna,or the atmosphere.An accurate calibration ofthe observations enables comparison of data observed at different telescopes and at different times.All fields of radio astronomy require accurate absolute calibration,especially variability studies of radio sources.Calibration of measurements also allows the means to determine the telescope efficiency,offering the possibility to study the properties (e.g.,surface RMS) of the antenna.

Using sources,like the ones given in The Astronomical Almanac,are the easiest way to calibratearadio telescope.A direct,absolute calibration is very difficult especially for large telescopes (if possible at all).The three primary standards-the strongest three radio sources (CasA,CygA,and Tau A)—are unsuitable for the calibration of large antennas.Absolute calibration of these three primary standards requires the use of small antennas given these sources,particularly at high frequencies,have finite sizesandareall partially resolved using largeantennas.It is necessary to havea list of secondary calibrators,the flux densities of which are determined with reference to these primary standards.Many,if not all,centimeter wavelength radio telescopes use the secondary flux density standards provided in this table.

The data in the table were originally taken from Baars et al.(1977)and updated nearly everyyearby Kraus,Krichbaum,Pauliny-Toth andWitzel with new measurements.The original data are derived as follows:

1.The absolute spectrum of Cas A,with its secular rate of decrease taken into account, was derived for epoch198o.0 between frequenciesof $3 0 0 \mathrm { M H z }$ and $3 0 \mathrm { G H z }$ .   
2.The absolute spectra of Cyg Aand Tau Awere derived from direct measurements and from measurements of the ratios of their flux density to that of CasA.   
3.Anaccurate spectrum for the somewhat weaker source Vir A was established from direct,accurate ratios of its flux density to those of Cas A and CygA.   
4.This VirA spectrum was used as a basis for obtaining accurate relative spectra of the secondary sources provided in the table.

Forflux calibration of interferometers and synthesis telescopes,only three sources are suitable:3C48,3C147,and 3C286.Positions for 3C48 and 3C147are taken from Elsmore and Ryle(1976).The position of 3C 286 is taken from the list of astrometric radio sources. Other sources may need acorrection for angular size when used with the largest single antennas at high frequencies.

There are two important notes of caution in using these standards.Extrapolation of flux densities beyond the given frequency range printed is not advisable.Also,some sources contain compact,opaque components that may be variable.For example,3C48 showssome variability around $1 . 6 \mathrm { G H z }$ and $2 0 \mathrm { G H z }$ (PerleyandCrane 1985).The flux density of 3C147is variable (Simonetal.1983;Andrew,MacLeod,andFeldman1981) asis its structure (Preuss etal.1984).Foraccurate calibrations offlux density measurements,several sources from the tableshouldbeutilizedinordertoaccountforsucheffects.

A table with polarization data for the most prominent sources is also provided by A. Kraus which gives the polarization degree and angle for a number of frequencies.

# 13.4.3 X-Ray and Gamma Ray Sources

In the broadest terms,an X-ray or gamma ray source can be defined as any source emitting X-rays or gamma rays.Since most $\mathrm { _ X }$ -rays and gamma rays are emitted when anabrupt change occurs in a charged particle's trajectory near the source,sources are required to have a strong gravitational/magnetic field.The types of X-ray and gamma ray sources have increased over the years as instruments became better able to detect such sources.It has long been known that our Sun is both an $\mathrm { \Delta X }$ -ray and gamma ray source (many X-ray sources turn out to be gamma ray sources,but not all),and ithasalso been known forawhile that black holesand neutronstars (both isolated onesand thosein binary systems,whetherapulsar or not) are both X-ray and gamma ray sources. Other sources are active galactic nuclei(AGNs),quasars,many galaxies,and supernovae.More recently,it has been discovered thatthe intergalactic medium in some parts of the sky emits copious amounts of X-rays.Gamma ray bursts (GRBs)are the most energeticof gammaray sources,but they have also been found to produce X-raysafter the initial burst of gamma rays.Even the source of so-called “cosmic rays”has been recently discovered to be many very faint X-ray and gamma ray sources,that mix theiremissions with those from more easily identified sources to produce the cosmic ray background that has been known formanyyears.

To detect X-ray sources,instruments must be placed outside the Earth's atmosphere, since it does a good job of blocking most X-rays from geting to the Earth'ssurface.Most gammarays are blocked by the atmosphere so many gammaray instruments are put into orbit also.However,the most energetic gamma rays (those in theGeV-TeVenergy range) can get through the Earth'satmosphere and so there are some gamma ray instruments on the ground that seek to detect these very energetic particles.

These sources are of interest to optical astronomers for multiple reasons.It is of interest to know,for example in the case ofX-ray binaries,what kindof star is supplying the matter that feeds the black hole or neutron star.By knowing its magnitude (whether visual or infrared) an educated guess can be made about the size and age of the companion star.This is useful for determining the orbit of the X-ray or gamma ray source and its mass and age.Itis also interesting to know how the X-ray/gamma ray source affects itscompanion star.The loss of mass from the companion to the X-ray/gammaray source can change how the star evolves. In some cases,it has been speculated that the known X-ray binary was initially two non X-Ray emitting starsina binary system,and their interaction caused a supernova that gave birth to theX-ray source that we now detect.In the case of isolated pulsars,many of which arefirstobserved at radio wavelengths,it is interesting to know if thesepulsars emit energy at other wavelengths.Manydo;the Crab pulsarand the Vela pulsar being two well-known examples.Even more recently,once GRBs were shown to have an X-ray afterglow,further optical observations revealed an optical afterglow as well.And of course,since the source of GRBs was (and still is) unknown,these optical observations often showed a galaxy at or near the supposed location of the GRB.The current understanding of GRB sources is that they occur in galaxies and might be even more energetic than Supernovae (one theory calls these sources“Hypernovae").Optical observations of galaxies,AGN,and Quasarscanagain give some insight to these X-ray/gamma ray sources by determining theirage,what kind of stars they are composed of,and other posible mechanisms that might help the high-energy astronomer understand the X-ray/Gamma ray emission process from these sources.

TheX-Ray and gammaraysources listed in The Astronomical Almanac arewel-studied andhistorically important sources drawn from published results,selected byindividualsin this particular field of research.The X-Ray binary sources are from the catalogs of van Paradijs (1995)and Liu,van Paradijs and van den Heuvel (2Ooo,20o1).Other sources are selected from the Fourth Uhuru Catalog (Forman etal.1978).Positions for X-ray sources are taken from NOMAD as described in $\ S \ 1 3 . 1 . 3 . 1$ (URL[3]).The gamma ray sources are selected from three catalogs to cover their broad energy range:low-energy gamma rays (photon energies $< 1 0 0 \ \mathrm { k e V }$ ）aretakenfromTheFourthIBIS/ISGRISoftGamma-Ray Survey Catalog (Bird etal.2O1O); high-energy gamma rays (photon energies between $1 0 0 \mathrm { M e V }$ and $1 0 0 \mathrm { G e V } )$ aretakenfrom FermiLargeArea TelescopeSecond SourceCatalog (Fermi-LATCollaboration 2O11);and very-high-energy gamma rays (photon energies above $1 0 0 \mathrm { G e V }$ )are taken from TeVCatOnlineCatalogforTeVAstronomy availableatURL[24]. Some sources are bright in two or all three energy ranges.

# Computational Techniques

R.L.DUNCOMBE

# 14.1 Introduction to Computing Techniques

In this section we will examine several computing techniques of special relevance to astronomical calculation.For a more general treatment,see textbooks on computing methods and numericalanalysis.A short note onelementary computing principles isalsoincludedin Interpolation and Allied Tables (H.M.Nautical Almanac Office 1956),which covers the sources of mistakes,the nature of checks,and the nature and effect of the inevitable errors due to rounding-off and other causes.These topics,although of fundamental importance,are not discussed here.

An important principle of computation is that the data used determine the maximum precision of a calculation.This precision can be reduced by poor computing—for example, byapoor choice of formula or by the failure to retain an adequate number of figures in the intermediate stages-but it can never be increased.Generally no simple relation exists between the errors (absolute or relative)of the final result andof the data,though arelation can readily be seen numerically by following through the calculation step by step,and often geometrically.It is misleading to quotearesult to more figures than is justifiedby the data on which it is based,and it is erroneous todo so ifan inadequate number of figures have been retained in the intermediate stages.

Retaining more figures than necessary is a waste of effort if it significantly increases the work involved.In some operations,however, especially in those done on a computer, extra figures involve very litleadditional work;but in other operations,anextra figure may require amore elaborate formula or extra word length,resulting in much more additional work.A suitable number of figures is that which offers the greatest convenience consistent with the building-up error,due to accumulation of rounding-offs,not exceeding the errorof the data; thismust be judged in relation to each calculation.

Mathematical formulae,if correct,adequately give a desired precision in the result;but they may be inefficient, inconvenient,and misleading.One example is the inefficiency of determining a small angle from its cosine;for a stated precision of angular measurement the number of decimals in the cosineis inversely proportional to the angle;and forafixed number of decimals in the cosine (the usual and convenient case)the precision obtainable decreases asthe angle decreases.Consider,for example,the formula for the third side of a spherical triangle,(Fig.14.1) given two sides and the included angle,

$$
\cos a = \cos b \cos c + \sin b \sin c \cos A = z
$$

![](../chunks/Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Almanac (Sean Urban, P. Kenneth Seidelmann) (z-_part_0015_pages_0561-0600/ocr/images/2dbae04a629c43e73ce2b5415ae5db661ba5c03c4c90aeb40fc9c6e160aa307f.jpg)  
Figure14.1 Spherical triangle

in which $a$ is to be determined from measured values of $b , c .$ and $A$ .When $a$ is small, it can be found to the same precision as the data only by the apparently incorrect procedure of retaining more decimals in each trigonometric function on the right-hand side(z)of the equation than the data appearto justify.This is legitimate because both $b - c$ and $A$ must be small so that their cosines are known to extra figures.There are circumstances,however,in which the inconvenience of such a procedure is outweighted by other factors.

The apparent failure of a mathematical formula should not be confused with real geometrical limitations of precision.In the spherical triangle $A B C$ ,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \sin a \sin B = \sin b \sin A = x } \\ { \sin a \cos B = \cos b \sin c - \sin b \cos c \cos A = y ; } \end{array}
$$

both $x$ and $y$ are small ( $y$ by the cancellation of two nearly equal components) if $a$ is small. It is not legitimate here to use extra figures in the trigonometric functions in $x$ and $y$ ,and $B$ can be found with a precision only proportional to csc $^ a$ ;thisprecision is,however,clearly adequate to fix the point $c$ (from $B A$ ).Similarargumentshold fortheangle $c$ .Neither $B$ nor $c$ canindividually be determinedaspreciselyas $^ b$ , $c$ ,or $A$ ;but the sum $B + C$ can be so determined,as may be seen from geometric considerations and from the equation

$$
\cot { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( B + C ) = { \frac { \cos { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( b + c ) } { \cos { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( b - c ) } } \tan { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } A .
$$

Important problems in astronomy may require the sum (or difference) of two angles to be known more precisely than is possible with either (for example,the elements of a planetary orbit);therefore,great care is required in handling them.The recommended method is to determine one of the angles and then treat it as exact in finding the other,which must not be found independently.

Although there may be some uncertainty of precision in determining an angle,there should be no uncertainty of quadrant.Thiscan always be achieved by adequate choice of formulae.For example,the followingformulae for the solution of a spherical triangle,in which two sidesand the included angleare given,are alwaysadequate if the sides are less than $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ .

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l r l } & { \sin a \sin B = \sin b \sin A } & & { = x } \\ & { \sin a \cos B = \cos b \sin c - \sin b \cos c \cos A } & & { = y } \\ & { \cos a = \cos b \cos c + \sin b \sin c \cos A } & & { = z } \\ & { \sin a \cos C = \sin b \cos c - \cos b \sin c \cos A } & \\ & { \sin a \sin C = \sin c \sin A . } & & { } \end{array} }
$$

The check $x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } - 1 = 0$ is useful. The sign of $z$ determines the quadrant of $a$ and the signs of $x$ and $y$ that of $B$ ；similarlywith $C$ .

In astronomical problems,the sides and angles of triangles on the celestial sphere may beofany magnitude,and itisundesirable to solve themby methods that restrict the sides to arcs less than $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Thepreceding general formulae are all valid for triangleswith sides ofany length,and may beapplied immediatelyto the general triangles of spherical astronomy without any restriction to values less than $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ .Furthermore,to make the solution determinate,itis only necessary to find thealgebraicsign of both the sine and the cosine of eacharc or angle thatmay exceed $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ tofixthe quadrantsinwhich they lie.Any of the casesof thegeneral triangleisdeterminatewhen,inadditiontothe threegivenparts,the algebraic sign of the cosine of one of the required partsis also given.In most practical problems the conditions of theproblem supply this sign.In general triangles the utmost care should be taken to specify unambiguously the direction of measurement of angles and arcs.

Collectively,the preceding formulae are suficient forthe solution of the general triangle without restriction on the magnitudes of the parts,but,in practice,the additional formulae

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \cos A = - \cos B \cos C + \sin B \sin C \cos a } \\ { \sin A \cos b = \cos B \sin C + \sin B \cos C \cos a } \\ { \sin A \cos c = \sin B \cos C + \cos B \sin C \cos a } \\ { \sin A \cot B = \sin c \cot b - \cos c \cos A } \\ { \sin A \cot C = \sin b \cot c - \cos b \cos A } \end{array}
$$

are very useful. (Collected formulae are given in Table 14.5.)

In practice,the trigonometric functions of $b , c ,$ and $A$ are taken out with a number of decimals depending on the precision of the data,and the capacity of the computer to be used. Table14.1 gives corresponding precisions of angle and number of decimals.

Ifno additional work is involved,itis clearly advantageous to use more decimals than the precision warrants,since the effectof rounding-off errors is then much reduced.For instance, seven decimals give just sufficient coverage for data known to about $0 _ { \cdot } ^ { \prime \prime } 0 2$ and for results to

Table 14.1 Precision of angle and number of decimals for trigonometric functions
